## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Differentiate between Yield Strength and Fracture Strength. Why is residual stress particularly important in MEMS thin-film structures?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Yield Strength (σ_y):** The maximum stress a material can withstand without undergoing *permanent (plastic)* deformation. Beyond this point, the material deforms irreversibly.
>
> **Fracture Strength (σ_f):** The stress at which the material physically breaks or ruptures. In brittle materials, σ_f ≈ σ_y (the material breaks before yielding).

**Importance of Residual Stress in MEMS:**
1. Residual stress arises during thin-film deposition (e.g., LPCVD polysilicon) due to mismatched thermal expansion, grain growth, or impurity incorporation.
2. **Compressive residual stress** can cause long fixed-fixed beams to *buckle* (Euler instability).
3. **Stress gradients** (through-thickness variation) cause cantilevers to *curl* out-of-plane, ruining device planarity.
4. Residual stress alters the effective spring constant and resonant frequency of beams.
5. High tensile stress can cause fracture; high compressive stress can cause delamination or stiction.

**KTU-Marking-Scheme-Aligned Scoring (3 marks):**
- Definition of σ_y (1 mark)
- Definition of σ_f (1 mark)
- Two valid reasons why residual stress matters in MEMS (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**List any three commonly used mechanical structures in MEMS and give one example commercial application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cantilever Beam** — Used in **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) probes** for surface topography measurement.
2. **Fixed-Fixed Beam** — Used in **MEMS accelerometers** as the suspension spring for the proof mass.
3. **Diaphragm (Plate)** — Used in **MEMS pressure sensors** (e.g., blood pressure transducers, barometric sensors).
4. *(Any other valid structure + application may be substituted)*

**KTU-Marking-Scheme-Aligned Scoring (3 marks):**
- 1 mark per valid structure-application pair (3 marks total)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** For 14-mark questions, KTU typically requires a two-part structure with internal choice between sub-parts. We provide two complete alternatives (Question A and Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Cantilever Beam Analysis

**Q.A. (a)** **[7 Marks]** — Derive the expression for the deflection of a cantilever beam of length L, rectangular cross-section (width w, thickness t), subjected to a uniformly distributed load q (N/m) over its entire length. Hence obtain the spring constant of the beam.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Load distribution:**
The total load is F = qL, distributed uniformly.

**Step 2 — Bending moment at position x (measured from clamped end):**
$$M(x) = \frac{q(L-x)^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Governing equation:**
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{q(L-x)^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to get slope:**
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{q (L-x)^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Apply BC at x = 0: dy/dx = 0:**
$$0 = -\frac{q L^3}{6} + C_1 \implies C_1 = \frac{qL^3}{6}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate to get deflection:**
$$EI y(x) = \frac{q (L-x)^4}{24} + \frac{qL^3}{6} x + C_2$$

**Step 7 — Apply BC at x = 0: y(0) = 0:**
$$0 = \frac{qL^4}{24} + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{qL^4}{24}$$

**Step 8 — Deflection profile:**
$$y(x) = \frac{q}{24 EI} \left[ (L-x)^4 + 4 L^3 x - L^4 \right]$$

**Step 9 — Tip deflection at x = L:**
$$\delta = y(L) = \frac{q}{24 EI} \left[ 0 + 4L^4 - L^4 \right] = \frac{3 q L^4}{24 EI} = \frac{q L^4}{8 EI}$$

**Step 10 — Total tip force equivalent: F_eq = qL:**

$$k = \frac{F_{eq}}{\delta} = \frac{qL}{\frac{qL^4}{8EI}} = \frac{8EI}{L^3}$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{k_{cantilever,UDL} = \frac{8 EI}{L^3} = \frac{2 E w t^3}{3 L^3}, \quad \delta_{tip,UDL} = \frac{qL^4}{8EI}}$$

---

**Q.A. (b)** **[7 Marks]** — A MEMS cantilever accelerometer has a polysilicon beam of length L = 500 μm, width w = 50 μm, and thickness t = 5 μm. Given E = 160 GPa, ρ = 2330 kg/m³ for polysilicon, calculate (i) the spring constant k, (ii) the static tip deflection when a 1 g acceleration (g = 9.81 m/s²) is applied to a proof mass of m = 10⁻⁷ kg attached at the tip, and (iii) the fundamental resonant frequency f₀.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Spring constant:**
The moment of inertia for the rectangular cross-section is:
$$I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 125 \times 10^{-18}}{12}$$
$$I = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-21}}{12} = 5.208 \times 10^{-22} \text{ m}^4$$

The spring constant:
$$k = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times (160 \times 10^9) \times (5.208 \times 10^{-22})}{(500 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$
$$k = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-10}}{1.25 \times 10^{-13}} = 2.0 \text{ N/m}$$

**(ii) Static tip deflection:**
The inertial force is:
$$F = m a = 10^{-7} \times 9.81 = 9.81 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}$$

The deflection is:
$$\delta = \frac{F}{k} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-7}}{2.0} = 4.905 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 0.49 \text{ μm}$$

**(iii) Fundamental resonant frequency:**
Cross-sectional area:
$$A = w t = (50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2$$

The resonant frequency:
$$f_0 = \frac{0.5615}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$
$$\frac{EI}{\rho A} = \frac{(160 \times 10^9)(5.208 \times 10^{-22})}{(2330)(2.5 \times 10^{-10})}$$
$$= \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-11}}{5.825 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.430 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} = 1.196 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{0.5615}{(500 \times 10^{-6})^2} \times 1.196 \times 10^{-2} = \frac{0.5615}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} \times 1.196 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$f_0 = 2.246 \times 10^6 \times 1.196 \times 10^{-2} = 2.686 \times 10^4 \text{ Hz} \approx 26.86 \text{ kHz}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** k = 2.0 N/m
- **(ii)** δ = 0.49 μm
- **(iii)** f₀ ≈ 26.86 kHz

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Fixed-Fixed Beam Analysis

**Q.B. (a)** **[7 Marks]** — With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a fixed-fixed MEMS beam structure. Discuss how residual stress affects its performance, and derive the Euler buckling load.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (with diagram description):**
A fixed-fixed beam is fabricated by:
1. Defining two anchor regions on a substrate (typically Si with a thin SiO₂ isolation layer).
2. Depositing a sacrificial layer (e.g., SiO₂) of thickness equal to the desired air gap.
3. Patterning anchor openings through the sacrificial layer.
4. Depositing the structural layer (e.g., polysilicon) by LPCVD.
5. Patterning the beam by photolithography and reactive-ion etching (RIE).
6. Releasing the beam by etching the sacrificial layer in HF (for SiO₂) — a process called *release etch*.

**Effect of Residual Stress on Performance:**

> **Biaxial residual stress (σ_r):**
> - **Compressive σ_r:** Causes axial compression. If the compressive load exceeds the Euler buckling load P_cr, the beam buckles out of plane — a catastrophic failure.
> - **Tensile σ_r:** Does not cause buckling but adds to the bending stress under load, potentially causing fracture.
> - **Stress gradient (∇σ):** Causes the beam to bow upward or downward (similar to a bimetallic strip), changing its as-fabricated shape.

**Derivation of Euler Buckling Load:**

The Euler buckling load is the axial compressive force P at which a slender column (beam) becomes unstable and buckles. For a fixed-fixed beam, the effective length is K·L where K = 0.5 (both ends fixed). The standard Euler formula is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(KL)^2}$$

For a fixed-fixed beam (K = 0.5):

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(0.5 L)^2} = \frac{4 \pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

For a rectangular cross-section (I = w t³ / 12):

$$P_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2 E w t^3}{12 L^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E w t^3}{3 L^2}$$

**Design Implication:** The corresponding critical stress is:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{wt} = \frac{\pi^2 E t^2}{3 L^2}$$

If the residual stress σ_r > σ_cr, the beam buckles. To prevent buckling, the designer must ensure:
$$\sigma_r < \frac{\pi^2 E t^2}{3 L^2}$$

This places a constraint on the **maximum allowable L/t ratio** for a given process.

**Result:**
$$\boxed{P_{cr,\text{fixed-fixed}} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2} \cdot 4 = \frac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \frac{4\pi^2 E w t^3}{12 L^2}}$$

---

**Q.B. (b)** **[7 Marks]** — A fixed-fixed MEMS beam resonator is designed with the following parameters: L = 200 μm, w = 10 μm, t = 2 μm, E = 160 GPa, ρ = 2330 kg/m³. Calculate the fundamental resonant frequency f₀, the spring constant k, and the static deflection under a center load of 10 μN. Comment on the suitability of this design for a 1 MHz oscillator application.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric quantities:**
$$I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{10^{-5} \times 8 \times 10^{-18}}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-24} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A = w t = (10 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Spring constant (center load on fixed-fixed):**
$$k = \frac{192 EI}{L^3} = \frac{192 \times (160 \times 10^9) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-24})}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

$$k = \frac{192 \times 1.067 \times 10^{-12}}{8 \times 10^{-15}} = \frac{2.0486 \times 10^{-10}}{8 \times 10^{-15}} = 25.6 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 3 — Static deflection:**
$$\delta = \frac{F}{k} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{25.6} = 3.906 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 0.39 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 4 — Fundamental resonant frequency:**
$$f_0 = \frac{1.028}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

$$\frac{EI}{\rho A} = \frac{(160 \times 10^9)(6.667 \times 10^{-24})}{(2330)(2 \times 10^{-11})}$$
$$= \frac{1.067 \times 10^{-12}}{4.66 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.289 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} = 4.785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1.028}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2} \times 4.785 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= \frac{1.028}{4 \times 10^{-8}} \times 4.785 \times 10^{-3} = 2.57 \times 10^7 \times 4.785 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$f_0 = 1.23 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} \approx 123 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Suitability for 1 MHz oscillator:**
The fundamental resonant frequency is ~123 kHz, which is far below the 1 MHz target. To achieve 1 MHz, the beam must be either:
1. **Shorter** (reduce L by factor √(123/1000) ≈ 0.35, i.e., L ≈ 70 μm), or
2. **Stiffer** (increase t, since f₀ ∝ t/L²), or
3. Operate in a higher mode (mode 3 or 4), or
4. Use a different material with higher E/ρ ratio.

**Conclusion:** As designed, this resonator is **not suitable** for 1 MHz operation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up fixed-fixed vs. simply-supported:** Students often write k = 192EI/L³ for a simply-supported beam, or k = 48EI/L³ for fixed-fixed. Memorize the ratio: **k(FF) : k(SS) : k(cantilever) = 192 : 48 : 3**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the cubic dependence on t:** A beam that is 2× thicker is 8× stiffer. Students often write "doubling t doubles the stiffness" — this loses marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing yield strength and fracture strength:** Remember: brittle materials (silicon) fracture before yielding, so σ_y ≈ σ_f.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the stress gradient:** Many students only consider biaxial residual stress and forget the through-thickness gradient, which causes cantilever curling.
>
> 5. **Unit errors:** Mixing μm with m, GPa with Pa. Always convert to SI units **before** plugging into formulas.
>
> 6. **Wrong effective length K in Euler buckling:** For fixed-fixed, K = 0.5; for cantilever (free end), K = 2; for simply-supported, K = 1.
>
> 7. **Numerical errors in f₀ calculation:** Forgetting the dimensionless prefactor (0.5615 for cantilever, 1.028 for fixed-fixed, 0.5615 for simply-supported) leads to a wrong answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Properties & MEMS Beams**
>
> **A. Material Properties:**
> - [ ] Young's Modulus (E) — silicon: 130 GPa ⟨100⟩, 190 GPa ⟨111⟩
> - [ ] Poisson's Ratio (ν) — silicon: 0.22–0.28
> - [ ] Density (ρ) — silicon: 2330 kg/m³
> - [ ] Yield Strength vs. Fracture Strength — silicon is brittle, so σ_y ≈ σ_f
> - [ ] Residual stress has two components: biaxial (σ_r) and gradient (∇σ)
> - [ ] Damping sources: squeeze-film, anchor loss, thermoelastic, intrinsic
> - [ ] Surface-to-volume ratio scales as 1/L → dominates at micro-scale
>
> **B. Beam Mechanics:**
> - [ ] Three canonical types: cantilever, fixed-fixed, simply-supported
> - [ ] Euler-Bernoulli equation: EI d⁴y/dx⁴ = w(x)
> - [ ] Moment of inertia for rectangle: I = wt³/12 (cubic in thickness)
> - [ ] Cantilever: k = 3EI/L³ = Ewt³/(4L³), δ = FL³/(3EI)
> - [ ] Fixed-fixed (center load): k = 192EI/L³ = 16Ewt³/L³
> - [ ] Simply-supported (center load): k = 48EI/L³ = 4Ewt³/L³
> - [ ] Euler buckling (fixed-fixed): P_cr = 4π²EI/L²
> - [ ] Cantilever f₀: 0.5615/L² × √(EI/ρA)
> - [ ] Fixed-fixed f₀: 1.028/L² × √(EI/ρA)
>
> **C. Commercial Applications:**
> - [ ] Cantilever → AFM, RF switches, bio/chemical sensors
> - [ ] Fixed-fixed → Accelerometer springs, MEMS resonators, gyroscope suspension
> - [ ] Simply-supported → Pressure sensor diaphragms
> - [ ] Comb drives → Capacitive actuation/sensing
> - [ ] Diaphragm → Microphones, pressure sensors
>
> **D. Numerical Conversions to Remember:**
> - [ ] 1 GPa = 10⁹ Pa
> - [ ] 1 μm = 10⁻⁶ m
> - [ ] 1 g acceleration = 9.81 m/s²
> - [ ] Prefactor for cantilever f₀ ≈ 0.5615
> - [ ] Prefactor for fixed-fixed f₀ ≈ 1.028
> - [ ] Stiffness ratio FF : SS : Cantilever = 64 : 16 : 1 (same L, w, t)
>
> **E. KTU 2024 Key Takeaways:**
> - [ ] Material properties decide whether a MEMS device will function, fail, or fatigue.
> - [ ] Beams are the most common mechanical structure — know all three types.
> - [ ] Always convert to SI units before calculating.
> - [ ] In derivations, state boundary conditions explicitly — they are worth marks.
> - [ ] Draw neat diagrams with labels — KTU evaluators reward clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cantilevers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cantilevers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **cantilever** is a **rigid, beam-like micro-mechanical structure** that is **anchored (clamped) at one end** and **free to deflect at the other end**. In Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), cantilevers are the **most fundamental and widely used structural building block**, typically fabricated from materials such as **monocrystalline silicon, polysilicon, silicon nitride, SU-8 polymer, or thin metal films** using surface or bulk micromachining processes.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a MEMS cantilever is described as a **one-dimensional (1-D) flexural mechanical transducer** that converts an input physical, chemical, or biological stimulus into a measurable mechanical response — namely **static deflection**, **change in resonance frequency**, or **change in surface stress**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A cantilever in MEMS is treated as a slender elastic beam whose length L is much greater than its width w and thickness t, i.e., **L >> w >> t**. This slender-beam assumption allows the use of **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory** for both static and dynamic analysis.

The cantilever is mathematically treated as a beam of length L, rectangular cross-section of width b and thickness t, with material properties: **Young's modulus E**, **density ρ**, and **Poisson's ratio ν**.

The relevant second moment of area (area moment of inertia) about the neutral axis is:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{b\,t^{3}}{12}
$$

The cross-sectional area is:

$$
A \;=\; b\,t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Metric:** The slenderness ratio is defined as $L/t$ and is typically in the range **10 to 1000** for MEMS cantilevers. Higher slenderness gives higher flexibility and lower spring constant, but reduces the first natural frequency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine holding a **diving board** at one end with your hands (the **fixed/clamped end**) while the other end hangs freely over a swimming pool (the **free end**). When a person stands on the free end, the board bends downward — this is a **cantilever in action**.

In the microscopic world, MEMS cantilevers behave exactly the same way, except:
- They are **thinner than a human hair** (typical thickness = 0.5 µm to 50 µm).
- They are made of **silicon** instead of wood.
- The "load" can be a **force, a mass, a molecule binding on the surface, or even thermal expansion**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — Diving board:** Clamped end = diving board's bolted support. Free end = board tip. Load at the tip = jumper's weight. Bending = tip deflection. Stiffness = how stiff the diving board feels.

## 1.3 Types of MEMS Cantilevers

Cantilevers are broadly classified based on their **geometry** and **sensing mechanism**:

| # | Type | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Application |
|---|------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Rectangular cantilever** | Uniform rectangular cross-section | Generic force/mass sensors |
| 2 | **T-shaped (Tersoff) cantilever** | Wider paddle at free end | **AFM probes** (reduces tip–sample convolution) |
| 3 | **V-shaped (V-lever) cantilever** | Two beams converging to a tip | **AFM imaging**, force sensing |
| 4 | **Piezoelectric cantilever** | Top/bottom electrode + piezo layer (ZnO, AlN, PZT) | Energy harvesting, active sensing |
| 5 | **Bimetallic (thermal) cantilever** | Two layers of different thermal expansion coefficient | IR sensors, thermal actuators |
| 6 | **Functionalized cantilever** | Surface coated with receptor molecules | **Biosensors** (DNA, proteins, gases) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The **V-shaped (V-lever) and T-shaped cantilevers** are the most commonly asked AFM probe geometries in the KTU university exam because they reduce mechanical drift and offer low spring constants (~ 0.01 N/m to 1 N/m).

## 1.4 Why Cantilevers are Central to MEMS

Cantilevers act as the **primary transduction element** in:
- **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** — tip–sample interaction
- **Mass/Chemical sensors** — frequency shift upon mass loading
- **Accelerometers** — inertial mass deflection
- **Pressure sensors** — diaphragm-coupled cantilever
- **RF MEMS switches** — electrostatic actuation
- **Energy harvesters** — vibration-to-voltage conversion
- **Biosensors** — surface stress changes upon molecular binding

The versatility arises from a single elegant principle: **a cantilever is a mechanical low-pass filter whose output (deflection or frequency) is exquisitely sensitive to small perturbations.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory — The Backbone of Cantilever Analysis

The Euler–Bernoulli theory rests on four core assumptions, all of which are **very well satisfied** by MEMS cantilevers due to their high slenderness:

1. The beam material is **linearly elastic** (Hooke's law valid).
2. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (shear deformation neglected).
3. The beam is **initially straight and prismatic** (uniform cross-section).
4. The beam length is much greater than its cross-sectional dimensions (slenderness condition).

> [!NOTE]
> For MEMS beams, the **Timoshenko beam correction** is required only when **L/t < 20** or when operating at very high frequencies. For typical AFM cantilevers with L/t > 100, Euler–Bernoulli is sufficient.

The governing differential equation for the static deflection $w(x)$ of a cantilever under load is:

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\mathrm{d}^{4}w(x)}{\mathrm{d}x^{4}} \;=\; q(x)
$$

where $q(x)$ is the distributed load per unit length.

For dynamic (free vibration) analysis, the equation becomes:

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\partial^{4}w(x,t)}{\partial x^{4}} \;+\; \rho\,A\,\frac{\partial^{2}w(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} \;=\; 0
$$

## 2.2 Static Deflection Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Point Load F at the Free End (Most Common Case)

This is the canonical KTU problem. The bending moment at a distance x from the free end is:

$$
M(x) \;=\; F\,(L - x)
$$

The Euler–Bernoulli equation gives the slope:

$$
\theta(x) \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; \frac{F}{2\,E\,I}\,\Big[\,L\,x^{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{3} - \frac{2\,L^{3}}{3}\,\Big]
$$

Integrating again with boundary conditions $w(0) = 0$ and $\theta(0) = 0$ (fixed end), the **tip deflection** becomes:

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; w(L) \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}
$$

The **maximum bending stress** at the fixed-end top/bottom surface is:

$$
\sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{M_{max}\,c}{I} \;=\; \frac{6\,F\,L}{b\,t^{2}}
$$

The **spring constant (stiffness)** of the cantilever is:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{F}{\delta_{tip}} \;=\; \frac{3\,E\,I}{L^{3}} \;=\; \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Point Load F at Distance a from Fixed End

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{F\,a^{2}\,(3L - a)}{6\,E\,I}
$$

### 2.2.3 Uniformly Distributed Load q Over Entire Length (UDL)

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{q\,L^{4}}{8\,E\,I} \;=\; \frac{3\,q\,L^{4}}{2\,E\,b\,t^{3}}
$$

### 2.2.4 Uniform Load Over Partial Length a (0 ≤ a ≤ L)

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{q\,a^{2}\,(6L^{2} - 4aL + a^{2})}{24\,E\,I}
$$

## 2.3 Dynamic / Vibration Analysis — The Eigenvalue Problem

The **free-vibration** cantilever equation is:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}^{4}w(x)}{\mathrm{d}x^{4}} \;-\; \beta^{4}\,w(x) \;=\; 0
$$

where $\beta^{4} = \dfrac{\omega^{2}\,\rho\,A}{E\,I}$.

Applying boundary conditions:
- At fixed end ($x = 0$): $w = 0$ and $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} = 0$
- At free end ($x = L$): $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}^{2}w}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}^{3}w}{\mathrm{d}x^{3}} = 0$

This produces the **transcendental eigenvalue equation**:

$$
\cos(\beta L)\,\cosh(\beta L) \;+\; 1 \;=\; 0
$$

The roots $\beta L$ are: **1.875, 4.694, 7.855, 10.996, ...**

The **fundamental (first-mode) natural frequency** is:

$$
f_{1} \;=\; \frac{(1.875)^{2}}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}} \;\approx\; 0.5605\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}
$$

For higher modes, replace the constant 0.5605 with $\dfrac{(\beta_{n}L)^{2}}{2\pi}$ where $\beta_{n}L$ are the successive roots.

## 2.4 Mass-Loading Effect — The Key to Cantilever Mass Sensors

When an additional mass $\Delta m$ is deposited on the tip of a vibrating cantilever, the **resonance frequency drops**. For $\Delta m \ll m_{beam}$ (small mass loading), the linearized frequency shift is:

$$
\Delta f \;\approx\; -\frac{f_{1}}{2}\,\frac{\Delta m}{m_{beam}}
$$

This is the operating principle of **cantilever-based mass sensors** with attogram (10⁻¹⁸ g) sensitivity.

## 2.5 Surface Stress and Stoney's Formula — Biosensing Mechanism

When molecules bind selectively to one surface of a functionalized cantilever, a differential **surface stress** $\Delta\sigma_{s}$ develops. This bends the beam, producing a curvature $\kappa$ given by **Stoney's formula**:

$$
\kappa \;=\; \frac{6\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}
$$

The tip deflection is:

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{3\,L^{2}\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Stoney's formula is derived assuming the coating thickness is much smaller than the substrate thickness. It is the **cornerstone of cantilever biosensor design** and is routinely asked in KTU board exams.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essential for OEICT612 Module 1.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Validity |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Area moment of inertia | $I = \dfrac{b\,t^{3}}{12}$ | m⁴ | Rectangular cross-section, bending about neutral axis |
| 2 | Cross-sectional area | $A = b\,t$ | m² | — |
| 3 | Mass per unit length | $m' = \rho\,A = \rho\,b\,t$ | kg/m | $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 4 | Tip deflection (point load at tip) | $\delta = \dfrac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}$ | m | Most common case |
| 5 | Spring constant (tip load) | $k = \dfrac{3\,E\,I}{L^{3}} = \dfrac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}$ | N/m | Fundamental KTU formula |
| 6 | Max bending stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6\,F\,L}{b\,t^{2}}$ | Pa | Occurs at fixed-end surface |
| 7 | Tip deflection (UDL) | $\delta = \dfrac{q\,L^{4}}{8\,E\,I}$ | m | Uniform load $q$ (N/m) |
| 8 | Tip deflection (point load at distance a) | $\delta = \dfrac{F\,a^{2}\,(3L - a)}{6\,E\,I}$ | m | $a$ measured from fixed end |
| 9 | Fundamental natural frequency | $f_{1} = 0.5605\,\sqrt{\dfrac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}$ | Hz | $0.5605 = (1.875)^{2}/(2\pi)$ |
| 10 | $n^{th}$ mode natural frequency | $f_{n} = \dfrac{(\beta_{n}L)^{2}}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}$ | Hz | $\beta_{n}L$ roots of $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$ |
| 11 | Mass-loading frequency shift | $\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_{1}}{2}\,\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{beam}}$ | Hz | For $\Delta m \ll m_{beam}$ |
| 12 | Stoney curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{6\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}$ | m⁻¹ | Surface stress $\Delta\sigma_{s}$ in N/m |
| 13 | Stoney tip deflection | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{3\,L^{2}\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}$ | m | For biosensors |
| 14 | Quality factor (vacuum) | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{k\,m_{eff}}}{c_{damping}}$ | dimensionless | Vacuum: $Q$ = 10⁴ – 10⁵ |
| 15 | Static sensitivity (mass) | $S_{m} = \dfrac{\partial f / \partial m}{f_{1}} = \dfrac{1}{2\,m_{eff}}$ | Hz/kg | Mass sensor figure of merit |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Cantilever Equations

The KTU formula sheet has direct industrial relevance:

- **MEMS Foundry Design Rule Checks (DRC):** Cantilever spring constant $k = Eb t^3 / (4 L^3)$ dictates whether a switch will close under a given electrostatic force.
- **AFM Probe Manufacturing:** Companies like **Bruker, Olympus, Nanosensors** tune $k$ and $f_1$ by varying L, b, t — directly using the formulas above.
- **Biosensor Design:** Stoney's formula lets designers predict the smallest detectable surface stress change, governing limits of detection in cancer-biomarker and pathogen detection.
- **Resonant Mass Sensing:** The mass-loading equation quantifies the **minimum detectable mass (MDM)** — typically 10⁻¹⁸ g in vacuum operation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Tip Deflection Under End Point Load (Euler–Bernoulli)

This is the most important derivation for KTU 2024 Module 1.

### Step 1 — Draw the Free Body Diagram and Write Bending Moment

Consider a cantilever of length L, fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. Apply a point load F at the free end. The bending moment at any section located at distance x from the free end is:

$$
M(x) \;=\; F \cdot (L - x)
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Euler–Bernoulli Moment–Curvature Relation

$$
M(x) \;=\; E\,I\,\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}w}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}}
$$

Equating:

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}w}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}} \;=\; F\,(L - x)
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Once to Get Slope $\theta(x)$

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; \int F\,(L - x)\,\mathrm{d}x \;=\; F\,L\,x \;-\; \frac{F\,x^{2}}{2} \;+\; C_{1}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Condition at Fixed End

At $x = 0$, the slope is zero: $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} = 0$.

$$
0 \;=\; 0 \;-\; 0 \;+\; C_{1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_{1} \;=\; 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; F\,L\,x \;-\; \frac{F\,x^{2}}{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Again to Get Deflection $w(x)$

$$
E\,I\,w(x) \;=\; \int \left( F\,L\,x \;-\; \frac{F\,x^{2}}{2} \right) \mathrm{d}x \;=\; \frac{F\,L\,x^{2}}{2} \;-\; \frac{F\,x^{3}}{6} \;+\; C_{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Deflection Boundary Condition

At $x = 0$, deflection $w = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; 0 \;-\; 0 \;+\; C_{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_{2} \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 7 — Final Deflection Equation and Tip Value

$$
w(x) \;=\; \frac{F}{6\,E\,I}\,\big[\,3\,L\,x^{2} \;-\; x^{3}\,\big]
$$

Substituting $x = L$:

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; w(L) \;=\; \frac{F}{6\,E\,I}\,\big[\,3\,L \cdot L^{2} \;-\; L^{3}\,\big] \;=\; \frac{F}{6\,E\,I} \cdot 2\,L^{3} \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}\;}
$$

### Step 8 — Spring Constant

$$
k \;=\; \frac{F}{\delta_{tip}} \;=\; \frac{F}{F\,L^{3} / (3\,E\,I)} \;=\; \frac{3\,E\,I}{L^{3}}
$$

For a rectangular cross-section, substitute $I = b t^{3} / 12$:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{3\,E}{L^{3}} \cdot \frac{b\,t^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;k \;=\; \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The spring constant scales as $t^{3}/L^{3}$. This means **doubling the thickness** increases stiffness by 8×, while **doubling the length** decreases stiffness by 8×. Thickness is the most sensitive design parameter.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fundamental Natural Frequency

### Step 1 — Assume Harmonic Motion

For free vibration, assume:

$$
w(x,t) \;=\; W(x)\,e^{j\omega t}
$$

where $\omega$ is the angular frequency and $W(x)$ is the mode shape.

### Step 2 — Substitute Into the Beam Equation

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\partial^{4}w}{\partial x^{4}} \;+\; \rho\,A\,\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial t^{2}} \;=\; 0
$$

Substituting the harmonic form and cancelling the exponential:

$$
E\,I\,\frac{\mathrm{d}^{4}W}{\mathrm{d}x^{4}} \;-\; \rho\,A\,\omega^{2}\,W \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Wave Number $\beta$

$$
\beta^{4} \;=\; \frac{\rho\,A\,\omega^{2}}{E\,I} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{\mathrm{d}^{4}W}{\mathrm{d}x^{4}} \;-\; \beta^{4}\,W \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 4 — General Solution

$$
W(x) \;=\; C_{1}\,\cos(\beta x) \;+\; C_{2}\,\sin(\beta x) \;+\; C_{3}\,\cosh(\beta x) \;+\; C_{4}\,\sinh(\beta x)
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Four Boundary Conditions

| Boundary | Condition | Equation |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| $x = 0$ (fixed) | $W = 0$ | $C_{1} + C_{3} = 0$ |
| $x = 0$ (fixed) | $W' = 0$ | $C_{2}\,\beta + C_{4}\,\beta = 0$ |
| $x = L$ (free) | $W'' = 0$ | $-C_{1}\cos(\beta L) - C_{2}\sin(\beta L) + C_{3}\cosh(\beta L) + C_{4}\sinh(\beta L) = 0$ |
| $x = L$ (free) | $W''' = 0$ | $C_{1}\sin(\beta L) - C_{2}\cos(\beta L) + C_{3}\sinh(\beta L) + C_{4}\cosh(\beta L) = 0$ |

### Step 6 — Form the Characteristic Equation

After solving the homogeneous system, the condition for non-trivial $C_{i}$ is:

$$
\boxed{\;\cos(\beta L)\,\cosh(\beta L) \;+\; 1 \;=\; 0\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Find the First Root

The smallest positive root of the transcendental equation is:

$$
\beta_{1}\,L \;\approx\; 1.875104
$$

### Step 8 — Compute the Natural Frequency

From $\beta^{4} = \rho A \omega^{2} / (E I)$:

$$
\omega_{1} \;=\; (\beta_{1}\,L)^{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}} \;=\; (1.875)^{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}
$$

$$
f_{1} \;=\; \frac{\omega_{1}}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{(1.875)^{2}}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{1} \;\approx\; 0.5605\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}\;}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A silicon cantilever has $L = 200 \,\mu m$, $b = 40 \,\mu m$, $t = 2 \,\mu m$. Properties of silicon: $E = 169$ GPa, $\rho = 2330 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\nu = 0.28$. A tip load $F = 1 \,\mu N$ is applied. Find (a) Spring constant, (b) Tip deflection, (c) Maximum bending stress, (d) Fundamental natural frequency.

### Given Data

$$
L = 200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m},\quad b = 40 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m},\quad t = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
E = 169 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa},\quad \rho = 2330\,\text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

### Step 1 — Area Moment of Inertia

$$
I \;=\; \frac{b\,t^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{(40 \times 10^{-6})\,(2 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12}
$$

$$
I \;=\; \frac{40 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8 \times 10^{-18}}{12} \;=\; \frac{320 \times 10^{-24}}{12} \;=\; 2.667 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{m}^{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Spring Constant

$$
k \;=\; \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}
$$

$$
k \;=\; \frac{(169 \times 10^{9})\,(40 \times 10^{-6})\,(8 \times 10^{-18})}{4\,(200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}
$$

$$
k \;=\; \frac{5.408 \times 10^{-11}}{4 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-15}} \;=\; \frac{5.408 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} \;=\; 1.69 \times 10^{3}\,\text{N/m} \;=\; 1.69\,\text{N/m (corrected, see below)}
$$

> **Recalculation check:**
> $E\,b\,t^{3} = 169 \times 10^{9} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 5.408 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{N·m}^{2}$
> $4 L^{3} = 4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 4 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{m}^{3}$
> $k = 5.408 \times 10^{-11} / 3.2 \times 10^{-14} = 1690\,\text{N/m}$

$$
\boxed{\;k \;\approx\; 1.69\,\text{N/m}\;}
$$

### Step 3 — Tip Deflection

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{F}{k} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N}}{1690\,\text{N/m}} \;=\; 5.92 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m} \;\approx\; 0.59\,\text{nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{tip} \;\approx\; 0.59\,\text{nm}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress

$$
\sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{6\,F\,L}{b\,t^{2}} \;=\; \frac{6 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{(40 \times 10^{-6})\,(2 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{40 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{-12}} \;=\; \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{1.6 \times 10^{-16}} \;=\; 7.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \;=\; 7.5\,\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{max} \;\approx\; 7.5\,\text{MPa}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Fundamental Natural Frequency

$$
A \;=\; b\,t \;=\; 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 8 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
f_{1} \;=\; 0.5605\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}}
$$

$$
\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}} \;=\; \frac{169 \times 10^{9} \times 2.667 \times 10^{-23}}{2330 \times 8 \times 10^{-11} \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{4}}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 4.508 \times 10^{-12},\quad \text{Denominator} \;=\; 2330 \times 8 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-20} \;=\; 2.982 \times 10^{-26}
$$

$$
\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}} \;=\; \frac{4.508 \times 10^{-12}}{2.982 \times 10^{-26}} \;=\; 1.512 \times 10^{14}
$$

$$
f_{1} \;=\; 0.5605 \times \sqrt{1.512 \times 10^{14}} \;=\; 0.5605 \times 1.230 \times 10^{7} \;\approx\; 6.89 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 6.89\,\text{MHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{1} \;\approx\; 6.9\,\text{MHz}\;}
$$

### Result Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Engineering Comment |
|----------|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Spring constant | $k$ | 1.69 N/m | Soft cantilever — suitable for AFM |
| Tip deflection | $\delta_{tip}$ | 0.59 nm | Detectable by optical lever |
| Max bending stress | $\sigma_{max}$ | 7.5 MPa | Far below silicon fracture stress (~7 GPa) |
| Natural frequency | $f_1$ | 6.9 MHz | In the high-frequency AFM regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** The calculated $f_1$ = 6.9 MHz is comparable to the **resonance frequencies of small AFM cantilevers**, confirming that the chosen dimensions are physically reasonable. Real AFM cantilevers typically operate between 10 kHz (contact mode) and 2 MHz (tapping mode); shorter beams are needed to reach the MHz range.

## 3.4 Derivation of Stoney's Formula (Surface-Stress Bending)

### Step 1 — Setup

Consider a thin film (the "coating") of thickness $t_f$ deposited on one side of a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$. The film exerts an in-plane force per unit width $F_{film} = \sigma_{s}$ (the surface stress).

### Step 2 — Force and Moment Equilibria

The net force on the substrate is zero, but a net **bending moment per unit width** $M = \sigma_{s} \cdot t_f / 2$ acts because the film is offset from the neutral axis by $t_f/2$.

### Step 3 — Equate to Moment–Curvature Relation

For a substrate of biaxial modulus $E' = E / (1 - \nu)$:

$$
M \;=\; \frac{E'\,I_{s}}{R} \;\approx\; \frac{E'\,t_{s}^{3}}{12\,R} \cdot 1 \quad \text{(per unit width, }I_s = t_s^3/12\text{)}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Curvature

Equating and solving for $1/R$:

$$
\frac{1}{R} \;=\; \frac{6\,\sigma_{s}\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t_{s}^{2}}
$$

For a thin coating ($t_f \ll t_s$):

$$
\boxed{\;\kappa \;=\; \frac{6\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}\;}
$$

For a cantilever of length L, the tip deflection is $\delta_{tip} = \kappa L^{2}/2$, giving:

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{3\,L^{2}\,(1 - \nu)}{E\,t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cantilever Functional Architecture

The following flowchart maps the **functional architecture** of a MEMS cantilever, covering its mechanical, electrical, and signal-readout paths. This is the standard KTU Module 1 system block representation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Stimulus] --> B[Transduction Layer]
    B --> C[Mechanical Cantilever Beam]
    C --> D[Mechanical Response]
    D --> E[Readout Mechanism]
    E --> F[Signal Output]
    C --> G[Boundary Conditions]
    C --> H[Geometric Parameters]
    C --> I[Material Properties]

    A1[Force] --> A
    A2[Mass] --> A
    A3[Molecule Binding] --> A
    A4[Temperature] --> A
    A5[Acceleration] --> A

    B1[Receptor Coating] --> B
    B2[Piezo Layer] --> B
    B3[Metal Electrode] --> B

    D1[Static Deflection] --> D
    D2[Resonance Shift] --> D
    D3[Surface Stress Bending] --> D

    E1[Optical Lever] --> E
    E2[Piezoresistive Bridge] --> E
    E3[Capacitive Readout] --> E
    E4[Piezoelectric Readout] --> E

    G1[Fixed Clamped End] --> G
    G2[Free Tip End] --> G

    H1[Length L] --> H
    H2[Width b] --> H
    H3[Thickness t] --> H

    I1[Young Modulus E] --> I
    I2[Density rho] --> I
    I3[Poisson Ratio nu] --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Cantilever Analysis Methodology

This flowchart represents the **step-by-step analytical procedure** that KTU examiners expect students to follow when solving cantilever problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Geometry and Load] --> S2[Step 2: Compute I and A]
    S2 --> S3{Step 3: Static or Dynamic}
    S3 -->|Static| S4[Step 4a: Apply Equilibrium Equations]
    S3 -->|Dynamic| S5[Step 4b: Solve Eigenvalue Problem]
    S4 --> S6[Step 5a: Tip Deflection]
    S4 --> S7[Step 5b: Spring Constant k]
    S4 --> S8[Step 5c: Maximum Stress]
    S5 --> S9[Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions]
    S9 --> S10[Step 7: Find Eigenvalues beta L]
    S10 --> S11[Step 8: Compute Natural Frequency f sub 1]
    S6 --> S9a[Step 9: Validate with Stoney or Energy Method]
    S7 --> S9a
    S8 --> S9a
    S11 --> S9a
    S9a --> S12[Step 10: Final Result Matrix]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Mind-Map — Cantilever Applications in MEMS


> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks to "draw and explain the working of a MEMS cantilever sensor," always include (a) the geometric sketch, (b) the transduction principle, (c) the readout method, and (d) one numerical example using the KTU formula sheet. This four-part structure consistently earns full marks.

| Fixed end (x = 0) | $w = 0$ | — | — |
| Fixed end (x = 0) | $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} = 0$ | — | — |
