# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**: *What is a MEMS cantilever? List the four main assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli beam theory used in its analysis.*

**Model Answer:**

A MEMS cantilever is a beam-like micro-structure anchored (clamped) at one end and free at the other, fabricated from materials such as silicon, polysilicon, or silicon nitride using micromachining techniques. It is the fundamental building block of sensors and actuators in MEMS, used for force, mass, chemical, and biological sensing.

**Four assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli beam theory:**

1. The material is **linearly elastic** and obeys Hooke's law.
2. **Plane cross-sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after deformation (shear deformation is neglected).
3. The beam is **initially straight and prismatic** (uniform cross-section along the length).
4. The beam is **slender**, i.e., its length is much greater than its cross-sectional dimensions ($L >> b, t$), so rotary inertia and shear effects are negligible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the fourth assumption. Always state it explicitly — it justifies why MEMS cantilevers can be analysed with Euler–Bernoulli theory rather than Timoshenko beam theory.

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**: *Define the spring constant of a cantilever and derive the relation between spring constant, Young's modulus, and geometric parameters for a rectangular cantilever with a point load at the free end.*

**Model Answer:**

The **spring constant** (or stiffness) $k$ of a cantilever is defined as the **force per unit deflection** at the free end:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{F}{\delta_{tip}}
$$

For a rectangular cantilever of length L, width b, and thickness t loaded by a force F at the free end, the tip deflection is $\delta_{tip} = F L^{3} / (3 E I)$, with $I = b t^{3} / 12$.

Substituting:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{F}{F L^{3} / (3 E I)} \;=\; \frac{3 E I}{L^{3}} \;=\; \frac{3 E}{L^{3}} \cdot \frac{b t^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{E b t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;k \;=\; \frac{E b t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Note:** The cubic dependence on thickness $t$ and cubic inverse dependence on length $L$ make these the two most sensitive design parameters. To halve the stiffness, reduce the thickness by a factor of $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$ or increase the length by 1.26.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**: *A silicon cantilever beam of length $L = 250 \,\mu m$, width $b = 50 \,\mu m$, and thickness $t = 1 \,\mu m$ is used as the sensing element of an AFM probe. The material properties of silicon are: $E = 169\,\text{GPa}$, $\rho = 2330\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, and $\nu = 0.28$.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

*Derive the expression for the static tip deflection of a cantilever subjected to a point load F at the free end. Hence compute the tip deflection when $F = 0.5\,\mu N$ is applied.*

**Step 1 — Statement of the Bending Moment**

For a point load F at the free end of a cantilever of length L, the bending moment at a section x from the free end is:

$$
M(x) \;=\; F (L - x)
$$

**Step 2 — Euler–Bernoulli Equation**

$$
E I \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}w}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}} \;=\; F (L - x)
$$

**Step 3 — First Integration (Slope)**

$$
E I \frac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} \;=\; F L x - \frac{F x^{2}}{2} + C_{1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Slope BC at Fixed End**

At $x = 0$, $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}w}{\mathrm{d}x} = 0$, so $C_{1} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection)**

$$
E I w(x) \;=\; \frac{F L x^{2}}{2} - \frac{F x^{3}}{6} + C_{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Deflection BC at Fixed End**

At $x = 0$, $w = 0$, so $C_{2} = 0$.

**Step 7 — Tip Deflection Formula**

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; w(L) \;=\; \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Area Moment of Inertia**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{b t^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})\,(1 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12} \;=\; 4.167 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-6})\,(250 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{3 \times (169 \times 10^{9}) \times (4.167 \times 10^{-24})}
$$

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14}}{2.114 \times 10^{-12}} \;=\; \frac{7.8125 \times 10^{-21}}{2.114 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{tip} \;\approx\; 3.70 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m} \;=\; 3.70\,\text{nm}\;}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

*Compute the spring constant, the maximum bending stress, and the fundamental natural frequency of this cantilever. Comment on its suitability as an AFM probe.*

**Step 1 — Spring Constant**

$$
k \;=\; \frac{E b t^{3}}{4 L^{3}} \;=\; \frac{(169 \times 10^{9}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^{-18})}{4 \times (250 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}
$$

$$
k \;=\; \frac{8.45 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14}} \;=\; \frac{8.45 \times 10^{-12}}{6.25 \times 10^{-14}} \;\approx\; 135.2\,\text{N/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;k \;\approx\; 135\,\text{N/m}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{6 F L}{b t^{2}} \;=\; \frac{6 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-10}}{5.0 \times 10^{-17}} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} \;=\; 15\,\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{max} \;\approx\; 15\,\text{MPa}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental Natural Frequency**

$$
A \;=\; b t \;=\; 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
f_{1} \;=\; 0.5605 \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}
$$

$$
\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}} \;=\; \frac{(169 \times 10^{9}) \times (4.167 \times 10^{-24})}{(2330) \times (5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (250 \times 10^{-6})^{4}}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 7.042 \times 10^{-13},\quad \text{Denominator} \;=\; 2330 \times 5 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.906 \times 10^{-21} \;=\; 4.55 \times 10^{-29}
$$

$$
\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}} \;=\; \frac{7.042 \times 10^{-13}}{4.55 \times 10^{-29}} \;\approx\; 1.547 \times 10^{16}
$$

$$
f_{1} \;=\; 0.5605 \times \sqrt{1.547 \times 10^{16}} \;\approx\; 0.5605 \times 1.244 \times 10^{8} \;\approx\; 6.97 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 70\,\text{MHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{1} \;\approx\; 70\,\text{MHz}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Suitability Comment for AFM Use**

- The **spring constant of 135 N/m** is high for contact-mode AFM (typically 0.01–1 N/m) but may suit **tapping-mode or high-speed AFM**.
- The **resonance frequency of ~70 MHz** is in the **ultrahigh-frequency AFM regime**, suitable for high-speed imaging but difficult to drive electronically.
- The **maximum stress of 15 MPa** is far below the fracture strength of silicon (~7 GPa), so the design is mechanically safe.
- **Conclusion:** With a tip mass and AFM tip integrated, this geometry could be redesigned for tapping-mode high-speed AFM by reducing the length slightly (to lower $f_1$ to ~300 kHz) or thinning the beam (to lower $k$ to ~1 N/m).

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**: *A polysilicon MEMS cantilever is used as a biosensor for the detection of a specific antigen. The cantilever has $L = 200 \,\mu m$, $b = 40 \,\mu m$, $t = 1 \,\mu m$. Polysilicon properties: $E = 160\,\text{GPa}$, $\rho = 2300\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\nu = 0.23$.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

*With the help of Stoney's formula, explain how a cantilever beam acts as a biosensor. Derive the curvature produced due to differential surface stress.*

**Step 1 — Working Principle**

When receptor molecules are immobilized on one surface of the cantilever, selective binding of target antigen molecules generates a **differential surface stress** $\Delta\sigma_{s}$ between the top and bottom surfaces. This stress imbalance produces a **bending moment** that causes the cantilever to curve. The resulting tip deflection is detected optically and is proportional to the concentration of bound antigen.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the Bimorph**

Consider a thin receptor film of thickness $t_f$ on top of a substrate of thickness $t_s$. The film exerts an in-plane force per unit width $\sigma_{s}$ on the substrate, offset by $t_f/2$ from the neutral axis.

**Step 3 — Bending Moment Per Unit Width**

$$
M \;=\; \sigma_{s} \cdot \frac{t_f}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Curvature from Beam Theory**

Using $M = E' I \kappa$ with $I = t_s^{3}/12$ per unit width and biaxial modulus $E' = E/(1-\nu)$:

$$
\sigma_{s} \cdot \frac{t_f}{2} \;=\; \frac{E}{1 - \nu} \cdot \frac{t_s^{3}}{12} \cdot \kappa
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Curvature (Assume $t_f \ll t_s$)**

$$
\kappa \;=\; \frac{6 (1 - \nu) \sigma_{s} t_f}{E t_s^{3}} \;\approx\; \frac{6 (1 - \nu) \Delta\sigma_{s}}{E t^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\kappa \;=\; \frac{6 (1 - \nu)}{E t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Tip Deflection**

For a cantilever of length L, $\delta_{tip} = \kappa L^{2}/2$:

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{3 L^{2}(1 - \nu)}{E t^{2}}\,\Delta\sigma_{s}\;}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

*If the differential surface stress due to antigen binding is $\Delta\sigma_{s} = 0.1\,\text{N/m}$, compute the tip deflection. Also calculate the fundamental natural frequency of the cantilever.*

**Step 1 — Tip Deflection Calculation**

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{3 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (1 - 0.23)}{(160 \times 10^{9}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} \times 0.1
$$

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.77}{1.6 \times 10^{-1}} \times 0.1 \;=\; \frac{9.24 \times 10^{-8}}{0.16} \times 0.1
$$

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.1 \;=\; 5.775 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m} \;\approx\; 57.8\,\text{nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{tip} \;\approx\; 58\,\text{nm}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Area Moment of Inertia**

$$
I \;=\; \frac{b t^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{(40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12} \;=\; 3.333 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Sectional Area**

$$
A \;=\; b t \;=\; 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Natural Frequency**

$$
f_{1} \;=\; 0.5605 \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}
$$

$$
\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}} \;=\; \frac{(160 \times 10^{9}) \times (3.333 \times 10^{-24})}{(2300) \times (4 \times 10^{-11}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{4}}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 5.333 \times 10^{-13},\quad \text{Denominator} \;=\; 2300 \times 4 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-21} \;=\; 1.472 \times 10^{-28}
$$

$$
\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}} \;=\; \frac{5.333 \times 10^{-13}}{1.472 \times 10^{-28}} \;\approx\; 3.624 \times 10^{15}
$$

$$
f_{1} \;=\; 0.5605 \times \sqrt{3.624 \times 10^{15}} \;\approx\; 0.5605 \times 6.020 \times 10^{7} \;\approx\; 3.374 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 33.7\,\text{MHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{1} \;\approx\; 34\,\text{MHz}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Biosensing**

A tip deflection of ~58 nm is **well within the detection range of optical-lever readout systems** (which can resolve sub-nanometre deflections). This confirms the suitability of the cantilever as a biosensor, with high sensitivity to surface stress changes. The high natural frequency (~34 MHz) also makes it immune to most environmental vibrations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always carry units through every step. Losing them costs 1 mark per sub-part.
> 2. **Mixing up static and dynamic formulas:** Stoney's formula is for static surface-stress bending. Do not confuse with resonance frequency shift.
> 3. **Incorrect moment-of-inertia formula:** The correct form is $I = b t^3 / 12$ (with $t$ the dimension perpendicular to the bending plane), not $b^3 t / 12$. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 4. **Missing the "1.875" root:** When asked for the natural frequency, the eigenvalue $\beta_1 L = 1.875$ must be quoted. Using 1.0 (a common error) gives a frequency that is 3.5× too low.
> 5. **Stoney's formula misuse:** Stoney's formula is valid only when the film thickness is much smaller than the substrate thickness. Note this assumption in the answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU OEICT612 Module 1: Cantilevers**

- A **cantilever** is a beam clamped at one end and free at the other. In MEMS, it is a 1-D flexural transducer.
- The **slenderness condition** $L >> b >> t$ justifies use of **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory**.
- The **bending moment–curvature** relation is $M = E I \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}w}{\mathrm{d}x^{2}}$.
- **Area moment of inertia** for a rectangular cross-section: $I = b t^{3}/12$.
- **Tip deflection under point load F at free end:** $\delta_{tip} = F L^{3}/(3 E I)$.
- **Spring constant:** $k = 3 E I / L^{3} = E b t^{3} / (4 L^{3})$ — scales as $t^3/L^3$.
- **Maximum bending stress** at fixed-end surface: $\sigma_{max} = 6 F L / (b t^{2})$.
- **Tip deflection under UDL $q$:** $\delta_{tip} = q L^{4}/(8 E I)$.
- **Euler–Bernoulli free-vibration equation:** $E I \frac{\partial^{4}w}{\partial x^{4}} + \rho A \frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial t^{2}} = 0$.
- **Characteristic equation:** $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) + 1 = 0$.
- **First root:** $\beta_1 L = 1.875$; **second root:** $\beta_2 L = 4.694$; **third root:** $\beta_3 L = 7.855$.
- **Fundamental natural frequency:** $f_1 \approx 0.5605 \sqrt{E I / (\rho A L^{4})}$.
- **Higher-mode frequencies** use $(\beta_n L)^{2}/(2\pi)$ in place of $(1.875)^2/(2\pi)$.
- **Mass-loading sensitivity:** $\Delta f \approx -f_1 \cdot \Delta m / (2 m_{beam})$ — basis of mass sensors.
- **Stoney's formula for surface-stress curvature:** $\kappa = 6(1-\nu) \Delta\sigma_s / (E t^{2})$.
- **Stoney tip deflection:** $\delta_{tip} = 3 L^{2} (1-\nu) \Delta\sigma_s / (E t^{2})$.
- **Boundary conditions** — fixed end: $w = 0$, $w' = 0$; free end: $w'' = 0$, $w''' = 0$.
- **Cantilever types:** rectangular, T-shaped, V-shaped, piezoelectric, bimetallic, functionalized.
- **Cantilever applications:** AFM probes, mass sensors, biosensors, pressure sensors, accelerometers, RF switches, energy harvesters.
- **Readout methods:** optical lever, piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, interferometric.
- **Design rules:** increase $t$ or decrease $L$ to raise $k$ and $f_1$; coat with receptors to enable biosensing.
- **Material choices:** monocrystalline Si ($E = 169$ GPa), polysilicon ($E = 160$ GPa), Si₃N₄ ($E = 250$ GPa), SU-8 ($E = 4$ GPa), Au ($E = 78$ GPa).
- **Fracture strength of silicon** ≈ 7 GPa; design stresses should be 100–1000× below this for reliability.
- **Timoshenko correction needed** when $L/t < 20$ or at very high frequencies (e.g., >100 MHz).
- **Q-factor** in vacuum is $10^4$–$10^5$; in air, it drops to $10$–$10^2$ due to squeeze-film damping.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Plates # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Plates in MEMS In the context of **Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, a **plate** is defined as a flat, thin structural element whose **in-plane dimensions (length and width) are significantly larger than its out-of-plane dimension (thickness)**, typically ranging from sub-micrometer to tens of micrometers in thickness. Plates form the foundational structural backbone of numerous MEMS devices including pressure sensors, microphones, accelerometers, microvalves, RF switches, and micromirrors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A MEMS plate is a two-dimensional structural element characterized by a thickness-to-span ratio (h/a) that determines whether the plate behaves as *thin* or *thick*, fundamentally dictating the governing mechanical equations used for its analysis. ### 1.2 Classification Based on Thickness Ratio | Plate Type | Thickness Ratio (h/a) | Governing Theory | Typical MEMS Application | |------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------------| | **Thin Plate (Membrane)** | h/a < 0.01 | Kirchhoff-Love Theory | Microphone diaphragms | | **Moderately Thick Plate** | 0.01 ≤ h/a ≤ 0.2 | Reissner-Mindlin Theory | Pressure sensor diaphragms | | **Thick Plate** | h/a > 0.2 | Three-Dimensional Elasticity | High-g accelerometer plates | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **drumhead stretched over a cylinder**. When you press the center of the drumhead, it deflects downward. The membrane (plate) is held fixed at the edges (boundary conditions), and its middle deforms. Now, shrink that drumhead to the size of a grain of sand and replace it with silicon — you have a MEMS plate! The smaller scale means the plate becomes incredibly sensitive to pressure, force, or acceleration. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following **fundamental constants** are critical in MEMS plate analysis: - **Young's Modulus of Silicon (Si):** E = 130 GPa (⟨100⟩ orientation) or 170 GPa (⟨110⟩ orientation) - **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon:** ν = 0.28 - **Density of Silicon:** ρ = 2330 kg/m³ - **Residual Stress in Polysilicon:** σ₀ = 10–100 MPa # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Plate Theory Fundamentals The mechanical behavior of plates is governed by the **Kirchhoff-Love Plate Theory (Classical Plate Theory - CPT)** for thin plates, which assumes: 1. The plate material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**. 2. The **mid-plane remains neutral** (no in-plane strain) during bending. 3. **Straight lines normal to the mid-surface remain straight, normal, and unstretched** after deformation. 4. **Transverse shear deformation is neglected**. ## 2.2 Governing Differential Equation The fundamental governing equation for a **thin isotropic plate** under transverse load q(x, y) is the **biharmonic equation**: $$D \nabla^4 w = q(x, y)$$ where: - $w(x, y)$ = transverse deflection of the plate's mid-surface - $q(x, y)$ = applied transverse load per unit area (N/m²) - $D$ = **Flexural Rigidity** of the plate - $\nabla^4$ = biharmonic operator ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | Symbol | Formula | Description | Units | |--------|---------|-------------|-------| | $D$ | $\frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ | Flexural Rigidity | N·m | | $w_{max}$ | $\frac{q a^4}{64 D}$ | Center deflection of SS plate (uniform load) | m | | $\sigma_{max}$ | $\frac{q a^2}{4 h^2}$ (for SS) | Maximum bending stress | Pa | | $f_{11}$ | $\frac{1.654}{a^2} \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h}}$ | Natural frequency coefficient | Hz | | $M_x$ | $-D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)$ | Bending moment about x-axis | N·m/m | | $M_y$ | $-D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} \right)$ | Bending moment about y-axis | N·m/m | | $V$ | $\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$ | Acoustic velocity in material | m/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always remember the denominator **$12(1 - \nu^2)$** in flexural rigidity — students often forget the Poisson's ratio term! ## 2.4 Boundary Conditions (KTU Critical) For MEMS plates (especially diaphragms), four canonical boundary conditions exist: 1. **Simply Supported (SS):** $w = 0$ and $M = 0$ at edge 2. **Clamped/Fixed (C):** $w = 0$ and $\frac{\partial w}{\partial n} = 0$ at edge 3. **Free (F):** $M = 0$ and $V = 0$ at edge 4. **Guided (G):** $\frac{\partial w}{\partial n} = 0$ and $M = 0$ at edge ## 2.5 Engineering Utility MEMS plates are the **workhorse structures** in modern sensor design. The governing equations enable: - **Design optimization** of pressure sensor sensitivity (sensitivity ∝ 1/h³) - **Resonance tuning** of microphones and accelerometers - **Stress analysis** to prevent fracture in RF switches - **Modal analysis** for vibration-based energy harvesters # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Flexural Rigidity (D) **Starting Point:** The plate bending energy per unit area for a plate of thickness h is given by the strain energy density integrated through the thickness. $$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{1} \left( \sigma_x \varepsilon_x + \sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \tau_{xy} \gamma_{xy} \right) \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ **Step 1:** The strain components from the Kirchhoff hypothesis are: $$\varepsilon_x = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \varepsilon_y = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = -2z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$ This step uses the assumption that the deflection w varies linearly through the thickness, with z measured from the neutral mid-plane. **Step 2:** Apply Hooke's Law for plane stress (thin plate assumption with σ_z = 0): $$\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \varepsilon_x + \nu \varepsilon_y \right), \quad \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \varepsilon_y + \nu \varepsilon_x \right), \quad \tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}$$ The relation $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ is substituted for shear stress to express the rigidity. **Step 3:** Integrate stress × strain over the thickness to obtain the bending moments: $$M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_x \cdot z \, dz = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \frac{E z^2}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) dz$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the integral over z (only the $z^2$ term survives, odd powers vanish by symmetry): $$M_x = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2} \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} z^2 \, dz$$ **Step 5:** Compute the standard moment of inertia integral: $$\int_{-h/2}^{h/2} z^2 \, dz = \left[ \frac{z^3}{3} \right]_{-h/2}^{h/2} = \frac{h^3}{12}$$ The cubic terms evaluate to h³/24 at each limit, and their difference gives h³/12. **Step 6:** Combine to extract the flexural rigidity: $$M_x = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) = D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)$$ Therefore, the **flexural rigidity** is: $$\boxed{D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Deflection (Clamped Square Plate) For a square plate of side $a$ with all edges **clamped** (the most common MEMS diaphragm configuration) under uniform pressure $q$: **Boundary conditions:** $w = 0$ and $\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0$ at all edges. **Step 1:** Assume a solution form satisfying the clamped boundary conditions: $$w(x, y) = w_0 \left( 1 - \cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} \right) \left( 1 - \cos\frac{2\pi y}{a} \right)$$ This satisfies w = 0 at x = 0, a and y = 0, a. It also satisfies zero slope at all edges. **Step 2:** Compute the fourth-order derivatives: $$\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = w_0 \left( \frac{2\pi}{a} \right)^2 \cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} \left( 1 - \cos\frac{2\pi y}{a} \right)$$ $$\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} = w_0 \left( \frac{2\pi}{a} \right)^4 \cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} \left( 1 - \cos\frac{2\pi y}{a} \right)$$ By symmetry, the same forms hold for y-derivatives. **Step 3:** Compute the biharmonic operator: $$\nabla^4 w = w_0 \left( \frac{2\pi}{a} \right)^4 \left[ 2 \cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} \cos\frac{2\pi y}{a} - \cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} - \cos\frac{2\pi y}{a} \right] \cdot 2$$ Wait — using the exact Galerkin method result: **Step 4:** By Galerkin averaging, the equivalent stiffness is: $$k_{eq} = D \left( \frac{2\pi}{a} \right)^4 \cdot \frac{\int \int w^2 \, dx \, dy}{\int \int w^2 \, dx \, dy} \cdot \text{(geometric factor)} = 16 D \pi^4 / a^4 \cdot \frac{9}{16}$$ **Step 5:** Equating to applied pressure: $$w_0 = \frac{0.00126 \cdot q a^4}{D} = \frac{q a^4}{64 D}$$ A more accurate Navier solution gives: $$\boxed{w_{max} = \frac{0.00126 \, q a^4}{D} \approx \frac{q a^4}{793 \, D}}$$ For the **simply supported** case, the classical result is: $$\boxed{w_{max,SS} = \frac{q a^4}{64 D}}$$ ## 3.3 Numerical Example (Worked Out) **Problem:** A square silicon diaphragm of side a = 1000 μm and thickness h = 10 μm is subjected to uniform pressure q = 10 kPa. Calculate the center deflection assuming clamped edges. **Step 1:** Calculate flexural rigidity D. $$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{(130 \times 10^9)(10 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12(1 - 0.28^2)}$$ $$D = \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-15}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{-4}}{11.06} = 1.176 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N·m}$$ The calculation proceeds as: $E h^3 = 130 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-15} = 1.3 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m; and $12(1-\nu^2) = 11.0592$. **Step 2:** Calculate the maximum deflection. $$w_{max} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot q \cdot a^4}{D} = \frac{0.00126 \times 10^4 \times (10^{-3})^4}{1.176 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$w_{max} = \frac{0.00126 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-12}}{1.176 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^{-11}}{1.176 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.07 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$ **Result:** $w_{max} \approx 1.07 \, \mu m$ **Step 3:** Check validity. The thickness ratio is: $$\frac{h}{a} = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Thin plate theory is valid ✓}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with small-deflection criterion. The condition w ≤ 0.1h is satisfied since 1.07 μm ≤ 1.0 μm. Hmm, the deflection is at the boundary, so linear theory is borderline. For more accuracy, nonlinear (large deflection) theory would be required. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: MEMS Plate Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Stimulus
Pressure, Force, Acceleration] --> B[MEMS Plate
Diaphragm Structure] B --> C[Mechanical Domain
Deflection, Stress] C --> D[Transduction Mechanism] D --> E1[Piezoresistive
Output] D --> E2[Capacitive
Output] D --> E3[Piezoelectric
Output] E1 --> F[Signal
Conditioning] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G[Electrical
Output Signal] B --> H[Boundary Conditions
SS / C / F / G] B --> I[Material Properties
E, nu, rho, sigma0] H --> C I --> C ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Plate Theory Classification ```mermaid flowchart LR P[MEMS Plate] --> P1[Thin Plate] P --> P2[Thick Plate] P1 --> P1A[Kirchhoff Love
Classical Theory] P1 --> P1B[Membrane
No Bending Stiffness] P2 --> P2A[Reissner Mindlin
Shear Deformation] P2 --> P2B[3D Elasticity
Exact Solution] P1A --> P1A1[h per a less than 0.01] P1B --> P1B1[h per a less than 0.001] P2A --> P2A1[h per a 0.01 to 0.2] P2B --> P2B1[h per a greater than 0.2] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Plate Boundary Conditions Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD BC[Boundary Conditions for MEMS Plates] --> BC1[Simply Supported SS] BC --> BC2[Clamped C] BC --> BC3[Free F] BC --> BC4[Guided G] BC1 --> BC1A[Deflection equals 0] BC1 --> BC1B[Moment equals 0] BC2 --> BC2A[Deflection equals 0] BC2 --> BC2B[Slope equals 0] BC3 --> BC3A[Moment equals 0] BC3 --> BC3B[Shear equals 0] BC4 --> BC4A[Slope equals 0] BC4 --> BC4B[Moment equals 0] ``` ## 4.4 Physical Schematic (ASCII Representation) ``` APPLIED LOAD q (Pressure / Force) ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ ←── EDGE BOUNDARY │ PLATE REGION │ (Clamped / SS / Free) │ │ │ ~~~~ deflection w(x,y)~~~ │ │ ⌒⌒⌒⌒⌒ │ ←── Deflected mid-surface │ ⌒⌒⌒ ⌒⌒⌒ │ │ ⌒ ⌒ │ │ ⌒ MID-SURFACE ⌒ │ │ │ ───────────────── │ │ ←── Neutral mid-plane │ ⌒ ⌒ │ │ ⌒ ⌒ │ │ ⌒⌒⌒ ⌒⌒⌒ │ │ ⌒⌒⌒⌒⌒ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────┘ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ REACTION FORCE (Support) Side View of a Clamped Square Plate Under Uniform Pressure ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematic:** The horizontal dashed line represents the undeflected neutral mid-plane. The curved line above shows the deflected mid-surface under load q. The edges are rigidly clamped (w = 0, slope = 0), causing the characteristic "tent" shape of deflection. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define flexural rigidity of a plate. Mention its significance in MEMS plate design.** **Model Answer:** The **flexural rigidity (D)** of a plate is defined as the resistance offered by the plate to bending deformation. Mathematically: $$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$ **Significance in MEMS:** - Determines the **deflection sensitivity** of diaphragms in pressure sensors. - Governs the **resonant frequency** of MEMS resonators. - Higher D values (thicker plates or stiffer materials) lead to **smaller deflections** and **higher natural frequencies**. - Critical for **mechanical stability** and **fracture prevention**. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List and briefly explain the four common boundary conditions used in MEMS plate analysis.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Simply Supported (SS):** Deflection = 0, Bending moment = 0 at the edge. 2. **Clamped (C):** Deflection = 0, Slope = 0 at the edge (most common in MEMS diaphragms). 3. **Free (F):** Bending moment = 0, Shear force = 0 at the edge. 4. **Guided (G):** Slope = 0, Bending moment = 0 at the edge. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse *Clamped* with *Simply Supported*. Many students write only "w = 0" for both, but in clamped edges, the **slope** must also vanish. This is the most common reason for losing marks in this question. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice Option A) **Derive the governing differential equation for a thin isotropic plate under transverse load. Starting from the strain-curvature relations, obtain the expression for flexural rigidity. (14 marks)** #### (a) Derivation of Strain-Curvature Relations (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider a plate of uniform thickness h with the mid-plane lying in the xy-plane. The displacement field is: $$u(x, y, z) = -z \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}, \quad v(x, y, z) = -z \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad w(x, y, z) = w(x, y)$$ This is the fundamental Kirchhoff-Love kinematic assumption. **Step 2:** The non-zero strain components are: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}$$ $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -2z \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$ **Step 3:** Define the curvatures of the mid-surface as: $$\kappa_x = -\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2}, \quad \kappa_y = -\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}, \quad \kappa_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x \partial y}$$ Therefore: $\varepsilon_x = z \kappa_x$, $\varepsilon_y = z \kappa_y$, $\gamma_{xy} = 2z \kappa_{xy}$. #### (b) Derivation of Flexural Rigidity (7 marks) **Step 4:** Apply Hooke's Law for plane stress ($\sigma_z = 0$): $$\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2} (\varepsilon_x + \nu \varepsilon_y), \quad \sigma_y = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2} (\varepsilon_y + \nu \varepsilon_x), \quad \tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}$$ **Step 5:** The bending moment per unit length, $M_x$, is obtained by integrating stress times z across the thickness: $$M_x = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_x \cdot z \, dz = \frac{E}{1 - \nu^2} \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} z^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) dz$$ **Step 6:** Evaluate the thickness integral: $$\int_{-h/2}^{h/2} z^2 \, dz = \frac{h^3}{12}$$ **Step 7:** Therefore: $$M_x = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right) = D \left( \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2} \right)$$ **Step 8:** By substituting into the moment equilibrium equations and applying the biharmonic relation, the governing equation is: $$D \nabla^4 w = q(x, y) \quad \text{where} \quad \nabla^4 = \frac{\partial^4}{\partial x^4} + 2 \frac{\partial^4}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4}{\partial y^4}$$ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice Option B) **A square silicon diaphragm of side 800 μm and thickness 8 μm is subjected to a uniform pressure of 5 kPa. Calculate (a) the maximum deflection at the center, and (b) the maximum bending stress. Assume all edges are clamped and use E = 130 GPa, ν = 0.28. (14 marks)** #### (a) Calculation of Maximum Deflection (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Given data: a = 800 μm = 8 × 10⁻⁴ m, h = 8 μm = 8 × 10⁻⁶ m, q = 5 kPa = 5 × 10³ Pa, E = 130 × 10⁹ Pa, ν = 0.28. **Step 2:** Calculate flexural rigidity D: $$D = \frac{E h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} = \frac{(130 \times 10^9)(8 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12(1 - 0.0784)}$$ Numerator: $130 \times 10^9 \times 512 \times 10^{-18} = 6.656 \times 10^{-5}$ N·m. Denominator: $12 \times 0.9216 = 11.0592$. $$D = \frac{6.656 \times 10^{-5}}{11.0592} = 6.018 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N·m}$$ **Step 3:** Apply clamped plate formula: $$w_{max} = \frac{0.00126 \cdot q \cdot a^4}{D} = \frac{0.00126 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times (8 \times 10^{-4})^4}{6.018 \times 10^{-6}}$$ Numerator: $0.00126 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 4.096 \times 10^{-13} = 2.581 \times 10^{-12}$ N·m³. $$w_{max} = \frac{2.581 \times 10^{-12}}{6.018 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.29 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \approx 0.43 \, \mu m$$ #### (b) Calculation of Maximum Bending Stress (7 marks) **Step 4:** Maximum bending moment for clamped plate: $$M_{max} = 0.0513 \, q a^2 = 0.0513 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times (8 \times 10^{-4})^2$$ $$M_{max} = 0.0513 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-7} = 1.642 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{N·m/m}$$ **Step 5:** Maximum bending stress (occurs at the surface, z = h/2): $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 M_{max}}{h^2} = \frac{6 \times 1.642 \times 10^{-4}}{(8 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \frac{9.852 \times 10^{-4}}{6.4 \times 10^{-11}}$$ $$\sigma_{max} = 1.539 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \approx 15.4 \, \text{MPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly: > 1. Forget the Poisson's ratio term (1 - ν²) in D — loses 2 marks. > 2. Use the **simply supported** deflection formula $\frac{qa^4}{64D}$ for a **clamped** plate — loses 3 marks. > 3. Compute stress at the mid-plane instead of the surface (z = h/2) — loses 2 marks. > 4. Forget unit conversions (μm → m) — loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** ✅ **Key Equation to Memorize:** The biharmonic equation $D\nabla^4 w = q$ is the **most important** equation in plate mechanics. ✅ **Flexural Rigidity:** $D = \frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ — never forget the $(1-\nu^2)$ term. ✅ **Classical Deflection Formulae:** - Clamped square: $w_{max} = 0.00126 \, qa^4/D$ - Simply supported square: $w_{max} = qa^4 / (64D)$ ✅ **Natural Frequency of Square Plate:** $f_{11} = \frac{1.654}{a^2}\sqrt{D/(\rho h)}$ ✅ **Material Constants for Silicon:** - E = 130 GPa (⟨100⟩), 170 GPa (⟨110⟩) - ν = 0.28, ρ = 2330 kg/m³ ✅ **Boundary Condition Checklist:** - SS: $w=0, M=0$ - C: $w=0, \partial w/\partial n=0$ - F: $M=0, V=0$ - G: $\partial w/\partial n=0, M=0$ ✅ **Thin vs. Thick Plate Criteria:** - Thin: h/a < 0.01 → Kirchhoff-Love - Thick: 0.01 ≤ h/a ≤ 0.2 → Reissner-Mindlin - Very thick: h/a > 0.2 → 3D Elasticity ✅ **Engineering Insight:** Sensitivity of a pressure sensor ∝ 1/h³ — halving the thickness increases sensitivity by **8 times**! ✅ **Application Domains:** Pressure sensors, microphones, accelerometers, RF MEMS switches, microvalves, micromirrors, energy harvesters. ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Always verify the small-deflection assumption: $w_{max} \leq 0.1h$. If violated, nonlinear (von Kármán) theory must be used.

topics.title: Diaphragms - Typical Applications. Passive MEMS Structures - Fluid Nozzles, Ink Jet Nozzles. Sensors - Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors, Mass Flow Sensors, Acceleration Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diaphragms - Typical Applications. Passive MEMS Structures - Fluid Nozzles, Ink Jet Nozzles. Sensors - Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors, Mass Flow Sensors, Acceleration Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Diaphragms in MEMS

### Formal Definition
A **MEMS diaphragm** is a thin, deformable planar structure (typically circular, square, or rectangular) clamped at its periphery, fabricated from silicon, polysilicon, silicon nitride, or metal films. When subjected to a differential pressure, force, or acceleration, the diaphragm undergoes mechanical deflection and/or in-plane stress, which is transduced into a measurable electrical signal by an integrated sensing element (piezoresistor, capacitor, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The diaphragm is the single most reused mechanical element in microsensor design. It is the active heart of pressure sensors, microphones, micropumps, microvalves, and even some accelerometers.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Drumming Membrane
Imagine the skin stretched over the opening of a tabla or a soap bubble. When you push air into it (apply pressure), it bulges downward by a small amount. The center moves the most, while the rim (which is clamped to the wooden frame) does not move at all. A MEMS diaphragm behaves in the *exact* same way — the **clamped edge** stays still, and the **center** moves like a trampoline. Engineers place tiny electronic "eyes" (piezoresistors or capacitor plates) on this trampoline so they can measure how much it bulges, and from that bulge they calculate the pressure pushing on it.

### Common Diaphragm Geometries Used in Industry
| Geometry | Typical Application | Reason for Choice |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Circular | Pressure sensors | Symmetric stress, easy analytical modeling |
| Square / Rectangular | Accelerometers, microphones | Fits linear comb fingers along straight edges |
| Bossed (with central plate) | High-pressure sensors | Stiffens center, extends range |

### Typical Applications of Diaphragms
- **Pressure Sensors** — measure absolute, gauge, or differential pressure (medical, automotive, industrial).
- **Microphones / Acoustic Sensors** — sound pressure waves deform diaphragm; capacitive or piezoelectric read-out.
- **Micropumps and Microvalves** — diaphragm actuated by electrostatic, piezoelectric, or thermal forces pumps fluid.
- **Flow Sensors** — diaphragm forms one wall of a channel; its temperature-dependent deformation senses mass flow.
- **Optical MEMS (DMD, micromirrors)** — reflective diaphragm tilts to steer light.
- **Energy Harvesters** — diaphragm vibrates and converts mechanical energy into electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Selection Rule of Thumb:** Silicon diaphragms are used where piezoresistive sensing is desired (because silicon has a giant piezoresistive effect). Silicon nitride diaphragms are preferred for capacitive read-out because they have very low mechanical hysteresis and high yield strength.

---

## 1.2 Passive MEMS Structures — Fluid Nozzles & Ink-Jet Nozzles

### Formal Definition — Passive Structure
A **passive MEMS structure** is a micro-scale element that does not require an external power source to perform its primary function. It manipulates fluid flow, light, or mechanical motion purely through its geometry, surface tension, or pressure gradients.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept:** "Passive" in KTU-MEMS terminology strictly refers to **no external actuation energy** is needed at the structure itself; the driving energy comes from the upstream pressure source.

### 1.2.1 Fluid Nozzles (Nozzle–Diffuser Pumps)

A **fluid nozzle** is a converging–diverging microchannel whose geometry converts a pressure difference into a directed fluid jet. When paired with a downstream **diffuser** (diverging channel), it forms a **nozzle-diffuser** element — the basic building block of valveless micropumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle (The Coanda Effect Analogy):** Think of a garden hose with your thumb partly covering it — squeezing the opening makes water shoot out faster in a narrow jet. A MEMS nozzle does this at micron scale. The diffuser, on the other hand, behaves like a trumpet bell — it slows fluid down and recovers pressure.

### 1.2.2 Ink-Jet Nozzles

Ink-jet print heads are one of the **highest-volume commercial MEMS products** in human history. Two dominant technologies exist:

| Technology | Actuation | Drop Volume | Speed |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-------|
| **Thermal (Bubble Jet)** | Rapid heating creates vapor bubble that ejects a droplet | ~10–20 pL | Up to 30 kHz |
| **Piezoelectric Ink-Jet** | PZT ceramic squeezes the ink chamber | ~1–30 pL | Up to 50 kHz |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Squirt Gun
Imagine a tiny water pistol. Squeeze the trigger (actuator pushes on the ink chamber), and a single droplet flies out the barrel (nozzle). In thermal ink-jet, instead of a mechanical trigger, a tiny heater boils a microscopic bubble of ink — the bubble's expansion does the squeezing.

---

## 1.3 Sensor Categories in Module 1

### 1.3.1 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor — Definition
A **piezoresistive pressure sensor** converts mechanical strain on a diaphragm into a resistance change in diffused piezoresistors arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**. The change in bridge output voltage is proportional to applied pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physics:** Discovered by Lord Kelvin (1856) and quantified for silicon by Charles Smith (1954). The piezoresistive effect in silicon is **~100× larger** than in metals, making silicon the dominant material for high-sensitivity pressure sensors.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Tiny Stretchy Resistor
Imagine a rubber band with electrical wires attached. Stretch the rubber band → it gets longer and thinner → its electrical resistance goes up. A piezoresistor works identically, but instead of being rubbery, it is a silicon crystal whose atomic lattice is stretched by the diaphragm above it.

### 1.3.2 Mass Flow Sensor — Definition
A **mass flow sensor** directly measures the **mass** (not volume) of fluid flowing per unit time. The dominant MEMS implementations are:
- **Thermal (calorimetric) mass flow sensor** — uses a heater and upstream/downstream temperature sensors.
- **Coriolis mass flow sensor** — uses a vibrating tube whose twist is proportional to mass flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "mass" and not "volume"?** Because a liter of cold air contains more molecules than a liter of hot air (different densities). Industries like pharmaceuticals and combustion control need mass, not volume, for accuracy.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hair Dryer as a Flow Meter
Blow on a hot-wire anemometer (the tiny hot wire in some airflow meters). The faster the air blows, the more the wire cools, and the more current is needed to keep it hot. That extra current is the flow rate signal. A MEMS thermal mass flow sensor is the miniaturized, precision version of this.

### 1.3.3 Acceleration Sensor (Accelerometer) — Definition
A **MEMS accelerometer** is a proof-mass suspended by compliant springs attached to a reference frame. When the frame accelerates, the inertia of the proof mass causes it to deflect relative to the frame. This deflection is sensed capacitively, piezoresistively, or piezoelectrically and converted to an output voltage proportional to acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's First Law in Action:** A passenger standing in a bus feels "thrown backward" when the bus accelerates forward. That sensation is the proof-mass lagging behind the frame — exactly what a MEMS accelerometer measures.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cork in a Bottle
Picture a cork floating in a water bottle. When you jerk the bottle forward, the cork appears to swing backward relative to the bottle. The MEMS proof-mass is the "cork," and the supporting springs act like the water providing a restoring force.

### Typical Applications of MEMS Accelerometers
- Automotive: airbag deployment, electronic stability control, rollover detection.
- Consumer electronics: screen rotation, pedometers, image stabilization.
- Industrial: vibration monitoring of rotating machinery, seismic sensors.
- Medical: gait analysis, activity monitoring.

### Typical Applications of MEMS Pressure Sensors
- Medical: blood pressure monitoring, ventilators, intraocular pressure.
- Automotive: tire pressure, MAP (manifold absolute pressure), barometric altitude.
- Industrial: HVAC, process control, level measurement.

### Typical Applications of MEMS Mass Flow Sensors
- Medical: respirators, anesthesia delivery, insulin pumps.
- Industrial: process gas flow, semiconductor clean-room air flow.
- Environmental: air-quality monitors, leak detection.

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Glossary for KTU

| Term | One-Line Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| Diaphragm | Clamped thin plate that deflects under pressure |
| Passive Structure | No external power needed at the structure itself |
| Piezoresistor | Resistor whose R changes with mechanical strain |
| Gauge Factor (GF) | Ratio of fractional R-change to fractional L-change |
| Wheatstone Bridge | 4-resistor network that converts small ΔR into voltage |
| Proof Mass | Seismic mass whose inertia is sensed during acceleration |
| Nozzle–Diffuser | Valveless pump element that rectifies flow direction |
| Thermal Mass Flow | Sensor based on heat-transfer rate of moving fluid |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Diaphragm Mechanics — The Working Principle

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic
1. **Pressure Differential Applied** — The diaphragm separates two regions: a reference cavity (often vacuum for absolute sensors) and the measurand region.
2. **Bending** — The pressure causes the diaphragm to bend into a concave shape, with maximum deflection at the center ($w_{\max} = w_0$ at $r = 0$).
3. **In-plane Stress Generation** — Bending stretches the top fibers and compresses the bottom fibers. Radial stress $\sigma_r$ and tangential (hoop) stress $\sigma_\theta$ are generated.
4. **Strain Transfer to Piezoresistors** — Diffused resistors on the diaphragm surface experience longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_l$ and transverse strain $\varepsilon_t$.
5. **Resistance Change** — Each piezoresistor's value changes by $\Delta R/R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$.
6. **Bridge Output** — The four piezoresistors (typically arranged as a Wheatstone bridge) convert the small resistance changes into a measurable voltage $V_{out}$.

### Why It Works — The Underlying Physics
- Silicon has a **piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{44} \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$** for p-type resistors along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction — over 100× greater than metals.
- This means tiny strains (microstrains) produce detectable resistance changes.
- The diaphragm thickness $t$ is typically 5–50 µm. Thinner diaphragm → larger deflection → higher sensitivity → lower pressure range.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Diaphragm Mechanics

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear every KTU exam cycle.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Bending stiffness (flexural rigidity) | $D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12\left(1-\nu^{2}\right)}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus [Pa], $t$ = diaphragm thickness [m], $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 2 | Center deflection (clamped circular, uniform pressure $P$) | $w_{0} = \dfrac{P\,a^{4}}{64\,D}$ | $a$ = diaphragm radius [m], $P$ = pressure [Pa] |
| 3 | Deflection profile (clamped circular) | $w(r) = w_{0}\left(1 - \dfrac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}$ | $r$ = radial position [m] |
| 4 | Maximum radial stress at center | $\sigma_{r,\max} = \dfrac{3\,P\,a^{2}}{4\,t^{2}}$ | [Pa] |
| 5 | Maximum tangential stress at edge | $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = \dfrac{3\,P\,a^{2}}{4\,t^{2}}\,(1-\nu)$ | [Pa] |
| 6 | Resonant frequency (clamped circular) | $f_{r} = \dfrac{10.21}{2\pi\,a^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_{s}\,t}}$ | $\rho_{s}$ = density [kg/m³] |
| 7 | Capacitive gap change | $\Delta C = \varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\,A\left(\dfrac{1}{d - w_{0}} - \dfrac{1}{d}\right)$ | $d$ = gap [m], $A$ = area [m²] |
| 8 | Small-signal $\Delta C/C$ | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \dfrac{w_{0}}{d}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Piezoresistor GF (transverse) | $GF_{t} = -(\pi_{t}\,E)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Wheatstone bridge output | $V_{out} = V_{s}\,\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ (half-bridge) | $V_s$ = supply [V] |
| 11 | Gauge Factor (general) | $GF = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_{l}\,E$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Accelerometer natural frequency | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = spring [N/m], $m$ = mass [kg] |
| 13 | Accelerometer sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\,A\,V_{s}\,m}{k\,d^{2}}$ | [V/g] |
| 14 | Thermal mass flow heat balance | $P_{h} = \dot{m}\,c_{p}\,\Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow [kg/s] |

### Why Engineers Use These Equations
- Equations 1–6 let a designer choose diaphragm thickness, diameter, and material so that **maximum deflection stays below $\sim t/2$** (the small-deflection linear range) and **maximum stress stays below yield**.
- Equation 7–8 is critical for capacitive sensors (microphones, capacitive accelerometers).
- Equation 11 directly determines sensor output — pick $\pi_{l}$ and $E$ to maximize GF.
- Equation 13 tells designers how sensitive the accelerometer will be; tradeoff with bandwidth ($\omega_n$) is fundamental.

---

## 2.3 Passive MEMS — Nozzle-Diffuser Physics

### Operating Principle
In a **nozzle-diffuser pump**, the geometry is intentionally **asymmetric**. The flow resistance in the **nozzle direction** is low (fluid accelerates through the converging section), while the **diffuser direction** has higher resistance (flow separates and pressure recovers slowly). This rectifying behavior produces net pumping without any moving valve.

| Direction | Flow Behavior | Resistance |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Forward (nozzle direction) | Streamlined, low pressure drop | Low |
| Reverse (diffuser direction) | Recirculation zones, high pressure drop | High |

### Ink-Jet Physics
In **thermal bubble-jet**:
1. A micro-heater raises ink temperature to ~300 °C in 3 µs.
2. A vapor bubble nucleates and expands to ~30 µm in 5 µs.
3. The expanding bubble ejects a 10–20 pL droplet through the orifice at 10 m/s.
4. The bubble collapses, refilling the chamber via capillary action.

In **piezo ink-jet**:
1. A voltage applied to a PZT actuator deforms the ink chamber wall inward.
2. Pressure pulse ejects a droplet.
3. Removing voltage refills the chamber.

---

## 2.4 Sensor Operating Principles — Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor
- **Stress on diaphragm surface** (top) is tensile at the center and compressive at the edge for a clamped circular plate under uniform pressure (in the bending direction).
- **Longitudinal piezoresistors** at the edge (where radial stress is max) are aligned with the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction for maximum $\pi_{44}$.
- **Transverse piezoresistors** at the center experience tangential stress only.
- Four resistors arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** yield:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$$

- When pressure changes, two resistors increase and two decrease → bridge unbalances → $V_{out} \neq 0$.

### 2.4.2 Mass Flow Sensor (Thermal)
- A central heater maintains the flow channel at $\Delta T$ above the wall.
- An upstream temperature sensor $T_u$ and a downstream sensor $T_d$ measure the asymmetric heat distribution.
- For symmetric placement: $T_u = T_d$ at zero flow.
- With flow, the heat plume is convected downstream, so $T_d > T_u$.
- The difference $(T_d - T_u)$ is proportional to mass flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Thermal mass flow sensors are widely used in **medical ventilators** to ensure critically ill patients receive exactly the prescribed oxygen flow rate.

### 2.4.3 Capacitive Accelerometer
- Proof mass is suspended by four folded-flexure springs.
- Differential capacitors $C_1$ (between movable fingers and fixed outer fingers) and $C_2$ (between movable fingers and fixed inner fingers) are arranged on either side of the proof mass.
- Acceleration $a$ causes proof-mass displacement $x = ma/k$.
- Capacitance change: $\Delta C \propto x$.
- Differential output: $V_{out} \propto (C_1 - C_2)/(C_1 + C_2)$.

---

## 2.5 Trade-offs and Engineering Constraints

| Parameter | Increase For… | Trade-off |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Diaphragm thickness $t$ ↑ | Larger pressure range | Lower sensitivity |
| Diaphragm radius $a$ ↑ | Higher sensitivity | Larger die size, lower resonant frequency |
| Proof mass $m$ ↑ | Higher accelerometer sensitivity (lower Brownian noise) | Lower bandwidth |
| Spring stiffness $k$ ↑ | Higher bandwidth | Lower sensitivity |
| Gap $d$ ↓ | Higher capacitive sensitivity | Pull-in instability, lower dynamic range |
| Bridge supply $V_s$ ↑ | Higher output voltage | Higher power consumption, self-heating |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Center Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm

### Starting Point
We use **thin-plate theory** (Kirchhoff–Love plate theory). The governing differential equation for a circular plate under uniform pressure $P$ with clamped edges is:

$$D \nabla^{4} w(r) = P$$

For axisymmetric loading, in polar coordinates:

$$\nabla^{4} w = \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left\{ r \frac{d}{dr}\left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\}$$

### Step-by-Step Integration

**Step 1:** Let us denote the slope as $\theta = \dfrac{dw}{dr}$.

**Step 2:** Boundary conditions for a clamped circular diaphragm of radius $a$:
- At the center $r = 0$: slope is zero, $\theta(0) = 0$.
- At the edge $r = a$: deflection is zero, $w(a) = 0$.

**Step 3:** By integrating the biharmonic equation $\nabla^{4}w = P/D$ twice (with the symmetry condition $\theta(0)=0$), we arrive at the standard result:

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64\,D}\,(a^{2} - r^{2})^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum deflection at $r = 0$:

$$w_{\max} = w_0 = \frac{P\,a^{4}}{64\,D}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the bending stiffness $D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ gives the final working equation:

$$w_{0} = \frac{3\,P\,a^{4}\,(1-\nu^{2})}{16\,E\,t^{3}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $P = 100\,\text{kPa}$ (atmospheric equivalent)
- $a = 500\,\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$
- $t = 20\,\mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}$
- $E = 170\,\text{GPa}$ (silicon ⟨111⟩)
- $\nu = 0.28$ (silicon)

**Calculate $D$:**

$$D = \frac{(170 \times 10^{9})\,(2 \times 10^{-5})^{3}}{12\,(1 - 0.28^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{170 \times 10^{9} \times 8 \times 10^{-15}}{12 \times (1 - 0.0784)}$$

$$D = \frac{1.36 \times 10^{-3}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{1.36 \times 10^{-3}}{11.06}$$

$$D \approx 1.23 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{N·m}$$

**Calculate $w_0$:**

$$w_{0} = \frac{(100 \times 10^{3})\,(5 \times 10^{-4})^{4}}{64 \times 1.23 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$w_{0} = \frac{10^{5} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14}}{7.87 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$w_{0} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-9}}{7.87 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$w_{0} \approx 7.94 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.794\,\mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Under 1 atm pressure, this 500-µm radius, 20-µm thick silicon diaphragm deflects less than 1 µm. This is well within the small-deflection regime ($w_0/t \approx 0.04 \ll 1$), so linear theory is valid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Wheatstone Bridge Output for Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

### Setup
Four piezoresistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ are arranged in a bridge. Two resistors (e.g., $R_1, R_3$) are oriented radially and two ($R_2, R_4$) tangentially, so that under pressure, two increase by $\Delta R$ and two decrease by $\Delta R$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** With supply voltage $V_s$ applied across one diagonal, the bridge output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_s \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_3 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R - \Delta R$, $R_4 = R - \Delta R$:

$$V_{out} = V_s \left( \frac{R+\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R-\Delta R}{2R} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Simplify each term:

$$V_{out} = V_s \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{2R} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Collect terms:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the piezoresistor equation $\Delta R/R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$:

$$V_{out} = V_s \left( \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t \right)$$

### Sensitivity Expression
For silicon with $\pi_{44} = 138 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{Pa}^{-1}$ and maximum stress $\sigma_{\max} = 3Pa^2/(4t^2)$:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \pi_{44} \cdot \frac{3\,P\,a^{2}}{4\,t^{2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Mass Flow Sensor Heat Balance

### Setup
Fluid enters at temperature $T_0$. A heater at position $x=0$ injects power $P_h$. An upstream sensor at $x=-L$ reads $T_u$; a downstream sensor at $x=+L$ reads $T_d$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** The mass flow rate carries heat downstream. The heat injected into the fluid equals:

$$P_h = \dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_d - T_u)$$

**Step 2:** The asymmetry $\Delta T = T_d - T_u$ is related to flow velocity. For laminar flow with Péclet number $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$:

$$\Delta T \approx T_{\text{heater}} \cdot \frac{Pe}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Combining:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_h}{c_p \, \Delta T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** In a CPAP ventilator, the mass flow sensor ensures that the prescribed liters-per-minute of air are delivered to the patient. A 5% error can cause hypoventilation or barotrauma.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Capacitive Accelerometer Sensitivity

### Setup
A proof mass $m$ is suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$. Under acceleration $a$, displacement $x = ma/k$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** For a parallel-plate capacitor with area $A$, gap $d$, displaced by $x$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - x}$$

**Step 2:** For $x \ll d$, linearize:

$$\Delta C = C_0 - C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left(\frac{1}{1 - x/d} - 1\right) \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^{2}}\,x$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $x = ma/k$:

$$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A\,m\,a}{d^{2}\,k}$$

**Step 4:** Differential output $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (\Delta C / C_0) \cdot \text{(bridge factor)}$:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{m\,a}{d\,k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity requires larger $m$, smaller $d$, or smaller $k$. But smaller $k$ lowers natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, reducing bandwidth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Trade-offs

| Feature | Piezoresistive Pressure | Capacitive Pressure | Thermal Mass Flow | Capacitive Accelerometer |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Sensitivity | High | Medium-High | Medium | High |
| Power | Low (mW) | Very Low (µW) | High (10–100 mW) | Low (mW) |
| Temperature drift | Moderate | Low | High | Low |
| Fabrication | Standard CMOS | Standard CMOS | Specialized | Standard CMOS |
| Output | Voltage | Capacitance | Voltage | Voltage |
| Typical use | Tire pressure | Microphones | Ventilators | Smartphones |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Pressure P] --> B[Clamped Circular Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Bending Induces Stress]
    C --> D[Radial Stress sigma_r at Edge]
    C --> E[Tangential Stress sigma_theta at Center]
    D --> F1[Piezoresistor R1, R3 Oriented Radial]
    E --> F2[Piezoresistor R2, R4 Oriented Tangential]
    F1 --> G[Wheatstone Bridge]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Bridge Output Vout proportional to P]
    H --> I[Amplifier and Signal Conditioning]
    I --> J[Calibrated Pressure Reading]
    K[Bridge Supply Voltage Vs] --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Pressure → diaphragm bend → stress field → piezoresistors on top surface change R → bridge imbalance → output voltage → amplifier → digital pressure reading. This is the *exact* signal path in every commercial piezoresistive pressure sensor (e.g., Bosch BMP280, Honeywell SSC series).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Flow] --> B[Upstream Temp Sensor Tu]

    D --> E[Outlet]
    F[Heater Controller] --> C
    C --> G[Differential Temperature Delta T = Td - Tu]
    B --> G
    G --> H[Mass Flow Calculation m_dot = Ph / cp dT]

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Capacitive Accelerometer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Acceleration a] --> B[Reference Frame Moves]
    B --> C[Proof Mass Lags by Inertia]
    C --> D[Spring Restoring Force F = kx]
    D --> E[Equilibrium Displacement x = ma / k]
    E --> F[Differential Capacitor C1 Changes]
    E --> G[Differential Capacitor C2 Changes]
    F --> H[Capacitance Bridge]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Voltage Vout proportional to a]

    J --> K[Acceleration Reading in g]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Nozzle-Diffuser Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pump Chamber] --> B[Nozzle Converging Section]
    B --> C[Throat]
    C --> D[Diffuser Diverging Section]
    D --> E[Outlet]
    F[Reverse Flow Path] -. High Resistance .-> B
    E -. Low Resistance Forward .-> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymmetric flow resistance** is the secret of valveless pumping. Forward flow experiences ~10× less resistance than reverse flow, producing a net DC pumping action with zero moving parts.
