# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor. Mention the role of the Wheatstone bridge.

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark structured response):**

A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of a thin silicon diaphragm (typically 10–50 µm thick) clamped at its edges over a sealed reference cavity. When external pressure is applied, the diaphragm bends, generating **in-plane radial and tangential stresses** on its top surface. Four piezoresistors are ion-implanted at locations of maximum stress (two at the edge in radial orientation, two at the center in tangential orientation). Under pressure, two resistors increase and two decrease in value, **unbalancing a Wheatstone bridge** connected across them. The bridge output voltage $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ is proportional to the applied pressure. This voltage is then amplified and digitized to display the pressure reading.

The **Wheatstone bridge** converts the small resistance changes (typically $\Delta R/R \sim 10^{-3}$) into a measurable voltage, while also providing **temperature compensation** (since all four resistors experience the same temperature).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between active and passive MEMS structures. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active MEMS | Passive MEMS |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| External power needed | Yes (electrical, thermal, optical) | No (geometry-driven) |
| Moving parts | Yes (actuated) | Usually no |
| Function | Transduction, actuation | Flow control, fluid manipulation |

**Active MEMS examples:** (1) Piezoresistive pressure sensor (needs $V_s$). (2) Capacitive accelerometer (needs $V_s$).

**Passive MEMS examples:** (1) Nozzle-diffuser element. (2) Ink-jet orifice plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative classification accepted by KTU:** Some texts classify any structure without *electrical* actuation as passive. Both classifications will be accepted; ensure you state your interpretation clearly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Module Internal Choice — *Question A (14 marks)*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum deflection of a clamped circular diaphragm under uniform pressure. Define bending stiffness and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square silicon diaphragm of side $L = 1\,\text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 25\,\mu\text{m}$ is subjected to a uniform pressure of $50\,\text{kPa}$. Young's modulus $E = 170\,\text{GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$. Calculate (i) the bending stiffness, and (ii) the maximum deflection at the center. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation. KTU expects the following key steps in the solution:

1. State the plate equation: $D \nabla^{4}w = P$.
2. State the boundary conditions: $w(a) = 0$, $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_{a} = 0$, $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_{0} = 0$.
3. Integrate twice and apply boundary conditions to get:

$$w(r) = \frac{P\,a^{4}}{64\,D}\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)^{2}$$

4. Maximum at $r = 0$:

$$w_{\max} = \frac{P\,a^{4}}{64\,D}$$

5. Define bending stiffness:

$$D = \frac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$$

6. **Physical significance:** $D$ quantifies the resistance of the plate to bending. Higher $D$ means stiffer diaphragm, smaller deflection for the same pressure. Depends on the cube of thickness — so halving $t$ reduces $D$ by 8×, increasing sensitivity 8×.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i): Bending stiffness**

$$D = \frac{(170 \times 10^{9})\,(25 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12(1 - 0.28^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{170 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14}}{12 \times 0.9216}$$

$$D = \frac{2.656 \times 10^{-3}}{11.06} \approx 2.40 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step (ii): Maximum deflection**

For a square diaphragm, KTU accepts either the exact Levy solution or the engineering approximation $a \approx L/\sqrt{\pi}$ for an equivalent circular plate. Using the equivalent radius:

$$a_{eq} = \frac{L}{\sqrt{\pi}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{\pi}} \approx 5.64 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$$

$$w_{0} = \frac{P\,a_{eq}^{4}}{64\,D} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{3})\,(5.64 \times 10^{-4})^{4}}{64 \times 2.40 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$w_{0} = \frac{5 \times 10^{4} \times 1.012 \times 10^{-13}}{1.536 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$w_{0} = \frac{5.06 \times 10^{-9}}{1.536 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 3.29 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.329\,\mu\text{m}$$

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Module Internal Choice — *Question B (14 marks, alternative)*

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the working principle of a MEMS thermal mass flow sensor. Derive the relation between mass flow rate and sensor output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 1\,\mu\text{g}$, spring constant $k = 1\,\text{N/m}$, and capacitor gap $d = 2\,\mu\text{m}$, plate area $A = 100 \times 100\,\mu\text{m}^{2}$, supply $V_s = 5\,\text{V}$. Find the natural frequency and the sensitivity (V/g) of the accelerometer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle and Derivation:**

Refer to Section 2.4.2 for the theory and Section 4.2 for the diagram. Key points for full marks:

1. A heater maintains a constant temperature above the wall.
2. Upstream $T_u$ and downstream $T_d$ thermopiles sense the asymmetric heat plume.
3. At zero flow: $T_u = T_d$. With flow: $T_d > T_u$.
4. Heat balance:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_h}{c_p (T_d - T_u)}$$

5. The heater power $P_h$ is electronically controlled to maintain constant overheat. Hence $\Delta T$ measurement directly gives $\dot{m}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Natural frequency:**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1}{10^{-6}}}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 1000 \approx 159.15\,\text{Hz}$$

**Sensitivity** (using $S = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V_s m}{k d^{2}}$ per Section 2.4.3 and Section 3.4):

Convert to acceleration in g: $a = g \cdot N$ where $N$ is the number of g's. We want output V per g:

For $a = g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$:

$$x = \frac{ma}{k} = \frac{(10^{-6})(9.81)}{1} = 9.81 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$$

Capacitance change:

$$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A\,x}{d^{2}} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-8})(9.81 \times 10^{-6})}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{8.69 \times 10^{-25}}{4 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.17 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{F}$$

Base capacitance:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-8})}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.43 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{F}$$

Sensitivity (V/g) using half-bridge factor:

$$S = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = 5 \times \frac{2.17 \times 10^{-13}}{4.43 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 24.5\,\text{V/g}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly forget to **convert the proof mass to kg first** (1 µg = 10⁻⁹ kg, not 10⁻⁶ kg). Another common mistake is to use area in cm² instead of m². Both errors will cascade and lose 5–7 marks. **Always re-state units before every substitution.**

---

## 5.3 Common Mistakes to Avoid (KTU Valuation Patterns)

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**

1. **Confusing maximum deflection location** — for a clamped circular plate under uniform pressure, max deflection is at $r=0$ (center), but max stress is at the edge. Students often state both occur at the center. **Marks lost: 1–2.**
2. **Using $D = Et^3/12$** instead of $D = Et^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$. The Poisson correction is non-trivial and KTU specifically tests it. **Marks lost: 1.**
3. **Forgetting $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$** when converting between V/g and V/(m/s²). **Marks lost: 1–2.**
4. **Wrong orientation of piezoresistors** — radial resistors must be at the edge and tangential at the center, or vice versa depending on the bridge configuration. Drawing them all at the center shows lack of understanding. **Marks lost: 1–2.**
5. **Not drawing a labeled block diagram** in 14-mark answers. KTU explicitly allocates 2 marks for a labeled diagram. **Marks lost: 2.**
6. **Mixing up active vs passive** MEMS classification. Some students write "ink-jet is active" because it ejects a droplet — but the droplet ejection is *thermally* driven by an external heater, so technically it is **active**. Either classification is defensible if argued well.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Diaphragm** is the most reused MEMS element — used in pressure sensors, microphones, micropumps, and as the seismic mass support in some accelerometers.
- [ ] **Center deflection of clamped circular diaphragm:** $w_0 = P a^4 / (64 D)$.
- [ ] **Bending stiffness:** $D = E t^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$ — depends on **cube** of thickness.
- [ ] **Maximum stress (radial, at center):** $\sigma_{r,\max} = 3 P a^2 / (4 t^2)$.
- [ ] **Resonant frequency** of diaphragm sets the upper frequency limit of any sensor built on it.
- [ ] **Passive MEMS** = no external actuation energy at the structure itself; relies on geometry (e.g., nozzle-diffuser) or upstream pressure.
- [ ] **Nozzle-diffuser** works because of asymmetric flow resistance — forward direction (nozzle) has ~10× less resistance than reverse direction (diffuser).
- [ ] **Ink-jet bubble-jet** boils ink in 3 µs; piezoelectric ink-jet squeezes ink via PZT — both produce 10–30 pL droplets.
- [ ] **Piezoresistive effect in silicon is ~100× metals** — this is why silicon dominates pressure sensing.
- [ ] **Wheatstone bridge output:** $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ for half-bridge.
- [ ] **Gauge factor:** $GF = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$; for silicon $\pi_l E$ term dominates.
- [ ] **Thermal mass flow sensor** uses the asymmetry between $T_u$ and $T_d$ to measure mass flow.
- [ ] **MEMS accelerometer** works on Newton's first law: proof-mass deflection $= m a / k$.
- [ ] **Natural frequency** of accelerometer: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$; this sets the bandwidth.
- [ ] **Sensitivity vs bandwidth trade-off** is fundamental — you cannot have both high sensitivity and high bandwidth simultaneously.
- [ ] **Always convert units to SI** (m, kg, s, Pa) before any numerical substitution.
- [ ] **Always draw a labeled diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark question — minimum 2 marks reserved.
- [ ] **Remember** that for square diaphragms, $a_{eq} = L/\sqrt{\pi}$ converts to an equivalent circular plate.

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- **"D-C-S"** — **D**eflection, **C**enter, **S**tress at edge — easy way to remember diaphragm behavior.
- **"P-T-R"** — **P**ressure → **T**hin plate bend → **R**esistance change → bridge output.
- **"Four-Pi-Rule"** — PiezoResistor GF in silicon is dominated by the $\pi_l E$ term ($\sim 100\times$ larger than $1+2\nu$).

### Likely KTU Follow-up Topics (for cross-module linkage)
- Capacitive sensing read-out circuits (covered in Module 2 typically).
- Packaging and stress-isolation (Module 3).
- BioMEMS applications using these sensors (Module 4 or 5).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators - Micro Mechanical Valves, Micro Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actuators - Micro Mechanical Valves, Micro Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Micro Actuators in Fluidic Domain

In Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), **actuators** are devices that convert electrical, thermal, magnetic, or chemical energy into mechanical motion or fluid displacement at the micro-scale. Within fluid-handling MEMS, two of the most industrially significant actuator-driven sub-systems are **Micro Mechanical Valves** and **Micro Pumps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OEICT612, Module 1):**
> *"Actuators – Micro Mechanical Valves, Micro Pumps"* is a high-weightage topic. The examiner typically tests (a) classification, (b) working principles with neat sketches, and (c) comparative design analysis of valve/pump mechanisms.

---

## 1.2 Micro Mechanical Valves – Formal Definition

A **Micro Mechanical Valve** is a micro-scale fluidic switching device that regulates, directs, or controls the flow of a fluid (gas or liquid) by opening, closing, or partially obstructing one or more passageways. It functions as the microscopic equivalent of a household tap or a hydraulic valve.

### Conceptual Analogy – The Tiny Traffic Police
Imagine a **traffic policeman standing at a narrow lane intersection**. Depending on hand signals:
- **Hand up** = valve **closed** (no flow)
- **Hand sideways** = valve **open** (flow permitted)
- **Partial hand tilt** = valve **partially open** (throttled flow)

Now, scale this policeman down to a 100 µm × 100 µm silicon flap. The "hand signal" is given by an **electrostatic, electromagnetic, piezoelectric, thermal, or shape-memory force**, and the "lane" is a microchannel etched in silicon or glass. That is essentially a **Micro Mechanical Valve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A Micro Mechanical Valve is a MEMS device consisting of a movable mechanical element (membrane, cantilever, or poppet) that physically blocks or unblocks a microchannel orifice in response to an externally applied actuation signal.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Micro Mechanical Valves

Micro valves can be categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories | Brief Note |
|------|------------|------------|
| **Actuation Principle** | Active (externally powered) vs. Passive (self-driven) | Active uses electrostatic/thermal/piezo force; passive uses pressure or check-flow |
| **Default State** | Normally Open (NO) vs. Normally Closed (NC) | Determines fail-safe behavior |
| **Geometry** | Poppet, Flap/Membrane, Ball, Nozzle-Diffuser | Geometry decides the flow characteristic |

### Common Actuation Mechanisms for Active Valves:
1. **Electrostatic** – Coulombic force between charged plates.
2. **Piezoelectric** – Inverse piezoelectric strain.
3. **Electromagnetic** – Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor.
4. **Thermopneumatic** – Expansion of heated fluid.
5. **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** – Phase transformation force.
6. **Bimetallic** – Differential thermal expansion.

---

## 1.4 Micro Pumps – Formal Definition

A **Micro Pump** is a MEMS device that actively transports a fluid (liquid or gas) through a microchannel network by converting an input energy form (electrical, pneumatic, piezoelectric) into controlled fluid displacement and pressure.

### Conceptual Analogy – The Microscopic Heart
A micro pump is to a microfluidic chip what the **human heart** is to the circulatory system:
- **Reciprocating pump** = heart (suck–push cycle)
- **Continuous pump** = rotating impeller (gear, centrifugal)
- The **check valves** at the inlet/outlet are like the **mitral and aortic valves** of the heart, ensuring unidirectional flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A Micro Pump is an active MEMS device that imparts momentum or pressure to a working fluid, generating a measurable flow rate (typically nL/min to mL/min range) against a hydraulic back-pressure.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Micro Pumps

| Class | Sub-Types | Moving Parts? | Typical Drive |
|-------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Mechanical (Displacement)** | Reciprocating – Peristaltic, Piezo-driven, PZT-diaphragm | Yes | Piezo, electrostatic, SMA |
| **Mechanical (Dynamic)** | Rotary – Gear, Centrifugal, Impeller | Yes | Electromagnetic, electrostatic |
| **Non-Mechanical** | Electroosmotic (EO), Electrohydrodynamic (EHD), Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD), Capillary, Bubble, Surface-tension | No (ideally) | Electric field, magnetic field, thermal |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic – "RPM ENE"** for pumps: **R**eciprocating, **P**eristaltic, **M**echanical-rotary / **E**lectroosmotic, **N**on-mechanical-thermal, **E**lectrohydrodynamic.

---

## 1.6 Why These Devices Matter – Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Micro valves and micro pumps are the **building blocks of micro Total Analysis Systems (µTAS), Lab-on-a-Chip (LoC), drug-delivery implants, and micro-propulsion systems** for CubeSats. Without them, fluids cannot be precisely dosed, mixed, separated, or circulated inside a portable micro-device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Micro Mechanical Valve – Operational Physics

### 2.1.1 Working Principle (Generalized)

A typical **active micro valve** consists of the following building blocks:

1. **Inlet microchannel** – fluid source.
2. **Outlet microchannel** – fluid destination.
3. **Valve seat / orifice** – the constriction to be opened or blocked.
4. **Movable element (membrane / cantilever / poppet)** – sealing or un-sealing component.
5. **Actuator** – provides the mechanical force to displace the movable element.
6. **Biasing element (spring)** – restores the valve to its default state.

**Operating logic (step-by-step):**
1. Fluid enters the inlet port under source pressure $P_{in}$.
2. The membrane is initially held against the seat by a **spring bias force** $F_s$ (NC valve) or away from it (NO valve).
3. When the **actuator force** $F_a$ exceeds $F_s$, the membrane lifts off the seat.
4. Fluid flows through the gap of height $h(t)$ until the actuator is de-energized.
5. The spring restores the membrane to its default position, halting flow.

### 2.1.2 Electrostatic Micro Valve – Detailed Logic

- Two parallel plates form a **variable capacitor**: the **lower fixed electrode** on the substrate, and the **upper movable membrane** as the second electrode.
- Applying voltage $V$ produces Coulombic attractive force:
  - $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial g} V^2$, where $g$ is the gap.
- As $g$ shrinks, $C$ increases, and the force grows non-linearly → **pull-in instability** occurs when $g$ drops to $\dfrac{2}{3} g_0$.
- This snap-through action gives a **bistable** or **fast-switching** characteristic, ideal for digital microvalves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters:** Electrostatic valves consume **virtually zero steady-state current** (insulator between plates), making them perfect for **battery-powered implants**.

### 2.1.3 Piezoelectric Micro Valve – Detailed Logic

- A **piezo disc** (PZT) is bonded onto a silicon diaphragm.
- When voltage $V$ is applied, the PZT disc generates an in-plane strain that translates into **out-of-plane bending** of the diaphragm.
- The diaphragm displacement $\delta$ can be computed as:
  - $\delta \propto d_{31} \dfrac{L^2}{t^2} V$  
  where $d_{31}$ is the transverse piezoelectric coefficient, $L$ is the diaphragm length, $t$ its thickness.
- Displacement is **linear and proportional** to voltage → excellent for **proportional control valves**.

### 2.1.4 Thermopneumatic / Bimetallic Valve

- **Thermopneumatic**: A sealed cavity contains a gas/liquid; resistive heating expands it, deflecting the membrane by tens of micrometers.
- **Bimetallic**: Two materials with different thermal expansion coefficients (e.g., Al and Si) bend when heated.
- Force is high, but **response time is slow (10 ms – 1 s)** because of thermal mass.

---

## 2.2 Micro Pump – Operational Physics

### 2.2.1 Reciprocating (Diaphragm) Micro Pump

This is the **most commonly examined KTU architecture**. It has three components:

1. **Pumping chamber** – bounded by a flexible diaphragm.
2. **Inlet check valve** – admits fluid during the suction stroke.
3. **Outlet check valve** – ejects fluid during the delivery stroke.

**Cycle of operation:**

| Phase | Diaphragm Action | Inlet Valve | Outlet Valve | Fluid Behavior |
|-------|------------------|-------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 (Suction) | Deflects **upward/inward** (chamber volume ↑) | **Open** (low pressure) | **Closed** | Fluid drawn in |
| 2 (Delivery) | Deflects **downward/outward** (chamber volume ↓) | **Closed** (high pressure) | **Open** | Fluid expelled |

The two check valves ensure **unidirectional net flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A single diaphragm with two passive check valves produces **pulsatile, unidirectional** flow. To obtain smoother flow, designers cascade **three chambers** driven in 120° phase-shifted sequences (peristaltic principle).

### 2.2.2 Peristaltic Micro Pump

Three or more actuator segments are fired in a **traveling-wave sequence**:
- Segment A squeezes → B squeezes (A relaxes) → C squeezes (B relaxes) → repeat.
- The "wave" of constriction travels along the channel, **pushing the fluid bolus forward**, much like the esophagus swallowing food.

### 2.2.3 Electroosmotic (EO) Pump

A non-mechanical pump exploiting the **electrical double layer (EDL)** that forms spontaneously at the glass/liquid interface.

- The EDL has $\zeta$-potential ~ –50 mV for glass–water.
- Applying an axial electric field $E$ drags the mobile counter-ions, which via viscosity drag the bulk fluid.
- Flow velocity (Smoluchowski equation):
  - $u_{eo} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta}{\eta} E = \mu_{eo} E$
  - $\mu_{eo}$ is the **electroosmotic mobility** (typical $\sim 5 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/V·s for water in glass).

### 2.2.4 Performance Parameters of a Micro Pump

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Flow rate | $Q$ | 1 nL/min – 10 mL/min | m³/s |
| Back pressure | $\Delta P$ | 1 kPa – 100 kPa | Pa |
| Power consumption | $P$ | µW – mW | W |
| Stroke volume | $V_s$ | nL – µL | m³ |
| Pumping frequency | $f$ | 1 Hz – 10 kHz | Hz |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | 5 % – 40 % | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stroke Volume Equation:**
> $V_s = A_d \cdot \delta$, where $A_d$ is the diaphragm area and $\delta$ is the peak deflection.
> **Average Flow Rate:**
> $Q = f \cdot V_s \cdot \eta_v$, where $\eta_v$ is the volumetric efficiency (0.5–0.9 for well-designed check valves).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2}$ | Electrostatic force on parallel-plate actuator | N |
| 2 | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | Pull-in voltage of electrostatic valve | V |
| 3 | $\delta = d_{31} \dfrac{L^2}{t^2} V$ | PZT diaphragm tip deflection | m |
| 4 | $Q = f \cdot A_d \cdot \delta \cdot \eta_v$ | Average flow rate of reciprocating pump | m³/s |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{f \cdot A_d \cdot \delta \cdot R_{hyd}}{A_d^2}$ | Maximum back pressure (function of hydraulic resistance) | Pa |
| 6 | $u_{eo} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta}{\eta} E$ | Smoluchowski EO velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho u D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (microfluidic: $Re \ll 100$, laminar regime) | – |
| 8 | $R_{hyd} = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille hydraulic resistance (circular) | Pa·s/m³ |
| 9 | $P_{in} \cdot A_{seat} = F_{spring} + F_{pressure}$ | Force balance on valve membrane | N |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_{hyd}}$ | Volumetric flow (Ohm's-law analog) | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX instead of `\vert` (which breaks markdown tables). Example: write $R_{hyd} \vert_{circular}$ using the LaTeX absolute-value macro `\vert R_{hyd} \vert`.

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- Equation 1 & 2 drive the **design of digital microvalves** in fuel-cell manifolds.
- Equation 3 is used by **medical infusion device** companies to size piezo stack actuators.
- Equation 4 is the **backbone calculation** in lab-on-chip dosing systems.
- Equation 6 dominates **DNA electrophoresis** and **capillary electrophoresis** design.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Engineering Judgment

| Feature | Active Valve | Passive Check Valve | Reciprocating Pump | EO Pump |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|----------|
| Power needed | Yes (transient) | No (flow-driven) | Yes | Yes (high V) |
| Moving parts | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Back-pressure capability | High (PZT) | Low | High | Low–Medium |
| Biocompatibility | Good (Ti, Si) | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Typical application | Drug dosing, inkjet | One-way flow in pump | Insulin pumps, µTAS | Capillary electrophoresis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written **exhaustively, step-by-step**, as required for KTU 14-mark questions. The examiner rewards every line of clear substitution.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Electrostatic Pull-In Voltage of a Micro Valve

### Problem Setup
A circular micro valve membrane of radius $R$ is suspended above a fixed electrode at rest gap $d_0$ via a linear spring of stiffness $k$. Derive the **pull-in voltage** $V_{PI}$ at which the membrane snaps onto the seat.

### Step 1 – Capacitance of the Parallel-Plate Geometry
For a circular plate of radius $R$ and gap $g$:

$$
C(g) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2}{g}
$$

**Logic:** Parallel-plate capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 A / g$ with $A = \pi R^2$.

### Step 2 – Electrostatic Force
Force is the negative gradient of stored energy:

$$
F_e = \dfrac{1}{2} V^2 \dfrac{dC}{dg} = \dfrac{1}{2} V^2 \left( -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2}{g^2} \right)
$$

Magnitude:

$$
F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2 V^2}{2 g^2}
$$

**Logic:** The negative sign means force is attractive (pulls plate down).

### Step 3 – Mechanical Restoring Force
For a spring-loaded membrane displaced by $\delta$ from rest:

$$
F_m = k \cdot (d_0 - g)
$$

**Logic:** When gap shrinks from $d_0$ to $g$, displacement is $d_0 - g$.

### Step 4 – Mechanical Equilibrium
At equilibrium: $F_e = F_m$:

$$
\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2 V^2}{2 g^2} = k (d_0 - g)
$$

### Step 5 – Condition for Pull-In Instability
Pull-in occurs when **small perturbations grow**, i.e., when the electrostatic stiffness exceeds the mechanical stiffness:

$$
\dfrac{\partial F_e}{\partial g} \bigg\vert_{PI} = \dfrac{\partial F_m}{\partial g} \bigg\vert_{PI}
$$

Compute derivatives:

$$
\dfrac{\partial F_e}{\partial g} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2 V^2}{g^3} \quad ; \quad \dfrac{\partial F_m}{\partial g} = -k
$$

Set magnitudes equal:

$$
\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2 V_{PI}^2}{g_{PI}^3} = k
$$

### Step 6 – Solve for the Critical Gap
Substitute $V_{PI}^2$ from equilibrium (Step 4):

$$
g_{PI}^3 \cdot k = \varepsilon_0 \pi R^2 \cdot \dfrac{2 g_{PI}^2 \, k (d_0 - g_{PI})}{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2}
$$

Simplify:

$$
g_{PI} = 2 (d_0 - g_{PI}) \;\Rightarrow\; g_{PI} = \dfrac{2 d_0}{3}
$$

**Logic:** The membrane becomes unstable when it has traveled **one-third** of the initial gap.

### Step 7 – Final Pull-In Voltage
Substitute $g_{PI} = 2 d_0 / 3$ back into equilibrium:

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k (d_0 - g_{PI}) g_{PI}^2}{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k \, (d_0/3) \, (4 d_0^2 / 9)}{\varepsilon_0 \pi R^2}}
$$

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \pi R^2}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Matches Formula #2 in the Formula Sheet.** This is a high-yield derivation — practice rewriting it from memory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Average Flow Rate of a Reciprocating Micro Pump

### Problem Setup
A piezo-driven diaphragm pump has a circular diaphragm of radius $R_d$ deflecting with peak amplitude $\delta$ at frequency $f$. The volumetric efficiency is $\eta_v$. Derive the **average flow rate**.

### Step 1 – Diaphragm Area
$$
A_d = \pi R_d^2
$$

### Step 2 – Stroke Volume
Volume displaced per stroke (swept volume):

$$
V_s = A_d \cdot \delta = \pi R_d^2 \delta
$$

**Logic:** Integrating the deflection profile over the diaphragm area gives $A_d \cdot \delta_{\text{peak}}$ (for a parabolic mode shape, the effective volume is $\approx 0.5 A_d \delta$, but KTU assumes the simple product).

### Step 3 – Volumetric Flow Rate
With $f$ strokes per second:

$$
Q_{ideal} = f \cdot V_s = f \pi R_d^2 \delta
$$

### Step 4 – Account for Volumetric Efficiency
Some back-flow occurs through imperfect check-valve sealing:

$$
Q = \eta_v \cdot f \pi R_d^2 \delta
$$

**Logic:** $\eta_v$ is the ratio of delivered to swept volume.

### Step 5 – Numerical Example
Let $R_d = 5\,\text{mm}$, $\delta = 10\,\mu\text{m}$, $f = 100\,\text{Hz}$, $\eta_v = 0.7$:

$$
Q = 0.7 \times 100 \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q = 0.7 \times 100 \times \pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
Q \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 55 \,\mu\text{L/s} = 3.3 \,\text{mL/min}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Electroosmotic Flow Velocity in a Microchannel

### Step 1 – Electrical Double Layer Concept
Near a charged wall, counter-ions form a thin layer of thickness (Debye length):

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 n_0 e^2 z^2}}
$$

For water at room temperature and 1 mM electrolyte: $\lambda_D \approx 10$ nm.

### Step 2 – Velocity Profile (Smoluchowski Limit)
When channel height $h \gg \lambda_D$, the velocity is **plug-like** (flat profile across the cross-section):

$$
u_{eo}(y) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta}{\eta} E = \mu_{eo} E
$$

**Logic:** A balance of electrical body force on the EDL and viscous shear yields the famous Smoluchowski result.

### Step 3 – Total Volumetric Flow
$$
Q = u_{eo} \cdot A_c = \mu_{eo} E \cdot w h
$$

where $A_c = w h$ is the channel cross-section.

### Step 4 – Numerical Example
Glass–water: $\zeta = -50$ mV, $\varepsilon_r = 80$, $\eta = 1$ mPa·s, $E = 100$ V/cm.

$$
\mu_{eo} = \dfrac{80 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\mu_{eo} \approx 3.54 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2 /(\text{V·s})
$$

$$
u_{eo} = 3.54 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^4 = 3.54 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} = 354 \,\mu\text{m/s}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparative Merits/Demerits

| Criterion | Piezo Pump | EO Pump | SMA Pump | Bimetallic Pump |
|-----------|------------|---------|----------|------------------|
| Drive voltage | 50–200 V | 100 V – 5 kV | 1–5 V (with heating) | 1–5 V |
| Stroke | µm scale | None (continuous) | Large (100 µm) | Large (10s of µm) |
| Flow rate | 1 µL – 1 mL/min | 1 nL – 10 µL/min | 1 µL – 1 mL/min | Low |
| Power | mW | mW–W | 100 mW | 10 mW |
| Biocompatibility | Good | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| Back-pressure | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| Best Application | Insulin pumps, µTAS | CE, lab chips | Drug delivery | Low-dosing systems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Differentiate between active and passive micro valves with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Valve | Passive Valve |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| External power | Required | Not required |
| Control | Externally actuated | Flow-driven |
| Example | Electrostatic, Piezo | Check valve, Nozzle-diffuser |

(1 mark each row, 3 rows = 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**State the principle of an electroosmotic micro pump and write the Smoluchowski equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An electroosmotic pump uses the **electrical double layer (EDL)** formed at a charged channel wall. When an axial electric field is applied, the mobile counter-ions in the EDL are dragged, and via viscous coupling, the bulk fluid moves plug-like through the channel (1.5 marks).

Smoluchowski equation:

$$
u_{eo} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \zeta}{\eta} E \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

where $\zeta$ is the zeta potential, $\eta$ viscosity, $\varepsilon_r$ relative permittivity, $E$ electric field (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Never** omit the **diagram** – 3 to 4 marks are reserved for a labelled sketch in valve/pump questions.
> - **Always** state the **assumptions** (e.g., "neglecting edge effects," "laminar flow regime") before derivations.
> - **Always** carry forward the **units** in numerical steps; examiners award 0.5 mark for dimensional consistency.
> - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** by failing to mention the **check valve** function in pump questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **piezoelectric diaphragm micro pump**. Discuss the role of inlet and outlet check valves in producing unidirectional flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average flow rate** of a reciprocating micro pump in terms of diaphragm area $A_d$, peak deflection $\delta$, operating frequency $f$, and volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$. A piezoelectric pump has a diaphragm of diameter **10 mm**, peak deflection **15 µm**, frequency **200 Hz**, and $\eta_v = 0.8$. Calculate the flow rate in µL/min. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution – Question A

#### (a) Working of Piezoelectric Diaphragm Micro Pump (7 marks)

1. **Block diagram** (3 marks):
   - Draw a labelled diagram: reservoir → inlet check valve → pumping chamber (with PZT-bonded diaphragm) → outlet check valve → discharge line → actuation electronics (oscillator + amplifier).

2. **Working principle** (2 marks):
   - When AC voltage is applied, the **PZT disc undergoes inverse piezoelectric strain**.
   - The strain bends the silicon diaphragm, periodically varying the **pumping chamber volume**.
   - During **suction** (volume ↑), the inlet check valve opens (low pressure) and the outlet stays closed; fluid enters the chamber.
   - During **delivery** (volume ↓), the outlet check valve opens (high pressure) and the inlet closes; fluid is expelled.

3. **Role of check valves** (2 marks):
   - The **passive check valves** act as **rectifiers**, ensuring that regardless of diaphragm direction, the net flow is always from inlet to outlet.
   - They convert the **bidirectional diaphragm motion** into **unidirectional net flow** — analogous to the mitral and aortic valves in the human heart.

#### (b) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- Stroke volume: $V_s = A_d \cdot \delta$
- Average flow: $Q = f \cdot V_s \cdot \eta_v = f A_d \delta \eta_v$

**Numerical (3 marks):**
- $A_d = \pi (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5}$ m²
- $\delta = 15 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $f = 200$ Hz
- $\eta_v = 0.8$

$$
Q = 200 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 15 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.8
$$

$$
Q = 200 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 12 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q = 200 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-10} = 1.885 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to µL/min (1 m³/s = $6 \times 10^{13}$ µL/min... actually 1 m³ = $10^9$ µL; 1 s = 1/60 min):

$$
Q = 1.885 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^9 \times 60 = 1.885 \times 10^4 \times 60 / 10^{-2} \;\mu\text{L/min}
$$

Cleaner step-by-step:
$1.885 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s = $1.885 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^9$ µL/s = $188.5$ µL/s.
$188.5 \times 60 = 11{,}310$ µL/min $\approx$ **11.3 mL/min**.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Shortcut:** Always keep the **final answer with the unit**; examiners confirm 0.5 mark for correct units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **micro mechanical valve** using **electrostatic actuation**. Derive the expression for the **pull-in voltage** and discuss the significance of the $2/3$ gap rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **mechanical and non-mechanical micro pumps** with at least **three criteria**. Discuss the application of electroosmotic pumps in **drug delivery** and **capillary electrophoresis**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution – Question B

#### (a) Electrostatic Micro Valve – Construction, Working, Pull-in (7 marks)

1. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - **Lower fixed electrode** deposited on a glass or silicon substrate.
   - **Spacer** (a few µm thick) defines the rest gap.
   - **Upper movable membrane** (polysilicon, metal, or metallized polymer) acts as the second electrode and as the valve seal.
   - **Inlet and outlet microchannels** etched through the substrate.

2. **Working (2 marks):**
   - With no voltage, the membrane rests on the seat due to **spring bias** → fluid is blocked.
   - When voltage $V$ is applied between the electrodes, the resulting **electrostatic attractive force** deflects the membrane, opening the gap, allowing flow.
   - Removing $V$ lets the spring restore the membrane, closing the valve.

3. **Pull-in derivation (2 marks):**
   - State $F_e = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2 g^2)$, $F_m = k(d_0 - g)$.
   - Set equilibrium and stability condition, get $g_{PI} = 2 d_0 / 3$, $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 \pi R^2)}$.

4. **Significance of 2/3 rule (1 mark):**
   - It defines the **maximum stable deflection**; beyond $g = 2 d_0 / 3$, the electrostatic force overwhelms mechanical stiffness and the plate **snaps in** (pull-in instability). Designers size the membrane so the **operating range is below** this threshold to avoid bistable or destructive collapse.

#### (b) Comparison and Applications (7 marks)

1. **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Mechanical Pump | Non-Mechanical Pump |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Moving parts | Yes | No |
| Wear & fatigue | Concern | Negligible |
| Back-pressure | High | Low–Medium |
| Fabrication | Complex (assembly) | Simpler (planar) |
| Fluid compatibility | Risk of contamination | Better for biological fluids |
| Power source | AC + Amplifier | DC high voltage |
| Examples | Piezo, peristaltic | EO, EHD, MHD |

2. **Applications of EO Pumps (3 marks):**
   - **Drug delivery:** precise dosing of microliter volumes for ocular, transdermal, or implantable systems; no pulsation, no moving parts → excellent biocompatibility.
   - **Capillary electrophoresis:** provides **plug-like flow** (no parabolic profile), eliminating the **band-broadening** seen with pressure-driven flows, thus improving separation resolution.
   - **Lab-on-a-Chip:** integrated fluidic networks in glass chips for analytical chemistry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Tick off before entering the exam hall):**

- [ ] A **micro mechanical valve** = MEMS device with a movable element that opens/blocks a microchannel.
- [ ] **Active valves** need external energy; **passive valves** rely on flow-induced pressure.
- [ ] **Normally Closed (NC)** valves require actuation to open; **Normally Open (NO)** valves require actuation to close.
- [ ] **Electrostatic valves** are bistable, fast, but limited in force.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric valves** give **proportional, linear** displacement — best for proportional control.
- [ ] **Thermopneumatic & bimetallic valves** give large strokes but slow response.
- [ ] A **reciprocating diaphragm pump** has three parts: **chamber + 2 check valves**.
- [ ] **Peristaltic pump** uses a **traveling wave** of segment actuation for smoother, non-pulsatile flow.
- [ ] **Electroosmotic pump** uses the **EDL** and produces **plug flow** (zero band-broadening).
- [ ] **Pull-in instability** in electrostatic actuators occurs at $g = 2 d_0 / 3$.
- [ ] **Smoluchowski velocity** $u_{eo} = (\varepsilon \zeta / \eta) E$.
- [ ] **Average flow rate** of reciprocating pump: $Q = f A_d \delta \eta_v$.
- [ ] **Reynolds number** in microchannels is **typically less than 100** → flow is **laminar**.
- [ ] **Hydraulic resistance** for circular channels: $R_{hyd} = 8 \mu L / (\pi r^4)$.
- [ ] **Force balance** on valve membrane: $F_{spring} + F_{pressure} = F_{actuator}$ at threshold.
- [ ] Remember to **draw labelled diagrams** — they can easily contribute 3–4 marks.
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** before derivations (laminar, ideal gas, parallel-plate, etc.).
- [ ] **Units** must accompany every numerical result to earn the **0.5-mark dimensional-consistency bonus**.
- [ ] For numerical problems, write **intermediate substitution steps** — partial marks are awarded for each correct substitution.
- [ ] Use **mermaid-style labelled flow diagrams** in your answer sheets wherever possible — KTU examiners are trained to reward visual clarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 – Actuators: Micro Mechanical Valves & Micro Pumps. Mastery of this topic accounts for approximately 15–20% of the KTU 2024 OEICT612 End-Semester Examination paper.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process of Micromachining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process of Micromachining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process of Micromachining (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Micromachining refers to a set of **fabrication technologies** used to manufacture **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** by selectively removing, depositing, or modifying materials at the **micrometer (µm) and sub-micrometer scale**. It encompasses a family of processes that combine the precision of **semiconductor integrated circuit (IC) fabrication** with specialized techniques for crafting three-dimensional (3D) mechanical and electromechanical microstructures from substrates such as **single-crystal silicon (Si)**, **polysilicon**, **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)**, **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)**, **polymers**, and **metals**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) curriculum, the *Process of Micromachining* is treated as the **heart of MEMS fabrication** — without mastering these processes, a student cannot analyze the operation of accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors, micro-pumps, or micro-mirrors.

The two principal pillars of micromachining are:
1. **Photolithography** – the *pattern-transfer engine* that defines *where* material is altered.
2. **Etching** – the *material-removal engine* that defines *how much* and *how deep* material is altered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEICT612, Module 2):**
> The KTU 2024 module explicitly focuses on **Photolithography**, **Wet & Dry Etching**, **Bulk Micromachining**, and **Surface Micromachining**. The student is expected to compare these processes, understand their underlying chemistry/physics, and apply them in device-level contexts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **master jeweler** tasked with carving an intricate miniature sculpture (a tiny eagle, say) out of a polished slab of marble. You cannot use a hammer and chisel — the eagle's wing is only a few hair-widths wide. Instead, you do the following:

1. **Cover the marble slab with a thin protective wax layer** (this is the **photoresist**).
2. **Press a stencil (mask) with the eagle's outline onto the wax** (this is **UV exposure**).
3. **Wash away the unhardened wax from regions you want to cut** (this is **development**).
4. **Apply acid that eats only the exposed marble, leaving the wax-protected eagle in 3D relief** (this is **etching**).
5. **Finally, melt away the wax to release the polished eagle** (this is **sacrificial layer release** in surface micromachining).

That is micromachining in plain English — *photonic patterning plus selective material removal (or deposition) at micrometer precision*. The **standard feature size** routinely achievable in academic labs is **1–100 µm**, while advanced industrial foundries push this to **sub-100 nm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The benchmark metric for any micromachining process is the **Aspect Ratio** — defined as *etch depth / lateral feature width*. A **High Aspect Ratio (HAR)** process delivers structures taller than they are wide, which is critical for inertial sensors.

### 1.3 Important Physical Constants

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | Typical i-line UV lithography wavelength | **365 nm** | nm |
| $E_g$ | Silicon bandgap energy | **1.12 eV** | eV |
| $\rho_{Si}$ | Density of single-crystal silicon | **2.33 g/cm³** | g/cm³ |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro's number | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** | mol⁻¹ |
| $k_B T$ | Thermal voltage at 300 K | **0.02585 eV** | eV |
| $\epsilon_r$ | Si relative permittivity | **11.7** | – |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Stages of the Process of Micromachining

The complete process chain is best understood as four sequential stages. KTU 2024 questions frequently test these stages in their proper order.

#### Stage 1 — Substrate Preparation
- The starting wafer (usually **single-crystal silicon**, either **(100)** or **(111)** oriented) is chemically cleaned using the **RCA process** (Standard Clean 1 / SC-1 with **NH₄OH + H₂O₂ + H₂O** and Standard Clean 2 / SC-2 with **HCl + H₂O₂ + H₂O**).
- A **thermal oxide (SiO₂)** or **Low Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD) silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)** layer is grown/deposited to act as the **etch mask** because SiO₂ and Si₃N₄ etch much more slowly than silicon in most relevant etchants.

#### Stage 2 — Photolithography (Pattern Transfer)
The photolithographic sub-process has **seven canonical steps**:

1. **Wafer Dehydration Bake** (~150–200 °C, removes adsorbed moisture).
2. **Priming / Vapor HMDS Treatment** (Hexamethyldisilazane promotes photoresist adhesion).
3. **Photoresist Spin-Coating** — drops of liquid photoresist (e.g., **AZ5214E** for positive, **SU-8** for negative thick) are spun at **2000–6000 rpm** to produce a uniform film of thickness:
$$t \;\approx\; \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta}}{\omega^{\gamma}}$$
   where $C$ is the resist solids concentration, $\omega$ is the angular spin speed, and $K, \beta, \gamma$ are empirically fitted constants.
4. **Soft Bake** (~90–110 °C on a hot plate) to drive off residual solvent.
5. **UV Exposure** through a **photomask** (Chrome-on-glass). Modern steppers use **λ = 193 nm (ArF excimer laser)** for sub-90 nm nodes; academic KTU labs use the **i-line (λ = 365 nm)**.
6. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — critical for chemically amplified resists.
7. **Development** in a developer solution (e.g., **TMAH 0.26 %** for positive resists) that dissolves only the exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution Limit (Rayleigh Criterion):**
> The minimum printable linewidth is governed by **Abbe's diffraction limit**:
$$R \;=\; \frac{k_1 \, \lambda}{NA}$$
> where $k_1 \approx 0.4$ (process factor) and $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the projection lens. For $\lambda = 365$ nm and $NA = 0.65$, $R \approx 0.225$ µm.

#### Stage 3 — Etching (Material Removal or Deposition)
- Material is selectively removed from the regions **uncovered by the photoresist** (and through the etch mask).
- The photoresist is then **stripped** (using acetone, **NMP**, or **O₂ plasma ashing**).
- The process is repeated in a **layer-by-layer** fashion for surface micromachining.

#### Stage 4 — Inspection & Packaging
- The wafer is inspected using **optical microscopy**, **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy)**, **stylus profilometry**, and **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy)**.
- Functional MEMS die is then **diced**, **packaged** (often in **LCC — Leadless Chip Carriers** or **CERDIP**), and wire-bonded.

### 2.2 Etching: The Heart of Micromachining

Etching is broadly classified into:

**A) Wet Etching** (chemical, liquid-phase)
- **Isotropic** — etches equally in all directions; produces severe undercut.
- **Anisotropic** — etches preferentially along certain crystallographic directions; produces well-defined 3D shapes like V-grooves, pyramids, and diaphragms.

**B) Dry Etching** (physical/chemical, plasma-phase)
- **Plasma Etching (PE)**, **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)**, **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE — Bosch process)**, **Ion Beam Etching (IBE)**.

### 2.3 Critical Etching Performance Metrics (KTU Formula Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Ideal Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Etch Rate** | $R_e$ | $R_e = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ (nm/min or µm/min) | Maximize | Determines wafer throughput. |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | $S$ | $S = \dfrac{R_e(\text{film})}{R_e(\text{mask})}$ | $\to \infty$ | Ensures the etch mask survives. |
| 3 | **Anisotropy Factor** | $A_f$ | $A_f = 1 - \dfrac{R_\text{lat}}{R_\text{vert}}$ | $\to 1$ | Sidewall verticality. |
| 4 | **Undercut** | $u$ | $u = R_\text{lat} \cdot t$ (lateral etch) | $\to 0$ | Mask misalignment tolerance. |
| 5 | **Aspect Ratio** | $AR$ | $AR = \dfrac{d}{w}$ (depth / width) | Maximize | Critical for inertial sensors. |
| 6 | **Uniformity** | $U$ | $U = \pm \dfrac{R_{e,\max} - R_{e,\min}}{R_{e,\max} + R_{e,\min}} \times 100\%$ | $\to 0\,\%$ | Across-wafer consistency. |
| 7 | **Mask Window Opening** | $w_0$ | – | – | Original lithographic dimension. |
| 8 | **Effective Cavity Width** | $w$ | $w = w_0 - 2u$ | – | Final dimension after isotropic etch. |

### 2.4 Anisotropic Wet Etching of Silicon — Detailed Physics

When single-crystal silicon is etched in alkaline solutions like **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)**, **TMAH**, or **EDP (Ethylenediamine Pyrocatechol)**, the etch rate depends strongly on the **crystallographic orientation** because the **number of available back-bonds** per surface atom differs:

- **(100) plane** — 2 back-bonds per atom → **fast etching**.
- **(110) plane** — 1 back-bond per atom → even faster (used to create suspended structures).
- **(111) plane** — 3 back-bonds per atom → **slow etching** (acts as a natural etch stop).

The relative etch rates follow:
$$R_{100} \;>\; R_{110} \;>\; R_{111}$$
with a typical ratio:
$$\frac{R_{100}}{R_{111}} \;\approx\; 100 \;\;\text{to}\;\; 400 \quad (\text{KOH at 80 °C})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Consequence of Anisotropy:** A square window aligned with the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions on a (100) wafer produces a **pyramidal pit** with sidewalls bounded by four (111) planes meeting at an angle of:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{2}\right) \;\approx\; 54.74^{\circ}$$
> measured from the wafer surface. The pit terminates when the four (111) planes intersect.

### 2.5 DRIE — Deep Reactive Ion Etching (Bosch Process)

To achieve **HAR structures (>20:1)** in silicon, the **Bosch process** alternates two steps cyclically inside an **Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP)** chamber:

1. **Etch Step (~5–15 s):** SF₆ plasma generates free **F• radicals** that spontaneously attack silicon:
$$\text{Si}(s) \;+\; 4\,\text{F}^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SiF}_4(g) \;\uparrow$$
2. **Passivation Step (~5–12 s):** C₄F₈ gas deposits a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon polymer** ($\text{–(CF}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$) on all exposed surfaces, protecting the sidewalls.

The cycle is repeated **hundreds of times**, producing a characteristic **scalloping** of ~50–500 nm on the sidewalls, but achieving **near-vertical (90°)** sidewalls with $AR > 50:1$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Required Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Airbag Accelerometer (ADXL50)** | Bulk + Surface Micromachining | Needs a proof mass (bulk) and capacitive fingers (surface). |
| **Inkjet Printhead (HP, Canon)** | Bulk Si anisotropic etching | 50 µm deep nozzle pits. |
| **Micro-gyroscope (L3G4200D)** | DRIE (Bosch) | HAR comb-drive structures. |
| **Micro-mirror Array (DLP)** | Surface micromachining | Al metallized plates on hinge. |
| **Pressure Sensor (Honeywell)** | Anisotropic KOH + Boron etch stop | Thin Si diaphragm (10–50 µm). |
| **Bio-MEMS (DNA chips)** | SU-8 + soft lithography | High-aspect polymer wells. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cavity Width Evolution Under Isotropic Etching

**Problem:** A square window of side $w_0$ is opened in an etch mask on a silicon wafer. The wafer is immersed in an **isotropic wet etchant (e.g., HNA — Hydrofluoric-Nitric-Acetic acid)** for a time $t$. The vertical etch rate is $R_v$ and the lateral etch rate is $R_l = R_v$ (by definition of *isotropic*). Derive the relationship between the final cavity width $w(t)$ and the etch depth $h(t)$.

**Step 1 — Define the etch rates.**
Under isotropic conditions, the lateral and vertical etch rates are *identical*:
$$R_v = R_l \quad \text{(definition of isotropic etching)}$$

**Step 2 — Express depth as a function of time.**
The etch depth is the integral of vertical rate over time:
$$h(t) \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} R_v(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Assuming a constant etch rate (a reasonable KTU-level approximation for short etch times):
$$h(t) \;=\; R_v \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Express the lateral undercut.**
Because lateral attack proceeds in *both* directions (left and right of the original mask window), the total undercut is:
$$u(t) \;=\; R_l \cdot t \quad \text{(single side)}$$
$$\text{Total lateral enlargement} \;=\; 2u(t) \;=\; 2\,R_l \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Final cavity width.**
$$w(t) \;=\; w_0 \;-\; 2\,u(t) \;=\; w_0 \;-\; 2\,R_l \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $R_l = R_v$ and $t = h/R_v$.**
$$w(t) \;=\; w_0 \;-\; 2\,R_l \cdot \frac{h}{R_v} \;=\; w_0 \;-\; 2\,h$$

**Step 6 — Final form (boxed result).**
$$\boxed{\,w(h) \;=\; w_0 \;-\; 2h\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The cavity *shrinks* as the etch goes deeper — this is the opposite of intuition! In isotropic etching, the **corner undercut is the worst**, so mask windows must be **sized UP** by $2h$ in advance to achieve the desired final dimension.

### 3.2 Derivation: Anisotropy Factor from Etch Rates

**Problem:** Given lateral etch rate $R_l$ and vertical etch rate $R_v$, derive the expression for the **Anisotropy Factor** $A_f$ such that $A_f = 1$ represents perfect anisotropy.

**Step 1 — Define the limiting cases.**
- *Isotropic etching:* $R_l = R_v$ → we want $A_f = 0$.
- *Perfectly anisotropic (vertical sidewalls):* $R_l = 0$ → we want $A_f = 1$.

**Step 2 — Normalize the lateral rate.**
$$A_f \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{R_l}{R_v}$$

**Step 3 — Verification of limits.**
- If $R_l = R_v$ (isotropic): $A_f = 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓
- If $R_l = 0$ (anisotropic): $A_f = 1 - 0 = 1$ ✓

**Step 4 — Final form.**
$$\boxed{\,A_f \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{R_l}{R_v} \;\in\; [0, 1]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real RIE processes have $A_f \approx 0.85$–$0.95$, while DRIE (Bosch) achieves $A_f > 0.99$. Wet KOH etching of (100) silicon gives $A_f \approx 0.97$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Selectivity and Minimum Mask Thickness

**Problem:** A silicon wafer is etched at a vertical rate $R_{Si}$ in KOH through a silicon-dioxide mask of thickness $t_{mask}$. The selectivity is defined as $S = R_{Si} / R_{SiO_2}$. Derive the relationship between required mask thickness and silicon etch depth.

**Step 1 — Express mask consumption rate.**
The mask is consumed at rate $R_{SiO_2}$:
$$R_{SiO_2} \;=\; \frac{R_{Si}}{S}$$

**Step 2 — Time required to etch to depth $h$.**
The vertical silicon etch time is:
$$t_\text{etch} \;=\; \frac{h}{R_{Si}}$$

**Step 3 — Mask thickness consumed in that time.**
$$\Delta t_{mask} \;=\; R_{SiO_2} \cdot t_\text{etch} \;=\; \frac{R_{Si}}{S} \cdot \frac{h}{R_{Si}} \;=\; \frac{h}{S}$$

**Step 4 — Condition for mask survival.**
For the mask not to be breached, the initial mask thickness must satisfy:
$$t_{mask} \;\geq\; \frac{h}{S} \;+\; \text{safety margin}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**
Suppose $R_{Si} = 1\,\mu\text{m/min}$ in 30 % KOH at 80 °C, and $S = R_{Si}/R_{SiO_2} = 200$. For a target depth $h = 200\,\mu\text{m}$:
$$t_{mask} \;\geq\; \frac{200}{200} \;=\; 1\,\mu\text{m}$$

A standard **1 µm thermal SiO₂** is thus sufficient (with negligible safety margin — engineers typically use **500 nm Si₃N₄** instead, since $S$ vs. Si is $>1000$).

### 3.4 Derivation: Etch Time to Fabricate a Square Pyramid

**Problem:** A square window of side $a$ is aligned to the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction on a (100) silicon wafer. Etching in KOH proceeds until the (111) sidewalls meet. Find the **etch depth $h_p$** and **etch time $t_p$**.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the pyramid.**
For a square pit bounded by four (111) planes inclined at $\theta = 54.74°$ to the (100) surface, the relationship between window half-width $a/2$ and etch depth $h_p$ is:
$$\tan\theta \;=\; \frac{a/2}{h_p} \;\Longrightarrow\; h_p \;=\; \frac{a/2}{\tan\theta} \;=\; \frac{a}{2\tan\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tan 54.74° = \sqrt{2}$.**
$$h_p \;=\; \frac{a}{2\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{a}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical result.**
$$\boxed{\,h_p \;=\; \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4} \;\approx\; 0.354\,a\,}$$

**Step 4 — Time required.**
$$t_p \;=\; \frac{h_p}{R_{100}} \;=\; \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4\,R_{100}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** To completely **stop** the etch at the pyramid apex (to avoid over-etching the corners), the etch time must not exceed $t_p$ by more than 10–20 %; otherwise the (111) planes start being attacked at their intersections.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bulk vs. Surface Micromachining

| Parameter | **Bulk Micromachining** | **Surface Micromachining** |
|---|---|---|
| **Substrate** | Etched *into* the bulk wafer | Built *on top* of the wafer |
| **Structural Material** | The wafer itself (Si) | Deposited polysilicon / SiO₂ / SiN |
| **Sacrificial Layer** | Not typically used | **Mandatory** (e.g., PSG, SiO₂) |
| **Etch Type** | Wet (KOH, TMAH, EDP) or Dry (DRIE) | Almost exclusively **HF release etch** |
| **Thickness Range** | 10 – 500 µm | 1 – 25 µm |
| **Lateral Dimensions** | Limited by wafer size (mm) | Limited by lithography (µm) |
| **Aspect Ratio** | Moderate (1:1 to 10:1) | Low (1:1 to 5:1) |
| **Process Complexity** | Lower (fewer steps) | Higher (multilayer, planarization) |
| **Stiction Risk** | Low | **High** (post-release capillary) |
| **Typical Devices** | Pressure sensors, inkjet nozzles | Accelerometers, gyroscopes |
| **Released Parts Movement** | Up/Down (out-of-plane) | In-plane (lateral) — but can be designed both |
| **Dimensional Control** | Set by etch time (variable) | Set by film thickness (precise) |
| **Cost** | Lower per wafer | Higher (CVD/LPCVD/PECVD overhead) |
| **IC Compatibility** | Excellent | Excellent (monolithic) |

### 3.6 LIGA Process — A High-Yield Extension

**LIGA** (German acronym for **Li**thographie, **G**alvanof ormung, **A**bformung → *Lithography, Electroplating, Molding*) is a non-silicon micromachining route used to fabricate **metal and polymer microstructures with sub-µm precision and mm-scale height**.

The four steps are:
1. **X-ray Deep Lithography** — A thick (100 µm – 1 mm) PMMA layer is exposed to **synchrotron X-rays** through a high-contrast gold mask.
2. **Development** — Unexposed PMMA is dissolved, leaving a deep negative mold.
3. **Electroplating** — The mold is filled with metal (Ni, Cu, Au) via **electrodeposition**.
4. **Molding (or further processing)** — The metal insert can be used as a master for hot embossing or injection molding of plastics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Takeaway:** LIGA achieves aspect ratios exceeding **100:1** — far beyond what DRIE can deliver — but the cost and need for a **synchrotron** limit it to research and specialty industrial production.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Process Flow of Micromachining (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Substrate Selection] --> A1[Si Wafer, 100 or 111 oriented]
    A1 --> A2[RCA Cleaning SC-1 and SC-2]
    A2 --> A3[Thermal Oxidation or LPCVD Si3N4 Deposition]
    A3 --> B0[Stage 1: Substrate Preparation Complete]
    B0 --> C1[Step 1: HMDS Priming for Adhesion]
    C1 --> C2[Step 2: Photoresist Spin Coating 3000 rpm]
    C2 --> C3[Step 3: Soft Bake 95 C, 90 sec]
    C3 --> C4[Step 4: UV Exposure through Photomask, i-line 365 nm]
    C4 --> C5[Step 5: Post Exposure Bake PEB]
    C5 --> C6[Step 6: Development in TMAH 0.26 percent]
    C6 --> C7[Step 7: Hard Bake 120 C]
    C7 --> D0[Stage 2: Photolithography Complete]
    D0 --> E1{Choose Etch Type}
    E1 -->|Wet Isotropic| F1[HNA Solution, Etch Depth h, Lateral Undercut u = h]
    E1 -->|Wet Anisotropic| F2[KOH or TMAH, 111 Plane Acts as Etch Stop]
    E1 -->|Dry Plasma| F3[RIE using CF4 or SF6]
    E1 -->|Deep RIE Bosch| F4[Alternating SF6 Etch and C4F8 Passivation]
    F1 --> G1[Photoresist Strip Acetone or O2 Plasma]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H0{Post Process Path}
    H0 -->|Bulk Process| I1[Released Si Microstructure in Wafer]
    H0 -->|Surface Process| I2[Additional Polysilicon Deposition, Sacrificial Layer, Final HF Release]
    I1 --> J1[Stage 3: Etching Complete]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Stage 4: Dicing, Wire Bonding, Packaging in LCC or CERDIP]
    K1 --> L1[Final MEMS Device e.g. Accelerometer, Gyroscope, Pressure Sensor]
```

### 4.2 Etching Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Etching in Micromachining] --> P1[Wet Chemical Etching]
    P0 --> P2[Dry Plasma Etching]
    P1 --> P1A[Isotropic e.g. HNA, HF H2O2]
    P1 --> P1B[Anisotropic e.g. KOH, TMAH, EDP]
    P1A --> P1A1[Large Undercut, No Direction Preference]
    P1B --> P1B1[100 Plane Etches Fast, 111 Plane Acts as Etch Stop]
    P2 --> P2A[Physical Sputtering e.g. Ion Beam Milling]
    P2 --> P2B[Chemical Reactive e.g. Plasma Etching PE]
    P2 --> P2C[Physico Chemical e.g. RIE, DRIE Bosch]
    P2A --> P2A1[High Directionality, Low Selectivity, Radiation Damage]
    P2B --> P2B1[Radical Attack, Mostly Isotropic]
    P2C --> P2C1[High Anisotropy, High Selectivity, HAR up to 50:1]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Comparison: Bulk vs. Surface Micromachining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph B[Bulk Micromachining]
        BW[Si Wafer Substrate] --> BE[KOH Anisotropic Etch Forms Pit]
        BE --> BR[Released Proof Mass or Diaphragm in Bulk Si]
    end
    subgraph S[Surface Micromachining]
        SW[Si Substrate] --> SL1[Sacrificial Layer SiO2 or PSG]
        SL1 --> SP[Structural Polysilicon Deposited by LPCVD]
        SP --> SR[HF Release Etch Sacrificial Layer Removed]
        SR --> SF[Free Standing MEMS Plate or Comb Drive]
    end
```

### 4.4 Anisotropic Etching — Crystallographic Plane Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Crystallographic Plane] --> Q1[100 Plane, 2 Back Bonds]
    Q0 --> Q2[110 Plane, 1 Back Bond]
    Q0 --> Q3[111 Plane, 3 Back Bonds]
    Q1 --> Q1A[Fastest Etch Rate in KOH]
    Q2 --> Q2A[Used for Suspended Cantilever Beams]
    Q3 --> Q3A[Slowest Etch Rate, Acts as Natural Etch Stop]
    Q1A --> Q4[Forms V Grooves and Pyramidal Pits at 54.74 degrees]
    Q3A --> Q5[Enables Precise 3D Microstructures]
```

### 4.5 Photolithography Process — Step-by-Step Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Cleaned Wafer] --> R1[Dehydration Bake, 200 C, 5 min]
    R1 --> R2[HMDS Vapor Priming, 30 sec]
    R2 --> R3[Spin Coat Photoresist, AZ5214E, 4000 rpm, 30 sec]
    R3 --> R4[Soft Bake on Hot Plate, 95 C, 90 sec]
    R4 --> R5[Mask Alignment using Karl Suss MA6 Aligner]
    R5 --> R6[UV Exposure, i line 365 nm, Dose 80 mJ per cm squared]
    R6 --> R7[Post Exposure Bake, 110 C, 60 sec]
    R7 --> R8[Development in TMAH 0.26 percent, 60 sec]
    R8 --> R9[Hard Bake, 120 C, 120 sec]
    R9 --> R10[Pattern Transferred, Ready for Etch]
```

### 4.6 Bosch DRIE — Cyclic Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Single Bosch Cycle, 10 to 25 seconds] --> S1[Phase 1, Etch, 5 to 15 sec]
    S0 --> S2[Phase 2, Passivation, 5 to 12 sec]
    S1 --> S1A[SF6 Plasma Generates F Radicals, Si + 4F gives SiF4 Gas]
    S2 --> S2A[C4F8 Plasma Deposits Fluorocarbon Polymer on All Surfaces]
    S1A --> S3[Net Effect of One Cycle, ~ 0.5 to 2 micrometer Si Removed Vertically]
    S2A --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Repeat Cycle N times for Target Depth]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define photolithography. List any four key steps involved in the photolithographic process with their typical process conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Photolithography is a pattern-transfer technique that uses UV light to project geometric shapes from a photomask onto a light-sensitive photoresist film coated on a wafer.

Four key steps:
1. **Photoresist spin-coating** at 2000–6000 rpm to obtain a uniform film of 0.5–2 µm thickness.
2. **Soft bake** at 90–110 °C to remove solvent and improve adhesion.
3. **UV exposure** through the photomask using i-line ($\lambda = 365$ nm) source, dose ≈ 50–100 mJ/cm².
4. **Development** in TMAH 0.26 % solution to dissolve the exposed (positive) resist regions.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between wet and dry etching techniques used in MEMS fabrication. Mention at least three points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Wet Etching | Dry Etching |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Liquid-phase chemical reaction (e.g., KOH, HNA). | Plasma or ion-assisted reaction in vacuum. |
| 2 | Isotropic (HNA) or anisotropic (KOH). | Highly anisotropic (RIE, DRIE). |
| 3 | Low equipment cost, no vacuum. | High capital cost, vacuum chamber. |
| 4 | High selectivity but poor resolution due to undercut. | Lower selectivity but sub-µm resolution. |
| 5 | Stiction risk during drying. | No stiction; suitable for HAR structures. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering micromachining questions, students **most commonly lose marks** because they:
> 1. **Confuse bulk and surface micromachining** — remember, bulk = *into the wafer*, surface = *on top of the wafer with a sacrificial layer*.
> 2. **Forget to state the process conditions** (KOH concentration, temperature, etch time) — KTU 2024 expects these.
> 3. **Skip the mask undercut calculation** in isotropic etching derivations — the factor of **2h** is critical.
> 4. **Use $|x|$ notation in tables** (forbidden in KTU typed sheets) — use $\vert x \vert$ in handwriting and write out *absolute value of x*.
> 5. **Omit etch stop mechanisms** (boron heavy doping, electrochemical, 111-plane) when discussing bulk micromachining.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Photolithography + Wet Etching Focus*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **seven steps of the photolithography process** with neat diagrams. Also state the **Abbe diffraction limit** and compute the **minimum feature size** for an i-line ($\lambda = 365$ nm) stepper with $NA = 0.65$ and $k_1 = 0.4$.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Wafer Cleaning & Dehydration Bake** — Removes organic/metallic contaminants; bake at 200 °C for 5 min on a hot plate to remove adsorbed water vapor.
2. **Priming with HMDS** — Hexamethyldisilazane is vapor-deposited to make the surface hydrophobic, ensuring good photoresist adhesion.
3. **Photoresist Coating** — Liquid photoresist (e.g., AZ5214E positive) is dispensed and the wafer is spun at 3000–5000 rpm for 30 sec. Centrifugal force spreads it to a uniform thickness of ≈ 1.4 µm.
4. **Soft Bake** — 95 °C for 90 sec on a hot plate to evaporate residual solvent and densify the film.
5. **Alignment & Exposure** — The wafer is aligned to the photomask on a contact/proximity/stepper aligner, then exposed to UV ($\lambda = 365$ nm) at a dose of ~80 mJ/cm². Photoactive compound in the resist decomposes (positive resist) to become soluble in the developer.
6. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — 110 °C for 60 sec; critical for chemically amplified resists to complete the photoacid-driven reaction.
7. **Development** — Immersion in **TMAH 0.26 %** for 60 sec, followed by DI water rinse and N₂ blow-dry. The exposed (positive) regions dissolve, leaving the patterned resist mask.

**Abbe Diffraction Limit:**
$$R \;=\; \frac{k_1 \, \lambda}{NA} \;=\; \frac{0.4 \times 365\,\text{nm}}{0.65} \;=\; \frac{146}{0.65} \;\approx\; 224.6\,\text{nm} \;\approx\; 0.225\,\mu\text{m}$$

> *Result: The minimum printable linewidth is approximately **0.225 µm**.* ✓

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A square mask window of side $w_0 = 200$ µm is opened on a (100) silicon wafer. The wafer is etched in **KOH (30 % wt, 80 °C)** with $R_{100} = 1.2$ µm/min and $R_{111} = 0.005$ µm/min. Calculate:
   (i) the **depth of the pyramidal pit** when the etch is complete,
   (ii) the **etch time required**,
   (iii) the **anisotropy factor**,
   (iv) the **selectivity** of KOH over a 500 nm SiO₂ mask (assume $R_{SiO_2} = 0.005$ µm/min, same as $R_{111}$).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Depth of pyramidal pit:**
$$h_p \;=\; \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 1.414}{4} \;=\; \frac{282.8}{4} \;=\; 70.7\,\mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Etch time:**
$$t_p \;=\; \frac{h_p}{R_{100}} \;=\; \frac{70.7}{1.2} \;\approx\; 58.9\,\text{minutes}$$

**(iii) Anisotropy factor:**
$$A_f \;=\; 1 - \frac{R_{111}}{R_{100}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{0.005}{1.2} \;=\; 1 - 0.00417 \;\approx\; 0.996$$

(extremely anisotropic — confirms KOH as a high-selectivity anisotropic etchant) ✓

**(iv) Selectivity over SiO₂ mask:**
$$S \;=\; \frac{R_{Si}}{R_{SiO_2}} \;=\; \frac{1.2}{0.005} \;=\; 240$$

The 500 nm oxide will be consumed:
$$\Delta t_{mask} \;=\; \frac{h_p}{S} \;=\; \frac{70.7}{240} \;\approx\; 0.295\,\mu\text{m} \;=\; 295\,\text{nm}$$

Since 295 nm < 500 nm, the **mask survives** the etch with a 205 nm safety margin. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Bulk vs. Surface Micromachining + Dry Etching Focus*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **bulk micromachining** and **surface micromachining** with respect to the process sequence, structural material, etch type, and at least four device/performance parameters. Draw a **labeled cross-section** of a surface-micromachined **polysilicon cantilever beam** before and after release.

**Model Solution:**

*See the Analytical Comparison Matrix in Section 3.5 above for the 13-point comparison.*

**Cross-Section of a Polysilicon Cantilever (textual schematic):**

*Before release:*
```
    [Air]                 ← ambient
    [Poly-Si (1.5 µm)]    ← structural layer
    [SiO₂ (1.0 µm)]       ← SACRIFICIAL LAYER
    [Si Substrate]        ← handle wafer
```

*After release (HF dip removes SiO₂ under the free end):*
```
    [Air]                 ← ambient
    [Poly-Si (1.5 µm)]    ← free end floats upward due to residual stress
    [Si Substrate]        ← handle wafer
         ↑
   anchor at one end remains bonded; other end is released.
```

**Key Steps of Surface Micromachining:**
1. Deposit **sacrificial SiO₂** (LPCVD or thermal) on Si substrate.
2. **Pattern** the sacrificial layer with photolithography + HF etch to define anchor points.
3. Deposit **structural polysilicon** (LPCVD at 580–620 °C).
4. **Pattern** the polysilicon to define beam, plate, or comb geometry.
5. Perform **sacrificial release etch** in **concentrated HF (49 %)** to remove the underlying SiO₂.
6. **Critical point dry (CPD)** to prevent stiction.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Bosch Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** process in detail. List the gases used, the chemistry of each step, and explain why it achieves aspect ratios exceeding 50:1. Mention one **drawback** of the process.

**Model Solution:**

The Bosch process (invented at Robert Bosch GmbH, patented 1994) is a **cyclic, time-multiplexed** dry etching process that combines plasma chemistry and sidewall passivation to achieve **vertical, high-aspect-ratio** structures in silicon.

**Step 1 — Etching Phase (~5–15 s):**
- **Gas:** SF₆ (sulfur hexafluoride) is introduced into the chamber.
- **Chemistry:** The plasma dissociates SF₆ into free **fluorine radicals (F•)**:
$$\text{SF}_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{plasma}}\; \text{SF}_x \;+\; \text{F}^{\bullet}$$
- These radicals spontaneously react with exposed silicon at the bottom of the trench:
$$\text{Si} \;+\; 4\,\text{F}^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SiF}_4(g) \;\uparrow$$
- Etch depth per cycle: ~0.5 – 2 µm.

**Step 2 — Passivation Phase (~5–12 s):**
- **Gas:** C₄F₈ (octafluorocyclopentene) is switched in.
- **Chemistry:** The plasma polymerizes a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon film** ($\text{–(CF}_2\text{)}_n\text{–}$) on *all* exposed surfaces (sidewalls + bottom):
$$\text{C}_4\text{F}_8 \;\xrightarrow{\text{plasma}}\; (\text{CF}_2)_n \text{ polymer}$$

**Step 3 — Why it works:**
- During the *next* etch cycle, ion bombardment (with the assistance of a **bias voltage**) preferentially removes the polymer from the **horizontal trench bottom** (where ions hit vertically), but the **vertical sidewalls** retain their protective polymer coating.
- This is **anisotropic protection** — only the bottom is etched.

**Step 4 — Why aspect ratio > 50:1 is possible:**
- The polymer layer continually prevents **lateral attack**, so the trench grows purely downward.
- Etch depth accumulates linearly with cycle count N, while the lateral dimension stays constant — yielding extremely high AR.

**One drawback:**
- **Scalloping** — the sidewalls have a periodic ripple of ~50–500 nm (set by cycle time) due to alternating etch/passivation. This roughens the sidewall and degrades optical and mechanical performance. Longer cycles reduce scallop frequency but increase its amplitude.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Process of Micromachining (KTU 2024):**

- ☐ **Photolithography** = pattern transfer using UV light + photoresist; 7 canonical steps; resolution limited by $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$.
- ☐ **Positive resist** dissolves in *exposed* regions; **negative resist** dissolves in *unexposed* regions.
- ☐ **Wet etching** is cheap, highly selective, but isotropic unless KOH/TMAH/EDP on single-crystal Si.
- ☐ **KOH on (100) Si** produces pyramidal pits bounded by (111) planes at **54.74°**; depth = $a\sqrt{2}/4$.
- ☐ **DRIE (Bosch)** alternates SF₆ etch and C₄F₸ passivation — AR > 50:1 with vertical sidewalls.
- ☐ **Bulk micromachining** = etch *into* the wafer; gives thick (10–500 µm), robust structures like diaphragms and proof masses.
- ☐ **Surface micromachining** = build *on top* of the wafer using a **sacrificial layer**; thickness limited to 1–25 µm.
- ☐ **Sacrificial release** uses **HF** to dissolve SiO₂, freeing polysilicon structures; followed by **Critical Point Drying** to avoid stiction.
- ☐ **Selectivity $S = R_\text{film} / R_\text{mask}$** — the higher, the better; Si₃N₄ vs. Si in KOH gives $S > 1000$.
- ☐ **Anisotropy factor $A_f = 1 - R_l / R_v$** — ranges from 0 (isotropic) to 1 (perfect anisotropy).
- ☐ **LIGA** = L ithography + G alvanoformung + A bformung; uses **synchrotron X-rays** to make tall (mm) metal/polymer structures.
- ☐ **Lateral undercut** in isotropic etching = $u = R_l \cdot t = h$ (assuming isotropy), so cavity shrinks as $w(t) = w_0 - 2h$.
- ☐ **Aspect Ratio AR = depth / width** — critical for inertial sensors; DRIE pushes it to > 50:1.
- ☐ **Etch stop mechanisms in bulk micromachining:** (i) (111) plane natural stop, (ii) **Boron heavy doping** ($> 5 \times 10^{19}$/cm³), (iii) **Electrochemical** stop using reverse-biased p-n junction.
- ☐ **Wet vs. Dry** trade-off: wet = high selectivity & low cost; dry = high resolution & verticality.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Process of Micromachining**
*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) examination pattern for OEICT612.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique. Silicon Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique. Silicon Growth ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique — Formal Definition **Additive pattern transfer** is a class of microfabrication processes in which the desired structural or functional material is **selectively deposited, plated, printed, or grown** onto a substrate through a predefined template (mask, mold, or stencil), rather than being carved out of a bulk material (as in subtractive etching). The "pattern" is essentially *built up* on the wafer surface, layer by layer, exactly where it is needed. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this falls under the broader umbrella of **thin-film deposition and selective material addition** techniques, and includes: - **Lift-off (or Lift-off lithography)** - **Shadow / Stencil masking** - **Screen and micro-contact printing** - **Electroplating (through a mold or seed layer)** - **Selective epitaxial growth (SEG)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** *Additive technique is a non-subtractive pattern transfer method in which the pattern is formed by selective addition of material through a temporary or sacrificial template, as opposed to the removal of material in etching-based (subtractive) processes.* ### 1.2 Silicon Growth — Formal Definition **Silicon growth** refers to the controlled crystallization of monocrystalline, polycrystalline, or amorphous silicon — either as a **bulk ingot** (from which wafers are later sliced) or as a **thin crystalline film on a substrate** (epitaxy). It is the foundational upstream process that ultimately determines the electronic, mechanical, and piezo-resistive quality of every MEMS and CMOS device. The two principal families are: - **Bulk crystal growth** — Czochralski (CZ) and Float Zone (FZ). - **Epitaxial / thin-film growth** — VPE, MBE, LPE, CVD-based epi. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a Cake vs Carving a Statue" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for Additive Pattern Transfer:** > Think of a *sandwich cake*. You do not carve the layers out of a single brick — you **stack and deposit** the filling (the pattern material) between bread slices (the mask/substrate) and only later trim the excess. Similarly, additive processes *add* the active material exactly where the mask allows it. The famous *lift-off* process is like icing a cake through a stencil: spread the icing, then peel off the stencil — the design stays behind on the substrate. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for Silicon Growth:** > Growing a silicon ingot is like pulling a single, perfect icicle out of a flask of supercooled water — but in reverse. You dip a tiny "seed" crystal into molten silicon, then slowly pull it up while rotating. New atoms attach themselves to the seed in the *exact same atomic arrangement*, so the seed grows into a long, single-crystal cylinder. **One seed, one giant crystal.** The same principle, scaled down, is how we *grow thin silicon films* on top of wafers for advanced MEMS. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Typical Range | |---|---|---| | Silicon atomic density (solid) | $N_{Si}$ | $5.0 \times 10^{22}\ \text{atoms/cm}^3$ | | Silicon melting point | $T_{m,Si}$ | **1414 °C** | | Lattice constant (Si) | $a_{Si}$ | **5.431 Å (0.5431 nm)** | | Thermal conductivity of Si | $k_{Si}$ | 148 W/m·K | | Resistivity (intrinsic Si) | $\rho_i$ | 2.3 × 10⁵ Ω·cm at 300 K | | Pulling speed (CZ) | $v$ | 0.5 – 5 mm/min | | Rotation rates (CZ) | $\omega_{crystal}, \omega_{crucible}$ | 5 – 30 rpm | ## 2.
