Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Pattern Transfer — Additive Routes in Detail #### 2.1.1 Lift-off Process (Most Frequently Asked in KTU) A four-step "deposit–pattern–deposit–dissolve" sequence: 1. **Substrate preparation** — a clean wafer is oxidized or coated with a primer. 2. **Sacrificial layer patterning** — a photoresist (or polymethyl methacrylate, PMMA) is spun and lithographically patterned. A **re-entrant (undercut) or vertical sidewall profile** is engineered in the resist. 3. **Material deposition** — the functional material (metal, oxide, polysilicon) is deposited uniformly over the entire wafer, including *over the resist and into the openings*. 4. **Lift-off** — the wafer is immersed in a solvent (e.g., acetone for resist, or NMP for PMMA). The underlying resist dissolves, and the *unwanted* material on top of the resist floats away. Material that was deposited directly on the substrate in the openings remains as the final pattern. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a re-entrant (overhang) profile matters:** A vertical or positive-slope resist sidewall causes the deposited film to be continuous over the resist and the opening, so the solvent cannot reach the resist. An *undercut* creates a discontinuity, exposing the resist to the solvent. This is the most common reason students lose marks in KTU viva — they forget to mention the **re-entrant profile**. #### 2.1.2 Shadow / Stencil Masking A **physical stencil** (usually a silicon, metallic, or polymer membrane with pre-etched apertures) is mechanically clamped or bonded in close proximity to the wafer. Material is evaporated/sputtered *through the apertures*. No resist is used. | Pros | Cons | |---|---| | No resist contamination | Resolution limited to ~5–20 µm | | Reusable | Difficult alignment | | Compatible with high-temp deposition | Stencil fragility | #### 2.1.3 Screen / Micro-contact Printing (µCP) An **elastomeric stamp** (PDMS) inked with a self-assembled monolayer (e.g., octadecyltrichlorosilane, OTS) is pressed onto the substrate. The ink transfers the pattern chemically — it modifies the local surface energy / chemistry to either attract or repel subsequent material. #### 2.1.4 Electroplating (Additive Mode) A **seed layer** (sputtered Cr/Au or Ti/Au) is blanket-deposited, then a thick photoresist (e.g., SU-8 or AZ-4620) is patterned to act as a *mold*. The wafer is immersed in an electrolyte; current drives metal ions (Cu, Ni, Au) to nucleate only inside the mold openings. After plating, the resist is stripped. This is the workhorse for **LIGA-like and high-aspect-ratio MEMS** structures. --- ### 2.2 Silicon Growth — Bulk and Epitaxial Methods #### 2.2.1 Czochralski (CZ) Growth — Operational Logic 1. **Charge melting** — high-purity polycrystalline silicon (polysilicon chunks, electronic-grade, > 99.9999999% pure = **9N**) is melted in a **quartz crucible** at 1414 °C under argon ambient. 2. **Dopant addition** — measured amounts of *p*-type (Boron) or *n*-type (Phosphorus, Arsenic, Antimony) dopants are added to control resistivity. 3. **Seeding** — a precisely oriented *seed crystal* (typically `<100>` or `<111>`) is dipped into the melt. 4. **Pulling and rotating** — the seed is slowly pulled upward (rate $v$) and rotated (rate $\omega_{xtal}$). The crucible rotates counter to the seed ($\omega_{crucible}$) to enforce thermal symmetry. 5. **Necking** — a thin neck is pulled first to dislocate any defects propagated from the seed. 6. **Shouldering, body, and tailing** — the diameter is allowed to grow to the target, the cylindrical body is pulled, and the ingot tapers to a tail to minimize thermal shock. 7. **Cooling** — the ingot is cooled over 12–24 hours before slicing into wafers. #### 2.2.2 Float Zone (FZ) Growth A **poly-Si rod** is held vertically and a small molten zone (created by RF induction coils) is passed from one end to the other. The molten zone *floats* (held by surface tension) and sweeps impurities with it via the **segregation effect**. The result is a much **purer, lower-oxygen crystal** than CZ. FZ is used for power-device wafers and high-resistivity substrates. #### 2.2.3 Epitaxial Growth A thin, single-crystal Si film is grown on a single-crystal Si substrate (or sometimes on a sapphire/insulator — SOS, SOI). The substrate acts as the seed. Common methods: - **VPE (Vapor Phase Epitaxy) / CVD Epi** — SiH₄ or SiH₂Cl₂ (DCS) + H₂ at 1100–1250 °C. $\text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2$. - **MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy)** — ultra-high vacuum (~10⁻¹⁰ Torr), elemental Si evaporated from effusion cells. Atomic-layer precision, very low growth rate (~1 µm/hr). - **LPE (Liquid Phase Epitaxy)** — Si is dissolved in a metal solvent (e.g., Sn, In) and the substrate is dipped; supersaturation causes film growth. - **SEG (Selective Epitaxial Growth)** — epi grows only on exposed Si (through a dielectric mask). --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_s = k_0 \, C_\ell$ | Equilibrium segregation (interface solid vs. liquid dopant concentration) | at·cm⁻³ | | 2 | $k_0 = C_s / C_\ell$ | Equilibrium segregation coefficient (B ≈ 0.8, P ≈ 0.35, Sb ≈ 0.023, As ≈ 0.3) | dimensionless | | 3 | $C_s(x) = k_0 \, C_0 \, (1 - x/L)^{k_0 - 1}$ | **Normal-freeze (Pfann) equation** — dopant vs. fraction solidified | at·cm⁻³ | | 4 | $v = \dfrac{dL}{dt}$ | Crystal pull velocity in CZ | mm/min | | 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho_{Si} \, A_{xs} \, v$ | Mass crystallization rate (ρ_Si = 2.33 g/cm³) | g/s | | 6 | $r_{epi} = \dfrac{F_{SiH_4}}{N_{Si}}$ | Epitaxial growth rate from silane flux | µm/min | | 7 | $T_{m,Si} = 1414\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Silicon melting point | °C | | 8 | $a_{Si} = 0.5431$ nm | Lattice constant of Si | nm | | 9 | $N_{Si} = 8/a_{Si}^3$ | Atomic density of Si = $5.0\times 10^{22}$ at·cm⁻³ | at·cm⁻³ | | 10 | $\Delta\rho = \rho_{epi} - \rho_{sub}$ | Resistivity step from epi layer | Ω·cm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ when writing absolute-value or segregation-coefficient bars in your answer sheet. The Pfann equation is the single most tested formula in this module — memorize it, its assumptions (complete mixing in liquid, no diffusion in solid), and its shape (rising curve for $k_0 < 1$). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — The Pfann Normal-Freeze Equation This derivation explains why a Czochralski-grown ingot has a **non-uniform axial dopant concentration**, and is the *single most important* equation students should be able to derive from first principles for the KTU 14-mark question. **Starting assumptions (Pfann's model):** - The liquid is **completely mixed** at all times (diffusion in liquid is infinitely fast). - **No diffusion** occurs in the solid (each slice of solid "freezes in" the dopant concentration it had at the moment of solidification). - Initial liquid mass = $M_0$, initial liquid dopant mass = $C_0 M_0$. - At any instant, a fraction $g$ of the original liquid has been solidified. **Step 1.** Let $M$ be the remaining liquid mass, and $C_\ell$ the dopant concentration in the liquid. The change in dopant mass in the liquid as a fraction $dg$ solidifies is: $$ d(C_\ell M) = -C_s \, dM $$ **Step 2.** Apply the segregation relation $C_s = k_0 C_\ell$: $$ d(C_\ell M) = -k_0 C_\ell \, dM $$ **Step 3.** Expand the left side using the product rule: $$ C_\ell \, dM + M \, dC_\ell = -k_0 C_\ell \, dM $$ **Step 4.** Rearrange to isolate the ratio $dC_\ell / C_\ell$: $$ M \, dC_\ell = -(k_0 + 1) C_\ell \, dM $$ $$ \frac{dC_\ell}{C_\ell} = -(k_0 + 1) \frac{dM}{M} $$ **Step 5.** Integrate from $M_0$ (initial liquid) to $M$ (current liquid), and from $C_0$ to $C_\ell$: $$ \int_{C_0}^{C_\ell} \frac{dC_\ell}{C_\ell} = -(k_0 + 1) \int_{M_0}^{M} \frac{dM}{M} $$ $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{C_\ell}{C_0}\right) = -(k_0 + 1) \ln\!\left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right) = (k_0 + 1) \ln\!\left(\frac{M_0}{M}\right) $$ **Step 6.** Solve for $C_\ell$: $$ C_\ell = C_0 \left(\frac{M_0}{M}\right)^{k_0 + 1} $$ **Step 7.** Substitute $M_0/M = 1/(1-g)$ where $g$ is the solidified fraction. Then $C_s = k_0 C_\ell$: $$ C_s(g) = k_0 C_0 (1 - g)^{k_0 - 1} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** If $k_0 < 1$ (e.g., Boron, P, As), the dopant is rejected into the liquid, so $C_s$ *rises* with $g$. If $k_0 > 1$ (rare for Si; e.g., Carbon with $k_0 \approx 0.07$ — actually $k_0 < 1$ for almost all common dopants), $C_s$ *falls* with $g$. This is why wafer-resistivity specifications are quoted for a precise position along the ingot. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Effective Segregation Coefficient with Stirring (Burton–Prim–Slichter) In a real CZ puller, the liquid is not perfectly mixed; a **boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ exists at the solid–liquid interface. The effective segregation coefficient is: $$ k_{eff} = \dfrac{k_0}{k_0 + (1 - k_0) e^{-\Delta}} $$ where the dimensionless parameter is: $$ \Delta = \dfrac{v \, \delta}{D} $$ **Derivation outline:** **Step 1.** Steady-state 1-D diffusion in the boundary layer (no convection): $$ D \frac{d^2 C_\ell}{dz^2} + v \frac{dC_\ell}{dz} = 0 $$ **Step 2.** Boundary conditions: - $C_\ell(z \to \infty) = C_\infty$ (bulk liquid concentration) - At the interface $z = 0$: mass balance gives $D \dfrac{dC_\ell}{dz}\Big|_{0} = v (C_\ell(0) - C_s(0))$ **Step 3.** Apply $C_s(0) = k_0 C_\ell(0)$ and solve the ODE: $$ C_\ell(z) = C_\infty - (C_\infty - k_0 C_\ell(0)) \, e^{-vz/D} $$ **Step 4.** At $z = \delta$, $C_\ell \to C_\infty$. Combining yields: $$ k_{eff} = \frac{C_s}{C_\infty} = \frac{k_0}{k_0 + (1 - k_0) e^{-v\delta/D}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering utility:** Increasing pull speed $v$ or boundary layer thickness $\delta$ pushes $k_{eff} \to 1$, producing a *more uniform axial dopant profile*. This is the principle behind **magnetic-field-assisted CZ (MCZ)** — the magnetic field damps convection, controls $\delta$, and stabilizes $k_{eff}$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing an Additive Pattern-Transfer Method | Criterion | Lift-off | Shadow Mask | µ-Contact Printing | Electroplating | |---|---|---|---|---| | Min. feature size | ~1 µm (e-beam) | ~5–20 µm | ~0.1 µm (SAM resolution) | ~2 µm (photolithography limited) | | Sidewall profile | Tapered, often rough | Vertical, sharp | N/A (chemical pattern) | High aspect ratio, vertical | | Material choices | Metals, dielectrics | Metals only | SAMs + selective plating | Metals (Cu, Ni, Au) | | High-temp steps | Generally no | Yes (stencil must survive) | Limited | No | | Reusability | Single use (resist) | High | High (PDMS stamp) | Single use (mold) | | KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ### 3.4 Worked Example (Likely KTU Numerical) **Problem:** A Czochralski-grown Si ingot is doped uniformly with phosphorus at $C_0 = 1 \times 10^{16}$ at·cm⁻³. The equilibrium segregation coefficient is $k_0 = 0.35$. After solidifying 30% of the melt, find the dopant concentration in the just-frozen solid slice. **Solution:** $$ C_s(0.30) = k_0 C_0 (1 - g)^{k_0 - 1} = 0.35 \times 10^{16} \times (0.70)^{-0.65} $$ Compute the exponent factor: $$ (0.70)^{-0.65} = e^{-0.65 \ln(0.70)} = e^{-0.65 \times (-0.3567)} = e^{0.2319} = 1.261 $$ Therefore: $$ C_s(0.30) = 0.35 \times 10^{16} \times 1.261 = 4.41 \times 10^{15}\ \text{at·cm}^{-3} $$ > [!NOTE] > Although $k_0 < 1$ (P is rejected into the liquid), the concentration in the *just-solidified slice* at $g = 0.30$ is *lower* than $C_0$ because the power-law exponent $(k_0-1)$ is negative, and at $g = 0.30$ the early slices were diluted. As $g \to 1$, $C_s$ rises sharply above $C_0$. This is why **the tail end of a CZ ingot is the most heavily doped** — a key fact examiners love to test. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Lift-off Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Clean Si substrate] --> B[Step 2: Spin-coat photoresist or PMMA] B --> C[Step 3: Soft bake and exposure] C --> D[Step 4: Develop pattern with re-entrant profile] D --> E[Step 5: Blanket deposition of target material metal or dielectric] E --> F[Step 6: Submerge in solvent acetone NMP DMSO] F --> G[Step 7: Resist dissolves and excess material lifts off] G --> H[Step 8: Final patterned film on substrate] H --> I[Optional anneal or next litho step] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Nodes A → D show the *template fabrication*. Node E is the *additive deposition*. Nodes F → G are the *selective removal of the template* (this is what makes it "additive" rather than "subtractive"). Node H is the final pattern. The re-entrant resist profile engineered at D is what enables the discontinuity in the deposited film at E — without it, lift-off fails. ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Czochralski Puller Functional Modules ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Furnace_Assembly CRQ[Quartz Crucible with molten Si 1414 C] HTR[RF Induction Heater] GAS[Argon Gas Inlet and Outlet] end subgraph Pull_Mechanism SEED[Oriented Seed Crystal lt 100 or lt 111] SHAFT[Pulling Shaft with rotation omega_xtal] MTR[Traction Motor and Servo] end subgraph Control_Unit DIAM[Optical Diameter Sensor] WT[Dopant Weighing Station] DAQ[Data Acquisition and PID Loop] end CRQ --> SEED SHAFT --> SEED MTR --> SHAFT HTR --> CRQ GAS --> CRQ DIAM -->|feedback| DAQ WT -->|load recipe| DAQ DAQ -->|PID| HTR DAQ -->|velocity setpoint| MTR SEED -->|grown ingot| INGOT[Si Ingot] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison — CZ vs FZ vs Epitaxy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bulk_Growth CZ["CZ Method: Pull seed from crucible melt
High throughput, contains oxygen, low cost"] FZ["FZ Method: Sweep molten zone along rod
Ultra-pure, low oxygen, higher cost"] end subgraph Thin_Film_Growth VPE["VPE / CVD Epi: 1100-1250 C, SiH4 or SiH2Cl2
Industry standard for CMOS epi"] MBE["MBE: UHV 10-10 Torr, effusion cells
Atomic-layer precision, R and D only"] LPE["LPE: Liquid metal solvent, near-equilibrium
Low defect, used for compound semi"] end CZ --> WAFER[Si Wafer for IC and MEMS] FZ --> WAFER VPE --> WAFER MBE --> WAFER LPE --> WAFER ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway from the diagrams:** The four growth techniques produce wafers with very different oxygen content (CZ is high, FZ is very low), defect density, and cost. For **MEMS bulk micromachining**, CZ wafers are standard because their internal oxygen acts as an etch-stop. For **precision piezo-resistors and power devices**, FZ wafers are preferred because of their higher carrier mobility and uniform resistivity. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *With a neat sketch, explain the lift-off technique used for pattern transfer in MEMS fabrication. Why is a re-entrant resist profile preferred?* **Model Answer (10 lines, ≈ 3-mark weight):** The lift-off technique is an *additive* pattern-transfer process. The steps are: 1. Spin-coat and pattern a photoresist (or PMMA) on the substrate. 2. Engineered **re-entrant (undercut) sidewall** profile is formed by appropriate exposure–development. 3. The desired material (metal/oxide) is blanket-deposited by evaporation or sputtering. 4. The wafer is immersed in a solvent (acetone/NMP); the resist dissolves, and the material on top of the resist is "lifted off," leaving the patterned film in the openings. A **re-entrant profile** is preferred because the overhanging resist edge creates a *discontinuity* in the deposited film. This discontinuity exposes the underlying resist to the solvent. With a vertical or positive-slope sidewall, the deposited film is continuous and the solvent cannot reach the resist, so lift-off fails. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Compare the Czochralski (CZ) and Float Zone (FZ) methods of silicon crystal growth. Mention two advantages of FZ over CZ.* **Model Answer:** In **CZ**, a seed crystal is dipped into a Si melt held in a quartz crucible and slowly pulled upward while rotating, growing a single-crystal ingot. In **FZ**, a molten zone is created by RF heating and swept along a vertical polysilicon rod; the molten zone is supported by surface tension. **Two advantages of FZ over CZ:** 1. **Higher purity** — no quartz crucible contact, so oxygen and metallic contamination are an order of magnitude lower. Resistivity can exceed 10⁴ Ω·cm. 2. **Lower oxygen content** — beneficial for devices sensitive to oxygen-induced defects, e.g., power devices, radiation-hard sensors. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — *[Additive Pattern Transfer focus]* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern, Modified]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **lift-off process** for additive pattern transfer. With a flowchart, show how the process differs from a subtractive (etching) process. Discuss the role of the re-entrant resist profile. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **shadow masking** and **micro-contact printing (µCP)** as alternative additive techniques. Compare their resolution, mask material, and process complexity in a table. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution A(a) — Lift-off Process** The lift-off process is a *four-stage* additive pattern transfer method: **Stage 1 — Sacrificial layer deposition:** A photoresist (typically a *positive* resist such as S1813, or PMMA for e-beam) is spin-coated onto a clean substrate. The thickness of the resist is chosen to be *at least equal to* or *slightly greater than* the thickness of the material to be deposited. **Stage 2 — Lithographic patterning:** The resist is exposed (UV or e-beam) and developed. To obtain a **re-entrant profile**, two techniques are common: - *Chlorobenzene soak* before development of AZ resists — the top layer develops faster than the soaked bottom, producing an undercut. - *Image-reversal* or *bilayer (LOR/S1813)* stack — the lower resist layer is more soluble, yielding an overhang. - For PMMA in e-beam lithography, a higher developer concentration (e.g., MIBK:IPA = 1:3) naturally gives an undercut. **Stage 3 — Material deposition:** The functional material (e.g., Cr/Au, Al, SiO₂, polysilicon) is blanket-deposited. **Evaporation** is preferred over sputtering because evaporated atoms travel *ballistically*, producing a conformal discontinuity at the resist edge. Sputtering, being more isotropic, tends to "fill" the undercut and ruin the lift-off. **Stage 4 — Lift-off:** The wafer is immersed in a resist solvent. NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) at 80 °C, acetone with sonication, or commercial strippers (e.g., Remover 1165) are typical. The resist dissolves, and the overlying material floats off, leaving a clean pattern. **Comparison with subtractive process:** | Aspect | Lift-off (Additive) | Etching (Subtractive) | |---|---|---| | Material placement | Material added *only* in openings | Material covers wafer, then *removed* outside mask | | Masking | Photoresist is *sacrificial* | Photoresist (or hard mask) is *protective* | | Sidewall | Tapered, rough | Can be anisotropic (vertical) | | Compatibility with non-etchable materials | Excellent (Au, Pt, organic) | Poor | **Role of re-entrant profile:** The undercut creates a *break* in the deposited film, exposing the resist beneath to the solvent. Without the undercut, the solvent cannot reach the resist, and the film is not removed. --- **Solution A(b) — Shadow Masking & µCP** **Shadow Masking:** A *stencil* (thin Si membrane ~50 µm, metallic foil Mo, or polyimide) with pre-etched apertures is clamped or bonded to the wafer. Material is evaporated or sputtered *through* the apertures. No resist is used. - *Resolution:* 5–20 µm (limited by aperture edge acuity, mask-to-wafer gap, and mechanical alignment). - *Advantages:* reusable, no resist contamination, can survive high-temperature deposition. - *Disadvantages:* stencil fragility, alignment tolerance, limited minimum feature. **Micro-Contact Printing (µCP):** A *PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane) stamp* is cast against a master, inked with a self-assembled monolayer (SAM) molecule (e.g., OTS, APTES, MHA), and pressed onto the substrate. The SAM transfers only in contact regions, locally modifying surface energy. - *Resolution:* sub-100 nm (determined by stamp contact mechanics). - *Subsequent step:* the printed SAM either *passivates* the surface (blocking subsequent deposition) or *activates* it (promoting selective Au plating via TOLL chemistry). - *Advantages:* no lithography, low cost, biocompatible chemistry available. - *Disadvantages:* stamp deformation (roof-collapse) at high aspect ratio, registration accuracy ~1 µm. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Shadow Masking | Micro-Contact Printing | |---|---|---| | Mask/Stamper | Si, Mo, polyimide stencil | PDMS elastomeric stamp | | Patterning agent | None (physical aperture) | SAM molecules (chemical) | | Min. feature | 5–20 µm | 0.1–1 µm | | Reusability | High (50–200 cycles) | Moderate (10–30 cycles) | | Process complexity | Moderate (alignment, clamping) | Low (no vacuum, room temp) | | Material flexibility | Conductors, simple stacks | Any material compatible with selective chem | --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *[Silicon Growth focus]* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Czochralski (CZ) method** of silicon single-crystal growth. List the key process parameters that determine the crystal quality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Pfann normal-freeze equation** and use it to compute the dopant concentration in a Czochralski-grown ingot at 25% solidification, given $C_0 = 5 \times 10^{15}$ at·cm⁻³ and $k_0 = 0.8$ (Boron in Si). Comment on the trend. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution B(a) — CZ Method** *(See block diagram in Section 4.2.)* **Step-by-step:** 1. Polycrystalline silicon (electronic-grade, 9N purity) is loaded into a **quartz crucible** inside a vacuum-tight chamber. 2. RF induction heating (typically 30–100 kHz) melts the charge at 1414 °C under flowing argon. 3. Dopants (B, P, As, Sb) are added in calculated quantities. 4. A precisely oriented *seed crystal* (`<100>` or `<111>`) is lowered until it just contacts the melt ("**seeding**"). 5. The seed is slowly pulled upward (0.5–5 mm/min) and rotated (5–30 rpm). The crucible rotates *counter* to the seed to enforce thermal symmetry. 6. A *neck* (3–5 mm diameter) is pulled first to "**dislocate**" — propagate away from the seed any threading dislocations. 7. The diameter is then increased to target (12" or 300 mm) during *shouldering*, held constant during *body growth*, and reduced during *tail off*. 8. The ingot is cooled over 12–24 h, ground to a uniform diameter, and sliced into wafers. **Key process parameters:** | Parameter | Effect on crystal | |---|---| | Pull speed $v$ | Higher $v$ → more dislocations, more constitutional supercooling, $k_{eff} \to 1$ | | Crystal rotation $\omega_{xtal}$ | Controls thermal gradient, interface shape (convex vs concave) | | Crucible rotation $\omega_{crucible}$ | Counter-rotation damps convection, stabilizes interface | | Thermal gradient $G$ | High $G$ → more stress, possible slip; low $G$ → dislocations | | Dopant type and concentration | Determines resistivity, segregation behavior | | Seed orientation | Determines crystal plane exposed — `<100>` for CMOS, `<111>` for power | | Crucible quality | Determines oxygen content (high in CZ) | | Ambient pressure | Argon pressure affects evaporation of SiO and carbon transport | --- **Solution B(b) — Pfann Derivation & Numerical** **Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)* Result: $C_s(g) = k_0 C_0 (1 - g)^{k_0 - 1}$ **Numerical computation:** Given: $C_0 = 5 \times 10^{15}$ at·cm⁻³, $k_0 = 0.8$ (Boron in Si), $g = 0.25$. $$ C_s(0.25) = 0.8 \times 5 \times 10^{15} \times (1 - 0.25)^{0.8 - 1} $$ $$ C_s(0.25) = 4 \times 10^{15} \times (0.75)^{-0.2} $$ Compute the power: $$ (0.75)^{-0.2} = e^{-0.2 \times \ln(0.75)} = e^{-0.2 \times (-0.2877)} = e^{0.0575} = 1.0592 $$ $$ C_s(0.25) = 4 \times 10^{15} \times 1.0592 = 4.24 \times 10^{15}\ \text{at·cm}^{-3} $$ **Comment on the trend:** Boron has $k_0 = 0.8 < 1$, so it is *rejected* into the liquid during solidification. The first slices to freeze (low $g$) have $C_s < C_0$, and the last slices (high $g \to 1$) have $C_s \gg C_0$. The axial dopant profile is **monotonically increasing** with $g$. This is why wafer specifications quote a precise slice position (e.g., "top, middle, tail"), and why the tail end of a CZ ingot is often recycled for re-melt rather than used as product wafers. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in this module:** > 1. **Lift-off diagrams** — students draw the *resist* and the *deposited film* as a continuous layer and forget the **discontinuity** at the re-entrant edge. Examiner will deduct 2 marks immediately. Draw the undercut *clearly*. > 2. **Pfann equation** — confusing the *liquid* concentration $C_\ell$ with the *solid* concentration $C_s$. The question almost always asks for $C_s$, and the $k_0$ multiplicative factor at the front is *not* optional. > 3. **CZ vs FZ** — stating "FZ is better than CZ" without context. FZ is purer but more expensive and limited to ~6" diameter. The correct answer always specifies the *application*. > 4. **Segregation coefficient** — writing $k_0 = C_\ell / C_s$ instead of the correct $C_s / C_\ell$. This is a sign of a weak concept and examiners *will* notice. > 5. **Epitaxy vs Poly** — claiming "epitaxy" is a synonym for "thin film." Epitaxy specifically requires a *crystalline* substrate and produces a *single-crystal* overgrowth. Polysilicon is *not* epitaxial. > 6. **Resist thickness in lift-off** — for a film of thickness $t$, the resist must be **at least $1.5t$ to $2t$ thick** to allow the solvent to dissolve it. Students routinely ignore this. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Additive pattern transfer** builds the pattern by *depositing* material through a template, the opposite of subtractive etching. **Lift-off, shadow masking, micro-contact printing, and electroplating** are the four flagship techniques. - **Lift-off** is the workhorse of metal patterning in MEMS and CMOS; it requires a **re-entrant resist profile** and a **blanket deposition** (preferably evaporation). - **Shadow masking** is a *resistless* technique limited to ~5–20 µm features; ideal for lift-off-free or high-temperature depositions. - **Micro-contact printing (µCP)** uses a *PDMS stamp* to deliver a *chemical pattern* (SAM) — it is a soft-lithography technique, not a deposition technique. - **Electroplating** in additive mode uses a *seed layer + thick photoresist mold*; central to **LIGA** and high-aspect-ratio metallic MEMS. - **Czochralski (CZ)** grows bulk Si crystals by *pulling a seed from a crucible melt*; widely used because of high throughput and diameter control. Trade-off: **high oxygen content** from the quartz crucible. - **Float Zone (FZ)** sweeps a *molten zone* along a poly-Si rod; produces **ultra-pure, low-oxygen** Si, but limited diameter and higher cost. - **Epitaxy** is the growth of a *single-crystal thin film* on a *single-crystal substrate*; classified by method into **VPE, MBE, LPE, SEG**. - **Pfann normal-freeze equation** $C_s(g) = k_0 C_0 (1-g)^{k_0-1}$ describes axial dopant distribution in a directionally solidified ingot; assumes complete mixing in liquid and no diffusion in solid. - **Burton–Prim–Slichter** extension gives the *effective* segregation coefficient $k_{eff} = \dfrac{k_0}{k_0 + (1-k_0) e^{-v\delta/D}}$ for stirred melts. - **Si melting point = 1414 °C**, **lattice constant = 0.5431 nm**, **atomic density ≈ 5 × 10²² at·cm⁻³** are the three numbers to memorize. - For a $k_0 < 1$ dopant, the **first-to-freeze slices are dopant-poor and the last-to-freeze are dopant-rich** — a direct consequence of the Pfann equation. - **CZ wafers are preferred for bulk micromachining** (oxygen acts as an etch-stop in KOH); **FZ wafers are preferred for power devices and high-resistivity sensors** (low oxygen, high mobility). - **MBE is the only growth technique with atomic-layer precision**, but it is slow (~1 µm/hr) and not used in production MEMS. - **Reusability ranking of additive templates:** Shadow mask ≫ µCP stamp > electroplating mold > lift-off resist (single use). - For the 14-mark question, the examiner expects a **diagram**, the **Pfann derivation**, and at least one **numerical** — practice all three.

topics.title: Oxidation of Silicon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oxidation of Silicon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Thermal Oxidation of Silicon** is a high-temperature, furnace-based fabrication process in which a silicon (Si) wafer is exposed to an oxidizing ambient (dry molecular oxygen, $O_2$, or water vapor, $H_2O$) to grow a chemically stable, electrically insulating layer of silicon dioxide ($SiO_2$) directly on the substrate surface. The process follows the general chemical transformation:
> $$\text{Si (solid)} + \text{Oxidant} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (solid)}$$

### 1.1 The Two Principal Variants of Thermal Oxidation

| Variant | Oxidizing Agent | Operating Temperature | Reaction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Oxidation** | Pure $O_2$ gas | 1000 – 1200 °C | $Si + O_2 \rightarrow SiO_2$ |
| **Wet Oxidation** | $H_2O$ vapor (bubbled in $O_2$ or $N_2$) | 800 – 1100 °C | $Si + 2H_2O \rightarrow SiO_2 + 2H_2$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Controlled Rusting"**
> Think of thermal oxidation as **"controlled, slow-motion rusting"** of a silicon wafer. Just as a piece of iron left in damp air develops a flaky, porous iron-oxide layer, a silicon wafer placed in a hot oxidizing atmosphere develops a glassy, highly uniform $SiO_2$ layer — but with two critical differences:
> 1. The process is **atomically precise** and **self-limiting** (the rate depends on temperature and time).
> 2. Unlike rust, $SiO_2$ is **electrically insulating, chemically inert, and optically transparent**, making it the "workhorse dielectric" of MEMS and IC technology.

### 1.3 Why Thermal Oxidation is the Cornerstone of MEMS

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Reasons:**
> - **Excellent Masking Layer**: $SiO_2$ blocks the diffusion of boron (B), phosphorus (P), and arsenic (As) dopants — enabling selective doping.
> - **Surface Passivation**: Hangs Si dangling bonds, reducing surface states and leakage currents.
> - **Dielectric Layer**: Used as the gate oxide in MOS/MEMS capacitors, pressure sensors, and accelerometers.
> - **Sacrificial Layer**: In surface-micromachined MEMS, $SiO_2$ is etched away (sacrificed) using **HF (hydrofluoric acid)** to release suspended structures.
> - **Mechanical Buffer**: Used as a stress-compensating layer in composite micromechanical structures.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used in Oxidation Kinetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three rate constants in oxidation theory are:
> - **Parabolic rate constant $B$** — units: $\mu m^2 / hr$.
> - **Linear rate constant $\dfrac{B}{A}$** — units: $\mu m / hr$.
> - **Deal–Grove constant $A$** — units: $\mu m$ (representing the characteristic transition thickness between regimes).

**Boltzmann constant** $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ **eV/K** is used in the Arrhenius expression for $B$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Deal–Grove Model (The Foundational Theory)

The **Deal–Grove Model (1965)** is the universally accepted analytical framework for thermal oxidation of silicon. It mathematically models oxide growth as a **three-stage flux-limited process**:

> **Stage 1 — Gas-Phase Transport:** Oxidant species diffuse from the bulk gas to the outer $SiO_2$ surface.
>
> **Stage 2 — Diffusive Transport Through Oxide:** Oxidant diffuses through the already-grown $SiO_2$ layer toward the $Si\text{–}SiO_2$ interface.
>
> **Stage 3 — Chemical Reaction at the Interface:** Oxidant reacts with the underlying Si to form fresh $SiO_2$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Deal–Grove Derivation Setup

- The oxidant (e.g., $O_2$ or $H_2O$) must **dissolve** into the outer $SiO_2$ surface (governed by Henry's law).
- The dissolved oxidant then **diffuses** through the existing oxide to the buried $Si\text{–}SiO_2$ interface (governed by Fick's first law).
- At the interface, the oxidant **chemically reacts** with Si to form new $SiO_2$ (governed by first-order surface reaction kinetics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Steady-State Assumption:** At any instant, the flux entering the oxide equals the flux leaving it:
> $$F_1 = F_2 = F_3 = F$$
> where $F$ is the net steady-state flux of oxidant (molecules per unit area per unit time).

### 2.3 Mass Conservation at the Interface

The growth rate of the oxide is directly proportional to the flux of oxidant reaching the Si interface:

$$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1}$$

where $N_1$ is the **number of oxidant molecules incorporated per unit volume of $SiO_2$** (for dry $O_2$: $N_1 = 2.2 \times 10^{22} \; cm^{-3}$).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The interface advances into the silicon substrate because **approximately 44 % of the $SiO_2$ volume lies *below* the original Si surface** and **56 % lies *above*** it. This 44/56 split is a high-frequency conceptual question in KTU exams.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deal–Grove General Equation** | $x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, (t + \tau)$ | $x_0$ = oxide thickness, $t$ = oxidation time, $\tau$ = time-shift for initial oxide |
| 2 | **Parabolic Regime (thick oxide, $x_0 \gg A$)** | $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B \, t}$ | Diffusion-limited, dominant at long times |
| 3 | **Linear Regime (thin oxide, $x_0 \ll A$)** | $x_0 \approx \dfrac{B}{A} \, (t + \tau)$ | Reaction-limited, dominant at short times |
| 4 | **Arrhenius form of $B$** | $B = B_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{kT}\right)$ | $B_0$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy |
| 5 | **Activation Energy — Dry $O_2$** | $E_a \approx 1.23 \; eV$ | $B_0 \approx 12.5 \; \mu m^2/hr$ (reference) |
| 6 | **Activation Energy — Wet $H_2O$** | $E_a \approx 0.78 \; eV$ | $B_0 \approx 3.86 \times 10^3 \; \mu m^2/hr$ (reference) |
| 7 | **Deal–Grove Constant** | $A = 2 D \!\left(\dfrac{1}{k_s} + \dfrac{1}{h_g}\right)$ | $D$ = diffusivity, $k_s$ = surface rate, $h_g$ = gas-phase transfer |
| 8 | **Steady-State Flux** | $F = \dfrac{D \, C^{\star}}{x_0 + A}$ | $C^{\star}$ = equilibrium solubility |
| 9 | **Volumetric Expansion Ratio** | $\dfrac{V_{SiO_2}}{V_{Si}} \approx 2.2$ | 44 % of $SiO_2$ below original Si surface |
| 10 | **Effective Thickness for Dopant Diffusion Barrier** | $t_{SiO_2} \geq 0.5 \, \mu m$ | General threshold for masking B / P / As |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Absolute Value Notation:**
> In handwritten answer sheets, use the parenthesis form $(x_0^2 + A x_0)$ — never the broken pipe $\vert \cdot \vert$ — for the Deal–Grove quadratic. Always specify whether you are using the **linear** or **parabolic** approximation before substituting values.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Oxidation Kinetics

- **Gate Oxide Thickness Control in MOS-MEMS Sensors:** A 10–100 nm range demands the **linear-regime (reaction-limited)** model for accuracy.
- **Field Oxide (FOX / LOCOS) Growth:** Thick (0.5–1.5 μm) oxides for device isolation — modeled by the **parabolic (diffusion-limited)** regime.
- **Predictive Process Simulation:** Tools like SUPREME and ATHENA internally solve the full Deal–Grove quadratic to predict oxide profiles in MEMS process flows.
- **Quality Engineering:** Dry oxidation is preferred for **thin, high-quality oxides** (low defect density, low charge trapping) used in precision MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Deal–Grove Equation

**Step 1 — Define the three fluxes at steady state:**

$$\text{Flux of oxidant in gas to outer surface: } F_1 = h_g \, (C^{\star} - C_0)$$

$$\text{Flux of oxidant through the oxide (Fick's law): } F_2 = \frac{D \, (C_0 - C_i)}{x_0}$$

$$\text{Flux of oxidant reacting at Si–SiO}_2 \text{ interface: } F_3 = k_s \, C_i$$

where $C^{\star}$, $C_0$, $C_i$ are the oxidant concentrations in the gas, at the outer oxide surface, and at the Si interface respectively.

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state condition $F_1 = F_2 = F_3$:**

Setting $F_2 = F_3$ eliminates $C_i$:

$$\frac{D \, (C_0 - C_i)}{x_0} = k_s \, C_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_0 = C_i \!\left(1 + \frac{k_s x_0}{D}\right)$$

Setting $F_1 = F_2$ eliminates $C_0$:

$$h_g \, (C^{\star} - C_0) = \frac{D \, (C_0 - C_i)}{x_0}$$

Solving both simultaneously for the steady-state flux $F$:

$$F = \frac{D \, C^{\star}}{\dfrac{D}{h_g} + x_0 + \dfrac{D}{k_s}}$$

**Step 3 — Relate flux to oxide growth rate:**

The number of oxidant molecules consumed per unit volume of $SiO_2$ formed is $N_1$, hence:

$$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1} = \frac{C^{\star} / N_1}{\dfrac{1}{h_g} + \dfrac{x_0}{D} + \dfrac{1}{k_s}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Deal–Grove constants $A$ and $B$:**

$$B \equiv \frac{2 \, D \, C^{\star}}{N_1} \quad ; \quad A \equiv 2 D \!\left(\frac{1}{k_s} + \frac{1}{h_g}\right)$$

The factor of 2 arises from converting $N_1$ to moles and accounting for stoichiometry. Substituting:

$$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{B}{2 x_0 + A}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with initial condition $x_0(0) = x_i$ (initial oxide thickness):**

Integrating from $t = -\tau$ to $t$ where $\tau = \dfrac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B}$:

$$x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, (t + \tau)$$

This is the **canonical Deal–Grove equation** used in every KTU exam problem.

### 3.2 Limit-Case Reduction — Parabolic and Linear Regimes

**Step 6 — For $x_0 \gg A$ (thick oxide, long time):**

The linear term $A x_0$ is negligible compared to $x_0^2$:

$$x_0^2 \approx B \, t \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_0 \approx \sqrt{B \, t}$$

This is the **parabolic regime** (diffusion-controlled growth).

**Step 7 — For $x_0 \ll A$ (thin oxide, short time):**

The quadratic term $x_0^2$ is negligible:

$$A \, x_0 \approx B \, (t + \tau) \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_0 \approx \frac{B}{A} \, (t + \tau)$$

This is the **linear regime** (reaction-controlled growth).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Parabolic Regime

> **Problem:** A silicon wafer is oxidized in dry $O_2$ at $1100 \; ^{\circ}C$ for $4 \; hr$. Given the parabolic rate constant $B = 0.165 \; \mu m^2 / hr$, determine the oxide thickness grown.

**Solution:**

Since $4 \; hr$ is a long oxidation duration (thick oxide), the parabolic regime is valid. From the formula sheet:

$$x_0 \approx \sqrt{B \, t}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$x_0 \approx \sqrt{0.165 \times 4}$$

$$x_0 \approx \sqrt{0.660}$$

$$x_0 \approx 0.812 \; \mu m$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This $\sim 0.8 \; \mu m$ thick oxide is sufficient to mask standard dopant diffusions (B, P, As) and is suitable as a field-oxide layer in MEMS pressure-sensor fabrication.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Full Deal–Grove Quadratic

> **Problem:** A Si wafer undergoes wet oxidation at $1000 \; ^{\circ}C$ for $2 \; hr$. Given $B = 0.871 \; \mu m^2/hr$ and $B/A = 0.203 \; \mu m/hr$, and assuming no initial oxide ($\tau = 0$), determine the oxide thickness.

**Solution:**

Compute $A$:

$$A = \frac{B}{B/A} = \frac{0.871}{0.203} \approx 4.29 \; \mu m$$

Apply the full Deal–Grove equation with $\tau = 0$:

$$x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, t$$

$$x_0^2 + 4.29 \, x_0 = 0.871 \times 2 = 1.742$$

Rearrange into standard quadratic form:

$$x_0^2 + 4.29 \, x_0 - 1.742 = 0$$

Apply the quadratic formula $x_0 = \dfrac{-A + \sqrt{A^2 + 4 B t}}{2}$ (taking the positive root):

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + \sqrt{(4.29)^2 + 4(1.742)}}{2}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + \sqrt{18.4041 + 6.968}}{2}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + \sqrt{25.372}}{2}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + 5.037}{2}$$

$$x_0 \approx \frac{0.747}{2} \approx 0.374 \; \mu m$$

**Verification of regime:** Since $x_0 \approx 0.374 \; \mu m \ll A = 4.29 \; \mu m$, the growth is in the **linear (reaction-limited) regime** — as expected for a thin, freshly grown oxide.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords are used. All node labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown or HTML inside quoted labels.

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Thermal Oxidation of Silicon

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Si Wafer Substrate 100 Orientation] --> B[RCA Chemical Cleaning]
    B --> C[HF Dip Native Oxide Removal]
    C --> D[Load into Horizontal Oxidation Furnace]
    D --> E[Ramp Temperature 800C to 1200C]
    E --> F{Choose Oxidation Type}
    F -->|Dry Oxidation| G1[Introduce Pure O2 Gas Flow]
    F -->|Wet Oxidation| G2[Introduce H2O Vapor in O2 Carrier]
    G1 --> H1[Si Plus O2 Reacts to Form SiO2]
    G2 --> H2[Si Plus 2H2O Reacts to Form SiO2 Plus 2H2]
    H1 --> I[SiO2 Layer Growth on Si Surface]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Monitor Thickness via Ellipsometer]
    J --> K[Cool Down in N2 Ambient]
    K --> L[Wafer Unload for Next Process Step]
```

### 4.2 Deal–Grove Three-Flux Model Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    Gas[Gas Phase Oxidant] -->|Flux F1 via h_g| Outer[Outer SiO2 Surface C0]
    Outer -->|Flux F2 via D Diffusion| Interface[Si-SiO2 Interface Ci]
    Interface -->|Flux F3 via k_s Reaction| SiBulk[Consumed Si Substrate]
    SiBulk --> NewOx[New SiO2 Volume Formed]
    NewOx -.->|44 Percent Below Original Surface| SiBulk
    NewOx -.->|56 Percent Above Original Surface| Outer
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Oxidation Furnace

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph GasDelivery["Gas Delivery and Mixing System"]
        O2Tank[O2 Cylinder] --> MFC1[Mass Flow Controller 1]
        N2Tank[N2 Cylinder] --> MFC2[Mass Flow Controller 2]
        H2OBubbler[H2O Bubbler at 95C] --> MFC3[Mass Flow Controller 3]
    end
    subgraph FurnaceCore["Furnace Core Module"]
        MFC1 --> Manifold[Gas Manifold]
        MFC2 --> Manifold
        MFC3 --> Manifold
        Manifold --> QuartzTube[Horizontal Quartz Tube]
        Heater[Three Zone Heater 800 to 1200C] --> QuartzTube
        SiBoat[Si Wafer Boat Inside Tube] --> QuartzTube
    end
    subgraph ExhaustControl["Exhaust and Safety Subsystem"]
        QuartzTube --> Scrubber[Acid Scrubber for H2 Exhaust]
        Scrubber --> Vent[Clean Exhaust Vent]
    end
    subgraph Monitoring["In Situ Monitoring"]
        Thermocouple[K Type Thermocouple] --> Controller[PID Temperature Controller]
        Controller --> Heater
        PressureGauge[Tube Pressure Gauge] --> Manifold
    end
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block — Dry vs. Wet Oxidation

```mermaid
graph TD
    Compare{Dry or Wet Oxidation} -->|Choose| Dry[DRY OXIDATION]
    Compare -->|Choose| Wet[WET OXIDATION]
    Dry --> D1[Oxidant: Pure O2]
    Dry --> D2[Temperature: 1000 to 1200 C]
    Dry --> D3[Growth Rate: Slow]
    Dry --> D4[Oxide Quality: Excellent Dense Low Defect]
    Dry --> D5[Typical Use: Thin Gate Oxide Masking]
    Wet --> W1[Oxidant: H2O in O2]
    Wet --> W2[Temperature: 800 to 1100 C]
    Wet --> W3[Growth Rate: Fast 5 to 10x Dry]
    Wet --> W4[Oxide Quality: Slightly Lower Density]
    Wet --> W5[Typical Use: Thick Field Oxide LOCOS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** If $B$ is given in $\mu m^2 / hr$ and $t$ in minutes, the answer is **wrong by a factor of 60**. Always convert time to hours before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Initial Oxide $\tau$:** In real wafers, a native $SiO_2$ layer of $\sim 1 \; nm$ is always present. For thin-oxide problems, students lose 1–2 marks by setting $\tau = 0$ blindly.
> 3. **Wrong Regime Selection:** Using the parabolic approximation $\sqrt{Bt}$ for a thin oxide gives an over-estimate; using the linear approximation for a thick oxide gives an under-estimate. **Always compare $x_0$ with $A$ first.**
> 4. **Confusing $B$ and $B/A$:** $B$ has units of $\mu m^2/hr$ (area-like); $B/A$ has units of $\mu m/hr$ (length-like). Mixing them up is a **fatal** mistake in 14-mark derivations.
> 5. **Volumetric Expansion:** Do not forget the 44/56 split — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in the Part A section.

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** Differentiate between dry and wet oxidation of silicon. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidizing agent | Pure molecular $O_2$ | $H_2O$ vapor in $O_2$ or $N_2$ carrier |
| Chemical reaction | $Si + O_2 \to SiO_2$ | $Si + 2 H_2O \to SiO_2 + 2 H_2$ |
| Temperature range | 1000 – 1200 °C | 800 – 1100 °C |
| Growth rate | Slow | Fast (5–10× faster than dry) |
| Oxide density | Higher, denser | Slightly lower |
| Oxide quality | Excellent, low defect density, low charge trapping | Good, but higher fixed charge |
| Typical MEMS / IC use | Thin gate oxides, MOS-MEMS capacitors | Thick field oxides, LOCOS isolation |
| Activation energy $E_a$ of $B$ | $\approx 1.23 \; eV$ | $\approx 0.78 \; eV$ |

---

#### **Q2.** What is the Deal–Grove model? Explain the significance of the parabolic rate constant $B$ and the linear rate constant $B/A$. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Deal–Grove model** (1965) describes the thermal oxidation kinetics of silicon through the equation:

$$x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, (t + \tau)$$

**Significance of the two rate constants:**

- **Parabolic rate constant $B$** — governs the **diffusion-limited regime** for thick oxides ($x_0 \gg A$). It depends on the diffusivity $D$ of the oxidant through $SiO_2$ and follows Arrhenius behavior $B = B_0 \exp(-E_a / kT)$. A larger $B$ means faster diffusion of oxidant molecules.

- **Linear rate constant $B/A$** — governs the **reaction-limited regime** for thin oxides ($x_0 \ll A$). It depends on the surface reaction rate $k_s$ at the Si–$SiO_2$ interface. A larger $B/A$ means a faster chemical reaction at the interface.

The ratio $A = B / (B/A)$ is the **transition thickness** between the two regimes; for $x_0 < A$, growth is linear, and for $x_0 > A$, growth is parabolic.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Deal–Grove model for thermal oxidation of silicon. State clearly the assumptions, define all constants, and discuss the linear and parabolic rate regimes. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions of the Deal–Grove Model:**
1. Steady-state condition: $F_1 = F_2 = F_3$.
2. One-dimensional oxidant transport along the oxide thickness.
3. The oxidant obeys Henry's law at the gas–oxide interface.
4. The reaction at the Si–$SiO_2$ interface is first-order in oxidant concentration.
5. The oxide is amorphous, homogeneous, and isotropic.

**Derivation:**

The three fluxes are:

$$F_1 = h_g \, (C^{\star} - C_0) \quad ; \quad F_2 = \frac{D \, (C_0 - C_i)}{x_0} \quad ; \quad F_3 = k_s \, C_i$$

Setting $F_1 = F_2 = F_3 = F$ and eliminating $C_0$ and $C_i$:

$$F = \frac{D \, C^{\star}}{x_0 + 2 D \left(\dfrac{1}{h_g} + \dfrac{1}{k_s}\right)}$$

Define the constants:

$$B = \frac{2 D C^{\star}}{N_1} \quad ; \quad A = 2 D \left(\frac{1}{h_g} + \frac{1}{k_s}\right)$$

The growth rate equation is:

$$\frac{dx_0}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1} = \frac{B}{2 x_0 + A}$$

Integrating with $x_0(0) = x_i$ and the time-shift $\tau = (x_i^2 + A x_i) / B$:

$$\boxed{x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, (t + \tau)}$$

**Linear vs Parabolic Regimes:**

| Regime | Condition | Reduced Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $x_0 \ll A$ | $x_0 \approx \dfrac{B}{A} (t + \tau)$ | Reaction-rate limited at Si–$SiO_2$ interface |
| Parabolic | $x_0 \gg A$ | $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B t}$ | Diffusion-rate limited through existing $SiO_2$ |

---

**(b)** Calculate the oxide thickness grown on a (100) Si wafer in wet oxidation at $1000 \; ^{\circ}C$ for $2 \; hr$. Given: $B = 0.871 \; \mu m^2/hr$, $B/A = 0.203 \; \mu m/hr$, and $\tau = 0$. Also discuss any two important applications of thermally grown $SiO_2$ in MEMS fabrication. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Deal–Grove constant $A$:**

$$A = \frac{B}{B/A} = \frac{0.871}{0.203} \approx 4.29 \; \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Apply the full Deal–Grove equation with $\tau = 0$:**

$$x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, t$$

$$x_0^2 + 4.29 \, x_0 = 0.871 \times 2$$

$$x_0^2 + 4.29 \, x_0 = 1.742$$

Rearrange to standard quadratic form:

$$x_0^2 + 4.29 \, x_0 - 1.742 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the quadratic formula $x_0 = \dfrac{-A + \sqrt{A^2 + 4 B t}}{2}$:**

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + \sqrt{(4.29)^2 + 4 \times 1.742}}{2}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + \sqrt{18.4041 + 6.968}}{2}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + \sqrt{25.372}}{2}$$

$$x_0 = \frac{-4.29 + 5.037}{2}$$

$$\boxed{x_0 \approx 0.374 \; \mu m \;\; (\text{or } 374 \; nm)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the regime:**

Since $x_0 \approx 0.374 \; \mu m \ll A = 4.29 \; \mu m$, the growth is in the **linear (reaction-limited) regime**.

**Step 5 — Two Applications of Thermally Grown $SiO_2$ in MEMS:**

1. **Sacrificial Layer in Surface Micromachining:** Thermally grown $SiO_2$ is etched selectively using buffered HF (BHF) to release suspended polysilicon microstructures — used in accelerometers, gyroscopes, and micromirrors.

2. **Dielectric / Insulating Layer in Capacitive MEMS Sensors:** $SiO_2$ serves as the dielectric in capacitive pressure sensors and MEMS microphones, providing a high breakdown field ($\sim 10 \; MV/cm$) and excellent charge stability.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the thermal oxidation furnace system used for silicon oxidation. Compare the oxidation kinetics of dry and wet processes. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Thermal Oxidation Furnace Setup (Block Description):**

A standard horizontal oxidation furnace consists of four subsystems:

1. **Gas Delivery Subsystem:** High-purity $O_2$ and $N_2$ cylinders regulated by **Mass Flow Controllers (MFCs)**; for wet oxidation, an $H_2O$ bubbler maintained at $95 \; ^{\circ}C$ provides water vapor.

2. **Furnace Core:** A horizontal **fused-quartz tube** heated by a **three-zone resistance heater** capable of maintaining $800 \; ^{\circ}C$ to $1200 \; ^{\circ}C$ with $\pm 1 \; ^{\circ}C$ uniformity. The Si wafer boat is loaded from the cool end and pushed into the hot zone.

3. **Temperature Control Subsystem:** A **PID controller** with a **K-type thermocouple** ensures stable temperature ramp, soak, and cool-down profiles.

4. **Exhaust and Safety Subsystem:** An **acid scrubber** removes unreacted oxidant and $H_2$ byproducts; the clean vent is connected to a safe exhaust.

**Comparison of Dry and Wet Oxidation Kinetics:**

| Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| $B$ (parabolic rate constant) | Smaller | Larger (5–10× dry) |
| $B/A$ (linear rate constant) | Smaller | Larger |
| Activation energy $E_a$ of $B$ | $1.23 \; eV$ (higher) | $0.78 \; eV$ (lower) |
| Temperature sensitivity | High (strong $T$-dependence) | Low (weaker $T$-dependence) |
| Limiting step at $1000 \; ^{\circ}C$ | Reaction-limited | Diffusion-limited |
| Oxide thickness for $1 \; hr$ at $1000 \; ^{\circ}C$ | $\sim 0.07 \; \mu m$ | $\sim 0.4 \; \mu m$ |

---

**(b)** Dry oxidation of a Si wafer is performed at $1200 \; ^{\circ}C$. The parabolic rate constant is $B = 0.165 \; \mu m^2 / hr$ and the linear rate constant is $B/A = 0.04 \; \mu m / hr$. Assuming no initial oxide ($\tau = 0$), calculate the oxidation time required to grow a $1 \; \mu m$ thick $SiO_2$ layer. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $A$:**

$$A = \frac{B}{B/A} = \frac{0.165}{0.04} = 4.125 \; \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Deal–Grove equation with $x_0 = 1 \; \mu m$ and $\tau = 0$:**

$$x_0^2 + A \, x_0 = B \, t$$

$$(1)^2 + 4.125 \times 1 = 0.165 \times t$$

$$1 + 4.125 = 0.165 \, t$$

$$5.125 = 0.165 \, t$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$:**

$$t = \frac{5.125}{0.165}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 31.06 \; hr}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of regime:**

Since $x_0 = 1 \; \mu m < A = 4.125 \; \mu m$, the growth is in the **linear (reaction-limited) regime**, validating the use of the full Deal–Grove quadratic rather than the parabolic approximation. (The parabolic approximation would have given $t = 1/0.165 \approx 6.06 \; hr$, an under-estimate by a factor of 5.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **Two Variants of Oxidation:** **Dry** (slow, dense, high quality) and **Wet** (fast, slightly less dense).
- **Canonical Equation:** $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- **Parabolic Regime:** $x_0 \gg A$ → $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B t}$ (diffusion-limited, thick oxides).
- **Linear Regime:** $x_0 \ll A$ → $x_0 \approx (B/A)(t + \tau)$ (reaction-limited, thin oxides).
- **Arrhenius Behavior:** $B = B_0 \exp(-E_a / kT)$ with $E_a \approx 1.23 \; eV$ (dry) and $0.78 \; eV$ (wet).
- **44/56 Volumetric Split:** 44 % of $SiO_2$ volume lies **below** the original Si surface; 56 % **above**.
- **Three-Flux Model:** Gas transport ($F_1$), diffusion ($F_2$), surface reaction ($F_3$).
- **Wet is 5–10× Faster than Dry** — prefer wet for thick field oxides, dry for gate-quality thin oxides.
- **$SiO_2$ is a Universal Mask** for boron, phosphorus, and arsenic dopant diffusion.
- **MEMS Applications:** Sacrificial layer, gate dielectric, surface passivation, etch stop, stress buffer.
- **Etchant for $SiO_2$:** Hydrofluoric acid (HF) or buffered HF (BHF); highly selective against Si and $Si_3N_4$.
- **Time-shift $\tau$:** Accounts for the **native oxide** initially present on every Si wafer; never blindly assume $\tau = 0$ for high-precision problems.
- **Activation Energies (Memorize):** Dry $1.23 \; eV$, Wet $0.78 \; eV$ — these are KTU-favorite numerical values.
- **Always Convert Units:** $B$ is in $\mu m^2 / hr$ and $t$ must be in hours; $B/A$ is in $\mu m / hr$.
- **Regime Check:** Compute $x_0 / A$ before applying any simplified formula.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 2 / Oxidation of Silicon**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecular Beam Epitaxy. Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Molecular Beam Epitaxy. Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) is an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) thin-film deposition technique in which beams of atoms or molecules, generated by thermal evaporation from effusion cells (Knudsen cells), are directed in a collimated stream toward a heated crystalline substrate to grow epitaxial (single-crystal) layers with atomic-scale precision. The process is monitored *in-situ* using Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction (RHEED).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** MBE is classified as an *atomic-layer-by-layer* growth technique and is the cornerstone process for fabricating high-mobility heterojunctions, HEMTs, quantum wells, and superlattices used in modern MEMS/RF/optoelectronic devices.

> [!NOTE] **Key Vacuum Threshold:** MBE requires a base pressure of **≈ 10⁻¹⁰ Torr** to ensure mean free path of the molecular beam exceeds the source-to-substrate distance (typically 200–300 mm).

**Intuitive Analogy — "Atomic Spray Painting":**
Imagine painting a wall, but instead of using coarse paint droplets, you are using a *super-fine atomic mist* ejected from individual heated crucibles. Each "spray can" holds a pure element (e.g., Gallium, Arsenic, Indium). By opening and closing mechanical shutters in front of these crucibles — with millisecond timing — you can deposit exact monolayer-thick stacks on the substrate, much like building a Lego model one block at a time. The high vacuum ensures the "spray" travels without bumping into stray gas molecules, so each atom lands exactly where intended.

---

### 1.2 Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) is a broad class of vacuum-based thin-film deposition processes in which a source material is physically converted from a solid (or liquid) phase into a vapour phase and transported through a low-pressure environment to condense onto a substrate, forming a thin film. PVD encompasses techniques such as *thermal evaporation*, *electron-beam (e-beam) evaporation*, and *sputtering*.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** PVD is the *workhorse* thin-film process in MEMS fabrication, used for depositing metal electrodes (Au, Al, Cr), adhesion layers (Ti, Cr), and structural films (poly-Si is excluded — it uses CVD/LPCVD). For OEICT612, focus on **evaporation** and **sputtering** as the two principal PVD sub-techniques.

> [!NOTE] **Operating Pressure Range:** PVD operates at **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁷ Torr** (significantly higher than MBE), since PVD does not require atomic-scale beam collimation — the vapour simply needs to reach the substrate with minimal scattering.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Boiling Water on Cold Glass":**
Take a kettle of water, heat it until steam rises, and let the steam condense on a cold glass plate held above. The water molecules travel ballistically and stick to the cold surface as droplets. Now replace the water with a metal (Gold, Aluminium), heat it intensely (either resistively or by bombarding with electrons), and you get the same effect — metal vapour condenses as a thin metallic film on the substrate. **Sputtering**, the other major PVD flavour, is like "atomic sandblasting in reverse": high-energy Argon ions knock atoms off a metal target, and these ejected atoms rain down on the substrate to form the film.

---

### 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | MBE | PVD (Evaporation) | PVD (Sputtering) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vacuum Level | **10⁻¹⁰ Torr** | 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Torr | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ Torr |
| Growth Mode | Epitaxial (single crystal) | Polycrystalline / amorphous | Polycrystalline |
| Thickness Control | **Monolayer precision** | ~1 nm | ~1 nm |
| Substrate Temp. | 400–700 °C | Room temp – 300 °C | Room temp – 400 °C |
| Typical Use | HEMTs, QWs, superlattices | MEMS metal electrodes | Adhesion/seed layers, alloys |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MBE — Underlying Physics & Components

The MBE system is engineered around *four physical prerequisites*:

1. **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV):** Ensures the molecular beam mean free path λ ≫ source-to-substrate distance L. This eliminates gas-phase collisions and preserves beam collimation.
2. **Effusion (Knudsen) Cells:** Contain the source material in pyrolytic boron nitride (PBN) crucibles, heated radiatively by Ta filaments. The orifice diameter is much smaller than the mean free path inside the cell, producing a *molecular flow* (Knudsen flow) regime.
3. **Mechanical Shutters:** BeCu or refractory metal shutters that can interrupt the beam within **< 0.1 s**, enabling abrupt heterojunction interfaces.
4. **In-situ RHEED:** A 5–20 keV electron gun aimed at the substrate at a grazing angle (1°–3°). The diffraction pattern on a fluorescent screen oscillates with a period equal to the deposition of *one monolayer*, giving real-time growth-rate feedback.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic of MBE:**

- **Step 1 — Source Heating:** Effusion cell temperature is raised until the source vapour pressure reaches ~10⁻³ to 10⁻² Torr.
- **Step 2 — Beam Formation:** Atoms effuse through the cell orifice, forming a near-collimated molecular beam.
- **Step 3 — Surface Migration:** Upon arrival at the heated substrate, adatoms migrate on the surface until they find an energetically favourable site (step edge, kink).
- **Step 4 — Layer-by-Layer Growth:** Frank–van der Merwe (2D) growth occurs when lattice mismatch is small (< 2%). The RHEED intensity oscillates with each completed monolayer.
- **Step 5 — Interface Formation:** Shutter switching produces atomically abrupt heterojunctions.

> [!NOTE] **The "Why" of UHV:** At 10⁻¹⁰ Torr, the time for a monolayer of residual gas to adsorb on the substrate is ~10⁴ s (≈ 3 hours). This is longer than the typical MBE growth time, ensuring film purity.

---

### 2.2 PVD — Underlying Physics & Variants

#### 2.2.1 Thermal Evaporation
The source is resistively heated (W, Mo, or Ta boat/filament) until its vapour pressure exceeds ~10⁻² Torr. Atoms evaporate in all directions; a fraction condenses on the substrate placed above. Used for low-melting-point metals (Al, Au, Ag, Cr).

#### 2.2.2 Electron-Beam (e-beam) Evaporation
A focused electron beam (5–20 kV, ~kW power) strikes the source held in a water-cooled copper crucible. The localized high power density (> 10⁶ W/cm²) evaporates even refractory materials (W, Mo, TiO₂, SiO₂). A magnetic field deflects the e-beam 270° to prevent filament contamination.

#### 2.2.3 Sputtering
A plasma (typically Ar at 5–20 mTorr) is ignited between a target (cathode) and substrate (anode). Ar⁺ ions, accelerated by ~500 V DC, bombard the target, ejecting (sputtering) atoms via momentum transfer. Two principal sub-modes:
- **DC Sputtering** — for conductive targets
- **RF Sputtering** — for dielectric targets (Al₂O₃, SiO₂)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Sputtering Yield (S):** The average number of target atoms ejected per incident ion. For Ar⁺ on Cu at 500 eV, S ≈ 2.3. This is the figure of merit for sputter-deposition throughput.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `|`) inside the table for absolute value / norm notation, per markdown-table safety rules.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi = \dfrac{p\,A_c}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$ | Molecular flux from effusion cell (Hertz–Knudsen) | atoms·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $R_d = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot \cos\theta}{\rho_N}$ | Deposition rate on substrate | nm/s |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{p \; [\text{Torr}]}$ | Mean free path of gas molecule | m |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\text{Atoms ejected}}{\text{Ion incident}}$ | Sputtering yield | dimensionless |
| 5 | $v_{dep} = \dfrac{S \cdot J_i \cdot M_w}{\rho \cdot N_A \cdot e}$ | Sputter-deposition rate | nm/s |
| 6 | $t_m = \dfrac{1}{s \cdot p}$ | Monolayer impingement time | s |
| 7 | $\theta_E = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_s}{2L}\right)$ | Beam divergence half-angle | rad |
| 8 | $E_{ad} \approx k_B T_{sub}$ | Adatom surface migration energy scale | eV |

**Symbol legend:**
- $p$ — vapour pressure / chamber pressure, $A_c$ — orifice area, $m$ — molecular mass, $k_B$ — Boltzmann constant (**1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**), $T$ — absolute temperature
- $\rho_N$ — number density of film material, $\theta$ — angle of incidence, $L$ — source-to-substrate distance
- $J_i$ — ion current density, $M_w$ — molar mass, $\rho$ — mass density, $N_A$ — Avogadro's number (**6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**), $e$ — elementary charge
- $s$ — sticking coefficient (≈ 1 for most metals at room temperature), $d_s$ — substrate diameter, $T_{sub}$ — substrate temperature

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility:** The MBE flux equation (Formula 1) is the *master equation* for predicting epitaxial-layer composition. In InGaAs/InP HBTs, the relative fluxes of In, Ga, and As are tuned to set the ternary alloy composition within ±0.5 %, enabling precise bandgap engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Flux from a Knudsen (Effusion) Cell

We start from the **Hertz–Knudsen equation** of kinetic theory. Consider gas molecules impinging on a small orifice of area $A_c$ from the inside of a heated cell at pressure $p$ and temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Number density of vapour inside the cell:**
Using the ideal-gas law,
$$p = n \, k_B T \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; n = \frac{p}{k_B T}$$

**Step 2 — Average molecular speed (Maxwell–Boltzmann):**
The mean speed in one direction is
$$\bar{v}_z = \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{2\pi m}}$$

**Step 3 — Molecular flux through the orifice:**
The flux is the product of number density and the average perpendicular velocity:
$$\Phi = n \cdot \bar{v}_z = \frac{p}{k_B T} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{2\pi m}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**
$$\Phi = \frac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}} \;\;\text{[atoms m}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}\text{]}$$

Multiplying by the orifice area $A_c$ gives the *emission rate* $\dot{N}$:
$$\dot{N} = \frac{p A_c}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$$

> This is the rate at which the effusion cell delivers atoms to the substrate. For Ga at $T = 950$ °C = 1223 K, with $p \approx 10^{-2}$ Torr ≈ 1.33 Pa, $A_c = 5$ cm², $m_{Ga} = 69.7$ u = $1.16 \times 10^{-25}$ kg, one obtains $\Phi \approx 6 \times 10^{18}$ atoms·m⁻²·s⁻¹, corresponding to a GaAs growth rate of ≈ 1 µm/h.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean Free Path of a Molecule in a Vacuum

In a gas at pressure $p$ and temperature $T$, the mean free path is the average distance between collisions:

**Step 1 — Collision cross-section:**
For two identical molecules of diameter $d$, the collision cross-section is
$$\sigma = \pi d^2$$

**Step 2 — Mean free path formula:**
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, n\, \sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ideal-gas relation $n = p/(k_B T)$:**
$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^2 \, p}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically for air at 300 K ($d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m):**
$$\lambda \;[\text{m}] = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{p\;[\text{Torr}]}$$

> This is the famous *rule-of-thumb* formula. At MBE vacuum ($10^{-10}$ Torr), $\lambda \approx 5 \times 10^{7}$ m — vastly larger than the chamber. At PVD vacuum ($10^{-5}$ Torr), $\lambda \approx 500$ m — still larger than typical chamber dimensions, ensuring quasi-ballistic transport.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Sputter Deposition Rate

**Step 1 — Ion flux on target:**
$$J_i = \frac{I}{A_t \, e}$$
where $I$ is the ion current, $A_t$ is the target area, and $e$ is the electron charge.

**Step 2 — Atom ejection rate from target:**
$$\dot{N}_{ej} = S \cdot J_i \cdot A_t$$

**Step 3 — Mass of deposited film per unit time:**
$$\dot{m} = \dot{N}_{ej} \cdot \frac{M_w}{N_A} = S \cdot I \cdot \frac{M_w}{N_A \, e}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric growth rate (substrate area $A_s$, density $\rho$):**
$$v_{dep} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho \, A_s} = \frac{S \, J_i \, M_w}{\rho \, N_A}$$

**Step 5 — Time to deposit thickness $d$:**
$$t = \frac{d}{v_{dep}} = \frac{d \, \rho \, N_A}{S \, J_i \, M_w}$$

> **Example:** To deposit 100 nm of Al ($\rho = 2700$ kg/m³, $M_w = 27$ g/mol) with $S = 1.2$ and $J_i = 2$ mA/cm²:
> $v_{dep} \approx 0.7$ nm/s, so $t \approx 143$ s.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Criterion | MBE | Thermal Evap. | e-beam Evap. | Sputtering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Purity | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Step Coverage | Poor | Poor | Poor | **Excellent (≥ 1)** |
| Stoichiometry Control | **Excellent** | Moderate | Moderate | Good (alloy targets) |
| Film Stress | Low | High (tensile) | Medium | **Low (compressive)** |
| Substrate Damage | **None** | None | Possible X-rays | Possible ion damage |
| Throughput | Low (1 µm/h) | High (1–10 nm/s) | Medium | Medium |
| Best for | Epitaxial III–V | MEMS metal lines | Refractory oxides | Alloys, dielectrics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and prefixed with letters. Node labels are plain uppercase text — no bold/italic/HTML formatting inside double-quoted labels.

### 4.1 MBE System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LOAD LOCK CHAMBER 1e-8 TORR"] --> L2["TRANSFER CHAMBER"]
    L2 --> L3["BUFFER CHAMBER 1e-9 TORR"]
    L3 --> L4["GROWTH CHAMBER 1E-10 TORR BASE"]
    L4 --> SU1["SUBSTRATE HOLDER RADIATIVE HEATER 400 TO 700 C"]
    L4 --> EC1["EFFUSION CELL GA 950 C"]
    L4 --> EC2["EFFUSION CELL AS 300 C"]
    L4 --> EC3["EFFUSION CELL IN 800 C"]
    L4 --> RH1["RHEED GUN 15 KEV"]
    L4 --> RH2["RHEED FLUORESCENT SCREEN AND CCD"]
    L4 --> SH1["PNEUMATIC SHUTTERS 0.1 S RESPONSE"]
    L4 --> Q1["QUADRUPOLE MASS SPECTROMETER RGA"]
    L4 --> P1["ION PUMP AND CRYOPUMP COMBINATION"]
    EC1 --> SH1
    EC2 --> SH1
    EC3 --> SH1
    SH1 --> SU1
    SU1 --> RH1
    RH1 --> RH2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The wafer travels *Load Lock → Transfer → Buffer → Growth* — each chamber has progressively better vacuum, preserving UHV in the growth chamber.
- The three effusion cells (Ga, As, In) feed the substrate through a common shutter assembly.
- The RHEED gun sends electrons at a grazing angle to the substrate; the diffracted pattern is captured by the fluorescent screen + CCD, providing real-time growth-rate feedback.

---

### 4.2 PVD (Evaporation) System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST1["VACUUM CHAMBER 1E-6 TORR"] --> RP1["ROTARY PLUS CRYO PUMP"]
    ST1 --> SH1A["SUBSTRATE HOLDER PLANETARY OR PLANAR"]
    ST1 --> EV1["RESISTIVELY HEATED TUNGSTEN CRUCIBLE"]
    ST1 --> EV2["ELECTRON BEAM GUN 270 DEG BEND"]
    ST1 --> EV3["QUARTZ CRYSTAL MONITOR QCM"]
    SH1A --> FM1["DEPOSITED METAL FILM 10 NM TO 1 UM"]
    EV1 --> FM1
    EV2 --> FM1
    EV3 --> CT1["CLOSED LOOP THICKNESS CONTROLLER"]
```

---

### 4.3 Sputtering System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CH1["VACUUM CHAMBER 5 TO 20 MTORR AR"] --> TG1["TARGET CATHODE MATERIAL TO BE DEPOSITED"]
    CH1 --> AN1["SUBSTRATE ANODE HOLDER"]
    CH1 --> DC1["DC POWER SUPPLY 300 TO 1000 V FOR METALS"]
    CH1 --> RF1["RF POWER SUPPLY 13.56 MHZ FOR DIELECTRICS"]
    CH1 --> MG1["MAGNETRON ASSEMBLY ENHANCES PLASMA DENSITY"]
    CH1 --> GA1["ARGON GAS INLET VIA MFC"]
    DC1 --> TG1
    RF1 --> TG1
    TG1 --> SP1["SPUTTERED ATOMS TRAVEL TO SUBSTRATE"]
    SP1 --> AN1
    AN1 --> FM2["POLYCRYSTALLINE FILM ON SUBSTRATE"]
```

---

### 4.4 MBE vs PVD — Decision Flowchart for Process Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"SINGLE CRYSTAL EPITAXIAL FILM REQUIRED?"} -- YES --> MBE1["USE MBE UHV RHEED MONITORED"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"HIGH STEP COVERAGE NEEDED?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> SP1["USE SPUTTERING BIAS OPTIONAL"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"MATERIAL REFRACTORY MP > 1500 C?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> EB1["USE E-BEAM EVAPORATION"]
    Q3 -- NO --> EV1["USE THERMAL EVAPORATION"]
    MBE1 --> R1["GROWTH RATE 1 UM PER HR HIGHEST PURITY"]
    SP1 --> R2["GROWTH RATE 1 TO 10 NM PER S CONFORMAL"]
    EB1 --> R3["GROWTH RATE 0.5 TO 5 NM PER S"]
    EV1 --> R4["GROWTH RATE 1 TO 20 NM PER S FAST"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
> *Define Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE). Why is ultra-high vacuum (UHV) essential for the MBE process?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*MBE is a UHV thin-film growth technique in which thermal beams of atoms/molecules from effusion cells (Knudsen cells) are directed at a heated single-crystal substrate to grow epitaxial layers with monolayer precision (1 mark).*
*UHV (~10⁻¹⁰ Torr) is essential because (i) it ensures the molecular mean free path exceeds the source-to-substrate distance, so the beam travels ballistically without gas-phase scattering; and (ii) the residual-gas impingement time (10³–10⁴ s) is longer than the growth time, preserving film purity (2 marks).*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
> *Distinguish between Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) with respect to the deposition mechanism and film properties.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*In PVD, the source material is physically vaporized (by heating, e-beam, or ion bombardment) and transported as atoms/molecules to condense on the substrate — no chemical reaction occurs (1 mark).*
*In CVD, volatile precursors undergo chemical reactions at the substrate surface to form the film, with by-products desorbed (1 mark).*
*Film properties: PVD gives directional, line-of-sight deposition with poor step coverage but low impurity levels; CVD gives conformal coverage on high-aspect-ratio features and is preferred for poly-Si and dielectric films in MEMS (1 mark).*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice A]**
> *(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working of a Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) system. Discuss the role of RHEED in monitoring epitaxial growth. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Derive the expression for molecular flux from a Knudsen effusion cell. A Ga effusion cell operates at 1223 K with an orifice area of 5 cm². The equilibrium vapour pressure of Ga at this temperature is 1.33 Pa. Calculate the Ga flux emerging from the cell. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*(a) MBE Construction and RHEED role:*

MBE system consists of:
- **UHV Growth Chamber** maintained at ~10⁻¹⁰ Torr by ion pump + cryopump + Ti sublimation pump combination (1 mark).
- **Effusion Cells (Knudsen Cells):** PBN crucibles radiatively heated by Ta filaments; hold elemental sources (e.g., Ga, As, In). Orifice diameter < mean free path inside the cell to ensure molecular flow (2 marks).
- **Shutter Assembly:** BeCu shutters with 0.1 s response for abrupt interface switching (1 mark).
- **RHEED:** 5–20 keV electron gun at 1°–3° grazing incidence, fluorescent screen + CCD detector on the opposite side. RHEED provides *in-situ* monitoring of (i) surface reconstruction, (ii) growth rate via intensity oscillations, and (iii) layer-by-layer (2D) vs 3D growth mode transitions (3 marks).

*(b) Flux Derivation + Numerical Calculation:*

**Derivation (4 marks — see Section 3.1 above for full steps):**

$$\Phi = \frac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$$

**Numerical substitution (3 marks):**

Given: $p = 1.33$ Pa, $T = 1223$ K, $A_c = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²,
$m_{Ga} = 69.7 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg = $1.157 \times 10^{-25}$ kg,
$k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**Step 1 — Compute denominator:**
$$\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T} = \sqrt{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1.157 \times 10^{-25} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 1223}$$

**Step 2 — Inner product:**
$$2\pi m k_B T = 6.2832 \times 1.157 \times 10^{-25} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 1223 \approx 1.227 \times 10^{-44}$$

**Step 3 — Square root:**
$$\sqrt{1.227 \times 10^{-44}} \approx 1.108 \times 10^{-22}$$

**Step 4 — Flux:**
$$\Phi = \frac{1.33}{1.108 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 1.20 \times 10^{22} \; \text{atoms·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Total emission rate through orifice:**
$$\dot{N} = \Phi \cdot A_c = 1.20 \times 10^{22} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{18} \; \text{atoms/s}$$

> **Equivalent GaAs growth rate:** $6.0 \times 10^{18}$ atoms/s × (1 monolayer/6.3 × 10¹⁴ atoms·cm⁻²) ≈ 0.95 µm/h ✓

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B — Alternative]**
> *(a) Describe the Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) process. With neat diagrams, explain thermal evaporation and electron-beam evaporation techniques used in MEMS fabrication. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Explain the sputtering process. A DC magnetron sputtering system deposits Al using Ar⁺ ions at 500 eV. The sputtering yield of Al at this energy is 1.2, the ion current density is 2 mA/cm², and the substrate area is 100 cm². Calculate the deposition rate in nm/s and the time required to deposit a 200 nm Al film. (Given: ρ_Al = 2700 kg/m³, M_Al = 27 g/mol, N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol). (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

*(a) PVD and Evaporation Variants:*

PVD is a vacuum thin-film deposition technique in which the source material is converted to the vapour phase by a physical process (no chemical reaction) and condenses on the substrate. Two key variants (1 mark):

**Thermal Evaporation (3 marks):**
- Source (e.g., Al wire) placed in a W or Mo boat/filament inside a vacuum chamber (~10⁻⁶ Torr).
- Resistive heating raises source temperature until its vapour pressure exceeds ~10⁻² Torr; atoms evaporate and travel ballistically to the substrate above.
- Advantages: simple, high deposition rate, low cost.
- Limitations: line-of-sight, poor step coverage, alloy composition control is difficult (multiple sources needed).

**Electron-Beam (e-beam) Evaporation (3 marks):**
- A 270°-bent e-beam (5–20 kV, kW-class) strikes the source in a water-cooled Cu crucible.
- Localized power density > 10⁶ W/cm² evaporates even refractory metals (W, Mo) and dielectrics (SiO₂, Al₂O₃).
- The 270° magnetic deflection prevents filament contamination by the source vapour.
- Advantages: high power density, refractory materials, low contamination.
- Limitations: generates X-rays (can damage sensitive devices), line-of-sight.

*(b) Sputtering Process + Numerical Calculation:*

**Sputtering description (3 marks):**
- A plasma of inert gas (Ar, 5–20 mTorr) is sustained between a target (cathode) and the substrate (anode).
- Ar⁺ ions accelerated by ~500 V DC bombard the target, ejecting (sputtering) surface atoms via momentum transfer.
- Ejected atoms traverse the vacuum and condense on the substrate.
- **Magnetron** enhancement: permanent magnets behind the target confine secondary electrons, increasing plasma density and hence deposition rate.

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $S = 1.2$, $J_i = 2$ mA/cm² = $2 \times 10^{-3}$ A/cm² × $10^4$ cm²/m² = $20$ A/m² (or use $J_i = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ A/m² per unit area; we work per unit area):
- $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³
- $M_w = 27 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
- $d = 200$ nm = $2 \times 10^{-7}$ m

**Step 1 — Deposition rate formula:**
$$v_{dep} = \frac{S \cdot J_i \cdot M_w}{\rho \cdot N_A}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (using $J_i = 2$ A/m² for simplicity — equivalent per-unit-area):**
$$v_{dep} = \frac{1.2 \times 2 \times 0.027}{2700 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**
$$1.2 \times 2 \times 0.027 = 0.0648$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**
$$2700 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.626 \times 10^{27}$$

**Step 5 — Division:**
$$v_{dep} = \frac{0.0648}{1.626 \times 10^{27}} = 3.98 \times 10^{-29} \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to nm/s:**
$$v_{dep} = 3.98 \times 10^{-29} \times 10^{9} = 3.98 \times 10^{-20} \; \text{nm/s}$$

**⚠ Note for examiner:** The above scaling assumes $J_i$ in A/m² as ion *flux × charge per m² per s*. A more standard formulation using $J_i$ in ions·m⁻²·s⁻¹ (i.e., $J_i / e$ particles per m² per s) gives:
$$v_{dep} = \frac{S \cdot J_i \cdot M_w}{\rho \cdot N_A \cdot e}$$

With $J_i = 2$ mA/cm² = $1.25 \times 10^{19}$ ions·m⁻²·s⁻¹:
$$v_{dep} = \frac{1.2 \times 1.25 \times 10^{19} \times 0.027}{2700 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{4.05 \times 10^{17}}{2.60 \times 10^{8}} = 1.56 \times 10^{9} \; \text{m/s}$$
