> 1. **Formula error:** Many students incorrectly write $E_{cluster} = n \cdot e \cdot V_{acc}$. This is wrong. The correct relation is $E_{cluster} = e \cdot V_{acc}$ (charge is always $+e$ for singly-charged clusters). Energy per atom is $E_{atom} = eV_{acc}/n$.
> 2. **Confusing energy units:** Use eV for atomic-scale problems; convert to Joules only if required. Mixed units cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the "comment" part:** Numerical answer without the engineering interpretation will lose at least 1 mark. Always *discuss* what the number means in the context of the material threshold.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the principle of **Low-Energy Cluster Beam Deposition (LECBD)**. How does it differ from GCIB in terms of energy regime and application? Mention any two applications of LECBD in MEMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between cluster velocity, acceleration voltage, and atomic mass. Show that the cluster velocity is **independent of cluster size** $n$. Calculate the velocity of an Ar cluster ($m = 40$ amu) accelerated through 30 kV. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Principle of LECBD (7 marks):**

**Definition & Principle (3 marks):**
LECBD is a variant of cluster beam technology in which **neutral or weakly-ionized clusters** are deposited onto a substrate with **very low per-atom energy** (typically **0.1 to a few eV per atom**). At such low energies, the cluster does not fragment upon impact — it undergoes **soft-landing**, preserving its original atomic structure on the substrate. This is in contrast to GCIB where high energy per cluster causes fragmentation, cratering, and sputtering.

**Difference from GCIB (2 marks):**

| Aspect | GCIB | LECBD |
|---|---|---|
| Energy per atom | 1–100 eV (mostly above threshold) | 0.1–1 eV (well below threshold) |
| Cluster fragmentation | Yes (impact causes breakup) | No (soft-landing) |
| Process type | Subtractive (etching/smoothing) | Additive (deposition) |
| Cluster state | Ionized | Often neutral |

**Applications in MEMS (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Fabrication of cluster-assembled nanostructured thin films** (e.g., Ag, Au nanocluster films for plasmonic MEMS optical sensors).
2. **Soft-landing of biomolecules** (proteins, DNA, enzymes) on Bio-MEMS chips without loss of bioactivity — enables next-generation lab-on-chip and biosensor platforms.

---

**(b) Derivation of Cluster Velocity (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Total kinetic energy of an $n$-atom cluster of mass $m$ per atom, accelerated from rest through $V_{acc}$:
$$E_{cluster} = q \cdot V_{acc} = e \cdot V_{acc}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Total mass of cluster:
$$M = n \cdot m$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Equate to translational kinetic energy:
$$\frac{1}{2} M v_{cluster}^{2} = e \cdot V_{acc}$$
$$\frac{1}{2} (n m) v_{cluster}^{2} = e \cdot V_{acc}$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Solve for $v_{cluster}$:
$$v_{cluster} = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_{acc}}{n m}}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Note that **$n$ cancels** from numerator (it is not in the expression!) — the velocity depends only on $V_{acc}$ and $m$, **not on cluster size $n$**. This is a unique and elegant feature of cluster ion beams.

**Numerical Calculation:**
For Ar ($m = 40$ amu $= 40 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg $= 6.64 \times 10^{-26}$ kg), $V_{acc} = 30$ kV:
$$v_{cluster} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (3 \times 10^{4})}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}}$$
$$v_{cluster} = \sqrt{\frac{9.6 \times 10^{-15}}{6.64 \times 10^{-26}}} = \sqrt{1.445 \times 10^{11}}$$
$$v_{cluster} \approx 3.8 \times 10^{5}\,\text{m/s} = 380\,\text{km/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for Question B):**
> 1. **Forgetting to cancel $n$:** Many students write $v_{cluster} \propto 1/\sqrt{n}$ — this is wrong because $n$ does not appear in $E_{cluster}$ for singly-charged clusters. The correct result is $v_{cluster}$ is **independent of $n$**.
> 2. **Unit conversion of amu:** Always convert atomic mass units to kg ($1$ amu $= 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg). Forgetting this is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the conceptual conclusion:** Always end the derivation with a clear statement: *"The cluster velocity is independent of n, so all clusters (large or small) accelerated to the same voltage reach the substrate simultaneously."*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Cluster:** Aggregate of 2–10,000 atoms bound by weak forces; size 1–100 nm.
- [x] **GCIB = Gas Cluster Ion Beam** → used for etching, smoothing, shallow implantation.
- [x] **LECBD = Low-Energy Cluster Beam Deposition** → used for soft-landing, nanostructure assembly.
- [x] **Clusters are ALWAYS singly charged** ($+e$), regardless of size — multi-ionization is forbidden by Coulomb explosion.
- [x] **Total cluster energy:** $E_{cluster} = e \cdot V_{acc}$ (independent of $n$).
- [x] **Energy per atom:** $E_{atom} = e \cdot V_{acc} / n$ (this is the *crucial* parameter).
- [x] **Cluster velocity:** $v_{cluster} = \sqrt{2 e V_{acc} / m}$ (independent of $n$).
- [x] **Si displacement threshold:** ~25 eV — keep $E_{atom}$ below this for non-damaging processing.
- [x] **Penetration depth in Si for GCIB:** ~3–10 nm (vs. ~30 nm for equivalent-energy single ions).
- [x] **Lateral sputtering** is the key to **surface smoothing** by GCIB.
- [x] **Mean cluster size** scales as $P_0^{2/3}$ — higher nozzle pressure = larger clusters.
- [x] **Nozzle type:** Converging-diverging (Laval nozzle) for supersonic expansion.
- [x] **Electron impact energy** for ionization: 70–200 eV.
- [x] **Key MEMS applications:** Optical-micromirror polishing, ultra-shallow junctions in CMOS, hard-mask deposition, plasmonic nanostructures, bio-molecule soft-landing.
- [x] **Advantage over conventional ion beam:** Low energy per atom + simultaneous multi-atom impact = shallow, smooth, non-damaging surface processing.
- [x] **Impact duration:** ~$10^{-13}$ seconds — a "nano-explosion" event.
- [x] **Empirical cluster sputtering yield:** $Y(n) \approx n^{1.5} \cdot Y_{mono}$ — increases super-linearly with cluster size.
- [x] **KTU expected answer length:** For a 7-mark sub-question, aim for ~1.5 pages including one diagram; for 14-mark full question, ~3–4 pages.
- [x] **Most common exam pitfall:** Confusing $E_{cluster}$ with $E_{atom}$ — always distinguish between *per cluster* and *per atom* energy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** is a thin-film deposition process in which volatile precursors (typically gaseous reactants) undergo chemical reactions on or near a heated substrate surface to form a solid, non-volatile thin film, with by-products being evacuated from the reaction chamber.

In MEMS and semiconductor fabrication, CVD is the **workhorse process** for depositing conformal, high-quality, and mechanically robust thin films of materials such as polysilicon, silicon dioxide (SiO₂), silicon nitride (Si₃N₄), tungsten, and various dielectrics on a substrate. The reaction is driven thermally, plasma-enhanced, or by photon/laser assistance.

**Standard Classification of CVD Variants (KTU Module 2 syllabus):**

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Operating Pressure | Energy Source |
|--------------|-----------|--------------------|---------------|
| APCVD | Atmospheric Pressure CVD | ~760 Torr | Thermal |
| LPCVD | Low Pressure CVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | Thermal |
| PECVD | Plasma-Enhanced CVD | 0.1 – 10 Torr | RF Plasma + Thermal |
| UHVCVD | Ultra-High Vacuum CVD | < 10⁻⁶ Torr | Thermal |
| MOCVD | Metal-Organic CVD | 10 – 760 Torr | Thermal (uses metal-organics) |
| LCVD | Laser-Induced CVD | Variable | Laser Photon |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Painting with Invisible Chemistry"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you want to paint a wall with a special paint, but instead of using a brush, you spray a *gas* onto the wall. The gas touches the warm wall and *chemically transforms* into a solid, durable coating right there — the "paint" is created only on contact. That's CVD. The wall is the **substrate**, the gas is the **precursor**, the warmth is the **activation energy**, and the resulting solid layer is the **thin film**. The leftover gases (by-products) are vented away, just like fumes from a paint job.

### 1.3 Why CVD Matters in MEMS

CVD is preferred in MEMS over Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) when:
- **Step coverage** must be conformal over high-aspect-ratio structures.
- **Film quality** (low pinhole density, excellent uniformity) is critical.
- Deposition of **dielectrics and doped films** is required.
- **Throughput** and **wafer scalability** are priorities (e.g., batch LPCVD furnaces).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Conformality / Step Coverage** is defined as:
> $$ \text{Step Coverage} = \frac{\text{Minimum Film Thickness (on sidewall/bottom of step)}}{\text{Film Thickness on Top Surface}} \times 100\% $$
> A perfect conformal coating gives a value of **100%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Sequential Steps of CVD

A CVD reaction does NOT happen in one instantaneous step. KTU examiners frequently test this sequential mechanism. The process is broken down into **five transport/reaction stages**:

1. **Bulk Transport of Reactants:** Precursor gases are transported from the inlet through the boundary layer above the substrate via forced/forced-convective flow.
2. **Gas-Phase Reactions:** Precursors may undergo homogeneous (gas-phase) reactions to form intermediate reactive species (radicals, atoms).
3. **Surface Adsorption & Diffusion:** Reactant species adsorb onto the substrate surface and migrate (surface diffusion) to find energetically favorable sites.
4. **Surface Heterogeneous Reaction:** The adsorbed species undergo surface chemical reactions (heterogeneous because the reactant phase differs from the solid product) to form the thin film.
5. **Desorption & Removal of By-Products:** Gaseous by-products (e.g., HCl, H₂) desorb from the surface and diffuse back into the bulk gas stream, exiting through the exhaust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rate-Limiting Step Concept:** The slowest step among the above five determines the overall deposition rate. KTU examiners love to ask: *"What happens to deposition rate if the process is surface-reaction-limited vs. mass-transport-limited?"*

### 2.2 Two Kinetic Regimes of CVD

**A. Surface-Reaction Limited Regime (Low Temperature)**
- The chemical reaction at the surface is the bottleneck.
- Deposition rate follows an **Arrhenius-type** temperature dependence.
- Film quality is excellent (uniform, low defect density).
- Arrhenius expression:
$$ R_d = R_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right) $$
Where $R_d$ = deposition rate, $E_a$ = activation energy, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $T$ = absolute temperature.

**B. Mass-Transport Limited Regime (High Temperature)**
- The diffusion of reactants through the boundary layer limits the rate.
- Deposition rate becomes **weakly dependent** on temperature (almost flat).
- Film may be less uniform, with possible gas-phase particle incorporation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** The *transition temperature* between these two regimes defines the operating window. For high-quality films, MEMS engineers deliberately operate in the **surface-reaction limited regime** (lower temperature side of the curve).

### 2.3 Standard CVD Reaction Examples (KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlights)

| Film Deposited | Precursor Gases | Reaction | Temperature |
|----------------|-----------------|----------|-------------|
| Polysilicon | SiH₄ (Silane) | SiH₄ → Si + 2 H₂ | 580–650 °C (LPCVD) |
| SiO₂ (LTO) | SiH₂Cl₂ + N₂O | SiH₂Cl₂ + N₂O → SiO₂ + 2 HCl + N₂ | 850 °C (LPCVD) |
| Si₃N₄ | SiH₂Cl₂ + NH₃ | 3 SiH₂Cl₂ + 4 NH₃ → Si₃N₄ + 6 HCl + 6 H₂ | 700–800 °C (LPCVD) |
| SiO₂ (PECVD) | SiH₄ + N₂O | SiH₄ + N₂O → SiO₂ + 2 N₂ + 2 H₂ | 250–400 °C |
| Tungsten (W) | WF₆ + H₂ | WF₆ + 3 H₂ → W + 6 HF | 300–450 °C |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|------|
| $R_d$ | Deposition Rate | $\frac{\text{Film Thickness}}{\text{Deposition Time}}$ | nm/min or Å/min |
| $E_a$ | Activation Energy | Slope of $\ln R_d$ vs. $1/T$ plot | eV or J/mol |
| $D_g$ | Gas-Phase Diffusivity | $D_g \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{P}$ | cm²/s |
| $\delta$ | Boundary Layer Thickness | $\delta \propto \sqrt{\frac{\mu x}{\rho u}}$ | cm |
| $N_i$ | Mass Flux of Species $i$ | $N_i = -D_g \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} + v C_i$ | mol/cm²·s |
| $k_s$ | Surface Reaction Rate Constant | $k_s = A \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ | cm/s |
| $\alpha$ | Sticking Coefficient | Fraction of incident molecules that react | dimensionless (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann Constant | $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$ | J/K |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | $\mathbf{8.314}$ | J/(mol·K) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Notation Pitfall:** Do not confuse the *gas constant* $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with the *deposition rate* $R_d$. Use clear subscripts in your derivations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of CVD

- **CMOS/MEMS integration:** Deposition of inter-layer dielectrics (ILD), gate oxides, and passivation layers.
- **MEMS structural layers:** Polysilicon for mechanical beams, membranes, and proof masses.
- **Anti-stiction coatings:** Vapor-deposited self-assembled monolayers and fluorocarbon films.
- **Solar cells:** Amorphous and polycrystalline silicon thin-film photovoltaics.
- **Optical coatings:** Anti-reflection and high-reflectivity multilayer stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deposition Rate from Arrhenius Kinetics

We now derive the explicit form of the temperature-dependent deposition rate in the **surface-reaction limited regime**. This derivation is a KTU-favorite and frequently carries 5–7 marks in Part B.

**Step 1: Start with the rate equation.** In the surface-reaction limited regime, the deposition rate $R_d$ is proportional to the rate constant $k_s$ of the slowest surface reaction:
$$ R_d = C \cdot k_s $$
where $C$ is a constant involving the reactant surface concentration (assumed constant in the kinetically-limited regime).

**Step 2: Apply the Arrhenius equation.** The surface reaction rate constant follows Arrhenius behavior:
$$ k_s = A \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$

Here, $A$ is the pre-exponential (frequency) factor, $E_a$ is the activation energy in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Step 3: Combine the two equations:**
$$ R_d = C \cdot A \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$

**Step 4: Redefine the pre-factor.** Let $R_0 = C \cdot A$, giving the compact canonical form:
$$ R_d = R_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$

**Step 5: Linearize for experimental extraction of $E_a$.** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$ \ln(R_d) = \ln(R_0) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

This is a linear equation of the form $y = m x + b$, where $y = \ln(R_d)$, $x = 1/T$, slope $m = -E_a/R$, and intercept $b = \ln(R_0)$.

**Step 6: Practical extraction formula:**
$$ E_a = -m \cdot R = -R \cdot \frac{\Delta \ln(R_d)}{\Delta (1/T)} $$

A plot of $\ln(R_d)$ versus $1/T$ therefore gives a straight line whose slope yields the activation energy.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A CVD polysilicon process produces a film of **$5000 \, \text{Å}$** in **30 minutes** at a temperature of **$620 \, \text{°C}$**. The same process at **$580 \, \text{°C}$** produces **$2000 \, \text{Å}$** in **30 minutes**. Calculate the apparent activation energy $E_a$ in eV.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute deposition rates.**
$$ R_1 = \frac{5000 \, \text{Å}}{30 \, \text{min}} = 166.67 \, \text{Å/min} $$
$$ R_2 = \frac{2000 \, \text{Å}}{30 \, \text{min}} = 66.67 \, \text{Å/min} $$

**Step 2: Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**
$$ T_1 = 620 + 273 = 893 \, \text{K} $$
$$ T_2 = 580 + 273 = 853 \, \text{K} $$

**Step 3: Apply the two-point Arrhenius form.**
$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$

**Step 4: Evaluate the left-hand side.**
$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{166.67}{66.67}\right) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163 $$

**Step 5: Evaluate the temperature bracket.**
$$ \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{853} - \frac{1}{893} $$
$$ = \frac{893 - 853}{853 \times 893} = \frac{40}{761{,}729} = 5.251 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 6: Solve for $E_a$.**
$$ E_a = \frac{0.9163 \times 8.314}{5.251 \times 10^{-5}} \, \text{J/mol} $$
$$ E_a = \frac{7.618}{5.251 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.4507 \times 10^{5} \, \text{J/mol} $$

**Step 7: Convert to eV.** (Using $1 \, \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$ and $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$)
$$ E_a = \frac{1.4507 \times 10^{5}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} $$
$$ E_a = \frac{1.4507 \times 10^{5}}{96{,}485} \approx 1.504 \, \text{eV} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** $E_a \approx \mathbf{1.5 \, \text{eV}}$ — this is consistent with literature values for polysilicon LPCVD from silane (typically 1.4–1.6 eV), confirming the surface-reaction limited regime.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing CVD Sub-Variants (High-Yield Comparison Table)

| Property | APCVD | LPCVD | PECVD |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|
| Operating Pressure | 760 Torr | 0.1–10 Torr | 0.1–10 Torr |
| Operating Temperature | 400–800 °C | 550–900 °C | 200–400 °C |
| Film Conformality | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Throughput | High (batch limited) | Very High | Medium |
| Equipment Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Typical Films | SiO₂ (TEOS) | Poly-Si, Si₃N₄, LTO | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ (low-T) |
| Step Coverage | < 70% | > 90% | ~80% |
| Pinhole Density | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Particle Defects | High | Low | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CVD Reactor Block Architecture (Hot-Wall LPCVD Furnace)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Gas Inlet SiH4] --> MFC1
    G2[Gas Inlet N2O] --> MFC2
    G3[Carrier Gas N2] --> MFC3
    MFC1 --> MIX[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    MFC2 --> MIX
    MFC3 --> MIX
    MIX --> QZ[Quartz Reaction Chamber]
    HTR[Resistance Heater Zone] --> QZ
    QZ --> SUS[Susceptor With Wafer Boat]
    SUS --> DEPO[Surface Reaction - Film Growth]
    DEPO --> BYP[By Product Gases H2 HCl]
    BYP --> EXH[Exhaust and Scrubber System]
    PUMP[Vacuum Pump] --> QZ
    TC1[Temperature Controller] --> HTR
    PC1[Pressure Controller] --> QZ
```

### 4.2 Five-Step CVD Process Flow (Mermaid Functional Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1 - Bulk Gas Transport] --> A2[Step 2 - Gas Phase Reaction]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 - Surface Adsorption and Diffusion]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 - Surface Heterogeneous Reaction]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 - By Product Desorption and Removal]
    A5 --> A6[Thin Film Deposited on Substrate]
    styleA1_Style:::processStyle
    classDef processStyle fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
```

### 4.3 CVD Sub-Variant Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start - Select CVD Variant] --> Q1{High Temperature Budget Available?}
    Q1 -- Yes and Needs Best Conformality --> LPC[LPCVD]
    Q1 -- No - Sub 450C Required --> PEC[PECVD]
    START --> Q2{Need Lowest Cost and Atmospheric Operation?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> APC[APCVD]
    Q2 -- No --> Q1
    LPC --> OUT1[Poly Si - Si3N4 - LTO Films]
    PEC --> OUT2[Low T Dielectrics for BEOL]
    APC --> OUT3[TEOS SiO2 and Sacrificial Layers]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Boundary Layer and Mass Transport

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BL[Boundary Layer Region]
    direction TB
    GS[Main Gas Stream with Precursor] --> BL_REGION[Diffusion Layer delta over wafer]
    BL_REGION --> SUB[Heated Wafer Substrate]
    SUB --> FILM[Thin Film Nucleation Zone]
    FILM --> NUCL[Surface Reaction Sites]
    NUCL --> BD[By Product Desorption]
    BD --> BL_REGION
    end
    style BL fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define Chemical Vapour Deposition. List any three advantages of CVD over Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) in MEMS fabrication.*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines, 3 marks):**

> **Definition:** CVD is a thin-film deposition technique in which volatile chemical precursors react (thermally, plasma-, or photon-activated) on a heated substrate to form a solid, non-volatile film, with gaseous by-products being removed from the chamber.
>
> **Three Advantages of CVD over PVD:**
> 1. **Superior step coverage** — CVD produces conformal coatings even over high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures, whereas PVD (e.g., evaporation, sputtering) gives poor sidewall coverage.
> 2. **Higher film quality** — CVD films exhibit lower pinhole density, better uniformity, and stronger adhesion.
> 3. **Material versatility** — CVD can deposit a wide range of materials (poly-Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, W, TiN) using suitable precursor chemistries, while PVD is more limited.
>
> *(Bonus point: CVD has higher throughput in batch furnace configuration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Distinguish between APCVD, LPCVD, and PECVD in terms of operating pressure, temperature, and typical application.*

**Model Answer (concise 3-row table + brief explanation, 3 marks):**

> | Feature | APCVD | LPCVD | PECVD |
> |---------|-------|-------|-------|
> | Pressure | 760 Torr | 0.1–10 Torr | 0.1–10 Torr |
> | Temperature | 400–800 °C | 550–900 °C | 200–400 °C |
> | Application | TEOS-based SiO₂, sacrificial layers | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, LTO dielectrics | Low-temperature BEOL dielectrics, passivation |
>
> **Explanation:** APCVD operates at atmospheric pressure using thermal activation. LPCVD operates at low pressure for superior film uniformity and conformality, widely used for MEMS structural polysilicon. PECVD uses RF plasma to enable deposition at much lower temperatures, suitable for metallized or temperature-sensitive substrates.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model QP Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **five sequential steps** involved in a typical CVD process with neat block-level schematics. Discuss the concept of the **rate-limiting step** and its implications on film quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Arrhenius-type deposition rate equation** for a surface-reaction limited CVD process. A polysilicon LPCVD run produces a film thickness of **$4000 \, \text{Å}$** in 25 minutes at $900 \, \text{K}$, and **$1500 \, \text{Å}$** in 25 minutes at $860 \, \text{K}$. Calculate the **activation energy in eV**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Sequential Steps (7 marks):**

> 1. **Bulk Gas Transport (1 mark):** Precursor gases enter the reaction chamber via mass flow controllers and are transported by forced convection through the main gas stream to the vicinity of the heated substrate.
> 2. **Gas-Phase Reactions (1 mark):** Some precursors (especially at high temperature) decompose homogeneously in the gas phase, forming intermediate reactive species such as radicals and atoms.
> 3. **Surface Adsorption and Diffusion (1 mark):** Reactant species adsorb onto the substrate surface and undergo surface diffusion to find energetically favorable nucleation sites (e.g., dangling bonds, defects).
> 4. **Surface Heterogeneous Reaction (2 marks):** The adsorbed species undergo surface chemical reactions (heterogeneous, since one phase is solid, one is gas) producing the desired solid thin film. The film nucleates and grows laterally to coalesce into a continuous layer.
> 5. **By-Product Desorption and Removal (1 mark):** Gaseous reaction by-products (HCl, H₂, CO₂, etc.) desorb from the surface, diffuse back into the bulk gas stream, and exit through the exhaust scrubber.
>
> **Rate-Limiting Step (1 mark):** The slowest of these five steps governs the overall deposition rate. At low temperatures, the surface reaction is rate-limiting (kinetically controlled regime). At high temperatures, mass transport becomes rate-limiting (diffusion-controlled regime). MEMS engineers prefer the kinetically limited regime because it produces uniform, high-quality films with predictable thickness.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
> Starting from the rate expression $R_d = C \cdot k_s$ and Arrhenius $k_s = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$:
> $$ R_d = R_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$
> Taking the natural log:
> $$ \ln(R_d) = \ln(R_0) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$
> A plot of $\ln(R_d)$ vs. $1/T$ is linear with slope $-E_a/R$, allowing experimental extraction of activation energy.

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**
> $R_1 = 4000/25 = 160$ Å/min at $T_1 = 900$ K
> $R_2 = 1500/25 = 60$ Å/min at $T_2 = 860$ K
> $$ \ln(160/60) = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{860} - \frac{1}{900}\right) $$
> $$ \ln(2.667) = 0.981 $$
> $$ \frac{1}{860} - \frac{1}{900} = \frac{40}{860 \times 900} = 5.168 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$
> $$ E_a = \frac{0.981 \times 8.314}{5.168 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.578 \times 10^{5} \, \text{J/mol} $$
> $$ E_a = \frac{1.578 \times 10^{5}}{96{,}485} \approx 1.64 \, \text{eV} $$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the construction and working of a **LPCVD reactor**. Compare **LPCVD with PECVD** in terms of temperature, film quality, conformality, and application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **why step coverage is critical in MEMS**. A trench of **depth 5 μm** is to be filled with polysilicon. The deposition rate at the **top surface is 100 Å/min**, while at the **bottom of the trench it is 80 Å/min**. Calculate the **step coverage** and comment on the process quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LPCVD Reactor and Comparison (7 marks):**

> **LPCVD Construction (4 marks):** A typical LPCVD system consists of:
> - **Quartz tube reactor** capable of withstanding high temperatures (up to 900 °C) and corrosive precursors.
> - **Three-zone resistance heater** surrounding the tube for precise axial temperature control (±1 °C).
> - **Wafer boat** holding 100–200 vertically-oriented wafers in a slotted quartz boat for high throughput.
> - **Vacuum pump** maintaining 0.1–10 Torr pressure.
> - **Mass flow controllers** for accurate precursor and carrier gas metering.
> - **Pressure control system** and **temperature profiling unit** for closed-loop process control.
>
> **Working:** Precursor gases (e.g., SiH₄ for polysilicon) enter at one end, flow over the heated wafers, decompose heterogeneously on the wafer surfaces, and the by-products are pumped out at the other end.
>
> **Comparison (3 marks):**
> | Parameter | LPCVD | PECVD |
> |-----------|-------|-------|
> | Temperature | 550–900 °C (thermal) | 200–400 °C (plasma-assisted) |
> | Conformality | Excellent (>90%) | Good (~80%) |
> | Application | MEMS structural poly-Si, furnace anneal oxides | Low-T passivation, inter-layer dielectric over metal |

**(b) Step Coverage Calculation (7 marks):**

> **Importance of Step Coverage (3 marks):** MEMS devices contain deep trenches, vias, and high-aspect-ratio microstructures. Inadequate step coverage causes **voids, keyholes, or thinning at sidewalls/bottoms**, leading to mechanical failure, electrical shorts/opens, and reduced device reliability. CVD's ability to coat sidewalls and bottoms uniformly is a decisive advantage over PVD.
>
> **Numerical Solution (4 marks):**
> $$ \text{Step Coverage} = \frac{\text{Bottom Thickness}}{\text{Top Thickness}} \times 100\% $$
> $$ = \frac{80}{100} \times 100\% = 80\% $$
>
> **Comment (1 mark):** An 80% step coverage is acceptable for shallow to moderate aspect-ratio MEMS features (depth 5 μm in this case), but for very high aspect ratios (>10:1), a more conformal process (e.g., LPCVD) yielding >95% step coverage is recommended. The current process is **acceptable but not optimal** for high-density MEMS integration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — CVD Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CVD with PVD:** Many students mix up evaporation/sputtering (PVD) with CVD. Clearly state the **chemical reaction** at the substrate in CVD; PVD has no chemistry, only physical transfer.
> 2. **Skipping the Five Steps:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for **each** of the five steps. Writing only 3 will cost you 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit Errors in $E_a$:** Activation energy is often required in **eV** for semiconductor problems, not J/mol. Convert using $1 \text{ eV} = 96{,}485$ J/mol (i.e., divide by Avogadro's number × 1.602×10⁻¹⁹).
> 4. **Forgetting the Linearized Arrhenius Form:** For plot-based questions, the form $\ln(R_d)$ vs. $1/T$ must be explicitly stated, not just the exponential form.
> 5. **Skipping the Boundary Layer Discussion:** Mass-transport limitation in CVD arises from the **boundary layer**. Don't say "diffusion" without specifying "across the boundary layer."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CVD definition:** Volatile precursors → chemical reaction on heated substrate → solid thin film.
- [x] **Five sequential steps:** Bulk transport → Gas-phase reaction → Surface adsorption/diffusion → Surface reaction → By-product desorption.
- [x] **Two kinetic regimes:** Surface-reaction limited (Arrhenius, low T) vs. Mass-transport limited (weak T-dependence, high T).
- [x] **Arrhenius equation:** $R_d = R_0 \exp(-E_a / RT)$; plot $\ln(R_d)$ vs. $1/T$ for $E_a$ extraction.
- [x] **Step coverage formula:** $\text{Coverage} = (\text{Bottom}/\text{Top}) \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Pressure regimes:** APCVD (760 Torr), LPCVD (0.1–10 Torr), PECVD (0.1–10 Torr + plasma).
- [x] **Temperature regimes:** APCVD 400–800 °C, LPCVD 550–900 °C, PECVD 200–400 °C.
- [x] **Standard reactions:** SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂ (polysilicon); SiH₂Cl₂ + N₂O → SiO₂ + byproducts.
- [x] **LPCVD advantages:** Best step coverage, low defects, high throughput, batch processing.
- [x] **PECVD advantages:** Low temperature — enables deposition on temperature-sensitive substrates (post-metallization, polymers).
- [x] **Boundary layer thickness:** $\delta \propto \sqrt{\mu x / (\rho u)}$ — depends on gas viscosity, position, density, and flow velocity.
- [x] **Activation energy for polysilicon from silane:** typically 1.4–1.6 eV (a great sanity check for numerical answers).
- [x] **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [x] **Sticking coefficient α:** Probability (0 to 1) that an incident molecule reacts at the surface; critical for low-pressure regimes.
- [x] **MEMS relevance:** Polysilicon (LPCVD) for mechanical structures; SiO₂/Si₃N₄ (LPCVD/PECVD) for electrical isolation and passivation; sacrificial SiO₂ (APCVD/thermal) for surface micromachining release.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Step Coverage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Step Coverage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Step Coverage** is a critical figure-of-merit in MEMS and IC fabrication that quantitatively measures the ability of a thin-film deposition process to uniformly coat a non-planar (stepped) substrate topology, such as trenches, vias, contacts, and sidewalls, with a conformal layer of the depositing material.

Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the minimum film thickness deposited on the step sidewall or bottom-corner region to the nominal film thickness on a planar (flat) reference surface far from the step edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Step coverage is a **mandatory topic** in Module 2 under thin-film deposition processes. It is directly linked to Course Outcome **CO2** (Understand micro-fabrication processes) and frequently appears in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **spray-painting a picket fence with narrow gaps** between the slats:

- **Scenario A — High Step Coverage (Conformal):** You use a high-pressure, low-viscosity paint that flows into every crevice, coats the sidewalls, the bottom of the gaps, and the back of the slats. The coating thickness is nearly the same everywhere — this is **conformal coverage**, ideal in MEMS.

- **Scenario B — Poor Step Coverage (Non-Conformal):** You use a directional, line-of-sight spray. The paint particles travel in straight lines. The sidewalls of the gaps receive little paint, and a "shadow" region forms at the bottom corners. This creates **poor step coverage** and is a real engineering problem in Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD).

- **Scenario C — Keyhole/Void Formation:** If the top corners get coated faster than the bottom (because deposition closes the opening before filling the bottom), a **void or keyhole** forms inside the trench. This is catastrophic for device reliability and is a key failure mechanism the engineer must avoid.

### 1.3 The Two Geometrically Distinct Regions of a Step

A substrate step consists of three regions of interest for the engineer:

| Region | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|
| **Top Horizontal Surface** | Reference (nominal) thickness $t_0$ |
| **Sidewall** | Must be coated without pinholes; thickness $t_s$ |
| **Bottom Corner (Foot)** | Worst-case region; thickness $t_b$ or $t_f$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics in Bold:**
> - **Step Coverage Ratio (S):** dimensionless, between 0 and 1 (or 0% to 100%)
> - **Aspect Ratio (AR):** depth-to-width ratio of the feature, dimensionless
> - **Nominal Thickness ($t_0$):** thickness on flat reference area
> - **Conformality:** ability to follow the contour, characteristic of CVD processes

### 1.4 Types of Step Coverage — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to identify and distinguish between the **three classical types of step coverage** in deposition:

1. **Conformal Coverage** — Film thickness on the sidewall equals the film thickness on the planar top surface ($t_s \approx t_0$). Achieved typically by **LPCVD** (Low-Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition) and **PECVD**.
2. **Non-Conformal / Directional Coverage** — Film thickness on the sidewall is much less than the planar thickness ($t_s \ll t_0$). Characteristic of **evaporation** (line-of-sight process).
3. **Step-Coverage with Tapered Profile** — The film thins gradually as it goes over the edge. Common in **sputtering** at moderate pressures (some gas scattering helps).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Step Coverage Varies

The step coverage behavior of any deposition process is governed by **how the depositing species (atoms, molecules, or ions) arrive at the substrate surface**. This arrival is described by the concept of **flux**.

There are two fundamental flux components at a stepped surface:

- **Direct (Line-of-Sight) Flux:** Species arriving in straight lines from the source. These particles **cannot see the bottom of a high-aspect-ratio trench** — they are blocked by the step edge. This flux dominates in **vacuum evaporation**.
- **Indirect (Diffuse) Flux:** Species that have undergone many gas-phase collisions before reaching the substrate. After collisions, their trajectories are randomized, so they arrive from **all directions** and can coat sidewalls and trench bottoms uniformly. This flux dominates in **high-pressure CVD** and **LPCVD**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean Free Path ($\lambda$):** the average distance a gas molecule travels between collisions. When $\lambda$ is much larger than the feature dimensions, the process is **line-of-sight** (poor step coverage). When $\lambda$ is much smaller than the feature dimensions, the process is **collision-dominated** (good step coverage).

### 2.2 Detailed Logic Flow for Step Coverage Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify the deposition process** (Evaporation, Sputtering, CVD, LPCVD, PECVD, etc.)

**Step 2 — Compare the mean free path $\lambda$ to the feature size $d$**
- If $\lambda \gg d$: line-of-sight regime, expect **non-conformal** coverage
- If $\lambda \ll d$: diffusive regime, expect **conformal** coverage

**Step 3 — Compute the aspect ratio of the feature**
- High AR ($\geq$ 2:1) features are very difficult to coat conformally by PVD
- High AR features demand **CVD-class processes**

**Step 4 — Determine the limiting mechanism**
- **Surface-reaction limited (CVD at high T):** the species diffuses to the surface, so the sidewalls and bottom get coated equally. **Conformal.**
- **Mass-transport limited (CVD at high pressure / high flow):** the surface reaction is fast, so coating is governed by flux arrival, which depends on geometry. **Non-conformal.**

**Step 5 — Quantify the step coverage ratio**

$$S = \frac{\text{minimum film thickness on the step (sidewall or bottom)}}{\text{nominal film thickness on the flat surface}}$$

**Step 6 — Validate against specifications**
- For inter-metal dielectric (IMD) and passivation layers, $S \geq 0.5$ is typically required
- For barrier layers in Cu damascene, $S \geq 0.8$ is required

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for Step Coverage. All quantities are dimensionless or in SI-compatible units. **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside table cells** (they are replaced with `\vert` for absolute-value and conditional separators).

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning | Typical Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_0}$ | Step coverage ratio; $t_{min}$ = minimum thickness on step, $t_0$ = nominal flat thickness | $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 2 | $AR = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | Aspect ratio; $d$ = depth of feature, $w$ = width of feature | MEMS: 0.5 to 10 |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, d_m^{2}\, p}$ | Mean free path; $k_B$ = Boltzmann const, $T$ = temperature, $d_m$ = molecular diameter, $p$ = pressure | At 1 mTorr, $\lambda \approx$ 50 mm |
| 4 | $\text{Kn} = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Knudsen number; $L$ = characteristic length of feature | Kn $\gg$ 1: molecular flow; Kn $\ll$ 1: viscous flow |
| 5 | $t_s \;=\; t_0 \cdot \cos\theta$ | Directional (line-of-sight) coverage on a sidewall inclined at angle $\theta$ from the source direction | $\theta = 90°$ gives $t_s = 0$ |
| 6 | $S_{CVD} \;\approx\; 1$ (ideal, surface-reaction limited) | Conformal coverage in CVD regime | $S$ approaches 1 as temperature increases |
| 7 | $\text{Rate}_{\text{PVD}} \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}$ | Deposition rate in evaporation; $r$ = source-to-substrate distance | Inverse square law |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

Step coverage is not just an academic metric — it is the **gatekeeper of device yield and reliability** in semiconductor manufacturing:

- **Barrier Layers (Ti/TiN, Ta/TaN):** in copper damascene interconnects, a single pinhole in the barrier causes Cu diffusion into silicon, killing the transistor. Requires $S \geq 0.8$.
- **Dielectric Fill (SiO₂, Si₃N₄):** in shallow-trench isolation (STI) and inter-metal dielectrics (IMD), voids in the dielectric cause stress-induced cracking.
- **MEMS Structures:** in deep reactive-ion-etched (DRIE) structures, conformal coatings are needed for electrode passivation, anti-stiction layers, and hermetic sealing.
- **Solar Cells and Displays:** large-area flexible electronics depend critically on conformal deposition over textured surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Process Mapping:**
> - **Best conformal coverage:** LPCVD, ALD (Atomic Layer Deposition) — $S$ can approach 1.0 even at AR = 30:1
> - **Moderate coverage:** PECVD — $S \approx 0.7$ to 0.9
> - **Poor coverage:** Evaporation, ion-beam deposition — $S$ can drop to 0.1 or lower at high AR
> - **Sputtering:** pressure-dependent — high pressure (5–30 mTorr) gives better coverage than low pressure (< 1 mTorr) due to gas scattering

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Coverage Ratio for a Directional (Line-of-Sight) Process

Consider a step with vertical sidewall of height $d$ and a planar top surface. A source emits depositing species isotropically. A point on the sidewall at depth $y$ below the top edge receives flux from a restricted solid angle subtended at the source.

For a **purely directional source** (collimated beam arriving at angle $\alpha$ from the substrate normal), the flux on a planar surface tilted by angle $\theta$ from the beam direction is given by **Lambert's Cosine Law**:

$$F(\theta) = F_0 \, \cos\theta$$

where $F_0$ is the flux on a surface perpendicular to the beam.

For a vertical sidewall, the angle between the beam normal (vertical) and the sidewall normal (horizontal) is $\theta = 90°$ :

$$F_{\text{sidewall}} = F_0 \cos(90°) = 0$$

This means a **perfectly directional beam cannot coat a vertical sidewall at all**. This is the geometric origin of poor step coverage in evaporation.

**Real beams are not perfectly collimated.** They have an angular spread. Let the source subtend a half-angle $\phi$ at the sidewall point. Then the flux on the sidewall is:

$$F_s = F_0 \int_{0}^{\phi} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta = \frac{F_0}{2} \sin^{2}\phi$$

The flux on the top surface is simply:

$$F_t = F_0$$

Therefore, the **step coverage ratio** becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{directional}} &= \frac{F_s}{F_t} = \frac{F_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \sin^{2}\phi}{F_0} \\
S_{\text{directional}} &= \frac{1}{2} \sin^{2}\phi
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** As the source half-angle $\phi$ increases (i.e., the source is closer to the substrate or larger in size), the step coverage improves. This is why **planetary rotation and large-area sources** are used in production evaporators.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Step Coverage in a Collision-Dominated (Diffusive) Regime

When $\lambda \ll L$ (mean free path much smaller than feature size), the depositing species reach the surface via **diffusion** through a stagnant boundary layer. The flux to any point on the surface is governed by **Fick's First Law**:

$$J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial n}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient, $C$ is the concentration of the precursor, and $n$ is the surface normal direction.

For a CVD process in the **surface-reaction limited regime**, the deposition rate at any point is:

$$R = k_s \, C_s$$

where $k_s$ is the surface reaction rate constant and $C_s$ is the precursor concentration at the surface. Since diffusion is fast, $C_s$ is approximately uniform everywhere on the surface (the boundary layer is thin), so:

$$R \approx \text{constant} \; \Rightarrow \; t_{\text{sidewall}} \approx t_{\text{bottom}} \approx t_{\text{top}}$$

Therefore:

$$S_{\text{surface-limited CVD}} = \frac{t_{\text{sidewall}}}{t_{\text{top}}} \approx 1$$

This is the **theoretical ideal** of conformal coverage, and is the reason LPCVD is the workhorse of high-aspect-ratio MEMS and IC fabrication.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Aspect Ratio on Bottom Coverage (Knudsen Diffusion Model)

For a trench of width $w$ and depth $d$, the **Knudsen number** is:

$$\text{Kn} = \frac{\lambda}{w}$$

When Kn $\gg$ 1 (low pressure, narrow trench), the mean free path exceeds the trench width, and the molecules effectively travel ballistically. The bottom of the trench sees a **restricted solid angle** from the gas phase. The flux to the trench bottom is reduced by a geometric factor:

$$F_{\text{bottom}} = F_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot (d/w)}$$

where $\alpha$ is a process-dependent sticking/reaction coefficient (typically between 0.5 and 2 for CVD).

The **step coverage at the trench bottom** is therefore:

$$S_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{F_{\text{bottom}}}{F_0} = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot AR}$$

**Numerical Example:** For AR = 4, $\alpha = 1$:

$$S_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1 \cdot 4} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

This is a **catastrophically poor** step coverage, which is why **high-AR features cannot be coated by low-pressure PVD** and require **CVD-class processes**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Deposition Processes

| Process | Pressure Regime | $\lambda$ vs Feature Size | Mechanism | Step Coverage $S$ | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Evaporation** | $< 10^{-5}$ Torr | $\lambda \gg L$ | Pure line-of-sight | 0.0 to 0.3 (poor) | Lift-off, contacts |
| **E-beam Evaporation** | $< 10^{-6}$ Torr | $\lambda \gg L$ | Line-of-sight, collimated | 0.0 to 0.2 (poor) | Thin film resistors |
| **Sputtering (low P)** | 1 to 5 mTorr | $\lambda \sim L$ | Mostly directional | 0.2 to 0.5 | Metallization |
| **Sputtering (high P)** | 10 to 30 mTorr | $\lambda < L$ | Gas scattering, quasi-diffuse | 0.4 to 0.7 | Barrier layers |
| **PECVD** | 0.1 to 5 Torr | $\lambda \ll L$ | Reactive + plasma-enhanced | 0.7 to 0.9 | IMD, passivation |
| **LPCVD** | 0.1 to 1 Torr | $\lambda \ll L$ | Surface-reaction limited | 0.9 to 1.0 | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, SiO₂ |
| **ALD (Atomic Layer Depo.)** | 0.1 to 10 Torr | Surface-saturated | Self-limiting chemistry | 0.95 to 1.0 (excellent) | High-AR barriers (AR 30:1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU exams:** As you move from vacuum evaporation $\to$ sputtering $\to$ PECVD $\to$ LPCVD $\to$ ALD, the **mean free path decreases** and the **diffusive component increases**, so step coverage monotonically improves. This single trend is a high-yield exam answer.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A trench of width $w = 1 \,\mu m$ and depth $d = 3 \,\mu m$ is to be coated with TiN using a sputtering process at pressure 3 mTorr. The sticking coefficient is $\alpha = 0.8$. Compute the step coverage at the trench bottom and determine if the process is acceptable for a barrier layer (specification: $S \geq 0.5$).

**Step 1 — Compute the aspect ratio:**

$$AR = \frac{d}{w} = \frac{3 \,\mu m}{1 \,\mu m} = 3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Knudsen diffusion model formula:**

$$S_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot AR} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.8 \cdot 3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$0.8 \cdot 3 = 2.4 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + 2.4 = 3.4$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$S_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{1}{3.4} \approx 0.294$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage and compare:**

$$S_{\text{bottom}} \approx 29.4\%$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

$$29.4\% < 50\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Process FAILS the barrier-layer specification.}$$

**Recommendation:** Switch to a more conformal process such as **LPCVD-TiN** or **ALD-TiN**, or use a **collimated sputtering** source, or **ionize the sputtered flux** (I-PVD) to improve direction control.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Step Coverage Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deposition Process Initiated] --> B{Mean Free Path lambda vs Feature Size L}
    B -- lambda much greater than L --> C[Line-of-Sight Regime]
    B -- lambda similar to L --> D[Transitional Regime]
    B -- lambda much less than L --> E[Diffusive Regime]
    C --> C1[Evaporation]
    C --> C2[Low-Pressure Sputtering]
    C1 --> C3[Poor Step Coverage S less than 0.3]
    C2 --> C3
    D --> D1[High-Pressure Sputtering]
    D --> D2[PECVD at Moderate Pressure]
    D1 --> D3[Moderate Step Coverage S between 0.4 and 0.7]
    D2 --> D3
    E --> E1[LPCVD]
    E --> E2[ALD]
    E --> E3[High-Density PECVD]
    E1 --> E4[Excellent Step Coverage S greater than 0.9]
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E4
    C3 --> F1[Risk: Sidewall Pinholes, Keyhole Voids]
    D3 --> F2[Acceptable for Low AR Features]
    E4 --> F3[Ideal for High AR MEMS and IC]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Modes in Poor Step Coverage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Thin Film Deposition on Stepped Surface] --> B{Trajectory of Depositing Species}
    B -- Directional arrival --> C[Sidewall Receives Reduced Flux]
    B -- Diffusive arrival --> D[Sidewall Receives Equal Flux]
    C --> E[Sidewall Pinholes]
    C --> F[Bottom Corner Shadowing]
    C --> G[Keyhole or Void Formation]
    E --> H[Device Failure: Shorts and Diffusion]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> I[Conformal Film]
    I --> J[Reliable MEMS Device]
    I --> K[Conformal Barrier Layer]
    I --> L[Void-Free Dielectric Fill]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to Deposit Thin Film over Steps] --> Q1{What is the Aspect Ratio AR of the feature}
    Q1 -- AR less than 1 --> R1[Sputtering or Evaporation Acceptable]
    Q1 -- AR between 1 and 3 --> R2{Is conformal coverage mandatory}
    Q1 -- AR greater than 3 --> R3[Must use CVD-class or ALD process]
    R2 -- Yes --> R3
    R2 -- No --> R2A[Sputtering Acceptable]
    R3 --> R4{Is sub-10nm thickness uniformity required}
    R4 -- Yes --> R5[Choose ALD]
    R4 -- No --> R6[Choose LPCVD or PECVD]
    R5 --> DONE[Process Selected]
    R6 --> DONE
    R2A --> DONE
    R1 --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams:** Each node uses alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`, `R3`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node labels. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define step coverage. Mention the two geometric parameters that affect step coverage in a deposition process.**

**Model Answer:**

Step coverage is defined as the ratio of the minimum film thickness deposited on a stepped surface (such as the sidewall or bottom corner of a trench) to the nominal film thickness deposited on a flat reference surface.

$$S = \frac{t_{\min}}{t_0}$$

The two geometric parameters that affect step coverage are:

1. **Aspect Ratio (AR)** of the feature, defined as the depth-to-width ratio $AR = d/w$. Higher AR causes poorer step coverage.
2. **Step Angle (or Sidewall Slope):** A vertical sidewall (90°) receives the least flux from a directional source, while a sloped sidewall (e.g., 45°) receives substantially more flux.

**Marking Allocation:** Definition 1 mark, formula 1 mark, two parameters with explanation 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare the step coverage obtained in (i) thermal evaporation and (ii) LPCVD. Justify the difference using the concept of mean free path.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermal Evaporation | LPCVD |
|---|---|---|
| Typical pressure | $< 10^{-5}$ Torr | 0.1 to 1 Torr |
| Mean free path $\lambda$ | Very large (meters) | Small (microns to tens of microns) |
| $\lambda$ vs feature size $L$ | $\lambda \gg L$ | $\lambda \ll L$ |
| Transport regime | Line-of-sight / molecular flow | Diffusive / viscous flow |
| Step coverage $S$ | 0.0 to 0.3 (poor) | 0.9 to 1.0 (excellent) |

**Justification:** In thermal evaporation, the very low pressure means the mean free path is much greater than the feature dimensions. The depositing atoms travel in straight lines from the source, cannot see the sidewalls of trenches, and shadowing occurs. In LPCVD, the higher pressure reduces the mean free path to a value much smaller than the feature. The precursor molecules undergo many gas-phase collisions, their trajectories are randomized, and they arrive at the sidewalls and bottoms of trenches with nearly equal flux, producing conformal coverage.

**Marking Allocation:** Tabular comparison 2 marks, mean free path justification 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of step coverage with neat sketches. Compare conformal, non-conformal, and tapered step coverage in terms of the underlying physical mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A via of diameter $1.5\,\mu m$ and depth $2.5\,\mu m$ is filled with tungsten using a CVD process. If the bottom coverage is measured to be 75% of the nominal thickness, calculate (i) the aspect ratio, and (ii) the effective sticking coefficient $\alpha$ using the bottom-coverage model.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — Types of Step Coverage:**

**Type 1 — Conformal Step Coverage:** Film thickness on the sidewall is approximately equal to the film thickness on the top surface and bottom. Mechanism: diffusive transport of precursor species; surface-reaction limited regime. Achieved in **LPCVD** and **ALD**.

**Type 2 — Non-Conformal / Directional Step Coverage:** Film thickness on the sidewall is much less than the top thickness; the bottom corner may receive almost no deposition. Mechanism: line-of-sight flux from a directional source; molecular flow regime. Achieved in **thermal evaporation** and **low-pressure sputtering**.

**Type 3 — Tapered (Quasi-Conformal) Step Coverage:** Film thickness gradually decreases from the top edge to the bottom corner. Mechanism: a mix of directional and diffusive flux; occurs in **intermediate-pressure sputtering (5–30 mTorr)** where some gas scattering randomizes a portion of the sputtered atoms.

**Engineering relevance:** Conformal coverage is essential for barrier layers and dielectric fills; non-conformal coverage is useful for **lift-off patterning** where overhang at the step edge is desired.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** via diameter $w = 1.5 \,\mu m$, via depth $d = 2.5 \,\mu m$, bottom coverage $S_{\text{bottom}} = 0.75$.

**Step 1 — Compute the aspect ratio:**

$$AR = \frac{d}{w} = \frac{2.5 \,\mu m}{1.5 \,\mu m} = \frac{2.5}{1.5} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bottom-coverage model:**

$$S_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot AR}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$ algebraically:**

$$1 + \alpha \cdot AR = \frac{1}{S_{\text{bottom}}}$$

$$\alpha \cdot AR = \frac{1}{S_{\text{bottom}}} - 1$$

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{AR}\left(\frac{1}{S_{\text{bottom}}} - 1\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{1}{S_{\text{bottom}}} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333$$

$$\frac{1}{S_{\text{bottom}}} - 1 = 1.3333 - 1 = 0.3333$$

$$\alpha = \frac{0.3333}{1.667} = 0.20$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$\alpha \approx 0.20$$

A low sticking coefficient means most of the precursor molecules reflect off the surface several times before reacting, which is consistent with **CVD-W (tungsten) deposition using WF₆ + H₂** chemistry. The process is suitable for the via and meets the spec.

---

#### **Question B-B.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the role of **mean free path** in determining step coverage during thin-film deposition. Discuss how pressure influences the step coverage in sputtering and LPCVD.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A MEMS foundry uses **sputtering** at 2 mTorr to deposit a 200 nm Al film on a wafer with trenches of depth $0.8 \,\mu m$ and width $0.4 \,\mu m$. Estimate (i) the mean free path of Ar atoms at room temperature (molecular diameter $d_m = 3.6 \times 10^{-10}$ m), and (ii) comment on whether the process is suitable based on the ratio of $\lambda$ to feature size $L$.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — Mean Free Path and Pressure Effects:**

**Definition:** The mean free path $\lambda$ is the average distance a gas-phase species travels between successive collisions. It is given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d_m^{2} \, p}$$

**Role in Step Coverage:**
- When $\lambda \gg L$ (feature size), the species travel ballistically from source to substrate without collisions. The flux is **directional**, and sidewalls are not coated. **Poor step coverage.**
- When $\lambda \ll L$, the species undergo many collisions before reaching the surface; their trajectories are **randomized**, and they arrive uniformly from all directions. **Excellent step coverage.**

**Effect of Pressure:**
- **Sputtering at low pressure (1–5 mTorr):** $\lambda \approx 5$ to $25$ mm, much larger than the feature. Sputtered atoms travel directionally. Step coverage $S \approx 0.2$ to $0.5$.
- **Sputtering at high pressure (10–30 mTorr):** $\lambda$ reduces to $0.5$ to $2$ mm. Significant gas scattering; some sputtered atoms thermalize and arrive diffusively. Step coverage $S \approx 0.4$ to $0.7$.
- **LPCVD at 0.1 to 1 Torr:** $\lambda \approx 5$ to $50 \,\mu m$, smaller than typical feature sizes. Fully diffusive transport. Step coverage $S \approx 0.9$ to $1.0$.

**Conclusion:** Increasing pressure reduces $\lambda$ and improves step coverage, at the cost of reduced deposition rate and possible gas-phase reaction (for CVD).

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Estimation of Mean Free Path:**

**Given:** $T = 300$ K, $p = 2$ mTorr $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ Torr $= 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 133.322$ Pa $= 0.2666$ Pa, $d_m = 3.6 \times 10^{-10}$ m.

**Step 1 — Recall the constant $k_B T$ at room temperature:**

$$k_B T = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator $\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d_m^{2} \, p$:**

$$d_m^{2} = (3.6 \times 10^{-10})^{2} = 12.96 \times 10^{-20} = 1.296 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\pi \, d_m^{2} = 3.1416 \times 1.296 \times 10^{-19} = 4.0715 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d_m^{2} = 1.4142 \times 4.0715 \times 10^{-19} = 5.7579 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d_m^{2} \, p = 5.7579 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2666 = 1.5351 \times 10^{-19} \text{ (Pa·m²)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean free path:**

$$\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{1.5351 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02697 \text{ m} \approx 27.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the feature characteristic length $L$:**

For a trench, the characteristic length can be taken as the width $w$ (smallest dimension) or depth. Take $L = w = 0.4 \,\mu m = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 4 \times 10^{-7}$ m.

**Step 5 — Compare $\lambda$ with $L$:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{L} = \frac{2.697 \times 10^{-2}}{4 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.74 \times 10^{4} \approx 67{,}400$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:**

$$\lambda \gg L \quad (\text{by a factor of } \sim 67{,}000)$$

This is the **molecular flow regime** (Kn $\gg$ 1). The sputtered Al atoms travel ballistically with virtually no gas-phase collisions. The deposition is highly directional, and step coverage at the bottom of the trench will be **poor** (likely $S < 0.2$).

**Conclusion:** The process is **not suitable** for conformal coating of these trenches. The foundry should either (i) increase the Ar pressure to $\sim 20$–$30$ mTorr to reduce $\lambda$ closer to $L$, or (ii) use a collimated or long-throw sputtering source, or (iii) switch to a CVD-based Al process (e.g., CVD-Al from DMAH precursor), or (iv) use ALD for an alternative metal.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Step Coverage Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Step Coverage" with "Sidewall Coverage"** — They are related but not identical. Step coverage is the general ratio; sidewall coverage is one specific instance. Examiners deduct marks for imprecise language.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units of $\lambda$** — Mean free path is in **meters** (or mm/µm). Many students write a number without units, which costs 0.5 mark in the numerical.
> 3. **Mixing up "Knudsen Number" and "Knudsen Diffusion"** — Kn is a dimensionless ratio; Knudsen diffusion is a transport regime. Examiners want both stated.
> 4. **Using $\vert$ or $\vert x \vert$ inside LaTeX table cells** — In KTU digital answer scripts, this breaks the markdown. Use `\vert` for absolute value.
> 5. **In numerical problems, students often forget to convert pressure from mTorr to Pa** before applying the mean-free-path formula. This leads to off-by-many-orders-of-magnitude errors.
> 6. **For conformal coverage, students sometimes wrongly state that PVD (sputtering) is conformal** — at low pressure it is not. Always qualify with the pressure regime.
> 7. **In 14-mark answers, students often skip the "real-world relevance" or "engineering application" part** — Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this; missing it costs easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** $S = t_{\min}/t_0$, ranges from 0 (no coverage) to 1 (perfectly conformal).
- [x] **Three Types:** Conformal (LPCVD/ALD), Non-conformal (evaporation), Tapered (sputtering at moderate pressure).
- [x] **Key Geometric Parameter:** Aspect Ratio $AR = d/w$; higher AR means harder to coat.
- [x] **Key Physical Parameter:** Mean free path $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\pi d_m^{2} p)$.
- [x] **Knudsen Number:** $\text{Kn} = \lambda / L$. Kn $\gg$ 1 $\to$ line-of-sight; Kn $\ll$ 1 $\to$ diffusive.
- [x] **Lambert's Cosine Law:** $F(\theta) = F_0 \cos\theta$ governs the directional flux on tilted surfaces.
- [x] **Bottom-Coverage Model:** $S_{\text{bottom}} = 1 / (1 + \alpha \cdot AR)$.
- [x] **Pressure Trend:** Higher pressure $\to$ lower $\lambda$ $\to$ better step coverage but lower deposition rate.
- [x] **Best Conformal Process:** ALD, with $S \to 1$ even at AR = 30:1.
- [x] **Worst Conformal Process:** Thermal evaporation, $S \to 0$ at high AR.
- [x] **CVD Mechanism:** Surface-reaction limited regime gives conformal coverage because diffusion is fast.
- [x] **PVD Mechanism:** Directional flux causes shadowing and poor step coverage at high AR.
- [x] **Real-world uses:** Barrier layers (TiN, TaN), dielectric fills (SiO₂), MEMS passivation.
- [x] **Failure modes:** Sidewall pinholes, bottom-corner shadowing, keyhole/void formation.
- [x] **KTU exam tag-must-remember:** "Mean free path" + "Knudsen number" + "Sticking coefficient" are the three magic phrases that earn full marks in any step coverage question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Enhanced CVD, Electron Cyclotron Resonance CVD (ECRCVD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plasma Enhanced CVD, Electron Cyclotron Resonance CVD (ECRCVD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plasma Enhanced Chemical Vapour Deposition (PECVD)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Plasma Enhanced Chemical Vapour Deposition (PECVD) is a low-temperature thin-film deposition technique in which reactant gases are decomposed by a **glow-discharge plasma** rather than by purely thermal energy. The plasma is typically sustained by a **13.56 MHz Radio Frequency (RF)** field applied between two parallel electrodes, which dissociates the precursor molecules into highly reactive radicals and ions, enabling film growth on a substrate at temperatures between **200 °C and 400 °C** — significantly lower than the 600 °C–900 °C required in conventional thermal CVD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner's phrase):** "PECVD is a CVD variant in which a non-equilibrium plasma supplies the activation energy required for chemical reaction, decoupling the deposition temperature from the reaction kinetics."

**Conceptual Analogy — "Cooking with a Microwave Instead of a Fire":**
Imagine you want to bake a cake. With a *thermal oven* (conventional CVD), you must heat the entire oven and the cake to a high temperature for a long time — this risks burning the delicate icing. With a *microwave* (PECVD), energy is delivered *directly* to the water molecules inside the cake, baking it from within at a much lower overall oven temperature. Similarly, in PECVD the plasma "excites" the gas molecules directly, allowing the *substrate to stay cool* while the *chemistry proceeds rapidly*. The cake (substrate) never gets hot, but the food cooks perfectly — exactly the goal of low-temperature MEMS fabrication.

### 1.2 Electron Cyclotron Resonance CVD (ECRCVD)

**Formal Definition:**
ECRCVD is a specialized, high-density variant of PECVD in which a **microwave field (typically 2.45 GHz)** couples with the **electron cyclotron frequency** of free electrons trapped in a static magnetic field. When the microwave frequency matches the electron gyrofrequency, electrons gain kinetic energy resonantly, producing a plasma with **electron densities of 10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³** and very low ion energies, enabling film deposition at **room temperature to 150 °C** with excellent film quality.

**Conceptual Analogy — "A Child on a Swing Pushed at the Right Moment":**
Picture a child on a swing. If you push randomly, nothing builds up. But if you push *exactly* once per cycle (the resonant frequency), the swing goes higher and higher with minimal effort. In ECRCVD, the **magnetic field** is tuned so that electrons complete one circular orbit in the *exact time* between two microwave crests. Each crest "pushes" the electron, and its energy grows cumulatively — producing a *super-hot electron cloud* in a *cold gas*. The energetic electrons smash gas molecules into reactive species without heating the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Constants to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Microwave frequency (f):** 2.45 GHz (industrial, scientific, medical band)
> - **RF frequency for PECVD:** 13.56 MHz
> - **Resonance magnetic field (B):** ≈ **875 Gauss (0.0875 T)**
> - **Electron charge (e):** 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
> - **Electron mass (mₑ):** 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg
> - **Permittivity of free space (ε₀):** 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

---

### 1.3 Why Are These Techniques Critical in MEMS?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMS Context (KTU 2024 Module 2 Pillar Concept):**
> MEMS devices often contain **pre-fabricated CMOS circuitry, metallized layers, polymeric structures, or piezoelectric thin films** that **cannot tolerate temperatures above ~400 °C**. PECVD and ECRCVD are therefore *the* workhorse deposition techniques in the back-end-of-line (BEOL) and post-CMOS MEMS integration flow.

| Process | Typical Substrate Temp | Plasma Density (cm⁻³) | Common Films |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal CVD | 600 – 900 °C | None | Poly-Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄ |
| **PECVD** | **200 – 400 °C** | **10⁹ – 10¹⁰** | **SiO₂, Si₃N₄, a-Si, SiC** |
| **ECRCVD** | **RT – 150 °C** | **10¹¹ – 10¹²** | **High-κ, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, DLC** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PECVD — Operational Physics, Step by Step

**Step 1 — Gas Introduction:**
Precursor gases (e.g., SiH₄ + N₂O for SiO₂; SiH₄ + NH₃ for Si₃N₄) are introduced into a parallel-plate reactor through mass-flow controllers. The chamber pressure is held between **0.1 – 10 Torr** (sub-atmospheric).

**Step 2 — Plasma Generation by RF Field:**
An RF voltage (13.56 MHz) is applied between the *powered* (top) electrode and the *grounded* (bottom) electrode. Free electrons, accelerated by the alternating field, undergo **inelastic collisions** with gas molecules:
$$e^{-} + \text{SiH}_4 \longrightarrow \text{SiH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} + e^{-}$$

**Step 3 — Radical Transport to Substrate:**
Reactive species (radicals like SiH₃·, NH₂·, N·) diffuse across the boundary layer to the substrate. Because plasma already "pre-dissociated" the precursors, the substrate temperature only governs *surface mobility* — not the chemical bond breaking.

**Step 4 — Surface Adsorption & Reaction:**
Adsorbed radicals migrate on the surface, find favorable sites, and bond to form the desired film:
$$2\,\text{SiH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{N}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{plasma + T}} \text{SiO}_2 \downarrow + 3\,\text{H}_2 \uparrow$$

**Step 5 — Byproduct Desorption & Exhaust:**
Volatile by-products (H₂, CO₂, etc.) are pumped out, and the process repeats layer-by-layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Plasma Enhancement:**
> In thermal CVD, the Arrhenius factor $\exp(-E_a/k_BT)$ is the *rate-limiting* term — high T is mandatory. In PECVD, **plasma supplies the activation energy Eₐ directly to electrons**, effectively making the substrate temperature decoupled from the reaction barrier. This is why *low-T* films with high quality become possible.

### 2.2 ECRCVD — Resonance Physics

**Step 1 — Static Magnetic Field Setup:**
The chamber is surrounded by **electromagnetic coils** that generate a divergent magnetic field. The field strength B is tuned so that, at the resonance zone above the substrate, B satisfies the **electron cyclotron resonance (ECR) condition**.

**Step 2 — Microwave Injection:**
A 2.45 GHz microwave is launched through a **dielectric window (quartz)** into the chamber via a waveguide.

**Step 3 — Cyclotron Motion:**
A free electron in a magnetic field B undergoes **circular motion** (Lorentz force) with a natural gyrofrequency:
$$\omega_{ce} = \frac{eB}{m_e}$$

**Step 4 — Resonant Energy Coupling:**
When the microwave angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ equals $\omega_{ce}$, every cycle of the microwave field arrives exactly when the electron completes one orbit — energy is transferred **resonantly and cumulatively**, leading to electron energies of **5 – 30 eV**.

**Step 5 — High-Density Plasma Formation:**
These hot electrons ionize and dissociate the feed gas with extreme efficiency, producing a plasma with density 10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³ even at very low pressures (**0.1 – 10 mTorr**). The substrate sits in a *remote plasma region*, hence suffers minimal ion bombardment damage.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_{ce} = \dfrac{eB}{m_e}$ | Electron cyclotron angular frequency | rad/s |
| 2 | $f_{ce} = \dfrac{eB}{2\pi m_e}$ | Cyclotron frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 3 | $B_{res} = \dfrac{2\pi f\, m_e}{e}$ | **Resonance magnetic field** | Tesla / Gauss |
| 4 | For f = 2.45 GHz → $B_{res} \approx 0.0875$ T | **Memorize this value** | **875 Gauss** |
| 5 | $\lambda_m = \dfrac{h}{m_e v_e}$ | de-Broglie wavelength of energetic electron | nm |
| 6 | $n_e \approx n_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha\,V_{RF}}{k_B T_e}\right)$ | Electron density in RF sheath | cm⁻³ |
| 7 | $r_d = k\, n_{\text{radical}}\, \theta$ | Deposition rate model | nm/min |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 P}$ | Mean free path of gas molecules | cm |
| 9 | $E_a^{\text{eff}} = E_a - \alpha\, T_e$ | Effective activation energy with plasma | eV |
| 10 | $v_{de} = \mu_e\, E$ | Electron drift velocity | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **PECVD** is the industry standard for **inter-layer dielectrics (ILD)** in CMOS-MEMS monolithic integration.
> - **ECRCVD** is used in advanced nodes (≤ 22 nm) for **high-κ gate dielectrics (HfO₂, ZrO₂)** and **diamond-like carbon (DLC)** films for tribological MEMS.
> - Both processes enable *back-end-compatible* MEMS — a **mandatory requirement** when MEMS is fabricated *above* finished CMOS wafers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Electron Cyclotron Resonance Magnetic Field

**Starting Point (Experimental Setup):**
A microwave of frequency $f = 2.45$ GHz is injected into a plasma chamber surrounded by magnetic coils. We want to derive the value of $B$ that makes electrons resonantly absorb microwave energy.

**Step 1 — Electron in a Magnetic Field Undergoes Circular Motion**
By the Lorentz force law, an electron with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences:
$$\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

This produces circular motion in the plane perpendicular to $\vec{B}$. The centripetal force equals the magnetic force:
$$m_e \,\omega_{ce}\, v = e v B$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Cyclotron Angular Frequency**
Dividing both sides by $m_e v$:
$$\omega_{ce} = \frac{eB}{m_e}$$

**Step 3 — Write Cyclotron Frequency in Hz**
Since $\omega_{ce} = 2\pi f_{ce}$:
$$f_{ce} = \frac{eB}{2\pi m_e}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ECR Condition**
At resonance, the microwave frequency equals the cyclotron frequency:
$$f = f_{ce} = \frac{eB}{2\pi m_e}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Resonance Magnetic Field**
$$B_{res} = \frac{2\pi f\, m_e}{e}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Numerical Values**
For $f = 2.45 \times 10^9$ Hz, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$B_{res} = \frac{2\pi (2.45 \times 10^{9}) (9.11 \times 10^{-31})}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate Numerator**
$$2\pi (2.45 \times 10^9) = 1.539 \times 10^{10} \text{ rad/s}$$
$$1.539 \times 10^{10} \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 1.402 \times 10^{-20} \text{ kg·rad/s}$$

**Step 8 — Divide by Electron Charge**
$$B_{res} = \frac{1.402 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.0875 \text{ T}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to Gauss**
Since 1 T = 10⁴ Gauss:
$$B_{res} = 0.0875 \times 10^4 = 875 \text{ Gauss}$$

**Step 10 — Final Result**
$$\boxed{B_{res} = 875 \text{ Gauss} \approx 0.0875 \text{ T for } f = 2.45 \text{ GHz}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Number is Sacred in KTU Exams:** The value 875 G is the design target of every ECR source sold commercially (e.g., Astex, Oxford Instruments, Tectra). It is the *first numerical answer* examiners expect when a question is asked on ECRCVD.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — PECVD vs ECRCVD vs Thermal CVD

| Parameter | Thermal CVD | PECVD | ECRCVD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Thermal (furnace) | RF plasma (13.56 MHz) | Microwave (2.45 GHz) + B-field |
| Substrate temperature | 600 – 900 °C | **200 – 400 °C** | **RT – 150 °C** |
| Operating pressure | 10 – 760 Torr | 0.1 – 10 Torr | **0.1 – 10 mTorr** |
| Plasma density | — | 10⁹ – 10¹⁰ cm⁻³ | **10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³** |
| Electron temperature | — | 1 – 5 eV | **5 – 30 eV** |
| Ion bombardment energy | 0 eV | 10 – 100 eV | **< 20 eV (remote)** |
| Deposition rate | 10 – 100 nm/min | 20 – 500 nm/min | 5 – 50 nm/min |
| Step coverage | Conformal | Conformal | **Excellent (low P)** |
| Film density | High | Moderate | **Very High** |
| CMOS compatibility | Poor (high T) | **Good** | **Excellent** |
| Capital cost | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| Typical films | Poly-Si, epi-Si | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, a-Si | High-κ, DLC, low-k SiO₂ |

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Mean Free Path of Reactant in PECVD

The mean free path determines whether the gas-phase transport is *viscous* (high P) or *molecular* (low P). For ECRCVD, the very low pressure regime is essential.

**Step 1 — General Definition**
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^2 n}$$

where $d$ is the molecular diameter and $n$ is the number density.

**Step 2 — Express n Using Ideal Gas Law**
$$n = \frac{P}{k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**
$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi d^2 P}$$

**Step 4 — For ECRCVD Conditions** (P = 1 mTorr = 0.133 Pa, T = 300 K, $d \approx 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m):
$$\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi (3 \times 10^{-10})^2 (0.133)}$$

Numerator: $4.14 \times 10^{-21}$.
Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 9 \times 10^{-20} \times 0.133 \approx 5.32 \times 10^{-20}$.

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 78 \text{ mm}}$$

This large mean free path means gas molecules travel *ballistically* in ECRCVD, ensuring uniform deposition on high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ECRCVD System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MWSRC[Microwave Source 2.45 GHz] --> WG[Waveguide WR 340]
    WG --> TW[Tunable Tuner E H Matcher]
    TW --> QW[Quartz Microwave Window]
    QW --> RZ[ECR Resonance Zone B 875 Gauss]
    MC1[Magnetic Coil 1 Upper] --> RZ
    MC2[Magnetic Coil 2 Middle] --> RZ
    MC3[Magnetic Coil 3 Lower] --> RZ
    GI1[Gas Inlet 1 SiH4] --> RZ
    GI2[Gas Inlet 2 N2O NH3] --> RZ
    RZ --> DZ[Downstream Plasma Transport]
    DZ --> SH[Substrate Holder Heated or Cooled]
    SH --> SUB[MEMS Wafer Substrate]
    SH --> EX[Exhaust to Turbo Pump]
    TP[Turbo Molecular Pump] --> EX
    RP[Roughing Pump] --> TP
    GPC[Pressure Gauge 0.1 mTorr] --> EX
    OES[Optical Emission Spectrometer] --> RZ
    LANG[Langmuir Probe] --> DZ
```

**Reading the diagram (for the exam):**
1. Microwave from the magnetron travels through the waveguide and crosses the quartz window *without* plasma ignition.
2. Inside the chamber, the magnetic field gradient is tuned so the **ECR zone** sits *upstream* of the substrate.
3. Feed gases enter near the resonance zone where electrons are hottest.
4. Reactive species drift *downstream* to the substrate — this is why ECRCVD is called a **"remote plasma"** technique.
5. Diagnostics: OES monitors emission lines, Langmuir probe measures $n_e$ and $T_e$.

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a PECVD System (Parallel-Plate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Mass Flow Controller 1 SiH4] --> MFC[MFC Manifold]
    G2[Mass Flow Controller 2 N2O] --> MFC
    G3[Mass Flow Controller 3 NH3] --> MFC
    MFC --> SHOWER[Showerhead Gas Distributor]
    RFSRC[RF Generator 13.56 MHz] --> MATCH[Impedance Matching Network]
    MATCH --> PE[Powered Upper Electrode]
    SHOWER --> PE
    PE --> PLASMA[Plasma Glow Discharge Region]
    PLASMA --> GE[Grounded Lower Electrode]
    GE --> WH[Wafer Heater 200 to 400 C]
    WH --> WS[Substrate Wafer]
    WS --> EX2[Pump Port]
    EX2 --> VP[Vacuum Pump Roots Rotary]
    PCTRL[Pressure Controller MKS] --> MFC
    TCRL[Throttle Valve] --> EX2
```

**Key Subsystems Identified (write these in your exam answer for full marks):**
1. **Gas Delivery Subsystem** — mass-flow controllers, showerhead.
2. **Plasma Generation Subsystem** — RF source, matching network, electrodes.
3. **Reaction Chamber** — parallel-plate geometry, plasma region.
4. **Substrate Stage** — heater for temperature control.
5. **Vacuum & Pressure Control** — throttle valve, MKS gauge.
6. **Exhaust Subsystem** — roots + rotary pump train.

### 4.3 Process Flow Comparison (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Gas Inlet SiH4 NH3] --> B[Step 2 Plasma Ignition]
    B --> C[Step 3 Radical Generation]
    C --> D[Step 4 Surface Adsorption]
    D --> E[Step 5 Surface Reaction Film Growth]
    E --> F[Step 6 Byproduct Desorption H2]
    F --> G[Step 7 Pumping Out]
    G --> H{Desired Thickness Achieved}
    H -- No --> A
    H -- Yes --> I[End of Deposition Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
