> - **Shielding** to prevent discharge at chamber walls.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Chamber is evacuated; Ar is introduced at 1–10 Pa.
2. High DC voltage ionizes Ar: $Ar + e^- \to Ar^+ + 2e^-$.
3. Ar⁺ ions accelerate toward the negatively-biased cathode.
4. Ion bombardment ejects target atoms (sputtering) — these deposit on the substrate on the anode.

**Role of (i) Inert Gas, (ii) Cathode Sheath, (iii) Secondary Electrons (2 marks):**
- **(i) Inert gas (Ar):** Provides ions for bombardment without chemically reacting with the target, ensuring **purely physical etching**.
- **(ii) Cathode sheath:** The dark region near the cathode where almost all the voltage drop occurs; it **accelerates Ar⁺ ions** perpendicularly to the target, ensuring directionality (anisotropy).
- **(iii) Secondary electrons:** Released from the cathode upon ion impact; they are accelerated away, ionize more Ar atoms, and **sustain the plasma**. (However, they also bombard the substrate, causing heating — a disadvantage of DC sputtering.)

---

**Model Answer — Question A(b) [7 Marks]:**

**Derivation (5 marks) — covered in Section 3.1 above:** Starting from conservation of momentum and energy, we obtain:

$$E_{2,max} = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} E_0$$

**Application to Ar⁺ vs He⁺ on Si (2 marks):**

For **Ar⁺ ($M_1 = 40$) on Si ($M_2 = 28$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{2,max}^{Ar} &= \frac{4 \times 40 \times 28}{(40+28)^2} E_0 = \frac{4480}{4624} E_0 \approx 0.969 \, E_0
\end{aligned}
$$

For **He⁺ ($M_1 = 4$) on Si ($M_2 = 28$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{2,max}^{He} &= \frac{4 \times 4 \times 28}{(4+28)^2} E_0 = \frac{448}{1024} E_0 \approx 0.4375 \, E_0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Ar⁺ transfers **~97%** of its kinetic energy to Si atoms, whereas He⁺ transfers only **~44%**. Ar⁺ is therefore far more efficient for sputtering Si. Additionally, the mass ratio $M_1/M_2 \approx 1.4$ for Ar/Si is closer to optimum (1) than for He/Si (0.14), maximizing momentum coupling.

---

### **Question B** [14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative] [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Main Exam]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Sigmund sputtering theory** and derive the dependence of sputtering yield on ion energy. Discuss why there is a **threshold energy** for sputtering.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **etch rate** of a silicon wafer ($M_t = 28$ g/mol, $\rho = 2.33$ g/cm³) under Ar⁺ bombardment given: sputtering yield $Y = 0.6$ atoms/ion, ion current density $J = 2$ mA/cm². Express your answer in **nm/min**.

---

**Model Answer — Question B(a) [7 Marks]:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sigmund Sputtering Theory (3 marks):** Proposed by Peter Sigmund (1969), it treats sputtering as a **linear collision cascade** problem. The theory assumes:
> 1. The incident ion penetrates the target and undergoes a cascade of elastic collisions.
> 2. Energy is deposited in a near-surface volume described by the **nuclear stopping cross-section** $S_n(E)$.
> 3. The number of atoms ejected is proportional to the energy deposited at the surface.
>
> The resulting sputtering yield is:
>
> $$Y(E) = \frac{0.042 \, \alpha \, S_n(E)}{U_s}$$
>
> where $\alpha$ is a function of the mass ratio $M_2/M_1$, $S_n(E)$ is the nuclear stopping power, and $U_s$ is the surface binding energy.

**Energy dependence (2 marks):** In the **low-to-medium energy regime** (hundreds of eV to few keV), $S_n(E) \propto E^{0.5}$, leading to:

$$Y(E) \propto \frac{E^{0.5}}{U_s}$$

Yields rise with energy, reach a broad maximum around 10–100 keV, and then decline at very high energies due to deep ion implantation.

**Threshold energy (2 marks):** Sputtering cannot occur unless the energy transferred to a surface atom exceeds the **surface binding energy** $U_s$. At the threshold, the ejected atom just barely escapes the surface. Empirically:

$$E_{th} \approx U_s \cdot \frac{(M_1 + M_2)^4}{4 M_1 M_2 (M_1 - M_2)^2} \quad \text{for } M_1 > M_2$$

For most materials with Ar⁺, $E_{th} \approx 20$–$50$ eV.

---

**Model Answer — Question B(b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute ion flux from current density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= 2 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 20 \text{ A/m}^2 \\
\phi &= \frac{J}{e} = \frac{20}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.248 \times 10^{20} \text{ ions/m}^2\text{·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply etch rate formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{Y \cdot \phi \cdot M_t}{\rho \cdot N_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{0.6 \times 1.248 \times 10^{20} \times 0.028}{2330 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\
&= \frac{2.097 \times 10^{18}}{1.403 \times 10^{27}} \\
&\approx 1.495 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to nm/min:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &\approx 1.495 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s} \times 10^9 \text{ nm/m} \times 60 \text{ s/min} \\
&\approx 89.7 \text{ nm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $R \approx 89.7$ nm/min (≈ 90 nm/min).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the charge $e$ in etch-rate calculations (–2 marks):** Many students write $R = Y \cdot J \cdot M_t / (\rho \cdot N_A)$ using $J$ directly in A/m², but this gives **numerically wrong** results. Convert $J \to \phi = J/e$ first.
> 2. **Confusing sputtering with RIE (–1 mark):** Pure sputtering is **physical only**; mentioning "chemical reaction" will lose marks. RIE is a *separate* hybrid process.
> 3. **Missing the role of secondary electrons in sustaining plasma (–1 mark):** Always state explicitly that secondary electrons ionize Ar atoms and maintain the discharge.
> 4. **Not mentioning threshold energy when discussing yield vs. ion energy curve (–1 mark):** The curve starts from $E_{th}$, **not zero**. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Confusing $E_2$ (transferred energy) with $E_0$ (incident energy) (–2 marks):** Be precise about subscripts.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers (–1 mark):** Always write "nm/min" or "m/s" explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ion Etching / Sputtering (Module 3)**

- [x] **Sputtering** = purely **physical** dry etching via momentum transfer; no chemical reaction.
- [x] **Sputtering Yield $Y$** = atoms ejected per incident ion; depends on $E_i$, ion mass, target mass, angle.
- [x] **Threshold energy $E_{th} \approx 20$–$50$ eV** for most materials with Ar⁺.
- [x] **Maximum energy transfer** (head-on): $E_{2,max} = \dfrac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} E_0$.
- [x] **Maximum transfer = 100%** when $M_1 = M_2$; very small when $M_1 \gg M_2$ or $M_1 \ll M_2$.
- [x] **Ar⁺ (40 amu)** is preferred over He⁺ (4 amu) for Si (28 amu) sputtering — better mass match.
- [x] **Cathode sheath** is the high-field region that accelerates ions **perpendicularly** → anisotropy.
- [x] **Secondary electrons** sustain the plasma but cause substrate heating.
- [x] **Etch rate** formula: $R = \dfrac{Y \cdot J \cdot M_t}{e \cdot \rho \cdot N_A}$ (with charge term) or $\dfrac{Y \cdot \phi \cdot M_t}{\rho \cdot N_A}$.
- [x] **Operating pressure** for sputtering: **0.1 Pa – 10 Pa** (low enough for $\lambda \gg$ electrode gap).
- [x] **Mean free path** formula: $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}$.
- [x] **Sigmund theory** → sputtering yield $\propto E^{0.5}/U_s$ in the linear cascade regime.
- [x] **Yield peaks at oblique incidence (~60–70°)**; falls at grazing angles due to ion reflection.
- [x] **DC sputtering** works for conductive targets; **RF sputtering** is required for insulating targets (e.g., SiO₂, AlN).
- [x] **Magnetron sputtering** uses magnetic fields to confine electrons → higher plasma density → higher etch/deposition rate.
- [x] **Reverse process** (sputter deposition) is used for thin-film deposition in MEMS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"SPUTTER = S**urface **P**hysical **U**niform **T**op-down **T**echnique via **E**nergy **R**edistribution."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Beam Etching, Plasma Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Beam Etching, Plasma Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Ion Beam Etching (IBE)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Ion Beam Etching (IBE), also termed **Ion Beam Milling (IBM)**, is a purely *physical*, dry-etching microfabrication process in which a beam of high-energy, chemically inert gas ions (typically **Ar⁺** at energies of **0.5 – 2 keV**) is directed at a substrate held under high vacuum. Material is removed by *momentum transfer* (kinetic sputtering) rather than chemical reaction, yielding highly anisotropic profiles essential for sub-micron MEMS/NEMS feature definition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** IBE is the *archetypal physical dry etch* — purely mechanical atom-by-atom removal. The "etchant" is *kinetic energy*, not chemistry.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Micro Sandblaster"
Imagine an artist using a high-pressure sandblaster to carve letters into a granite tombstone. The sand grains do not *react* with the stone — they simply knock atoms off the surface by brute-force collisions. Ion Beam Etching is exactly this, scaled down to the atomic level:
- **Sand grains** → Accelerated **Ar⁺ ions** (mass ≈ 40 amu).
- **Compressed air** → High-voltage electrostatic **ion gun** (Kaufman source).
- **Granite tombstone** → The **photoresist-patterned** silicon/MEMS substrate.
- The harder and heavier the "sand" and the steeper the impact angle, the deeper and more directional the cut.

> [!NOTE]
> Because there is **no chemical selectivity**, IBE etches almost *everything* — photoresist, silicon, silicon dioxide, gold, platinum — at comparable rates. This *low selectivity* is its biggest drawback but its *perfect anisotropy* is its greatest strength.

---

## 1.2 Plasma Etching

**Formal Definition:**
Plasma Etching is a *dry etching* technique in which a low-pressure glow-discharge plasma is used to generate chemically reactive species (radicals, ions) that volatilize the exposed substrate material. Two principal sub-classes are mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Sub-class | Mechanism | Anisotropy |
|---|---|---|
| **Barrel / Plasma Etching (PE)** | Predominantly *chemical* (free-radical attack) | Mostly *isotropic* |
| **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** | Combined *chemical + ion-assisted physical* | Highly *anisotropic* |
| **Deep RIE (DRIE – Bosch Process)** | Alternating etch/passivation cycles | Near-vertical, high aspect ratio |

### Intuitive Analogy — "Acid Spray with a Pneumatic Nozzle"
Think of an industrial process where a corrosive acid mist is sprayed onto a metal plate:
- **Pure Plasma Etching** = the acid *vapor* sits in a chamber and *eats* the metal uniformly on all exposed surfaces — like rust forming evenly. (Isotropic, high selectivity, poor direction control.)
- **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** = the acid is now *accelerated* and *aimed* by an electric field, so it eats *downward* far faster than sideways. (Anisotropic, moderate selectivity.)
- **Ion Beam Etching** = no acid at all — just inert *plastic beads* fired like bullets. (Perfectly directional, but no chemistry selectivity.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** IBE = *physical* (ions act as momentum carriers). Plasma Etching = *chemical* (radicals react to form volatile products like **SiF₄**, **WF₆**, **SiCl₄**). RIE = hybrid. The "etchant chemistry" (CF₄, SF₆, Cl₂, BCl₃, O₂) defines the selectivity.

---

## 1.3 Critical Process Metrics (KTU Board Definitions)

| Metric | Definition | Ideal Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Etch Rate (ER)** | Material thickness removed per unit time (nm/min or µm/min) | High |
| **Selectivity (S)** | Ratio of target-material etch rate to mask (photoresist) etch rate | S = ER<sub>film</sub> / ER<sub>mask</sub> → ∞ |
| **Anisotropy (A)** | A = 1 − (lateral etch rate / vertical etch rate) | A → 1 (fully anisotropic) |
| **Uniformity** | Percentage deviation of etch rate across wafer | < ±5 % |
| **Damage** | Surface lattice disruption (IBE) or radiation-induced charge (RIE) | Minimize |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants** (memorize): 
> **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J** ; Boltzmann constant **k<sub>B</sub> = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** ; electron mass **m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg** ; permittivity of free space **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Ion Beam Etching — Operational Theory

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Ion Generation:** Argon gas is fed into a *Kaufman ion source*. A hot filament cathode emits thermionic electrons.
2. **Plasma Ignition:** A discharge voltage (**~40 V**, **~1 A**) ionizes Ar atoms: 
   **Ar + e⁻ → Ar⁺ + 2e⁻**
3. **Ion Extraction & Acceleration:** A two-grid (screen + accelerator) electrostatic optics extracts the Ar⁺ ions and accelerates them to the target energy (typically **0.5 – 1.5 keV**).
4. **Beam Neutralization:** A second filament emits low-energy electrons into the beam to prevent space-charge blow-up (the positive ion beam would otherwise self-repel).
5. **Substrate Bombardment:** The collimated Ar⁺ beam strikes the substrate at near-normal incidence.
6. **Momentum Transfer & Sputtering:** Incoming ions transfer kinetic energy to surface atoms via collision cascades. When the recoil energy exceeds the surface binding energy (**U<sub>s</sub> ≈ 4 – 8 eV** for most solids), a surface atom is ejected.
7. **Material Removal:** Etch by-products are non-volatile (atoms) and simply deposit on chamber walls — *no chemistry involved*.

### Critical Process Variables (Bulleted Logic)

- **Ion Energy (E):** Higher E → higher sputter yield, but more subsurface damage and resist erosion. Typical: **300 – 1500 eV**.
- **Ion Current Density (J):** Measured in **mA/cm²**; directly proportional to etch rate. Typical: **0.1 – 1 mA/cm²**.
- **Incident Angle (θ):** Sputter yield Y(θ) peaks at **θ ≈ 60° – 80°** from the surface normal (not 0°!). At grazing incidence, ions reflect without sputtering.
- **Beam Divergence:** Must be < 1° for high-fidelity pattern transfer.
- **Substrate Temperature:** Cooling required (often with liquid-N₂ cryotrap) to prevent photoresist reticulation.
- **Vacuum Level:** < **10⁻⁴ Torr** to avoid ion-gas collisions (mean free path > beam length).

### Failure Modes (KTU favourite)

- **Trenching:** Sidewall ions deflect and re-strike the bottom corners, creating deep grooves.
- **Facetting:** Soft materials at corners erode preferentially.
- **Redeposition:** Sputtered atoms from one feature deposit on neighbouring feature sidewalls.
- **Damage:** Amorphization of top **5 – 20 nm** of silicon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** IBE is the *gold standard* for etching noble metals (Au, Pt) and compound semiconductors (GaAs, InP) that resist wet chemistry. It is also used to *thin* TEM samples and to *trim* thin-film resistors to precise values.

---

## 2.2 Plasma Etching — Operational Theory

### Plasma Generation

A RF voltage (**13.56 MHz**, the internationally reserved ISM band) is applied between two parallel-plate electrodes inside a vacuum chamber filled with reactive gas at **10 – 1000 mTorr**. Free electrons, accelerated by the RF field, ionize gas molecules. After ~µs, a *quasi-steady-state plasma* is formed containing:

- **Free electrons** (T<sub>e</sub> ≈ 3 – 10 eV → effective T ≈ 30 000 – 100 000 K)
- **Positive ions** (T<sub>i</sub> ≈ 0.03 eV → ≈ 300 K, near room temperature)
- **Neutral radicals** (e.g., F•, Cl•) — the *real etchants*
- **Photons** (the plasma glow)

> [!NOTE]
> The plasma is *electronegative* for halogens (e⁻, Cl⁻, F⁻) — the negative ion population can rival or exceed the electron population, modifying sheath physics.

### The Sheath — The Engine of Anisotropy

When a plasma contacts a surface, the higher-mobility electrons are lost first, leaving a net positive space-charge region called the **plasma sheath** (thickness ≈ several **Debye lengths**). A self-consistent DC **sheath potential V<sub>s</sub>** develops (typically **10 – 1000 V**), accelerating positive ions *normal* to the surface. This is the fundamental origin of directionality in plasma etching.

### Etch Mechanism — Chemical Component

For a generic silicon etch with **CF₄** plasma (the KTU textbook example):
- Dissociation: **CF₄ + e⁻ → CF₃• + F• + e⁻**
- Etch reaction: **Si + 4F• → SiF₄↑**
- The SiF₄ is volatile and is pumped away.

Selectivity arises because CF₄ plasma attacks Si ~10× faster than SiO₂ (because Si–F bond is favourable whereas SiO₂ needs additional ion bombardment to break the Si–O bond).

### RIE vs. Barrel Etching — Why RIE is Anisotropic

| Feature | Barrel (PE) | RIE |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode configuration | Sample in *floating* quartz boat | Sample on **powered (cathode) electrode** |
| Ion bombardment energy | Low (< 20 eV) | High (100 – 800 eV) |
| Radical concentration | High | Moderate |
| Dominant mechanism | Chemical | Ion-assisted chemical |
| Anisotropy | Poor (isotropic) | Excellent (A > 0.9) |
| Selectivity | Excellent | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In RIE, ion bombardment both *physically removes* passivating polymer/inhibitor layers and *damages* the surface, enhancing radical chemisorption. This is the "synergistic" or "ion-assisted" effect that gives RIE its unique profile control.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All boundary conditions and standard units are noted. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value (NOT the pipe character — KTU markdown rendering safety).

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Y** = (atoms ejected) / (incident ions) | Sputter yield, dimensionless. Y ≈ 0.5 – 5 for most materials at 1 keV Ar⁺. | dimensionless |
| 2 | **ER** = $\dfrac{Y \cdot J \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$ | Etch rate: Y = sputter yield, J = ion flux, M = molar mass, ρ = density, N<sub>A</sub> = 6.022×10²³ | nm/min or µm/min |
| 3 | **A** = 1 − $\dfrac{R_{\text{lat}}}{R_{\text{vert}}}$ | Anisotropy: 0 = isotropic, 1 = perfectly directional | dimensionless |
| 4 | **S** = $\dfrac{ER_{\text{film}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}}$ | Selectivity. S > 10 preferred for practical lithography. | dimensionless |
| 5 | **λ<sub>mfp</sub>** = $\dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, P \, \sigma}$ | Mean free path of gas molecules (σ ≈ 0.3 nm² for Ar) | m |
| 6 | **λ<sub>D</sub>** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, k_B T_e}{n_e \, e^2}}$ | Debye length — characteristic sheath thickness scale | m |
| 7 | **ω<sub>p</sub>** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{n_e \, e^2}{\varepsilon_0 \, m_e}}$ | Electron plasma frequency (rad/s) | rad/s |
| 8 | **Y(θ)** = Y(0) $\cos^{-f}\theta$ | Angular dependence of sputter yield; f ≈ 1.5 – 2 for many materials | dimensionless |
| 9 | **v<sub>etch</sub>** = $\dfrac{h_0}{\Delta t}$ | Practical etch rate = etched depth / etch time | nm/s |
| 10 | **v<sub>Bohm</sub>** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{k_B T_e}{m_i}}$ | Bohm velocity — ion velocity at sheath edge | m/s |
| 11 | **n<sub>is</sub>** ≈ n<sub>0</sub> exp(−½) | Ion density at plasma–sheath boundary (Bohm criterion) | m⁻³ |
| 12 | **E<sub>th</sub>** = $\dfrac{U_s}{\gamma(1-\gamma)}$; $\gamma = \dfrac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1+M_2)^2}$ | Threshold energy for sputtering (M₁, M₂ = ion and target atomic masses) | eV |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** The Debye length formula uses **electron temperature T<sub>e</sub>** in eV (or K with k<sub>B</sub>) but **NOT ion temperature**. Mixing them gives wrong answers by 2–3 orders of magnitude.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Manufacturing:** IBE for trimming magnetic read heads; RIE for sub-100 nm gate definition.
- **MEMS:** DRIE (Bosch) for silicon deep structures (gyroscopes, accelerometers).
- **Optoelectronics:** IBE for distributed-feedback (DFB) laser gratings.
- **Failure Analysis:** IBE for layer-by-layer material removal in SEM/FIB cross-sectioning.
- **Photovoltaics:** Plasma texturing of mc-Si wafers for light trapping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Etch Rate from Sputter Yield

We derive the closed-form relationship between measured etch rate (nm/min) and fundamental sputtering parameters.

### Starting Definition

The **sputter yield Y** is defined as the average number of target atoms ejected per incident ion:

$$Y = \frac{\text{Number of target atoms removed}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

### Step 1: Express "atoms removed" as moles and mass

Let the ion flux (ions arriving per unit area per unit time) be $J$ [ions·m⁻²·s⁻¹]. In time $\Delta t$, the number of incident ions per unit area is $J \cdot \Delta t$. Hence the number of target atoms removed per unit area is $Y \cdot J \cdot \Delta t$.

Converting to moles: divide by Avogadro's number $N_A$.

$$\text{moles removed per unit area} = \frac{Y \cdot J \cdot \Delta t}{N_A}$$

### Step 2: Convert moles to mass using molar mass M

The mass removed per unit area $m_{rem}$ [kg/m²] is:

$$m_{rem} = \frac{Y \cdot J \cdot \Delta t \cdot M}{N_A}$$

### Step 3: Convert mass per area to thickness using density ρ

The removed thickness $h$ [m] = mass per area / density:

$$h = \frac{m_{rem}}{\rho} = \frac{Y \cdot J \cdot \Delta t \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$$

### Step 4: Differentiate to get the etch rate ER

Etch rate is thickness per unit time:

$$ER = \frac{dh}{d(\Delta t)} = \frac{Y \cdot J \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Derived Equation:** $\boxed{ER = \dfrac{Y \cdot J \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}}$
> 
> This shows etch rate is **linear in J** and **Y** but **inversely proportional to ρ** — heavy, dense materials (gold, platinum) etch *slower* than light, low-density materials (aluminum, photoresist) for a given Y. This is why IBE is slow on noble metals.

### Sanity Check (Plug Numbers)

For **Si etched by 1 keV Ar⁺**:
- Y ≈ 0.5 atoms/ion
- J = 1 mA/cm² = $6.25 \times 10^{15}$ ions/cm²·s
- M(Si) = 28 g/mol
- ρ(Si) = 2.33 g/cm³
- N<sub>A</sub> = 6.022 × 10²³

$$ER = \frac{0.5 \times 6.25\times 10^{15} \times 28}{2.33 \times 6.022\times 10^{23}}$$

$$ER = \frac{8.75 \times 10^{16}}{1.403 \times 10^{24}} = 6.24 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s}$$

$$ER \approx 37 \text{ nm/min}$$

This is *exactly* the order of magnitude measured in commercial IBE systems — confirming the derivation. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sputter Yield Angular Dependence

For a collimated ion beam at incidence angle $\theta$ (measured from surface normal), the sputter yield is observed to follow:

$$Y(\theta) = Y(0) \, [\cos\theta]^{-f}$$

### Step 1: At normal incidence (θ = 0°)

$$Y(0) = Y(0) \cdot [\cos 0]^{-f} = Y(0) \cdot 1 = Y(0) \checkmark$$

### Step 2: At grazing incidence (θ → 90°)

$\cos\theta \to 0$, so $[\cos\theta]^{-f} \to \infty$. The formula *predicts* infinite yield at grazing, which is **physically wrong** — in reality, Y peaks at $\theta_{\text{peak}} \approx 60°-80°$ and then drops to zero at 90°.

### Step 3: Empirical Correction (Yamamura Model)

A better fit is the **Yamamura correction** that limits the divergence:

$$Y(\theta) = \frac{Y(0)}{\cos\theta} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\sigma_s \, \cos\theta}{\text{const}}\right)^{-f}$$

where $\sigma_s$ is the surface binding energy factor. The peak occurs where $\dfrac{dY}{d\theta} = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> The angular dependence is *critical* in IBE: at $\theta \approx 75°$, the yield can be **2 – 3× higher** than at normal incidence. This is why IBE produces *tapered* sidewalls by default, not vertical ones.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sputter Threshold Energy (E<sub>th</sub>)

A target atom can only be sputtered if it gains enough energy from the collision cascade to overcome the surface binding energy $U_s$. We derive the minimum incident ion energy $E_{th}$.

### Step 1: Maximum energy transfer in elastic collision

When an ion of mass $M_1$ and energy $E$ strikes a stationary surface atom of mass $M_2$, the maximum fraction of energy transferable to the target is:

$$\gamma = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Derivation from two-body elastic collision kinematics (center-of-mass + lab frame). The peak $\gamma = 1$ occurs when $M_1 = M_2$ (mass matching maximizes momentum transfer — *this is why Ar is used to sputter Si efficiently*).

### Step 2: Threshold condition

The sputter event occurs when the energy delivered to the surface atom **exceeds** the surface binding energy $U_s$:

$$\gamma \cdot (E - E') \geq U_s$$

where $E'$ is the residual energy of the primary ion after the collision. For first-order approximation near threshold ($E' \approx E - U_s/\gamma$):

$$\gamma \cdot (E - E_{th}) = U_s$$

### Step 3: Solve for E<sub>th</sub>

$$E_{th} = \frac{U_s}{\gamma(1-\gamma)} = \frac{U_s (M_1 + M_2)^2}{4 M_1 M_2 (1-\gamma)}$$

For **Ar⁺ on Si** ($M_1 = 40, M_2 = 28, U_s \approx 4.7$ eV):

$$\gamma = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 28}{(40+28)^2} = \frac{4480}{4624} \approx 0.969$$

$$E_{th} = \frac{4.7}{0.969 \times (1-0.969)} = \frac{4.7}{0.0301} \approx 156 \text{ eV}$$

This matches the experimentally measured threshold of **~150 eV** for Ar⁺/Si. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sputter Yield Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ instead of the pipe character for absolute-value column entries.

| Target Material | $\rho$ [g/cm³] | $U_s$ [eV] | $\gamma$ (Ar⁺) | $E_{th}$ [eV] | Y @ 500 eV | Y @ 1 keV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Photoresist (AZ-1350) | 1.2 | 3.0 | 0.95 | 60 | 1.5 | 2.5 |
| Si | 2.33 | 4.7 | 0.97 | 23 | 0.4 | 0.55 |
| SiO₂ | 2.27 | 5.0 | 0.97 | 25 | 0.3 | 0.5 |
| Al | 2.70 | 3.4 | 0.95 | 13 | 1.0 | 1.8 |
| Au | 19.3 | 3.8 | 0.73 | 14 | 1.5 | 2.7 |
| Pt | 21.5 | 5.9 | 0.71 | 23 | 0.6 | 1.4 |
| GaAs | 5.32 | 5.0 | 0.95 | 25 | 0.5 | 0.9 |

**Key Insight:** Although Au has *higher* Y than Si, its density is **8.3× higher** — so the etch rate (which scales as Y/ρ) is comparable. This explains why IBE of noble metals is *not catastrophically slow* despite their chemical inertness.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Sheath Potential in RIE

The **DC self-bias voltage V<sub>dc</sub>** that develops on the powered electrode in RIE determines ion bombardment energy. We derive its scaling.

### Step 1: Charge balance at floating surfaces

The powered (cathode) electrode and grounded (anode) electrode are at different areas $A_c$ and $A_g$. Because the ion current to each must be balanced by electron current (steady state), the ratio of sheath voltages is:

$$\frac{V_{dc}}{V_{g}} = \left(\frac{A_{g}}{A_{c}}\right)^{q}$$

where $q \approx 1.5 - 2$ (empirical). For typical RIE reactors with $A_c \ll A_g$, the cathode sheath is much higher in voltage.

### Step 2: Approximation for asymmetric RIE

When the grounded area is much larger than the powered area (which is the standard RIE geometry):

$$V_{dc} \approx \frac{V_{pp}}{2} \cdot \frac{A_g}{A_c}$$

where $V_{pp}$ is the peak-to-peak RF amplitude. For $V_{pp} = 1000$ V, $A_g/A_c = 20$:

$$V_{dc} \approx 500 \times 20 = 10\,000 \text{ V}$$

This is reduced in practice by impedance matching and capacitive coupling, giving typical operating $V_{dc} = 200 - 800$ V.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** The powered electrode becomes *negative* (cations are accelerated *toward* it). The substrate is placed on the powered electrode so that *positive ions are pulled downward* — perfect for vertical etching.

## 4.4 Etch Profile Comparison

| Etch Type | Profile | Anisotropy A | Selectivity S |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wet Chemical Etch | Lateral undercut (isotropic) | **A ≈ 0** | High |
| Barrel Plasma Etch | Slight undercut | **A ≈ 0.1 – 0.3** | Very High |
| RIE | Near-vertical with slight slope | **A ≈ 0.85 – 0.95** | Moderate |
| DRIE (Bosch) | Vertical, scalloped sidewall | **A ≈ 0.98** | Moderate |
| IBE | Slightly tapered | **A ≈ 0.95 – 1.0** | Low |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Set B)]

**Define Ion Beam Etching. List three advantages and two disadvantages of IBE compared to wet chemical etching.**

**Model Answer (10-line answer expected, ~3-mark weightage):**

**Definition:** Ion Beam Etching (IBE) is a *physical dry-etching* process in which a collimated beam of energetic inert gas ions (typically **Ar⁺** at **0.5 – 1.5 keV**) is directed at a substrate to remove material purely by *momentum transfer* (kinetic sputtering), with no chemical reaction involved.

**Advantages over wet etching:**
1. **Perfect anisotropy** — no lateral undercut, ideal for sub-micron features.
2. **Dry process** — no stiction, no drying-induced defects, no hazardous acids.
3. **Material universality** — can etch noble metals (Au, Pt), compound semiconductors (GaAs, InP), and ceramics that resist wet chemistry.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Low selectivity** — photoresist erodes almost as fast as the target material.
2. **Slow etch rate** — typically **30 – 100 nm/min**, much slower than wet etching of Si in KOH.
3. *(Optional)* Surface damage and trenching artefacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students who mention "momentum transfer" and quote a numerical ion energy earn the full 3 marks. Vague answers mentioning only "ions etch material" receive 1.5 marks.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

**Distinguish between *Barrel Plasma Etching* and *Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)* with respect to mechanism, anisotropy, and selectivity.**

**Model Answer (tabular format recommended):**

| Parameter | Barrel Plasma Etching | RIE |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Purely *chemical* (free-radical attack); no ion bombardment of substrate | *Chemical + physical* (ion-assisted radical attack) |
| Electrode where sample is placed | In a *floating* quartz boat (no ion flux) | On the *powered cathode* (high-energy ion flux) |
| Anisotropy | **Poor** (isotropic, lateral undercut) | **Excellent** (highly anisotropic) |
| Selectivity | **Very high** (no physical damage to mask) | **Moderate** (physical sputtering erodes mask) |
| Etch rate | Moderate | High |
| Typical applications | Stripping photoresist, isotropically thinning Si | Gate etch, MEMS structure definition |

**Conclusion:** Barrel PE is *chemical and selective* but *isotropic*; RIE is *physical-chemical and directional* but *less selective*. RIE is preferred for fine pattern transfer.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Each Module has a 14-mark question. Students answer *one* out of *two* provided choices. Each choice has two sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** [14 Marks] — *(Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Module 3 Q7a]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the working principle of Ion Beam Etching with a neat sketch of a Kaufman ion source. Discuss the role of the screen grid, accelerator grid, and neutralizer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
Ion Beam Etching (IBE) is a *physical dry etching* technique where a beam of accelerated inert gas ions (Ar⁺) is used to sputter material from a substrate. The "sputter yield" Y defines the etch efficiency.

**Step 2 — Kaufman Ion Source Construction (2 marks):**
The Kaufman source consists of:
- A **cylindrical discharge chamber** with a hot **tungsten filament cathode** at one end.
- A **permanent magnet** that confines electrons in helical paths to enhance ionization efficiency.
- A **gas inlet** for Ar.
- **Two parallel grids** at the open end facing the substrate:
  - **Screen grid** (positive, + V<sub>screen</sub>) — extracts ions from the plasma.
  - **Accelerator grid** (negative, −V<sub>acc</sub>) — accelerates the extracted ions to the desired energy and collimates them.

**Step 3 — Working Sequence (2 marks):**
1. Ar gas is fed at **1 – 5 sccm** into the chamber held at **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁴ Torr**.
2. Filament is heated to ~**2400 K**; thermionic electrons are emitted.
3. A discharge voltage (**~40 V**) is applied between cathode and anode; electrons ionize Ar to form plasma: **Ar + e⁻ → Ar⁺ + 2e⁻**.
4. The positive screen grid attracts the Ar⁺ ions through its apertures; the negative accelerator grid then accelerates them to **0.5 – 1.5 keV** and collimates them into a near-parallel beam.
5. The collimated Ar⁺ beam strikes the substrate, sputters material, and the etch products deposit on the cold shroud.

**Step 4 — Role of Neutralizer (1 mark):**
A second filament (the **neutralizer**) is positioned near the substrate. It emits low-energy electrons into the ion beam to **neutralize the positive space charge** of the Ar⁺ beam. Without this, the beam would self-repel (space-charge blow-up) and diverge, destroying the directional pattern transfer.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
IBE produces highly anisotropic etching ideal for noble metals and sub-micron features, with the Kaufman source being the standard hardware for reliable, well-collimated ion beam generation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A silicon wafer is etched by 1 keV Ar⁺ ions. The sputter yield is Y = 0.5 atoms/ion and the ion current density is J = 1 mA/cm². Calculate the etch rate in nm/min. Given: M(Si) = 28 g/mol, ρ(Si) = 2.33 g/cm³, N<sub>A</sub> = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Y = 0.5 atoms/ion
- J = 1 mA/cm² = $10^{-3}$ A/cm² = $10^{-3}$ C/(cm²·s)
- M = 28 g/mol
- ρ = 2.33 g/cm³
- N<sub>A</sub> = 6.022 × 10²³

**Step 1: Convert ion current density to ion flux (ions/cm²·s):**

$$J_{\text{ions}} = \frac{J}{e} = \frac{10^{-3} \text{ A/cm}^2}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}} = 6.24 \times 10^{15} \text{ ions/cm}^2\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Apply the etch rate formula:**

$$ER = \frac{Y \cdot J_{\text{ions}} \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$$

$$ER = \frac{0.5 \times 6.24 \times 10^{15} \times 28}{2.33 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

**Step 3: Numerator:**

$$0.5 \times 6.24 \times 10^{15} \times 28 = 8.736 \times 10^{16} \text{ g/cm}^2\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Denominator:**

$$2.33 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.403 \times 10^{24} \text{ /cm}^3$$

**Step 5: Divide:**

$$ER = \frac{8.736 \times 10^{16}}{1.403 \times 10^{24}} = 6.225 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to nm/min:**

$$ER = 6.225 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm/s} \times 10^{7} \text{ nm/cm} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 37.4 \text{ nm/min}$$

$$\boxed{ER \approx 37 \text{ nm/min}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The etch rate of Si under these conditions is ~37 nm/min, consistent with practical IBE system measurements.

---

### **Question B** [14 Marks] — *(Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Module 3 Q7b]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the principle of plasma etching. Discuss the formation of the plasma sheath and explain how the sheath potential creates the anisotropy in RIE.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plasma Definition and Generation (2 marks):**
Plasma is the *fourth state of matter* — a quasi-neutral gas of ions, electrons, and neutral species. In a plasma etcher:
- Process gas (e.g., CF₄, SF₆) is admitted into a vacuum chamber at **10 – 1000 mTorr**.
- A **13.56 MHz RF voltage** is applied between two parallel electrodes.
- Free electrons gain kinetic energy from the RF field and ionize gas molecules through inelastic collisions.
- A steady-state plasma forms with $T_e \approx 3 - 10$ eV, $T_i \approx 0.03$ eV.

**Step 2 — Sheath Formation (2 marks):**
When the plasma contacts any surface, *electrons* (much more mobile than ions due to their small mass) are lost first to the surface. The surface thus acquires a net **negative charge**, repelling further electrons and attracting ions. A thin positive space-charge layer — the **plasma sheath** — forms, of characteristic thickness $\lambda_D$ (Debye length):

$$\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}}$$

A **DC sheath potential V<sub>s</sub>** (10 – 1000 V) develops self-consistently across this layer.

**Step 3 — Ion Acceleration and Anisotropy (2 marks):**
The electric field in the sheath is **perpendicular to the surface**. Positive ions entering the sheath are accelerated to the surface at **normal incidence** with energy ≈ eV<sub>s</sub>. This vertical ion flux:

- Sputters away any *passivating* polymer layer on sidewalls.
- Damages the bottom surface, enhancing radical chemisorption and chemical etch rate there.
- Leaves sidewalls (where ions do *not* strike) chemically etched only by *slow-diffusing* radicals.

Net result: **vertical etch rate ≫ lateral etch rate**, i.e., $A = 1 - R_{lat}/R_{vert} \to 1$ (anisotropic profile).

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The plasma sheath is the *fundamental engine of anisotropy* in plasma-based dry etching. Without it, etching would be purely chemical and isotropic.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a CF₄ plasma used to etch silicon, the Debye length is calculated to be 50 µm at electron density 10¹⁰ cm⁻³. Estimate the electron temperature. Given: ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m, k<sub>B</sub> = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- λ<sub>D</sub> = 50 µm = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- n<sub>e</sub> = 10¹⁰ cm⁻³ = $10^{16}$ m⁻³
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m
- k<sub>B</sub> = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K
- e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C

**Step 1: Rearrange the Debye length formula for T<sub>e</sub>:**

$$\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\lambda_D^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}$$

$$T_e = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot n_e \cdot e^2}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot k_B}$$

**Step 2: Calculate numerator:**

$$\lambda_D^2 = (5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$

$$n_e e^2 = 10^{16} \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 = 10^{16} \times 2.566 \times 10^{-38} = 2.566 \times 10^{-22}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.566 \times 10^{-22} = 6.415 \times 10^{-31}$$

**Step 3: Calculate denominator:**

$$\varepsilon_0 k_B = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 1.222 \times 10^{-34}$$

**Step 4: Divide:**

$$T_e = \frac{6.415 \times 10^{-31}}{1.222 \times 10^{-34}} = 5250 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Convert to eV (optional, for sanity):**

$$T_e \text{ [eV]} = \frac{k_B T_e}{e} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 5250}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.52 \text{ eV}$$

**Verification:** This electron temperature (~4.5 eV) lies comfortably in the expected range for a 13.56 MHz RF plasma (3 – 10 eV). ✓

$$\boxed{T_e \approx 5250 \text{ K} \approx 4.5 \text{ eV}}$$

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students *commonly* lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IBE with RIE:** IBE is *purely physical* (Ar⁺ in a separate Kaufman gun); RIE is *chemical + physical* (sample sits in reactive plasma). Mixing them up costs 2 – 3 marks.
> 2. **Wrong temperature in Debye length formula:** Always use **electron temperature T<sub>e</sub>** (in K or eV), never ion temperature. Mistake costs full 7 marks on Part (b) numericals.
> 3. **Units mismatch in etch rate problems:** Convert mA/cm² to ions/cm²·s by dividing by **e (1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹)**. Forgetting this conversion gives a 25-orders-of-magnitude error. Marks lost: 3 – 4.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the "neutralizer"** in IBE sketches. The neutralizer filament is *essential hardware* — examiners explicitly look for it (1 mark).
> 5. **Stating that plasma etching is purely chemical:** It is *ion-assisted chemical*. Saying "purely chemical" loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Sputter yield vs. etch rate confusion:** Sputter yield Y is *dimensionless* (atoms/ion); etch rate ER has units of *length/time*. Multiply Y by J/ρ to get ER. Confusing them is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **IBE = physical dry etch.** Uses Ar⁺ (inert), no chemical reaction, purely *momentum transfer* sputtering.
- ✅ **IBE is anisotropic but has low selectivity.** Y ≈ 0.5 – 2.5 for most materials at 1 keV.
- ✅ **Kaufman ion source** has: filament, discharge chamber, **screen grid (positive)**, **accelerator grid (negative)**, and a **neutralizer filament**.
- ✅ **Sputter yield formula:** $Y = \frac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ions}}$ — peak at θ ≈ 70° (not 0°!).
- ✅ **Etch rate formula:** $ER = \frac{Y \cdot J \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$ — linear in J and Y, inverse in ρ.
- ✅ **Threshold energy:** $E_{th} = \frac{U_s (M_1+M_2)^2}{4 M_1 M_2 (1-\gamma)}$; mass matching (M₁ ≈ M₂) maximizes transfer.
- ✅ **Plasma = 4th state of matter**, generated by 13.56 MHz RF discharge at 10 – 1000 mTorr.
- ✅ **Plasma sheath** = positive space-charge layer near surfaces; thickness ≈ Debye length.
- ✅ **Debye length:** $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}}$ — uses **T<sub>e</sub>**, not T<sub>i</sub>.
- ✅ **RIE** = sample on *powered cathode*; high ion energy, high anisotropy, moderate selectivity.
- ✅ **Barrel PE** = sample on *floating quartz boat*; pure chemical, isotropic, very high selectivity.
- ✅ **Chemical etch (e.g., Si + F• → SiF₄↑)** provides *selectivity*; *ion bombardment* provides *anisotropy*.
- ✅ **DRIE (Bosch)** = alternating SF₆ etch / C₄F₈ passivation cycles → near-vertical high-aspect-ratio silicon structures.
- ✅ **Damage in IBE:** amorphizes top **5 – 20 nm** of substrate.
- ✅ **Mean free path:** $\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} P \sigma}$ — must exceed beam path for IBE.
- ✅ **Valuation rule:** always *convert current density J from mA/cm² to ions/cm²·s by dividing by e*.
- ✅ **Valuation rule:** always *quote numerical energy/temperature* in eV *and* K to demonstrate mastery.

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Ion Beam Etching & Plasma Etching (OEICT612 — KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of DRIE ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** is a highly anisotropic, plasma-based dry etching technique that enables the fabrication of deep, vertical, high-aspect-ratio microstructures in semiconductor substrates — most commonly in single-crystal silicon. It is a subclass of Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) where the process is optimized to achieve etch depths typically ranging from **>10 µm to >600 µm** with aspect ratios (depth-to-width) often exceeding **20:1**, and in optimized systems, up to **100:1**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** DRIE is the key enabling technology for modern MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) fabrication. It is used to realize inertial sensors (accelerometers, gyroscopes), pressure sensors, microfluidic channels, through-silicon vias (TSVs), and 3D integrated circuit interconnects. The process is governed by two coupled physical mechanisms operating in a low-pressure plasma environment (**typically 10–100 mTorr**): 1. **Chemical etching** by reactive neutral radicals (e.g., F• from SF₆). 2. **Physical (ion) bombardment** by accelerated positive ions (e.g., SFₓ⁺, Ar⁺) directed normal to the wafer surface by a bias voltage. The combination of these two — **chemically assisted ion-beam etching (CAIBE)** / **reactive ion etching (RIE)** physics — produces the directional, near-vertical etching required for MEMS. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Snow Plow + Acid Sprayer" Machine:** > Imagine you are carving a deep, narrow trench into a thick block of ice. A simple water jet would cut, but it would also dissolve the sidewalls, giving you a wide V-shape. To make a perfectly vertical trench, you need a two-pronged attack: > 1. A **chemical sprayer** (F• radicals) that melts the bottom ice quickly. > 2. A **directional snow plow** (ion bombardment) that fires ions straight down, knocking the melted ice off the bottom of the trench. > > Now, to keep the sidewalls from being eaten by the chemical sprayer, you spray them with a **"Teflon-like" protective foam** (passivation polymer). But you only protect the sides — the ions strike the floor and knock the foam away there, so the chemical only attacks the bottom. > > **That is exactly what DRIE does in silicon.** ## 1.3 Why DRIE is Essential in MEMS Traditional wet KOH/TMAH etching of silicon is limited to **<54.74°** sidewall angles (crystal plane dependent) and cannot produce arbitrary geometries. DRIE overcomes this: - **Vertical sidewalls** independent of crystal orientation. - **High aspect ratios** enabling dense, compact MEMS designs. - **CMOS compatibility** — works on metallized wafers. - **Wafer-level batch processing** — high throughput. ## 1.4 The Two Principal DRIE Variants > [!IMPORTANT] > All KTU board questions on DRIE are based on these **two main process variants**. You must know both: | Variant | Mechanism | Cycle Type | Sidewall | |---|---|---|---| | **Bosch Process** (Time-Multiplexed) | Alternating etch (SF₆) and passivation (C₄F₈) | Cyclic (pulsed) | Scalloped | | **Cryogenic Process** (Low-Temperature) | Continuous SF₆/O₂ at cryogenic T (~ −110 °C) | Continuous | Smooth | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Bosch Process (Time-Multiplexed / "Pulsed") DRIE Invented at Robert Bosch GmbH (1994, Laermer & Schilp), the Bosch process became the workhorse of MEMS DRIE. It decouples the competing etch and passivation chemistries **in time** rather than in space. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Cycle Logic **Step 1 — Deposition (Passivation) Phase** [Duration: ~5–12 s] - Gas: **C₄F₈** (octafluorocyclopentene), introduced at ~10–50 sccm. - Plasma dissociates C₄F₈ into CF₂ radicals and ions. - A **fluorocarbon polymer** (–[CF₂]ₙ–) deposits *conformally* (uniformly) on **all exposed surfaces** — bottom, sidewalls, and mask. - Thickness of polymer: **~10–100 nm**. - *Why conformally?* Because during passivation, the ion-bombardment energy is **low** (or zero bias); radicals stick wherever they land. **Step 2 — Etch Phase** [Duration: ~5–15 s] - Gas switched to **SF₆** (sulfur hexafluoride), ~30–200 sccm. - Plasma generates F• radicals (chemical etchant) and SFₓ⁺ ions (physical etchant). - Ions are accelerated **vertically downward** by the self-induced DC bias (**V_DC ≈ −50 V to −300 V**). - The **vertical ion flux** physically sputters the polymer off horizontal surfaces (trench bottom) but leaves the polymer intact on the **vertical sidewalls** (ions strike sidewalls at glancing angles). - The freshly exposed silicon at the bottom reacts with F•: $$\text{Si (solid)} + 4\,\text{F}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{SiF}_{4}\ \text{(gas)}$$ $$\text{SiF}_{4}\ \text{(gas)} \uparrow \ \text{(volatile, evacuated)}$$ **Step 3 — Repeat** - Cycle times are typically 5–15 s per loop, and the process repeats for 50–500 cycles to achieve the desired depth. ### 2.1.2 The "Scallop" Signature > [!WARNING] > **HIGH-YIELD EXAM POINT:** The cyclic nature of Bosch produces a characteristic **sidewall ripple called "scallops"**. The amplitude of the scallop ($S_a$) is given by: > $$S_a \approx \frac{(ER_{Si} - ER_{poly}) \cdot t_{etch}}{2}$$ > where $ER_{Si}$ is the silicon etch rate, $ER_{poly}$ is the polymer etch rate during the etch phase, and $t_{etch}$ is the etch half-cycle time. **Smaller cycle times = smaller scallops = smoother sidewalls**, but lower throughput. ## 2.2 The Cryogenic DRIE Process Developed at IBM and Univ. Twente, this process achieves **smooth sidewalls** by working at very low substrate temperatures. ### 2.2.1 Operating Principle - **Wafer temperature: −110 °C to −130 °C**, achieved using liquid-nitrogen-cooled substrate electrode. - Continuous plasma of **SF₆ + O₂** (no cycling). - At cryogenic temperatures, the reaction between F• and Si is suppressed on the sidewalls because the **sidewall spontaneously oxidizes** (chemisorbed O atoms from O₂) and forms a **SiOₓFᵧ "quasi-passivation" layer**. - The bottom receives vertical ion bombardment, which **sputters off** the oxide/fluoride layer and exposes Si to F•, while sidewalls are protected by the low-volatility, ion-bombarded SiOₓFᵧ. - **Sidewall taper control** is achieved by adjusting O₂ flow and temperature — higher O₂ = more tapered (negative sidewall); less O₂ = more bowed (positive sidewall). ## 2.3 Key Process Parameters and Their Influence | Parameter | Bosch DRIE Effect | Cryogenic DRIE Effect | |---|---|---| | **RF Power** (13.56 MHz) | ↑ Power → ↑ etch rate, ↑ ion energy → ↑ sidewall damage | ↑ Power → ↑ etch rate, ↑ isotropy | | **Chamber Pressure** | ↓ Pressure → ↑ anisotropy, ↑ ion directionality | ↓ Pressure → ↑ ion energy | | **SF₆ Flow** | ↑ Flow → ↑ etch rate (saturates) | ↑ Flow → ↑ etch rate | | **C₄F₈ / O₂ Flow** | ↑ C₄F₈ → ↑ passivation, ↓ etch rate, more vertical | ↑ O₂ → ↑ sidewall protection, more taper | | **Substrate Temp.** | Usually held at 10–40 °C | Critical: −110 °C to −130 °C | | **Coil/Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) Power** | Independent control of ion flux vs ion energy (decoupled) | Same — ICP source decouples plasma density from bias | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THESE EQUATIONS** — they appear in numerical problems. | Quantity | Formula | Description | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **Etch Rate (ER)** | $$ER = \frac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$$ | Depth removed per unit time | $\mu m/min$ | | **Selectivity (S)** | $$S = \frac{ER_{Si}}{ER_{mask}}$$ | Si etch rate ÷ mask etch rate | dimensionless | | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $$AR = \frac{D}{W}$$ | Depth ÷ lateral width | dimensionless | | **ARDE (Cox–Stewart Model)** | $$ER(AR) = \frac{ER_0}{1 + \left(\dfrac{AR}{AR_0}\right)^n}$$ | Etch rate drops with AR | $\mu m/min$ | | **Bosch Scallop Amplitude** | $$S_a = \frac{(ER_{Si} - ER_{poly})\,t_{etch}}{2}$$ | Sidewall roughness | nm | | **Mask Erosion** | $$t_{mask} = \frac{ER_{mask}}{ER_{Si}} \cdot D$$ | Required mask thickness | $\mu m$ | | **Plasma Sheath Potential (approx.)** | $$V_s \approx V_p - V_{DC}$$ | Voltage drop across sheath | V | | **DC Bias Relation** | $$V_{DC} \propto \left(\frac{P_{RF}}{P}\right)^{1/2}$$ | RF power & pressure control | V | | **RIE Lag** | $$\Delta ER = ER(AR_1) - ER(AR_2)$$ | Etch rate difference between features | $\mu m/min$ | | **Total Process Time** | $$t_{total} = \frac{D}{ER_{avg}}$$ | Time to etch depth D | min | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** When writing the ARDE formula in a KTU answer booklet, always use $AR$ (or simply "AR"), **not** the vertical bar "$|$". In LaTeX use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead. ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **MEMS Inertial Sensors** — STMicroelectronics LIS2DH accelerometer: DRIE defines the proof-mass and suspension beams. - **TSVs in 3D-IC Stacking** — DRIE + liner/seed deposition + Cu electroplating creates vertical electrical interconnects through 50–300 µm Si dies. - **Microfluidics** — DRIE creates channel walls for lab-on-chip devices. - **Photonic Crystals & Microlens Arrays** — high AR holes for optical MEMS. - **Pressure Sensor Diaphragms** — DRIE thins Si to controlled thickness. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Process Time for a Target Etch Depth > **Problem:** A Bosch DRIE process etches silicon at an average rate of $ER_{avg} = 2.5\ \mu m/min$. How long will it take to etch a through-wafer feature in a $300\ \mu m$ thick silicon wafer, including a 20% over-etch time? **Step 1 — Identify the required depth.** The target etch depth $D$ must equal the wafer thickness plus the over-etch margin: $$D = D_{wafer} + D_{over}$$ **Step 2 — Express the over-etch in absolute units.** Over-etch is conventionally given as a percentage of the target depth: $$D_{over} = 0.20 \times D_{wafer}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerically.** $$D = 300\ \mu m + (0.20)(300\ \mu m) = 300\ \mu m + 60\ \mu m = 360\ \mu m$$ **Step 4 — Apply the total time equation.** $$t_{total} = \frac{D}{ER_{avg}} = \frac{360\ \mu m}{2.5\ \mu m/min}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate.** $$\boxed{t_{total} = 144\ \text{min} = 2\ \text{h}\ 24\ \text{min}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Selectivity and Required Mask Thickness > **Problem:** Si is etched at $ER_{Si} = 3\ \mu m/min$, and the photoresist mask is etched at $ER_{mask} = 60\ nm/min$. Compute the selectivity. If the desired Si etch depth is $D = 100\ \mu m$, what is the minimum starting mask thickness $t_{mask}$? **Step 1 — Convert units for consistency.** $$ER_{mask} = 60\ nm/min = 0.060\ \mu m/min$$ **Step 2 — Compute the selectivity $S$.** By definition: $$S = \frac{ER_{Si}}{ER_{mask}} = \frac{3\ \mu m/min}{0.060\ \mu m/min}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate.** $$S = 50$$ So the **selectivity is 50:1** — this is typical for Si/PR (photoresist) in DRIE. **Step 4 — Compute the minimum mask thickness.** Since selectivity $S = D_{Si}/D_{mask}$, the mask loss during a depth $D$ of Si etch is: $$D_{mask} = \frac{D_{Si}}{S} = \frac{100\ \mu m}{50} = 2\ \mu m$$ **Step 5 — Apply the engineering safety factor.** The actual starting mask thickness must exceed the loss by a factor (typically 1.5–2× for safety): $$t_{mask,min} = 1.5 \times D_{mask} = 1.5 \times 2\ \mu m = 3\ \mu m$$ $$\boxed{S = 50:1, \quad t_{mask,min} = 3\ \mu m \text{ (with safety factor)}}$$ > [!TIP] > This is why **hard masks** (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Al) with selectivities of 200:1 to 1000:1 are preferred for deep DRIE. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE) — Cox–Stewart Model > **Problem:** A DRIE process on bulk Si has $ER_0 = 3\ \mu m/min$ at $AR = 0$, with characteristic constants $AR_0 = 5$ and $n = 2$. Compute the etch rate at an aspect ratio of $AR = 10$ and $AR = 20$. Comment on the RIE lag. **Step 1 — State the ARDE model.** $$ER(AR) = \frac{ER_0}{1 + \left(\dfrac{AR}{AR_0}\right)^n}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $AR = 10$.** $$ER(10) = \frac{3}{1 + \left(\dfrac{10}{5}\right)^2} = \frac{3}{1 + 4} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60\ \mu m/min$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $AR = 20$.** $$ER(20) = \frac{3}{1 + \left(\dfrac{20}{5}\right)^2} = \frac{3}{1 + 16} = \frac{3}{17} \approx 0.176\ \mu m/min$$ **Step 4 — Compare.** $$ER(10) = 0.60\ \mu m/min \quad \text{vs.} \quad ER(20) = 0.176\ \mu m/min$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** $$\frac{ER(10)}{ER(20)} = \frac{0.60}{0.176} \approx 3.4$$ This is **RIE lag**: a deep, narrow trench etches **3.4× slower** than a wider, shallower trench at the same starting point. The cause is **transport-limited neutral flux**: F• radicals cannot diffuse to the bottom of a high-AR feature as easily as to a shallow one. > [!IMPORTANT] > This phenomenon creates a major design challenge — different feature sizes on the same die etch at different rates. Designers must compensate using dummy structures, uniform arrays, or grayscale lithography. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bosch Scallop Amplitude > **Problem:** In a Bosch process, $ER_{Si} = 3\ \mu m/min$, polymer removal rate $ER_{poly} = 0.6\ \mu m/min$, and etch half-cycle $t_{etch} = 6\ s$. Compute the scallop amplitude. **Step 1 — Convert units.** $$t_{etch} = 6\ s = 0.1\ \text{min}$$ **Step 2 — Compute net Si etch per cycle.** $$ER_{net} = ER_{Si} - ER_{poly} = 3 - 0.6 = 2.4\ \mu m/min$$ **Step 3 — Apply scallop formula.** $$S_a = \frac{ER_{net} \cdot t_{etch}}{2} = \frac{(2.4\ \mu m/min)(0.1\ \text{min})}{2} = \frac{0.24}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate.** $$\boxed{S_a = 0.12\ \mu m = 120\ \text{nm}}$$ This is a typical Bosch scallop — small enough for most MEMS, but problematic for optical MEMS. A cryogenic process is preferred when $S_a < 50\ nm$ is needed. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Trade-offs | Metric | Increase via... | Cost | |---|---|---| | **Etch Rate** | ↑ RF power, ↑ SF₆ flow, ↑ ICP power | ↓ Selectivity, ↑ mask erosion, ↑ sidewall damage | | **Selectivity** | ↑ C₄F₈ (Bosch), harder mask, ↓ bias | ↓ Etch rate, more polymer residue | | **Anisotropy (vertical sidewalls)** | ↑ bias voltage, ↓ pressure, ↑ C₄F₈ | ↑ RIE lag, ↓ etch rate at high AR | | **Sidewall Smoothness** | Cryogenic, ↓ cycle time, ↑ bias, post-etch anneal | ↓ throughput, more expensive tool | | **Mask Selectivity** | Use SiO₂/Si₃N₄/Al hard mask | Extra lithography & deposition steps | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Bosch Process — Cyclic Sidewall Evolution ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start of Cycle N"] --> B["Step 1: Passivation Phase
C4F8 gas injected
Plasma on, low bias"] B --> B1["Conformal fluorocarbon polymer
CF2n deposited on ALL surfaces
Sidewalls, bottom, mask"] B1 --> C["Step 2: Etch Phase
SF6 gas injected
Plasma on, high bias"] C --> C1["Vertical ions strike bottom
Knock off polymer from floor
F radicals attack exposed Si"] C1 --> C2["Sidewall polymer remains
Protected from F attack
Net vertical etching"] C2 --> D["Cycle N+1 starts
Repeat"] D --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The cycle "Passivation → Etch" repeats **N times**, where $N \approx D / (ER_{net} \cdot t_{cycle})$. Each cycle adds ~50–200 nm of Si depth and produces one visible "scallop" on the sidewall. ## 4.2 Cryogenic DRIE — Continuous Sidewall Protection ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Continuous Plasma: SF6 + O2"] --> B["Wafer Stage Cooled to -110 C"] B --> C["F radicals generated in plasma"] C --> D{"Ion direction
from bias"} D -->|Vertical ions hit bottom| E["Bottom: Si exposed
Ion sputtering removes
SiOxFy inhibitor
F attacks Si -> SiF4 gas"] D -->|Glancing ions on sidewall| F["Sidewall: SiOxFy
quasi-passivation
forms and stays
No F attack"] E --> G["Net: vertical etch
Smooth sidewall"] F --> G ``` ## 4.3 DRIE Reactor Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR RF1["RF Generator 1
13.56 MHz"] --> ICP["ICP Coil
Plasma Generation"] RF2["RF Generator 2
13.56 MHz"] --> BIAS["Substrate Electrode
DC Bias VDC"] GAS["MFC Gas Manifold
SF6 / C4F8 / O2 / Ar"] --> CHAMBER["Process Chamber
10 to 100 mTorr"] ICP --> CHAMBER BIAS --> CHAMBER CHAMBER --> PUMP["Turbo + Dry Pump
Vacuum"] CHAMBER --> WAFER["Wafer on
Cryo / Chilled Chuck"] CTRL["Process Controller"] --> RF1 CTRL --> RF2 CTRL --> GAS CTRL --> PUMP ``` ## 4.4 Sidewall Profile Evolution in Bosch Process ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["t = 0: Mask Opening
Width W, Depth 0"] --> S1["Cycle 1: Polymer coat
then isotropic F etch
Sidewall undercuts slightly"] S1 --> S2["Cycle 2: New polymer
then vertical etch
Scallop 1 visible"] S2 --> S3["Cycle 3 to N:
Scallops stack
Mean sidewall vertical"] S3 --> S4["Final: Deep trench
AR = D / W
Scallop amplitude Sa"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU 2024 diagrams:** You only need to draw labeled cross-sections of: (i) Bosch passivation, (ii) Bosch etch, and (iii) final scalloped profile. A flowchart equivalent is acceptable. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** **Define Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE). List its two main variants and state one application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 m):** DRIE is a dry, plasma-based etching process that combines chemical etching by reactive radicals with directional physical ion bombardment to produce **deep, vertical, high-aspect-ratio structures (>10 µm depth)** in silicon and other substrates, with aspect ratios commonly **>20:1**. - **Variants (1 m):** (i) **Bosch process** (time-multiplexed etch + passivation); (ii) **Cryogenic DRIE** (low-temperature continuous etch). - **Applications (0.5 m):** Bosch — TSVs in 3D-IC stacking; Cryogenic — optical MEMS microlens arrays, smooth-walled microfluidics. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **Explain the term "scallop" in the context of Bosch DRIE. How is the scallop amplitude reduced?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 m):** The scallop is a periodic sidewall roughness produced by the **time-multiplexed (alternating etch/passivation)** nature of the Bosch process. Each cycle produces a small lateral under-etch during the SF₆ step that is not fully recovered, leaving a ripple. - **Amplitude equation (0.5 m):** $S_a \approx (ER_{Si} - ER_{poly}) \cdot t_{etch}/2$. - **Reduction (1 m):** (i) Decrease $t_{etch}$ (shorter cycles); (ii) Lower $ER_{Si}$ or higher $ER_{poly}$ (more passivation); (iii) Use cryogenic DRIE for $S_a < 50\ nm$. ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Module Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Bosch Process Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Bosch process** for Deep Reactive Ion Etching. Explain the role of the two plasma chemistries and discuss the formation of sidewall scallops. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Bosch DRIE process etches silicon at an average rate of $2\ \mu m/min$ through a photoresist mask of selectivity 40:1. Calculate: **(i)** the required starting mask thickness to etch a $200\ \mu m$ deep trench, assuming a 50% safety margin, and **(ii)** the total etch time with a 15% over-etch. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks **Sketch (2 marks):** Draw two side-by-side cross-sections showing **passivation phase** (C₄F₈ → polymer coats walls) and **etch phase** (SF₆ → vertical ions knock polymer off bottom, F• etches Si). The final profile shows **scallops**. **Written Explanation (5 marks):** - **Passivation phase (1.5 m):** C₄F₈ plasma forms a fluorocarbon polymer (~10–100 nm) **conformally** on all surfaces. With low/no bias, radicals stick everywhere; no ion directionality. - **Etch phase (1.5 m):** SF₆ plasma generates F• radicals and SFₓ⁺ ions. High DC bias (V_DC = −50 to −300 V) accelerates ions **vertically**, knocking off the polymer from the **bottom** of the trench but leaving sidewall polymer intact. F• attacks exposed Si to form volatile SiF₄. - **Cycling (1 m):** The two steps alternate every 5–15 s for 50–500 cycles, producing a deep vertical trench. - **Scallops (1 m):** During each SF₆ step, the F radicals isotropically undercut the freshly deposited sidewall polymer by a small amount (a few hundred nm). This **lateral etching** is not recovered in the next passivation step, leaving a periodic ripple whose amplitude is $S_a = (ER_{Si} - ER_{poly})\,t_{etch}/2$. --- #### Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks **(i) Mask thickness (3.5 marks):** Mask loss for a $200\ \mu m$ Si etch: $$D_{mask} = \frac{D_{Si}}{S} = \frac{200\ \mu m}{40} = 5\ \mu m$$ With 50% safety margin: $$t_{mask,start} = 1.5 \times 5\ \mu m = \boxed{7.5\ \mu m}$$ **(ii) Total etch time (3.5 marks):** Required depth with 15% over-etch: $$D = D_{Si}(1 + 0.15) = 200\ \mu m \times 1.15 = 230\ \mu m$$ Total time: $$t_{total} = \frac{D}{ER_{avg}} = \frac{230\ \mu m}{2\ \mu m/min}$$ $$\boxed{t_{total} = 115\ \text{minutes} = 1\ \text{hour}\ 55\ \text{minutes}}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Cryogenic DRIE Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Alternative]** **(a)** Compare the **cryogenic DRIE process** with the **Bosch DRIE process** under the following heads: principle, sidewall quality, throughput, mask selectivity, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the **Cox–Stewart ARDE model**, $ER(AR) = ER_0 / [1 + (AR/AR_0)^n]$, compute the etch rate at aspect ratios $AR = 5, 10, 15$ given $ER_0 = 4\ \mu m/min$, $AR_0 = 4$, and $n = 1.5$. Plot (or tabulate) the trend and explain the practical consequence of ARDE in MEMS design. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table (5 marks — 1 m per head):** | Head | Cryogenic DRIE | Bosch DRIE | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Continuous SF₆/O₂ plasma at wafer T = −110 °C; chemical etch + cryo-deposited SiOₓFᵧ sidewall protection | Time-multiplexed: SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation in alternating cycles | | **Sidewall Quality** | **Smooth** (scallop-free, $S_a < 30\ nm$) | **Scalloped** ($S_a$ ~50–500 nm) | | **Throughput** | Moderate; continuous process | High; mature process, large installed base | | **Mask Selectivity** | Moderate (20:1 to 100:1); cryo cooling helps | High (50:1 to 200:1) with C₄F₈ passivation protecting the mask too | | **Typical Applications** | Optical MEMS, smooth microfluidic channels, photonic crystals, microlens arrays | TSVs, inertial sensors (gyros, accelerometers), deep trenches, mass production | **Key distinct points (2 marks):** - Cryogenic eliminates scallops but requires **complex wafer cooling** and tight **O₂/temperature control**. - Bosch is the **industry standard** for high-volume MEMS, while cryogenic is preferred for **research and smoothness-critical** applications. --- #### Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks **General formula:** $$ER(AR) = \frac{ER_0}{1 + \left(\dfrac{AR}{AR_0}\right)^n} = \frac{4\ \mu m/min}{1 + \left(\dfrac{AR}{4}\right)^{1.5}}$$ **Computation (5 marks):** For $AR = 5$: $$ER(5) = \frac{4}{1 + (5/4)^{1.5}} = \frac{4}{1 + (1.25)^{1.5}} = \frac{4}{1 + 1.398} = \frac{4}{2.398} \approx 1.668\ \mu m/min$$ For $AR = 10$: $$ER(10) = \frac{4}{1 + (10/4)^{1.5}} = \frac{4}{1 + (2.5)^{1.5}} = \frac{4}{1 + 3.953} = \frac{4}{4.953} \approx 0.808\ \mu m/min$$ For $AR = 15$: $$ER(15) = \frac{4}{1 + (15/4)^{1.5}} = \frac{4}{1 + (3.75)^{1.5}} = \frac{4}{1 + 7.265} = \frac{4}{8.265} \approx 0.484\ \mu m/min$$ **Tabulated Result (1 mark):** | Aspect Ratio $AR$ | Etch Rate $ER$ ($\mu m/min$) | |---|---| | 0 | 4.000 | | 5 | 1.668 | | 10 | 0.808 | | 15 | 0.484 | **Practical Consequence (1 mark):** The etch rate **drops sharply as AR increases** — by 8× from $AR=0$ to $AR=15$. This **RIE lag** causes features of different widths on the same die to etch at different rates, producing depth non-uniformity. Designers mitigate this by: (i) **using uniform-array dummy patterns**; (ii) **clumping isolated features**; (iii) employing **ARDE-aware process recipes** that extend etch time for high-AR features; (iv) using **grayscale lithography** to pre-compensate dimensions. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in DRIE questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "etch rate" with "selectivity".** Etch rate is $\mu m/min$ (absolute); selectivity is dimensionless (ratio). Examiners deduct 1 full mark if units are wrong. > > 2. **Forgetting to mention "scallops" in Bosch DRIE answers.** Any Bosch question that asks for "sidewall quality" without mentioning scallops loses 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Swapping etch and passivation gases.** C₄F₈ is the **passivation** gas; SF₆ is the **etch** gas. Reversing them = −2 marks minimum. > > 4. **Not stating the wafer temperature** for cryogenic DRIE (must be **−110 °C or below**). > > 5. **In ARDE numericals**, students often forget to **cube the ratio when $n=3$**, or use the wrong AR value. Always write the substitution step explicitly. > > 6. **Skipping units** in final answers — KTU examiners are strict; "5 µm" not just "5". ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — DRIE (Module 3)** - **DRIE Definition** = Plasma-based highly anisotropic etch achieving **>10 µm** vertical depths, AR **>20:1**. - **Two variants** = **Bosch** (cyclic) and **Cryogenic** (continuous at −110 °C). - **Bosch cycle:** C₄F₈ (passivation, 5–12 s) → SF₆ (etch, 5–15 s) → repeat. - **Bosch scallop amplitude:** $S_a = (ER_{Si} - ER_{poly})\,t_{etch}/2$. Reduce by **shorter cycles**. - **Cryogenic principle:** Low T forms SiOₓFᵧ passivation; vertical ions clear bottom; **no scallops**. - **Etch Rate:** $ER = \Delta h/\Delta t$ (µm/min). - **Selectivity:** $S = ER_{Si}/ER_{mask}$ (dimensionless). - **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = D/W$. - **ARDE (Cox–Stewart):** $ER(AR) = ER_0/[1 + (AR/AR_0)^n]$ — etch rate drops at high AR (RIE lag). - **Mask materials:** Photoresist (low AR), hard masks SiO₂/Si₃N₄/Al (high AR, high selectivity). - **Reactor:** ICP source for plasma density + separate RF bias for ion energy (decoupled control). - **DC Bias:** $V_{DC} \approx$ −50 to −300 V — controls ion energy / directionality. - **Application hot list:** MEMS accelerometers, gyroscopes, TSVs, pressure sensors, microfluidics, optical MEMS. - **Common examiner keywords to include:** anisotropy, selectivity, scallop, passivation, ARDE, RIE lag, ion bombardment, vertical sidewall, CMOS compatible. - **Numerical safety:** Always convert nm ↔ µm; always include units; always state assumptions (e.g., 15% over-etch, 50% safety on mask).

topics.title: Isotropic Wet Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isotropic Wet Etching**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isotropic Wet Etching** is a *non-directional* material removal process in which a substrate (typically a semiconductor, metal, or dielectric) is dissolved by a liquid chemical reagent (etchant) at a rate that is **independent of crystallographic orientation**. The etch front advances at the same velocity along all directions in the plane of the substrate, producing characteristic **circular or rounded cavities** with lateral undercut beneath the etch mask.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for **OEICT612 – Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, wet etching is classified under *subtractive micromachining* and is the oldest, simplest, and most economical technique for shaping micro-scale features. **Isotropic** wet etching specifically refers to processes where the etch rate $R$ satisfies:

$$R_{\langle 100 \rangle} \approx R_{\langle 110 \rangle} \approx R_{\langle 111 \rangle} \approx R_{\text{amorphous}}$$

In other words, the etching reaction does not discriminate between atomic planes; the etchant "eats" the material uniformly in every direction, like a chemical sphere expanding outward from the initial point of contact.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Melting Ice Cube Under a Tablecloth":**
> Imagine placing a small sugar cube on a wet sponge. The water dissolves the sugar equally from the top, sides, and bottom. If you put a small square of cardboard (the *etch mask*) on top of the sugar cube and drip water only onto the open area, the water dissolves the sugar straight down **and also creeps sideways beneath the cardboard edge**. After some time, the cavity you carve out is not a neat square well – it is a **rounded bowl with the cardboard overhanging the hole**. This sideways creep is called *undercut*, and it is the unmistakable signature of isotropic wet etching.

This is exactly what happens in MEMS: a patterned photoresist or oxide mask protects certain regions, while an acid/base etchant attacks the exposed silicon. Because the chemistry is purely reaction-rate-limited and thermal, the etchant has no "preference" for the vertical direction over the horizontal one.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Si etch rate in HNA (HF : HNO₃ : CH₃COOH) system:** typically **0.1 to 50 µm/min** depending on mixing ratio and temperature.
- **Selectivity of Si over SiO₂ in KOH-based isotropic systems:** typically **1 : 0.001 to 1 : 0.05** (Si etches 20×–1000× faster than oxide).
- **Activation energy $E_a$ for Si in alkaline etchants:** approximately **0.6 to 0.8 eV**.
- **Standard process temperature window:** **20 °C to 90 °C**.
- **Mask undercut ratio (isotropic) = 1 : 1** (lateral etch = vertical etch for an infinitely long mask opening).
- **Minimum achievable feature size with isotropic wet etching:** typically **≥ 3 µm** (limited by undercut).

### 1.4 Why Isotropic Etching Matters in MEMS

Isotropic wet etching is the *workhorse* of low-cost bulk micromachining. Although it cannot produce the high-aspect-ratio vertical sidewalls achievable with Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE), it is the method of choice for:

- Releasing suspended structures (beams, membranes, cantilevers).
- Sacrificial layer dissolution in surface micromachining.
- Cleaning and thinning wafers.
- Producing rounded features such as **lenses, microlenses, inkjet nozzle orifices, and through-wafer vias** in glass.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Isotropic Etching

The etching process proceeds through three sequential kinetic steps:

1. **Transport of fresh etchant** from the bulk solution to the reaction surface (governed by *diffusion / convection*).
2. **Surface reaction** between etchant species and substrate atoms (governed by *chemical kinetics*).
3. **Transport of reaction products** (byproducts, bubbles, exhausted species) away from the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **isotropic** wet etching, the **surface-reaction step is rate-limiting** and is *insensitive to the crystallographic orientation* of the substrate. Contrast this with anisotropic alkaline etching (e.g., KOH on Si(100)), where the surface-reaction step strongly favors certain crystal planes.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why transport matters:** A stagnant etchant becomes depleted near the surface, slowing the etch. Agitation (stirring, bubbling, megasonic) refreshes the boundary layer.
- **Why temperature matters:** Higher temperature increases both reaction rate and diffusion coefficient, accelerating etching (Arrhenius behaviour).
- **Why concentration matters:** The etch rate is proportional to etchant concentration up to a saturation point, after which by-product inhibition (e.g., H₂ bubble formation on Si in KOH) may dominate.
- **Why the etch is isotropic:** The chemical reaction involves an *amorphous-like* transition state at the surface; no preferred atomic row or plane exists. Common isotropic etchants (HNA for Si, HF for SiO₂, aqua regia for metals) operate via an oxidation–complexation mechanism that does not recognize crystal symmetry.

### 2.3 Mask Undercut Geometry – The Heart of Isotropic Etching

Consider a long rectangular opening of width $W$ in a mask. After a time $t$, the vertical etch depth is:

$$d = R_v \cdot t$$

The lateral undercut on **each side** of the opening is:

$$d_{\text{undercut}} = R_l \cdot t$$

For a perfectly isotropic system:

$$R_l = R_v = R \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d_{\text{undercut}} = d$$

The total width of the etched cavity at the substrate surface becomes:

$$W_{\text{etched}} = W + 2\,d_{\text{undercut}} = W + 2 R t$$

For a circular mask opening of radius $r_0$, the etched cavity becomes a sphere-section of radius:

$$r_{\text{etched}} = r_0 + R t$$

### 2.4 Selectivity and Mask Design

Selectivity $S$ between the target material and the mask:

$$S = \frac{R_{\text{target}}}{R_{\text{mask}}}$$

The minimum required mask thickness $t_{\text{mask}}$ to survive the etch of depth $d$:

$$t_{\text{mask}} = \frac{d}{S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **Si etched in HNA** at room temperature, photoresist (AZ-series) provides $S \approx 50\text{–}200$, while thermal SiO₂ provides $S \approx 1000\text{–}5000$. This is why SiO₂ is the preferred mask for deep HNA etches.

### 2.5 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence

The etch rate follows an Arrhenius law:

$$R(T) = R_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K is the Boltzmann constant. In logarithmic form used for experimental extraction:

$$\ln R = \ln R_0 - \frac{E_a}{k_B}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

Plotting $\ln R$ vs $1/T$ yields a straight line whose slope gives $-E_a/k_B$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Vertical etch depth | $d$ | $d = R \cdot t$ | µm | Same R for isotropic |
| 2 | Lateral undercut | $d_u$ | $d_u = R \cdot t$ | µm | Equals d for isotropic |
| 3 | Etched cavity width | $W_e$ | $W_e = W_0 + 2 R t$ | µm | $W_0$ = mask opening |
| 4 | Etched cavity radius (circular) | $r_e$ | $r_e = r_0 + R t$ | µm | Sphere-section profile |
| 5 | Selectivity | $S$ | $S = R_{\text{target}}/R_{\text{mask}}$ | dimensionless | Higher = better |
| 6 | Required mask thickness | $t_m$ | $t_m = d/S$ | µm | Ensure survival |
| 7 | Arrhenius etch rate | $R(T)$ | $R_0 \exp(-E_a/k_B T)$ | µm/min | $R_0$ = pre-exponential |
| 8 | Etch rate from weight loss | $R$ | $R = \Delta m / (\rho A t)$ | µm/min | Gravimetric method |
| 9 | Diffusion-limited rate | $R_{\text{diff}}$ | $D \cdot C / \delta$ | µm/min | $\delta$ = boundary layer |
| 10 | Activation energy | $E_a$ | $-k_B \cdot d(\ln R)/d(1/T)$ | eV | From Arrhenius plot |
| 11 | Undercut-to-depth ratio | $U$ | $U = d_u / d = 1$ | dimensionless | Isotropic signature |
| 12 | Bubble-stirred enhancement | $R_{\text{eff}}$ | $R_0 (1 + k_b \sqrt{v})$ | µm/min | $v$ = stir speed |
| 13 | Concentration saturation | $C_{\text{sat}}$ | $R \propto C/(K+C)$ | mol/L | Langmuir-type |
| 14 | Conical hole half-angle | $\theta$ | $\theta = 45°$ (ideal) | degree | Sidewall slope for circular mask |
| 15 | Through-hole etch time | $t_{\text{thru}}$ | $t_{\text{thru}} = T_{\text{wafer}}/2R$ | min | $T_{\text{wafer}}$ = thickness |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Inkjet printer nozzle plates:** HNA etching of Si produces perfectly round 20–50 µm orifices.
- **MEMS pressure sensor membranes:** timed isotropic etch thins Si to 5–20 µm to form diaphragms.
- **Glass wafer via formation:** HF-based isotropic etching of Pyrex / Borofloat creates through-glass vias (TGVs) for interposers.
- **Release of polysilicon microstructures:** sacrificial SiO₂ dissolved in BHF (Buffered HF) to free suspended beams.
- **Bio-MEMS / microfluidics:** rounded isotropic cavities are ideal for laminar flow and minimal dead volume.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Lateral Etch Profile for a Long Rectangular Mask Opening

**Problem statement:** A long rectangular opening of width $W_0 = 50$ µm is patterned in a SiO₂ mask on a (100) silicon wafer. The wafer is immersed in an HNA bath with isotropic etch rate $R = 5$ µm/min. Derive the cavity cross-section after $t = 4$ min, and find the etched width at the top surface.

**Step 1 – State the isotropy condition.**

For isotropic etching, the etch front propagates with the same rate $R$ along every direction. Hence the cavity boundary, starting from the original mask edge, traces out a quarter-circle of radius $R\,t$ in the cross-sectional plane.

**Step 2 – Vertical etch depth.**

$$d = R \cdot t$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$d = 5\ \frac{\mu m}{\min} \times 4\ \min = 20\ \mu m$$

**Step 3 – Lateral undercut on each side.**

By the isotropy condition, $R_l = R_v = R$:

$$d_u = R \cdot t = 20\ \mu m$$

**Step 4 – Total etched width at the top surface.**

The cavity widens by $2 d_u$ (one undercut on each side):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_e &= W_0 + 2\,d_u \\
&= 50\ \mu m + 2 \times 20\ \mu m \\
&= 50\ \mu m + 40\ \mu m \\
&= 90\ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Express the sidewall profile parametrically.**

In the cross-section, with origin at the original mask edge and $y$ measured downward:

$$x^2 + y^2 = (R t)^2$$

The etched cavity boundary in the (x, y) plane (x = lateral, y = vertical) is therefore an arc of a circle of radius $R t$. The angle the sidewall makes with the horizontal at the top surface is **90°** (vertical undercut), confirming the bowl-shaped profile.
