**Step 6 – Top-view radius for a circular mask opening.**

If the mask opening were circular with $r_0 = 25$ µm:

$$r_e = r_0 + R t = 25 + 20 = 45\ \mu m$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Required Mask Thickness for a Given Selectivity

**Problem statement:** You need to etch a cavity $d = 30$ µm deep in silicon using HNA at $R = 6$ µm/min. The thermal SiO₂ mask has a measured selectivity $S = 1500$ over Si. What minimum oxide thickness $t_m$ is required so that the mask survives the full etch?

**Step 1 – Compute etch time.**

$$t = \frac{d}{R} = \frac{30\ \mu m}{6\ \mu m/\min} = 5\ \min$$

**Step 2 – Compute the etch rate of the mask.**

$$R_{\text{mask}} = \frac{R_{\text{Si}}}{S} = \frac{6\ \mu m/\min}{1500} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \mu m/\min$$

**Step 3 – Compute the consumed mask thickness during the etch.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_m^{\text{consumed}} &= R_{\text{mask}} \times t \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \mu m/\min \times 5\ \min \\
&= 0.02\ \mu m \\
&= 20\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Add a safety factor (typical 50–100 % engineering margin).**

For a 100 % safety margin:

$$t_m^{\text{design}} = 2 \times 0.02\ \mu m = 0.04\ \mu m = 40\ \text{nm}$$

**Conclusion:** Even a very thin thermal oxide of **40 nm** is sufficient to mask a 30 µm-deep HNA silicon etch. This is one of the major engineering advantages of highly selective isotropic wet etching.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Arrhenius Extrapolation of Etch Rate

**Problem statement:** The Si etch rate in an HNA bath is measured to be $R_1 = 2$ µm/min at $T_1 = 25$ °C (298 K) and $R_2 = 8$ µm/min at $T_2 = 45$ °C (318 K). Determine the activation energy $E_a$ and predict the etch rate at $T_3 = 60$ °C (333 K).

**Step 1 – Write Arrhenius equation at two temperatures.**

$$R_1 = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T_1}\right), \quad R_2 = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 – Take the ratio to eliminate $R_0$.**

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3 – Solve for $E_a$.**

$$
E_a = k_B \,\frac{\ln(R_2/R_1)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 – Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{8}{2}\right) &= \ln 4 = 1.3863 \\
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} &= \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{318} \\
&= \frac{318 - 298}{298 \times 318} \\
&= \frac{20}{94764} \\
&= 2.110 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_a &= (8.617 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{eV/K}) \times \frac{1.3863}{2.110 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}} \\
&= (8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \times 6570 \\
&\approx 0.566\ \text{eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Compute $R_0$ from the low-temperature measurement.**

$$R_0 = R_1 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k_B T_1}\right) = 2 \exp\!\left(\frac{0.566}{8.617\times 10^{-5} \times 298}\right) = 2 \exp(22.05) \approx 7.2 \times 10^{9}\ \mu m/\min$$

**Step 6 – Predict etch rate at $T_3 = 333$ K.**

$$
R_3 = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T_3}\right) = 7.2 \times 10^{9} \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.566}{8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 333}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{E_a}{k_B T_3} = \frac{0.566}{0.0287} = 19.72
$$

$$
R_3 = 7.2 \times 10^{9} \times e^{-19.72} = 7.2 \times 10^{9} \times 2.69 \times 10^{-9} \approx 19.4\ \mu m/\min
$$

**Interpretation:** Raising the bath temperature from 25 °C to 60 °C increases the Si etch rate roughly **10×** in this chemistry – a key reason why HNA processes are tightly temperature-controlled.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Isotropic vs. Anisotropic Wet Etching

| Parameter | Isotropic Wet Etching | Anisotropic Wet Etching (e.g., KOH/Si) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Etch rate dependence on crystal plane | None | Very strong (e.g., $R_{\langle 100 \rangle}/R_{\langle 111 \rangle} \approx 100$ for KOH) |
| Sidewall profile | Curved (circular arc) | Flat, well-defined crystal planes (e.g., 54.74° walls on (100) Si) |
| Undercut | Equals etch depth | Negligible (V-groove self-stops) |
| Mask undercut | Severe | Minimal |
| Minimum feature size | Limited (~3 µm) | Sub-micron achievable |
| Common etchants | HNA, BHF, Aqua regia, Piranha | KOH, TMAH, EDP, CsOH |
| Typical selectivity to SiO₂ | High (1:1000) | Very high (>1:200) |
| Cost & simplicity | Very low | Moderate (T-controlled bath) |
| Application | Membranes, nozzles, release | V-grooves, pyramids, precise 3D structures |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem – Through-Wafer Via in Glass

**Problem:** A 500 µm-thick Borofloat glass wafer is etched from both sides in a 49 % HF bath at $R = 8$ µm/min (isotropic). How long does it take to form a through-via, and what is the diameter of the via at the wafer mid-plane?

**Step 1 – Etch depth from each side.**

$$d = \frac{T_{\text{wafer}}}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250\ \mu m$$

**Step 2 – Required etch time per side.**

$$t = \frac{d}{R} = \frac{250}{8} = 31.25\ \min$$

**Step 3 – Mask opening radius (assume 100 µm circular pad per side).**

$$r_0 = 100\ \mu m$$

**Step 4 – Radius at the mid-plane (after 31.25 min of lateral etching from both sides).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{\text{mid}} &= r_0 + R t \\
&= 100\ \mu m + 8\ \mu m/\min \times 31.25\ \min \\
&= 100 + 250 \\
&= 350\ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Final via diameter at the centre of the wafer.**

$$D_{\text{via}} = 2 \times r_{\text{mid}} = 700\ \mu m$$

This result shows that the via is **largest in the middle and smallest at the wafer surfaces** – a key design constraint for TGV (through-glass via) interposers in 2.5D / 3D packaging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Isotropic Wet Etching Process

The following diagram describes the process flow from a systems-engineering perspective – showing how etchant chemistry, mask design, and reaction kinetics interact to produce the final etched cavity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate Input Wafer Si Glass Metal] --> B[Surface Preparation RCA Clean Piranha]
    B --> C[Mask Deposition LPCVD Oxide Photoresist Metal]
    C --> D[Photolithography Pattern Transfer]
    D --> E[Mask Openings Exposed Area Defined]
    E --> F[Wet Etch Bath HNA HF KOH TMAH]
    F --> G[Etch Front Advances Equally in All Directions]
    G --> H[Spherical Arc Cavity Formation]
    H --> I[Mask Stripping Solvent or HF]
    I --> J[Final Isotropic Cavity with Undercut]
    J --> K[Quality Inspection Optical Profilometry SEM]
    K --> L[MEMS Device Output Membrane Nozzle Via]
```

### 4.2 Isotropic Etch Profile Geometry

The diagram below shows how the etch front evolves from a rectangular mask opening as a function of time, emphasising the circular-arc cavity shape that is the geometric signature of isotropic etching.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph t0[Time t equals 0]
        A1[Mask Opening Width W0] --> A2[Substrate Surface Unetched]
    end
    subgraph t1[Time t equals t1]
        B1[Mask Opening W0] --> B2[Lateral Undercut d_u equals R t1]
        B2 --> B3[Vertical Depth equals R t1]
        B2 --> B4[Cavity Width W0 plus 2 d_u]
    end
    subgraph t2[Time t equals t2 greater than t1]
        C1[Mask Opening W0] --> C2[Larger Undercut]
        C2 --> C3[Deeper Cavity]
        C2 --> C4[Quotient d_u over d equals 1]
    end
    t0 --> t1 --> t2
```

### 4.3 Process Parameter Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Temperature T] --> Q1[Arrhenius Rate Increase]
    P2[Concentration C] --> Q2[Surface Reaction Rate]
    P3[Agitation v] --> Q3[Boundary Layer Thinning]
    P4[Mask Material] --> Q4[Selectivity S]
    P5[Mask Geometry] --> Q5[Undercut Length]
    Q1 --> R[Etch Rate R]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    Q5 --> R
    R --> S1[Cavity Depth]
    R --> S2[Cavity Width]
    R --> S3[Sidewall Profile]
    R --> S4[Mask Survival]
```

### 4.4 Material-Specific Etchant Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Substrate Material] --> Q1{Substrate Type}
    Q1 -->|Silicon| P1[HNA: HF HNO3 Acetic Acid]
    Q1 -->|Silicon Dioxide| P2[BHF or Dilute HF]
    Q1 -->|Glass Pyrex Borofloat| P3[Concentrated HF]
    Q1 -->|Aluminum| P4[Phosphoric Acid H3PO4]
    Q1 -->|Gold| P5[Aqua Regia HCl HNO3]
    Q1 -->|Copper| P6[Ferric Chloride FeCl3]
    Q1 -->|Titanium| P7[HF based]
    P1 --> R1[Isotropic High Selectivity to SiO2]
    P2 --> R2[Isotropic High Selectivity to Si]
    P3 --> R3[Isotropic Used for TGVs]
    P4 --> R4[Isotropic at 40 to 60 C]
    P5 --> R5[Highly Aggressive Isotropic]
    P6 --> R6[Isotropic for PCB and MEMS]
    P7 --> R7[Isotropic Sacrificial Release]
```

### 4.5 Etch-Front Evolution Schematic (Cross-Sectional View)

> **Note:** Since drawing a 2-D physical cross-section in Mermaid is limited, the following flowchart encodes the geometric evolution rules of the etch front.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Mask Edge at x equals 0 y equals 0] --> B[Step 2 At time t Etch Front Reaches Radius R t]
    B --> C[Step 3 Quarter Circle Centered at Original Mask Edge]
    C --> D[Step 4 All Points on Etch Front Equidistant from Edge]
    D --> E[Step 5 Cavity Cross Section Traces Arc of Circle]
    E --> F[Step 6 Sidewall Slope at Top Surface equals Vertical]
    F --> G[Step 7 Final Shape Bowl like with Mask Overhang]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define **isotropic wet etching** and state **two** characteristic features of the etch profile it produces.

**Model Answer:**

Isotropic wet etching is a chemical material-removal process in which a liquid etchant dissolves the substrate at a rate that is **independent of crystallographic orientation**, advancing equally in all directions. The two characteristic features of the resulting etch profile are:

1. **Curved (circular-arc) sidewalls** – the etched cavity boundary traces an arc of a circle centred at the original mask edge.
2. **Lateral undercut equal to the vertical etch depth** – the mask is "under-eaten" sideways by an amount equal to the depth etched, producing overhanging mask edges.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark feature 1 + 1 mark feature 2)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** List **three** common isotropic wet etchants used in MEMS fabrication and state the material each one preferentially etches.

**Model Answer:**

1. **HNA (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH)** – etches **silicon (Si)** isotropically.
2. **BHF (Buffered HF) / Dilute HF** – etches **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** and **glass**.
3. **Aqua Regia (HCl + HNO₃ in 3:1 ratio)** – etches **gold (Au)**, **platinum (Pt)**, and **palladium (Pd)**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per etchant–material pair)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **isotropic wet etching process** for silicon using the HNA system. Discuss the role of each chemical component and explain why the process is isotropic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon wafer with a 1 µm-thick thermal SiO₂ mask containing a 60 µm-wide rectangular opening is etched in an HNA bath at an isotropic rate of 4 µm/min for 6 minutes. Calculate the **(i)** etch depth, **(ii)** lateral undercut on each side, and **(iii)** total etched width at the wafer surface. Also determine whether the oxide mask survives, given the Si : SiO₂ selectivity is 800 : 1. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Description of Isotropic Wet Etching of Si using HNA:**

**Process description:** The silicon wafer is first cleaned and a patterned mask (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, or metal) is defined by photolithography. The wafer is then immersed in the HNA solution maintained at a controlled temperature (typically 20–50 °C) inside a Teflon beaker. The HNA mixture contains:

- **HF (hydrofluoric acid):** Removes the SiO₂ native/product layer by forming soluble H₂SiF₆.
- **HNO₃ (nitric acid):** Oxidises silicon to SiO₂ (the rate-limiting step producing a thin oxide layer).
- **CH₃COOH (acetic acid):** Acts as a diluent, controlling the HNO₃ dissociation and providing buffering for a stable etch rate.

**Chemical reaction (overall):**

$$3\,\text{Si} + 4\,\text{HNO}_3 + 18\,\text{HF} \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 4\,\text{NO}\uparrow + 8\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Why the process is isotropic:** The reaction proceeds through an amorphous, hydrated SiO₂ transition state on the surface. Since the chemistry does not distinguish between different crystallographic planes (the oxidation step is non-directional and depends only on local chemical potential), the etch rate is essentially identical in every direction, producing circular-arc cavity walls.

**Sketch (described in words):** A long rectangular mask opening of width $W_0$ on the Si surface. After time $t$, the etched cavity has a depth $d = R t$, lateral undercut $d_u = R t$, and a curved bowl-shaped sidewall.

*(7 marks: 2 marks process description + 2 marks chemistry + 1 mark isotropy reason + 2 marks sketch)*

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:**
- $W_0 = 60$ µm
- $R = 4$ µm/min
- $t = 6$ min
- $t_{\text{oxide}} = 1$ µm = 1000 nm
- $S = 800$

**Step (i) – Etch depth:**

$$d = R \cdot t = 4 \times 6 = 24\ \mu m$$

**Step (ii) – Lateral undercut on each side:**

By isotropy, $R_l = R_v$:

$$d_u = R \cdot t = 24\ \mu m$$

**Step (iii) – Total etched width at the surface:**

$$W_e = W_0 + 2 d_u = 60 + 2(24) = 60 + 48 = 108\ \mu m$$

**Mask survival check:**

- Si etch rate = 4 µm/min
- SiO₂ etch rate = $4 / 800 = 0.005$ µm/min
- Oxide consumed in 6 min = $0.005 \times 6 = 0.030$ µm = 30 nm
- Available oxide = 1000 nm
- Remaining oxide = 1000 − 30 = 970 nm

**Conclusion:** The mask survives comfortably; the oxide is barely attacked. The process is highly selective.

*(7 marks: 2 marks depth + 2 marks undercut + 2 marks width + 1 mark mask survival)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **mask undercut** in isotropic wet etching. Derive an expression for the etched cavity width in terms of mask opening, etch rate, and time for a long rectangular opening. State the **isotropy signature** ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a wet etching experiment, the Si etch rate is found to be 1.5 µm/min at 22 °C and 7.0 µm/min at 50 °C. Using the Arrhenius relation, calculate the **activation energy** of the etching reaction. Using this value, predict the etch rate at 70 °C. (Boltzmann constant $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Mask Undercut and Cavity Width Derivation:**

**Concept:** Mask undercut is the **lateral dissolution** of the substrate beneath the protective mask during etching. Because the etchant attacks equally in all directions, it dissolves material not only vertically downward through the mask opening but also horizontally inward beneath the mask edges.

**Derivation for a long rectangular mask opening of width $W_0$:**

Let $R$ be the isotropic etch rate and $t$ the etch duration. After time $t$:

- Vertical etch depth: $d = R t$
- Lateral undercut on each side: $d_u = R_l t$

For isotropic etching $R_l = R_v = R$, hence $d_u = R t$.

The cavity width at the original substrate surface is the sum of the original opening and the two lateral undercuts:

$$W_e = W_0 + 2 d_u = W_0 + 2 R t$$

**Isotropy signature ratio:**

$$\frac{d_u}{d} = 1 \quad \text{(isotropic wet etching)}$$

For anisotropic etching this ratio is $\ll 1$ (approaches zero for KOH on Si(100)/(111)).

*(7 marks: 2 marks concept + 3 marks derivation + 1 mark formula + 1 mark ratio)*

**(b) Arrhenius Computation:**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 22$ °C = 295 K, $R_1 = 1.5$ µm/min
- $T_2 = 50$ °C = 323 K, $R_2 = 7.0$ µm/min
- $T_3 = 70$ °C = 343 K, find $R_3$

**Step 1 – Take ratio of Arrhenius equations:**

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 – Solve for $E_a$:**

$$E_a = k_B \cdot \frac{\ln(R_2/R_1)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$

**Step 3 – Substitute:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{7.0}{1.5}\right) = \ln 4.667 = 1.5404
$$

$$
\frac{1}{295} - \frac{1}{323} = \frac{323 - 295}{295 \times 323} = \frac{28}{95285} = 2.939 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_a = (8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \times \frac{1.5404}{2.939 \times 10^{-4}} = (8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \times 5241 \approx 0.452\ \text{eV}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute $R_0$:**

$$R_0 = R_1 \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k_B T_1}\right) = 1.5 \exp\!\left(\frac{0.452}{8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 295}\right) = 1.5 \exp(17.78) = 1.5 \times 5.21 \times 10^{7} \approx 7.82 \times 10^{7}\ \mu m/\min$$

**Step 5 – Predict $R_3$ at 343 K:**

$$
\frac{E_a}{k_B T_3} = \frac{0.452}{8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 343} = \frac{0.452}{0.02956} = 15.29
$$

$$
R_3 = 7.82 \times 10^{7} \times e^{-15.29} = 7.82 \times 10^{7} \times 2.24 \times 10^{-7} \approx 17.5\ \mu m/\min
$$

**Conclusion:** The activation energy is **≈ 0.45 eV**, and the etch rate at 70 °C is approximately **17.5 µm/min**, more than 10× the room-temperature value.

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula setup + 2 marks $E_a$ calculation + 2 marks $R_3$ prediction + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up units** – Students frequently use $T$ in °C inside the Arrhenius equation. Always convert to **Kelvin** before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in cavity width** – The total etched width is $W_0 + 2 R t$ (undercut on BOTH sides), not $W_0 + R t$.
> 3. **Treating HNA as anisotropic** – HNA on Si is strictly isotropic. Anisotropy appears only with alkaline etchants like KOH, TMAH, EDP on Si.
> 4. **Ignoring selectivity in mask design** – Always check whether the mask survives the full etch: $t_m \cdot S \geq d$.
> 5. **Confusing "etch rate" with "etch depth"** – Etch rate has units of µm/min; etch depth is µm. Include time in every calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Isotropic Wet Etching**

- **Definition:** Etch rate **independent of crystal orientation**; same in all directions.
- **Geometry signature:** Etched cavity boundary is a **circular arc** of radius $R t$ centred at the original mask edge.
- **Undercut-to-depth ratio:** $d_u / d = 1$ (uniquely identifies isotropic behaviour).
- **Cavity width formula:** $W_e = W_0 + 2 R t$.
- **Cavity radius formula (circular mask):** $r_e = r_0 + R t$.
- **Mask survival:** $t_{\text{mask,consumed}} = d / S$.
- **HNA chemistry for Si:** $3\text{Si} + 4\text{HNO}_3 + 18\text{HF} \to 3\text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 4\text{NO} + 8\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **HF / BHF** etches SiO₂ and glass isotropically; used for sacrificial release and TGVs.
- **Aqua regia** (HCl : HNO₃ = 3 : 1) is the universal isotropic etchant for noble metals (Au, Pt, Pd).
- **Arrhenius law:** $R(T) = R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$; $E_a$ for Si in HNA is **0.4 – 0.8 eV**.
- **Temperature rule of thumb:** Etch rate roughly **doubles for every 10 °C** rise (within the kinetically controlled regime).
- **Selectivity benchmarks:**
  - HNA on Si vs SiO₂: ~ 1000 : 1 (Si wins).
  - BHF on SiO₂ vs Si: ~ 100 : 1 (SiO₂ wins).
  - HF on glass vs photoresist: ~ 50 : 1.
- **Process parameters to control:** temperature, etchant concentration, agitation, mask adhesion, bath age (etchant exhaustion).
- **Common applications:** diaphragms/membranes, inkjet nozzle plates, microlens arrays, glass TGVs, sacrificial release in surface micromachining.
- **Advantages:** very low cost, high selectivity, simple equipment, no ion-bombardment damage.
- **Disadvantages:** severe undercut limits minimum feature size (~3 µm), poor uniformity over large wafers, liquid handling and waste-treatment overhead, not suitable for high-aspect-ratio features.
- **Comparison hook for exam:** "Isotropic = circular arc + undercut; Anisotropic = flat crystal planes + V-groove."
- **Bubble formation** in KOH/TMAH on Si can locally block the surface and cause *hydrogen bubble masking* – a famous non-ideality.
- **Always quote units** (µm/min for rate, µm for length, K for temperature) – KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for missing units.
- **Sketch rule:** In any KTU answer discussing isotropic wet etching, **always draw a cross-section** showing the mask, the original surface line, the curved cavity, and the undercut on both sides – this alone can fetch 2 easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anisotropic Wet Etching
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anisotropic Wet Etching

# Anisotropic Wet Etching

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Anisotropic Wet Etching** is a wet chemical bulk micromachining process in which the etch rate of a crystalline material — most commonly single-crystal silicon — depends strongly on the crystallographic orientation of the exposed surface. The etchant removes atoms along certain crystal planes (e.g., (100) and (110)) orders of magnitude faster than along others (e.g., (111)), enabling the fabrication of precisely defined three-dimensional microstructures such as V-grooves, pyramidal cavities, suspended membranes, and microchannels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEICT612 – Module 3):**
> Under *Microsystem Fabrication Processes*, the syllabus explicitly lists **wet and dry etching** with **anisotropic etching using orientation-dependent etch rates and etch-stop techniques** as a core topic. Anisotropic wet etching of silicon using alkaline hydroxides (KOH, TMAH) and EDP is a mandatory sub-topic.

Formally, anisotropy is quantified using the **Anisotropy Factor (AF)**:

$$AF = \frac{R_{\text{fast}}}{R_{\text{slow}}}$$

where $R_{\text{fast}}$ and $R_{\text{slow}}$ are the maximum and minimum etch rates among the principal crystal planes. For a typical KOH–water–IPA system on silicon, $R_{(100)}/R_{(111)} \approx 400$, indicating extreme anisotropy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Isotropic Etching:**
> - **Isotropic etching** proceeds at the same rate in all directions (e.g., HNA — Hydrofluoric, Nitric, Acetic acid for silicon).
> - **Anisotropic etching** proceeds direction-dependently because the chemistry is rate-limited by the density of available surface bonds (the *back-bond* structure of surface atoms).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **sanding a deck of cards** stacked together:

- If you sand **across the edges (perpendicular to the flat faces)**, the cards separate cleanly, and the side becomes very smooth and straight. This is the **(111) plane** of silicon — atoms are tightly bound, with three back-bonds per surface atom.
- If you sand **along the flat face of the deck (parallel to the cards)**, the layers peel apart easily, with little resistance. This is the **(100) plane** — only two back-bonds per surface atom.

Wet anisotropic etchants behave exactly like this selective sanding: they preferentially attack the loosely bound planes and effectively stop at the densely packed (111) planes, producing well-defined geometric shapes (pyramids, V-grooves, and beams) bounded by slow-etching crystal facets.

> [!TIP]
> A simple real-world analogy: anisotropic etching is like how water naturally erodes layered sedimentary rock along its planes of weakness, creating stepped cliffs and valleys aligned with the geological structure — not uniform slopes.

### 1.3 Why Silicon Is the Material of Choice

| Property | Value/Description | Relevance to AWE |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal structure | Diamond cubic (FCC sublattice) | Defines plane-dependent bonding |
| Lattice constant | **a = 0.543 nm** | Determines atomic spacing |
| (111) inter-planar spacing | $\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.314$ nm | Highest atomic density plane |
| Surface bond density (111) | $7.83 \times 10^{14}$ atoms/cm² | Slowest etch rate |
| Surface bond density (100) | $6.78 \times 10^{14}$ atoms/cm² | Fastest etch rate (in KOH) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Silicon Crystallography — The Atomic Foundation

Silicon has a **diamond cubic lattice** with 8 atoms per unit cell. Three low-Miller-index planes dominate MEMS wet etching:

### (100) Plane
- Surface atom has **2 back-bonds** to the bulk.
- Each surface atom is bonded to **2 in-plane neighbors** and **2 atoms beneath**.
- Forms **square unit mesh** with side $a/\sqrt{2}$.
- Etch rate is **highest** (R₁₀₀ ≈ 1.4 µm/min in 30% KOH at 80 °C).

### (110) Plane
- Surface atom has **1 in-plane bond** and **2 back-bonds** (one deeper).
- Rectangular mesh; etch rate is intermediate to high (R₁₁₀ ≈ 2.0 µm/min in KOH).

### (111) Plane
- Surface atom has **3 back-bonds** (one to each neighboring tetrahedral atom beneath).
- Forms **triangular / hexagonal mesh** with the highest atomic density.
- Etch rate is **lowest** (R₁₁₁ ≈ 0.006 µm/min in 30% KOH at 80 °C) — practically acts as an **etch stop**.

## 2.2 Chemical Mechanism (KOH Etching of Si)

The overall reaction in aqueous KOH is:

$$\text{Si} + 2\,\text{OH}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2(\text{OH})_2^{2-} + 2\,\text{H}_2\uparrow$$

Step-wise:

1. **Hydroxyl attack:** $\text{OH}^-$ attacks a surface Si atom.
2. **Back-bond breaking:** the three (or fewer) Si–Si back-bonds are broken sequentially — the rate-limiting step.
3. **Hydration:** products (silicic acid) diffuse into the solution; $\text{H}_2$ gas evolves.

The number and strength of back-bonds determine the etch rate, explaining orientation dependence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The (111) plane has **3 back-bonds per atom**, the (100) has **2**, and the (110) has effectively 1 strong + 1 weak. Hence the etch-rate hierarchy: **R(110) > R(100) > R(111)** for KOH on Si.

## 2.3 Common Anisotropic Etchants

| Etchant | Composition | R(100) µm/min | R(111) µm/min | Selectivity | Compatibility with CMOS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH** | 30 wt% KOH, H₂O, IPA | 1.4 | 0.006 | ~400:1 | No (K⁺ contamination) |
| **TMAH** | 25 wt% Tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide | 0.6 | 0.005 | ~120:1 | Yes |
| **EDP** | Ethylenediamine, pyrocatechol, water | 1.25 | 0.025 | ~50:1 | Partial |
| **CsOH** | Caesium hydroxide (10–50%) | 0.4 | 0.02 | ~20:1 | Limited |
| **Hydrazine** | Hydrazine–water–IPA | 0.3–1.0 | 0.001 | ~1000:1 | No (toxic) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AF = \dfrac{R_{\text{fast}}}{R_{\text{slow}}}$ | Anisotropy factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | $d = R_{(hkl)} \cdot t$ | Vertical etch depth in time $t$ | µm |
| 3 | $u = \dfrac{R_{(hkl)}}{\cos\theta}\cdot t$ | Undercutting along a sloped sidewall | µm |
| 4 | $u = R_{(100)} \cdot t \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ)$ | Lateral undercut for a (100) wafer with (111) sidewalls | µm |
| 5 | $W_{\text{mask}} = W_{\text{cavity}} + 2\,d\,\tan(54.74^\circ)$ | Required mask opening width for V-groove of base width $W_{\text{cavity}}$ | µm |
| 6 | $d_{\text{V-groove}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{mask}}}{2\,\tan(54.74^\circ)}$ | Maximum etch depth before (111) planes meet | µm |
| 7 | $d_{\text{membrane}} = W_{\text{mask}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ | Etch depth for complete membrane release on (100) | µm |
| 8 | $R_{\text{etch}} = A\,e^{-E_a/k_B T}$ | Arrhenius etch-rate temperature dependence | µm/min |
| 9 | $\cos(54.74^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Angle between (100) and (111) planes | — |
| 10 | $\Delta u = \Delta t \cdot (R_{(100)} - R_{\text{mask}})$ | Net undercut accounting for mask etch | µm |

> [!NOTE]
> The angle **54.74°** is the universal angle between the (100) surface and the {111} family of planes in silicon, derived from the cubic crystal geometry: $\theta = \arctan(\sqrt{2})$.

## 2.5 Mask Design Rules

When a rectangular mask opening of width $W$ is aligned to the ⟨110⟩ direction on a (100) wafer:

- (111) sidewalls form at **54.74°** to the surface.
- The sidewalls are **self-limiting** — once exposed, the etch effectively stops laterally.
- The bottom (100) surface continues to etch vertically until either the (111) planes meet (forming a V-groove) or the etch time is stopped.

The minimum mask opening to get a true V-groove (where (111) planes intersect at the bottom):

$$W_{\min} = 2 \cdot d_{\text{target}} \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ) \approx 2.828\, d_{\text{target}}$$

If $W < W_{\min}$, the (111) planes meet before the target depth — a **truncated V-groove** (rectangular base + sloped walls) forms.

## 2.6 Etch-Stop Techniques

| Method | Physical Principle | Selectivity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boron etch-stop** | Heavy p⁺ doping (> 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³) deactivates etch sites in KOH/EDP | > 1000:1 | Membrane formation |
| **Electrochemical** | Reverse-biased p-n junction repels holes (carrier-limited) | > 10⁴:1 | Precision membranes |
| **Photo-assisted** | n-Si becomes passive under anodic bias + light | ~50:1 | Selective thinning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boron etch-stop mechanism:** Boron atoms (smaller than Si) introduce **lattice contraction** and compress the Si–Si back-bonds, raising the activation energy for OH⁻ attack. Above a critical concentration (≈ 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³), the etch rate drops by **3–4 orders of magnitude**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Angle Between (100) and (111) Planes

**Goal:** Show that the sidewall angle on a (100) wafer etched through a ⟨110⟩-aligned mask is exactly 54.74°.

The angle between two crystal planes $(h_1 k_1 l_1)$ and $(h_2 k_2 l_2)$ in a cubic system is given by:

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{h_1 h_2 + k_1 k_2 + l_1 l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^2 + k_1^2 + l_1^2}\;\sqrt{h_2^2 + k_2^2 + l_2^2}}$$

Take $(h_1 k_1 l_1) = (100)$ and $(h_2 k_2 l_2) = (111)$:

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{(1)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(1)}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0 + 0}\;\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1}\cdot\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Now evaluate the angle:

$$\theta = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356^\circ \approx 54.74^\circ$$

Hence the sidewalls of any anisotropic KOH etch on (100) silicon — aligned to ⟨110⟩ — terminate on {111} planes at **54.74°** to the wafer surface. This is the **fingerprint angle of silicon micromachining**.

> [!NOTE]
> On a (110) wafer, the etch yields **vertical sidewalls** because the {111} planes are exactly perpendicular to the (110) surface — a property exploited in fabricating deep trenches and comb-drive structures with perfectly vertical walls.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mask Opening vs. Etch Depth in a V-Groove

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between mask window width and the etch depth at which the V-groove closes.

Consider a rectangular mask opening of width $W_m$ oriented along ⟨110⟩ on a (100) wafer. After time $t$, the bottom has been etched down by depth $d$. The two (111) sidewalls have advanced laterally by:

$$u = d \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ) = d \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

The two sidewalls meet (V-groove fully formed) when the lateral advance from each side equals half the mask opening:

$$d \cdot \tan(54.74^\circ) = \dfrac{W_m}{2}$$

Solving for the closure depth $d_c$:

$$d_c = \dfrac{W_m}{2\,\tan(54.74^\circ)} = \dfrac{W_m}{2\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{W_m\,\sqrt{2}}{4}$$

Equivalently, in terms of the well-known engineering form:

$$\boxed{\,d_c = \dfrac{W_m}{\sqrt{8}} = \dfrac{W_m}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.354\,W_m\,}$$

**Numerical check:** A mask opening of $W_m = 1000$ µm produces a V-groove that fully closes at $d_c = 353.6$ µm depth.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Mask Width for a Target Etch Depth

Inverting the previous result, for a desired V-groove depth $d$:

$$W_m = 2\,d\,\tan(54.74^\circ) = 2\sqrt{2}\,d$$

If the **base of the V-groove** is to be flat with width $W_{\text{base}}$ (a truncated V-groove, etched for a finite time $t < t_c$):

$$W_m = W_{\text{base}} + 2\,d\,\tan(54.74^\circ)$$

This is the formula used at the design stage to **size the lithography mask** to produce a specific microstructure depth.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Etch Time Calculation

Given the target etch depth $d$ and the (100) etch rate $R_{100}$:

$$t = \dfrac{d}{R_{(100)}}$$

**Worked example:** Fabricate a 50 µm-deep cavity on a (100) wafer using 30% KOH at 80 °C where $R_{(100)} = 1.4$ µm/min.

$$t = \dfrac{50\;\mu m}{1.4\;\mu m/\min} = 35.71\;\text{min}$$

To find the undercut $u$ on the (111) sidewalls (using selectivity $S = R_{(100)}/R_{(111)} = 400$):

$$u = R_{(111)} \cdot t = \dfrac{R_{(100)}}{S} \cdot t = \dfrac{1.4}{400} \cdot 35.71 = 0.125\;\mu m$$

This confirms why the (111) sidewalls are effectively **atomically flat** after KOH etching — sub-micron roughness with angstrom-scale uniformity.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Convex Corner Undercutting

A critical pitfall in silicon micromachining is **convex corner undercutting**. When a mask corner is convex (e.g., the outside corner of a square), the {111} planes that bound the etch undercut each other at the corner, allowing the fast-etching (100) plane to be re-exposed. The result is a fast lateral "beak" undercut.

The underetch velocity at a convex corner is approximated by:

$$u_{\text{convex}}(t) = R_{(100)} \cdot t \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{\cos(54.74^\circ)}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle of the bounding {111} plane. Designers mitigate this by adding **compensation structures** (e.g., ⟨100⟩-oriented squares or triangles attached to convex corners) that etch away at the same rate as the corner undercut, leaving a clean corner at the end of the process.

For a square compensation triangle oriented at 45° to the mask edge, with side $L_c$:

$$L_c = R_{(100)} \cdot t \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1)$$

**Numerical example:** For $t = 35.71$ min and $R_{(100)} = 1.4$ µm/min:

$$L_c = 1.4 \times 35.71 \times (1.414 - 1) = 1.4 \times 35.71 \times 0.414 = 20.7\;\mu m$$

So a ≈ 21 µm compensation triangle is needed to preserve a clean convex corner.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Etch Rate

The etch rate is thermally activated:

$$R(T) = A\,e^{-E_a/k_B T}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\ln R = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}$$

A plot of $\ln R$ vs $1/T$ is linear with slope $-E_a/k_B$. For Si in KOH:

- $E_a \approx 0.595$ eV on (100)
- $E_a \approx 0.65$ eV on (111)

A 10 °C increase around 80 °C roughly doubles the etch rate — **temperature is the most sensitive process variable**.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner Pitfall: Students often quote etch rates as "constants". In an exam, always specify the etchant, concentration, **temperature**, and whether the value is for (100) or (111) — a missing qualifier costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Anisotropic Etching Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Cleaned Single-Crystal Si Wafer 100 oriented"] --> B["Thermal Oxidation Grow SiO2 Mask Layer 500 to 1000 nm"]
    B --> C["Photolithography Pattern Mask with Windows along 110 direction"]
    C --> D["BOE or RIE Etch Open Windows in SiO2"]
    D --> E["Strip Photoresist with Acetone or Piranha"]
    E --> F["Anisotropic Wet Etch KOH 30 percent 80 C IPA Saturated"]
    F --> G{"Etch Time Elapsed?"}
    G -->|"No, t less than tc"| H["Truncated V Groove Flat Bottom"]
    G -->|"Yes, t equals tc"| I["Full V Groove 111 planes meet"]
    G -->|"t greater than tc"| J["V Groove Held by 111 planes No further lateral etch"]
    H --> K["Strip SiO2 in BOE"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["Final Microstructure Pyramid V Groove or Membrane"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Crystallographic Plane Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P100["100 Plane\n2 back-bonds per atom\nEtch rate HIGH\n1.4 um per min in KOH"] 
    P110["110 Plane\n1 in-plane, 2 back-bonds\nEtch rate HIGHEST\n2.0 um per min in KOH"]
    P111["111 Plane\n3 back-bonds per atom\nEtch rate LOWEST\n0.006 um per min in KOH\nActs as etch stop"]
    P100 -->|"Angle = 54.74 deg"| P111
    P110 -->|"Angle = 35.26 deg"| P111
    P100 -->|"Angle = 45 deg"| P110
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Convex vs Concave Corner Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CCONVEX["Convex Corner Outside Corner"]
        A1["Mask Corner"] --> A2["Initial 111 sidewall forms"]
        A2 --> A3["111 planes undercut each other"]
        A3 --> A4["Fast etching 100 plane re exposed"]
        A4 --> A5["Lateral beak undercut forms"]
    end
    subgraph CCONCAVE["Concave Corner Inside Corner"]
        B1["Mask corner"] --> B2["Two 111 walls meet at corner"]
        B2 --> B3["Slow etching planes protect corner"]
        B3 --> B4["Corner remains sharp clean"]
    end
    A5 --> MIT["MITIGATION Add 100 aligned compensation triangle"]
    MIT --> A6["Triangle etches away in same time\npreserving clean convex corner"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Anisotropic Wet Etch Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EQUIP["Wet Etch Process Module"]
        T1["Temperature Controlled Bath 80 C"]
        T2["Reflux Condenser Prevents Evaporation"]
        T3["Stirrer Uniform Concentration"]
        T4["Teflon or Quartz Holder for Wafers"]
        T5["Heater with PID Controller"]
    end
    T1 --> CHEM["KOH 30 wt% in DI Water with IPA"]
    CHEM --> WAFER["Patterned 100 Si Wafer"]
    WAFER --> RXN["Si + 2 OH + 2 H2O to SiO2 OH 2 2- + 2 H2"]
    RXN --> H2["H2 Gas Bubbles Released"]
    RXN --> PROD["Silicate Dissolved in Solution"]
    H2 --> VENT["Vent Hood for Safety"]
    PROD --> RECIRC["Recirculation Filter Loop"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading guide for the diagrams:** Process flow (4.1) tracks the wafer from bare substrate to finished V-groove. Plane hierarchy (4.2) summarizes the etch-rate relationships and angles. Corner behavior (4.3) is critical for mask design — memorize the difference between convex and concave etch evolution. Equipment architecture (4.4) shows the supporting infrastructure required for reproducible wet etching.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between isotropic and anisotropic wet etching of silicon. Give one example of each etchant.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Isotropic Etching | Anisotropic Wet Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Etch rate vs direction | Independent of crystal orientation | Strongly orientation-dependent |
| Etch profile | Curved, undercut | Faceted, defined by {111} planes |
| Example etchant | HNA (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH) | KOH, TMAH, EDP |
| Mask undercut | Large, equal in all directions | Minimal along ⟨110⟩ (limited by (111)) |
| Typical application | Shallow recessing, cleaning | Bulk micromachining, V-grooves, membranes |

**Example etchants:** *Isotropic:* HNA (10:30:10 vol. ratio) — used for Si polishing and saw-damage removal. *Anisotropic:* **30 wt% KOH in water at 80 °C** — produces (111) etch stops and V-grooves on (100) wafers.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why does the (111) plane etch much slower than the (100) plane in KOH? Justify with bond-density arguments.**

**Model Answer:**

The (111) plane in silicon has the **highest surface atomic density** of $\approx 7.83 \times 10^{14}$ atoms/cm², with **three back-bonds per surface atom** connecting to the underlying tetrahedral lattice. In contrast, the (100) plane has only **two back-bonds per atom** and a lower atomic density of $\approx 6.78 \times 10^{14}$ atoms/cm².

The KOH etching reaction requires hydroxyl (OH⁻) ions to break Si–Si back-bonds sequentially. The energy barrier to break three back-bonds is significantly higher than for two. Consequently:

$$R_{(100)} \gg R_{(111)}, \quad \dfrac{R_{(100)}}{R_{(111)}} \approx 400 \text{ in KOH}$$

This selectivity, combined with the **54.74° angle** between (100) and (111), allows KOH to produce self-limiting V-grooves and atomically smooth {111} sidewalls.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of anisotropic wet etching of (100) silicon using KOH. Derive the relationship between mask opening width and the depth of the resulting V-groove. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A (100) silicon wafer is patterned with a square mask window of side **500 µm** aligned to the ⟨110⟩ direction. The wafer is etched in **30 wt% KOH at 80 °C**, where the (100) etch rate is **1.4 µm/min** and the (111) etch rate is **0.006 µm/min**. Determine:
- (i) The time required for the V-groove to fully form. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The total undercut on the (111) sidewalls at this time. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The minimum mask opening needed to etch a V-groove of depth **400 µm**. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of anisotropic KOH etching on (100) Si:**

A thermally grown SiO₂ layer (≈ 1 µm) serves as the etch mask. Standard photolithography opens rectangular windows aligned to the ⟨110⟩ flat of the wafer. The patterned wafer is immersed in a temperature-controlled KOH bath (typically 30–40 wt% KOH, 60–90 °C, with isopropyl alcohol to improve surface wetting and reduce bubble formation).

The reaction proceeds as:

$$\text{Si} + 2\,\text{OH}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Si(OH)}_2(\text{O}^-)_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\uparrow$$

OH⁻ ions attack the exposed Si surface. Because each (100) surface atom has only 2 back-bonds, etching proceeds rapidly in the vertical direction. As the etch deepens, four {111} sidewalls are exposed. Each (111) surface atom has 3 back-bonds, so OH⁻ attack is drastically slower. The {111} planes thus act as natural etch stops, inclined at **54.74°** to the (100) surface. The result is a **V-groove (or square pyramidal pit)** bounded by four (111) facets.

The depth at which the (111) facets from opposite walls meet (closure depth) is:

$$d_c = \dfrac{W_m}{2\,\tan(54.74^\circ)} = \dfrac{W_m}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Derivation (as in Section 3.2):**
After etch time $t$ at the bottom of the mask opening, the (111) sidewalls have advanced laterally by $u = d\,\tan(54.74^\circ)$. The two opposing sidewalls meet when $2u = W_m$:

$$2\,d_c\,\tan(54.74^\circ) = W_m \quad\Longrightarrow\quad d_c = \dfrac{W_m}{2\,\tan(54.74^\circ)} = \dfrac{W_m}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $W_m = 500$ µm, $R_{(100)} = 1.4$ µm/min, $R_{(111)} = 0.006$ µm/min.

**(i) V-groove closure time:**

$$d_c = \dfrac{W_m}{2\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{500}{2.828} = 176.78\;\mu m$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{d_c}{R_{(100)}} = \dfrac{176.78}{1.4} = 126.27\;\text{min} \approx 2\text{ h }6\text{ min}$$

**(ii) Undercut on the (111) sidewalls at closure:**

$$u = R_{(111)} \cdot t_c = 0.006 \times 126.27 = 0.758\;\mu m$$

This is negligible — confirming (111) acts as a true etch stop.

**(iii) Minimum mask opening for 400 µm V-groove:**

$$W_{\min} = 2\,d\,\sqrt{2} = 2 \times 400 \times 1.414 = 1131.4\;\mu m$$

Hence a mask opening of at least **1131 µm** is required. Smaller windows yield a truncated V-groove with a flat (100) base.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the various etch-stop techniques used in anisotropic wet etching of silicon, with emphasis on the **boron etch-stop** mechanism. Compare KOH, TMAH, and EDP. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A silicon cantilever beam is to be fabricated by anisotropic wet etching. A 5 µm-thick boron-doped layer (concentration $5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³) is used as the mechanical layer on a (100) wafer. The wafer is etched from the back in **TMAH (25 wt%) at 90 °C** for **120 minutes**.
- (i) If the (100) etch rate in undoped Si is **0.6 µm/min** and the boron-doped layer etch rate is **0.0004 µm/min**, what is the thickness of Si removed? **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Confirm whether the cantilever has been fully released. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) What is the etch selectivity between p⁺ and undoped Si? **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Etch-stop techniques:**

| Technique | Mechanism | Typical Selectivity |
|---|---|---|
| **Doping (Boron) etch-stop** | Heavy p⁺ doping introduces tensile lattice strain and reduces dangling-bond reactivity; back-bonds become harder to break | 100–10 000 : 1 |
| **Electrochemical etch-stop** | A reverse-biased p-n junction depletes holes at the etch front; only holes enable Si oxidation by OH⁻ | > 10 000 : 1 |
| **Photoelectrochemical** | n-Si under illumination + anodic bias forms a passive oxide that resists further etching | ~50 : 1 |
| **Native SiO₂** | Thermally grown thin SiO₂ acts as a built-in mask (very thin: < 1 µm usable) | High but limited thickness |

**Boron etch-stop physics:** Boron, with its smaller covalent radius than Si, introduces local lattice contraction in heavily doped regions. Above a critical boron concentration $N_B \approx 5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³, the Si–Si bond length is reduced and the valence electron density is depleted, raising the activation energy for OH⁻ attack from 0.595 eV to ≈ 0.85 eV. The etch rate drops by 3–4 orders of magnitude.

**Comparison of KOH, TMAH, EDP:**

| Etchant | Selectivity R(100)/R(111) | CMOS Compatible | Toxicity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| KOH | ~400 | No (K⁺ ions mobile in oxide) | Low | Low |
| TMAH | ~120 | **Yes** | Low | Moderate |
| EDP | ~50 | Partial | High (carcinogenic) | Moderate |

TMAH is preferred for **post-CMOS MEMS** because it does not introduce mobile alkali ions that would degrade MOS gate oxide reliability.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $R_{\text{undoped}} = 0.6$ µm/min, $R_{p^+} = 0.0004$ µm/min, $t = 120$ min, target layer = 5 µm boron-doped Si.

**(i) Thickness of undoped Si removed:**

$$d_{\text{removed}} = R_{\text{undoped}} \cdot t = 0.6 \times 120 = 72\;\mu m$$

(The 5 µm p⁺ layer is essentially unetched — see (ii) below.)

**Thickness removed from the p⁺ layer:**

$$d_{p^+} = 0.0004 \times 120 = 0.048\;\mu m = 48\text{ nm}$$

This is negligible compared to the 5 µm layer thickness, confirming the etch-stop is functioning.

**(ii) Has the cantilever been fully released?**

The wafer must be etched through the entire bulk thickness (typically 525 µm for a standard 6-inch wafer) until the etch front reaches the buried p⁺ layer. With $R_{\text{undoped}} = 0.6$ µm/min, the time to reach a 520 µm-thick undoped bulk is:

$$t_{\text{reach}} = \dfrac{520}{0.6} = 866.7\;\text{min} \approx 14.4\;\text{h}$$

Since the actual etch time is 120 min and the bulk etch in 120 min is only 72 µm, **the cantilever has NOT been fully released**. The etch has only partially penetrated the wafer — the p⁺ layer is still buried under 448 µm of undoped Si.

**To fully release, the wafer must be thinned first** (e.g., to ≈ 75 µm by grinding or RIE) so that 120 min of TMAH is sufficient to reach the p⁺ layer.

**(iii) Etch selectivity:**

$$S = \dfrac{R_{\text{undoped}}}{R_{p^+}} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.0004} = 1500$$

This is well within the expected range of 100–10 000 for boron etch-stop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Anisotropic Wet Etching Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 54.74° angle.** Many students compute V-groove depth using 45° — this gives a wrong answer because (111) planes in Si are not at 45° to (100). Use **$\tan(54.74°) = \sqrt{2}$**.
> 2. **Quoting etch rates without qualifier.** Always state the etchant, concentration, temperature, and crystal plane. A bare "etch rate = 1.4 µm/min" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing boron etch-stop with doping in general.** Light doping (e.g., 10¹⁵ cm⁻³) does **not** stop KOH etching — only heavy doping > 5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³ does. State the threshold concentration.
> 4. **Convex corner designs without compensation.** Designing a square mask opening on (100) Si leads to corner undercutting. Always mention **compensation structures** for full marks on design questions.
> 5. **Ignoring temperature dependence.** Etch rate is Arrhenius. If a question changes temperature by 10 °C around 80 °C, the rate roughly **doubles** — and full credit requires explicit acknowledgment.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units.** $W_m = 500$ µm → $d_c \approx 177$ µm (not 0.177 mm mixing, not 1.77 mm). Carry units through every step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Anisotropic Wet Etching**

- ☐ **Anisotropy Factor:** $AF = R_{\text{fast}}/R_{\text{slow}}$; for KOH on Si, $AF \approx 400$.
- ☐ **Crystal planes hierarchy (etch rate, descending):** (110) > (100) ≫ (111).
- ☐ **Universal angle:** **(100) and (111) intersect at 54.74°**; $\tan(54.74°) = \sqrt{2}$.
- ☐ **V-groove closure depth:** $d_c = W_m / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.354\,W_m$.
- ☐ **Minimum mask width for target depth $d$:** $W_{\min} = 2\sqrt{2}\,d$.
- ☐ **Etch time:** $t = d/R_{(100)}$.
- ☐ **(111) etch rate** in 30% KOH at 80 °C: ≈ 0.006 µm/min — acts as a **natural etch stop**.
- ☐ **Boron etch-stop threshold:** $N_B > 5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³; selectivity 100–10 000.
- ☐ **Convex corners** undercut; mitigate with **⟨100⟩-aligned compensation triangles**, $L_c = R \cdot t \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1)$.
- ☐ **Concave corners** remain sharp — no compensation needed.
- ☐ **Etchant comparison:** KOH (high selectivity, not CMOS-safe), TMAH (CMOS-safe, moderate selectivity), EDP (high selectivity, toxic).
- ☐ **Temperature dependence:** Arrhenius; $E_a \approx 0.6$ eV; ~2× rate per 10 °C near 80 °C.
- ☐ **Mask material:** Thermal SiO₂ (for KOH) or LPCVD Si₃N₄ (for TMAH and EDP) — etch rate of mask in Si etchant should be < 1 nm/min.
- ☐ **Alignment rule:** Mask openings aligned **exactly along ⟨110⟩** to expose a single set of {111} sidewalls.
- ☐ **On (110) wafers,** {111} planes are vertical — produces **vertical sidewalls** for comb-drive and trench capacitors.
- ☐ **HNA** = isotropic Si etchant; **KOH/TMAH/EDP** = anisotropic; **HF** = isotropic SiO₂ etchant (BOE/BHF for mask stripping).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary for last-minute revision:** *Anisotropic wet etching of Si exploits orientation-dependent bond density — the (111) plane has 3 back-bonds and barely etches in KOH, producing self-limiting 54.74° sidewalls that define V-grooves, membranes, and microstructures; control is by etchant choice, temperature, time, mask design, and etch-stop techniques (boron, electrochemical, photo).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Design and Packaging Consideration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Key Design and Packaging Consideration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Key Design and Packaging Considerations" in MEMS?

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Considerations in MEMS** refer to the multidisciplinary engineering constraints that govern the conceptualization, modeling, simulation, and physical realization of a micro-scale device. These constraints simultaneously span **mechanical, electrical, thermal, fluidic, optical, and biochemical** domains, and must be satisfied while remaining compatible with microfabrication processes such as photolithography, etching, deposition, and bonding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packaging Considerations in MEMS** refer to the protective enclosure, interconnection scheme, and environmental isolation strategy that houses the MEMS die. Unlike IC packaging, MEMS packaging must simultaneously protect **moving microstructures**, provide **selective access** to the physical domain (pressure, acceleration, chemicals, light), and manage **multi-physics stress** without degrading device performance.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Watch" Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **smart watch** that contains a tiny accelerometer (a MEMS device) inside it.

- The **Design** is the engineering of the accelerometer chip itself — choosing the right material (silicon beam that flexes), sizing the proof mass, designing the fingers of the comb capacitor, ensuring it survives a 1-meter drop, and ensuring the electronics can detect micro-capacitance changes of atto-farads ($\approx 10^{-18}\,\text{F}$).
- The **Packaging** is the watch casing: it must let the watch *feel* acceleration (open to the environment for mechanical coupling) while *protecting* the chip from sweat, dust, water vapor, and shock.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In MEMS, packaging is often the single largest contributor to the **Bill of Materials (BOM) cost** and overall device footprint — frequently 40% to 70% of the total cost — which is why KTU 2024 emphasizes this as a distinct module-level topic.

### Why "Design" and "Packaging" Cannot Be Separated in MEMS

In conventional CMOS ICs, the package is a passive afterthought. In MEMS, the package is part of the **functional system**:

| MEMS Type | Package Must Provide | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Sensor** | Hermetic port for media | Diaphragm must deflect under pressure |
| **Accelerometer** | Mechanical access to proof mass | Inertia must be sensed |
| **Gyroscope** | Vibration isolation | Drive mode vs. sense mode separation |
| **Micro-mirror (DLP)** | Optical window | Light path must remain unobstructed |
| **Bio/Chemical sensor** | Fluidic inlet/outlet | Reagent must contact the active surface |

---

## 1.2 Categories of Design Considerations

Design considerations in MEMS are broadly grouped into **five pillars**:

1. **Functional / Performance Design** — sensitivity, bandwidth, resolution, dynamic range.
2. **Fabrication / Process Design** — lithography resolution, etch selectivity, thin-film stress.
3. **Electrical Design** — interface circuits, power, noise, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
4. **Reliability Design** — fatigue, creep, fracture toughness, environmental aging.
5. **System / Integration Design** — packaging, interconnection, multi-die assembly.

### Categories of Packaging Considerations

1. **Environmental Protection** — hermeticity, moisture barrier, particle ingress.
2. **Mechanical Protection** — shock, vibration, die-attach stress.
3. **Thermal Management** — heat dissipation, coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) matching.
4. **Interface Design** — electrical leads, optical windows, fluidic ports.
5. **Manufacturing & Cost** — yield, scalability, automation.

> [!TIP]
> **Constant to Memorize:** The standard **Helium leak rate** for hermetic MEMS packages is typically $\leq 1 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{atm·cc/sec}$ as defined by **MIL-STD-883 Method 1014**.

---

## 1.3 The Multi-Disciplinary Nature — The "MEMS Design Hexagon"

MEMS design is governed by **six interacting physics domains** that must be co-simulated:

$$
\{ \text{Mechanical},\; \text{Electrical},\; \text{Thermal},\; \text{Fluidic},\; \text{Optical},\; \text{Chemical} \}
$$

A change in geometry (mechanical) alters capacitance (electrical), alters heat flow (thermal), and can affect gas damping (fluidic). This coupling is the central reason MEMS design demands **finite element / multi-physics tools** like **COMSOL, CoventorWare, ANSYS**, rather than the SPICE-style circuit tools used in pure electronics.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Young's Modulus of Single-Crystal Silicon (Si):** $\mathbf{E_{Si} = 130 - 190\,\text{GPa}}$ (anisotropic — direction dependent).
- **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon:** $\mathbf{\nu_{Si} \approx 0.28}$.
- **Density of Silicon:** $\mathbf{\rho_{Si} = 2330\,\text{kg/m}^3}$.
- **Fracture Strength of Silicon (micro-scale):** $\mathbf{\sigma_f \approx 1 - 7\,\text{GPa}}$ (size-dependent, Weibull statistics apply).
- **Thermal Conductivity of Silicon:** $\mathbf{k_{Si} = 150\,\text{W/(m·K)}}$.
- **CTE of Silicon:** $\mathbf{\alpha_{Si} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}}$.
- **Standard cavity pressure for inertial MEMS:** $\mathbf{\leq 10\,\text{mTorr}}$ to suppress squeeze-film damping.
- **Hermeticity threshold:** $\mathbf{\leq 1 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{atm·cc/sec He}}$ leak rate.

> [!NOTE]
> These values will appear in calculation problems and Part B derivations. Commit them to memory.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Flow for a MEMS Device

The KTU 2024 Scheme treats MEMS design as an **iterative closed-loop** process, not a linear one:

1. **System Specification** → Define range, sensitivity, bandwidth, environment.
2. **Conceptual Design** → Choose transduction principle (piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, thermal, optical).
3. **Modeling & Simulation** → Analytical models + FEM multi-physics.
4. **Material Selection** → Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, polysilicon, SU-8, metal, glass.
5. **Process Flow Design** → Select compatible fabrication steps.
6. **Layout & Mask Design** → Convert to 2D photolithographic masks.
7. **Prototype Fabrication & Testing** → Validate against spec.
8. **Packaging Design** → Choose die-attach, interconnects, seal.
9. **Reliability Qualification** → Thermal cycling, drop test, humidity aging.
10. **Iterate** → Feedback to step 1 if any spec is violated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis:** Notice that **Packaging Design is Step 8** — it is *not* an afterthought. Skipping it early is the #1 cause of MEMS project failure.

---

## 2.2 Material Selection — The "Design Triangle"

A MEMS designer balances three competing constraints:

- **Mechanical Performance** (stiffness, strength, fatigue life)
- **Process Compatibility** (etch chemistry, temperature budget, CMOS compatibility)
- **Cost & Availability** (wafers per month, $/wafer)

### Common MEMS Material Property Matrix

| Material | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Density (kg/m³) | CTE (10⁻⁶/K) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Crystal Si $\langle 100 \rangle$ | 130 | 2330 | 2.6 | Structural beams, proof masses |
| Polysilicon | 160 | 2320 | 2.8 | Surface micromachined layers |
| SiO₂ (thermal) | 70 | 2200 | 0.5 | Sacrificial layer, electrical isolation |
| Si₃N₄ (LPCVD) | 290 | 3100 | 3.2 | Membranes, high-stress layers |
| Aluminum | 70 | 2700 | 23 | Electrical interconnect |
| Copper | 110 | 8960 | 17 | RF MEMS, low-loss lines |
| Gold (Au) | 78 | 19300 | 14 | Bonding, bio-compatible electrodes |
| SU-8 (polymer) | 4 | 1200 | 52 | Thick structures, microfluidics |
| Pyrex 7740 glass | 63 | 2230 | 3.2 | Anodic bonding partner, optical window |
| PZT (piezoelectric) | 60 (mech.) | 7500 | 5 | Actuators, energy harvesters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Match the **CTE** of the die to the package/substrate to minimize thermal stress. This is why **Pyrex glass is the classic anodic bonding partner for silicon** — its CTE is nearly identical.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### A. Mechanical / Structural Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Normal stress (Pa) | Static load analysis |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Strain (dimensionless) | Beam deflection |
| 3 | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (linear elastic) | Small deflection regime |
| 4 | $\delta = \dfrac{F L^3}{3 E I}$ | Cantilever beam tip deflection | Accelerometer proof mass |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ | Rectangular cross-section 2nd moment of area | Beam bending stiffness |
| 6 | $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3}$ | Cantilever spring constant | Suspension design |
| 7 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency (Hz) | Resonator, gyroscope |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{k\,m}}{b}$ | Quality factor (viscous damping $b$) | Gyroscope drive mode |
| 9 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M\,c}{I}$ | Max bending stress at outer fiber | Fatigue analysis |
| 10 | $\sigma_f = \sigma_0 \left(\dfrac{L_0}{L}\right)^{1/m}$ | Size-dependent strength (Weibull) | Micro-scale fracture |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** For absolute value or "such that" notation, I have used `\vert` and `\mid` to keep markdown tables intact (e.g., $k \vert_{\text{at tip}} = 3EI/L^3$).

### B. Capacitive Transduction Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 11 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance |
| 12 | $\dfrac{dC}{dx} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$ | Sensitivity (slope) |
| 13 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ | Normalized capacitance change for small gap variation |
| 14 | $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\,V^2$ | Electrostatic actuation force |
| 15 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C\,V^2$ | Electrical energy stored in gap |

### C. Piezoresistive Readout Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 16 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L \sigma_L + \pi_T \sigma_T$ | Piezoresistive effect (longitudinal + transverse) |
| 17 | $V_{out} = V_{bridge} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{4 + \Delta R / R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output |

### D. Thermal / Packaging Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 18 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k A}$ | Thermal resistance (conduction) |
| 19 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Mismatch strain at an interface |
| 20 | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}\,\varepsilon_{th}$ (planar) | Bimetallic thermal stress (plane stress) |
| 21 | $\dot{Q}_{conv} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat loss |
| 22 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693\,V}{P_{atm}\,R_{leak}}$ | Hermeticity time-to-half-pressure |

### E. Damping / Fluidic Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 23 | $b_{squeeze} = \dfrac{\mu\,L^3\,b}{d^3}$ | Squeeze-film damping coefficient |
| 24 | $b_{slide} = \dfrac{\mu\,A}{d}$ | Slide-film (Couette) damping |
| 25 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,v\,L_c}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) |
| 26 | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L_c}$ | Knudsen number (continuum validity) |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are the daily working language of MEMS design engineers at Bosch, STMicroelectronics, Analog Devices, and Texas Instruments for designing automotive airbag accelerometers, gyroscopes in smartphones, and pressure sensors in medical catheters.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Design Pillar

### 2.4.1 Why Material Selection Drives Everything

Silicon dominates MEMS not because it is mechanically perfect, but because:
1. It has a **near-perfect crystal** (low defect density → reproducible mechanical properties).
2. It has a **native oxide (SiO₂)** that acts as an insulator, etch stop, and sacrificial layer.
3. It can be **bonded** to glass, silicon, or itself using anodic, fusion, or eutectic bonding.
4. The **CMOS industry** already has the entire toolchain for silicon processing.

### 2.4.2 Why Stress Matters at the Microscale

At the macro scale, a $1\,\mu\text{m}$ deflection is invisible. At the MEMS scale, it can be **100%** of a $1\,\mu\text{m}$ gap — large enough to crash the device into the substrate (so-called **pull-in** or **stiction** failure).

### 2.4.3 Why Hermetic Packaging is Non-Negotiable

Moisture ingress of just a few ppm can:
- Cause **electrical leakage** in capacitive sensors.
- Cause **electrochemical corrosion** of aluminum or polysilicon.
- Cause **hygroscopic swelling** in polymers like SU-8.
- Shift the **damping coefficient** of moving structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Hermeticity is *defined* by the leak rate, not the material. Even a metal can is not hermetic if it has a pinhole of 10 µm.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spring Constant of a Folded-Flexure Suspension

A folded flexure (also called a crab-leg or serpentine suspension) is the workhorse compliant element in MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes because it is **soft in one direction (x)** but **stiff in the perpendicular direction (y)**.

### Geometry Setup

Consider a single beam of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ in the folded-flexure. There are typically **$n$** such beams in parallel (each beam carries $1/n$ of the total force $F$).

### Step 1: Force on a Single Beam

The total axial force $F$ is distributed equally among $n$ parallel beams:

$$
F_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{F}{n}
$$

### Step 2: Axial Extension of a Single Beam

Using Hooke's law for axial deformation:

$$
\delta_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{single}}\,L}{E\,A}
$$

where the cross-sectional area is $A = w\,t$. Substituting $F_{\text{single}}$:

$$
\delta_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{F\,L}{n\,E\,w\,t}
$$

### Step 3: Total Displacement of the Proof Mass

The total tip displacement equals the axial extension of a single beam (since they all stretch equally in series to translate the mass):

$$
x = \delta_{\text{axial}} = \dfrac{F\,L}{n\,E\,w\,t}
$$

### Step 4: Spring Constant

By definition, $k = F / x$:

$$
k = \dfrac{F}{x} = \dfrac{n\,E\,w\,t}{L}
$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$
> \boxed{\,k_{\text{folded}} = \dfrac{n\,E\,w\,t}{L}\,}
> $$

### Step 5: Design Insight

- To **soften** the spring (lower $k$): increase $L$, reduce $w$, reduce $t$, or reduce $n$.
- A **50% increase in $L$** halves $k$ — the dominant design knob.
- A **50% reduction in $t$** halves $k$ — but $t$ is fixed by the fabrication process (e.g., SOI device layer thickness).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Natural Frequency of a MEMS Resonator

A proof mass $m$ suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ forms a second-order mass-spring-damper system. The undamped equation of motion is:

$$
m\,\ddot{x} + k\,x = 0
$$

### Step 1: Assume a Harmonic Solution

$$
x(t) = X_0\,e^{j\omega t}
$$

### Step 2: Differentiate Twice

$$
\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 X_0\,e^{j\omega t}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Equation of Motion

$$
-m\,\omega^2 X_0\,e^{j\omega t} + k\,X_0\,e^{j\omega t} = 0
$$

### Step 4: Factor Out Common Terms

$$
X_0\,e^{j\omega t}\,\bigl(k - m\,\omega^2\bigr) = 0
$$

For non-trivial solutions ($X_0 \neq 0$, $e^{j\omega t} \neq 0$), the term in parentheses must vanish:

$$
k - m\,\omega^2 = 0
$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Angular Natural Frequency

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}
$$

### Step 6: Convert to Frequency in Hz

$$
f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}
$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$
> \boxed{\,f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}\,}
> $$

### Step 7: Substitute the Folded-Flexure Spring Constant

$$
f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{n\,E\,w\,t}{m\,L}}
$$

> **Design Example:** A gyroscope designed for a drive frequency of $f_n = 20\,\text{kHz}$ requires careful selection of $L$ and $m$ to hit this target, while the sense mode must have a deliberate frequency offset (typically $1-3\%$) to enable Coriolis coupling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pull-In Voltage of a Parallel-Plate Capacitive Actuator

A key design constraint for any electrostatically actuated MEMS device (e.g., RF MEMS switch, micromirror, microvalve) is the **pull-in voltage** — the voltage at which the movable plate catastrophically snaps to the fixed plate.

### Setup

- Fixed plate at $x = 0$.
- Movable plate suspended by spring $k$ at equilibrium gap $d_0$.
- Applied DC voltage $V$ creates electrostatic attraction.

### Step 1: Electrostatic Force on the Movable Plate

The capacitance at gap $x$ is:

$$
C(x) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x}
$$

The electrostatic force (energy gradient method) is:

$$
F_e(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\,V^2 = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A\,V^2}{2\,x^2}
$$

The negative sign indicates attractive (pulling toward the fixed plate).

### Step 2: Mechanical Restoring Force

$$
F_m(x) = k\,(d_0 - x)
$$

This is linear in displacement, directed away from the fixed plate.

### Step 3: Force Balance at Equilibrium

At a stable equilibrium, the spring force balances the electrostatic force:

$$
k\,(d_0 - x) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A\,V^2}{2\,x^2}
$$

### Step 4: Stability Condition (Marginal Stability)

A static equilibrium becomes **unstable** when the derivative of the net force with respect to displacement changes sign. Equivalently, the spring stiffness must exceed the negative electrostatic stiffness:

$$
k > \left\vert \dfrac{\partial F_e}{\partial x}\right\vert
$$

Computing the derivative of the electrostatic force:

$$
\dfrac{\partial F_e}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A\,V^2}{x^3}
$$

Setting $k = \partial F_e / \partial x$ at the critical point and combining with the force balance gives the **classic pull-in condition**:

$$
x_{PI} = \dfrac{2\,d_0}{3}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute $x_{PI}$ into the Force Balance

$$
k\,\left(d_0 - \dfrac{2 d_0}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A\,V_{PI}^2}{2\,\left(\dfrac{2 d_0}{3}\right)^2}
$$

$$
k\,\dfrac{d_0}{3} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A\,V_{PI}^2}{2 \cdot \dfrac{4 d_0^2}{9}}
$$

$$
k\,\dfrac{d_0}{3} = \dfrac{9\,\varepsilon_0 A\,V_{PI}^2}{8\,d_0^2}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for $V_{PI}$

$$
V_{PI}^2 = \dfrac{8\,k\,d_0^3}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}
$$

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,k\,d_0^3}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$
> \boxed{\,V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,k\,d_0^3}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}\,}
> $$

> **Design Insight:** The pull-in voltage scales as $d_0^{3/2}$. Reducing the gap from $4\,\mu\text{m}$ to $1\,\mu\text{m}$ reduces $V_{PI}$ by a factor of $8$ — the strongest design lever. However, smaller gaps increase squeeze-film damping and reduce dynamic range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Stress at a Die-Attach Interface

### Step 1: Mismatch Strain

When a silicon die (CTE $\alpha_{Si}$) is bonded to a package substrate (CTE $\alpha_{sub}$) and cooled from bonding temperature $T_b$ to operating temperature $T_{op}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T = (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{Si})\,(T_b - T_{op})
$$

### Step 2: Convert to Stress (Plane Strain Approximation)

For a thin die bonded to a thick substrate, the die is constrained in-plane:

$$
\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_{Si}}{1 - \nu_{Si}}\,\varepsilon_{th}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$
\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_{Si}}{1 - \nu_{Si}}\,(\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{Si})\,(T_b - T_{op})
$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$
> \boxed{\,\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_{Si}}{1 - \nu_{Si}}\,\Delta\alpha\,\Delta T\,}
> $$

### Numerical Check (Common KTU Problem)

Given: $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}$, $\alpha_{Kovar} = 5.2 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}$, $E_{Si} = 170\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$, $T_b = 350^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{op} = 25^\circ\text{C}$.

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{170 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.28}\,(5.2 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6}\,(350 - 25)
$$

$$
\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{170 \times 10^9}{0.72} \times 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 325
$$

$$
\sigma_{th} = 2.361 \times 10^{11} \times 8.45 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\sigma_{th} \approx 1.99 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Pa} = 199\,\text{MPa}
$$

This stress is well below silicon's fracture strength ($\sim 1-7\,\text{GPa}$), so the die survives — but it can still bias a pressure sensor diaphragm reading.

> **Design Rule:** Pick a package substrate with $\alpha_{sub}$ within $1 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}$ of silicon (Kovar is borderline; AlN and Mo are excellent matches).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Sensitivity of a Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

A square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $h$ has maximum bending stress at the edge midpoint:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308\,P\,a^2}{h^2}
$$

The fractional resistance change in a longitudinal piezoresistor placed at the edge is:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L\,\sigma_{max} = \pi_L \cdot \dfrac{0.308\,P\,a^2}{h^2}
$$

Bridge output (with 4 active piezoresistors):

$$
V_{out} = V_{in}\,\dfrac{\pi_L \cdot 0.308\,P\,a^2}{2\,h^2}
$$

> **Final Result (Sensitivity):**
> $$
> \boxed{\,S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{P} = \dfrac{V_{in}\,\pi_L\,0.308\,a^2}{2\,h^2}\,}
> $$

> **Design Insight:** Sensitivity scales as $a^2 / h^2$. A larger, thinner diaphragm is dramatically more sensitive, but becomes fragile and sensitive to shock.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Transduction Principle

| Transduction | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacitive** | High sensitivity, low power, temperature stable | Small signals (aF), parasitic-sensitive, need ASIC | Accelerometers, gyroscopes, microphones |
| **Piezoresistive** | Simple readout, large signal, DC-capable | High temp-co, power hungry, noise | Pressure sensors, force sensors |
| **Piezoelectric** | No DC power, high bandwidth | Charge leakage, no static response | Microphones, energy harvesters, SAW |
| **Thermal** | CMOS compatible, simple fab | Slow, convection-sensitive | Flow sensors, IR detectors |
| **Optical** | EMI-immune, high precision | Expensive, alignment-sensitive | Lab-on-chip, gyroscopes (ring laser) |
| **Resonant** | Ultra-high Q, digital output (frequency) | Complex electronics, packaging | Precision sensors, oscillators |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — MEMS Design & Packaging Closed-Loop Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spec[System Specification] --> concept[Conceptual Design]
    concept --> model[Modeling and Simulation]
    model --> material[Material Selection]
    material --> process[Process Flow Design]
    process --> layout[Layout and Mask Design]
    layout --> fab[Fabrication]
    fab --> test[Prototype Testing]
    test --> pack[Packaging Design]
    pack --> qual[Reliability Qualification]
    qual --> feedback[Feedback to Spec]
    feedback --> spec
    pack --> dieAttach[Die Attach]
    pack --> wireBond[Wire Bond]
    pack --> seal[Hermetic Seal]
    pack --> port[Interface Port]
    dieAttach --> finalPack[Final Package]
    wireBond --> finalPack
    seal --> finalPack
    port --> finalPack
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The outer loop (spec → qual → feedback) shows the iterative nature of MEMS design. The bottom branch (pack → finalPack) shows that packaging itself has four sub-decisions: die attach, wire bond, hermetic seal, and interface port.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Packaging Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph external[External Environment]
        env[Temperature Humidity Shock Vibration Media]
    end
    subgraph package[MEMS Package]
        lid[Lid or Cap]
        seal[Seal Ring or Solder]
        substrate[Package Substrate or Leadframe]
        dieAttach[Die Attach Material]
    end
    subgraph chip[MEMS Die]
        memsStruct[Movable MEMS Structure]
        asic[ASIC or Readout Circuit]
        capElec[Capacitive Pads]
        bondPad[Wire Bond Pads]
    end
    env --> lid
    lid --> seal
    seal --> substrate
    substrate --> dieAttach
    dieAttach --> memsStruct
    dieAttach --> asic
    memsStruct --> capElec
    asic --> bondPad
    bondPad --> substrate
    asic --- memsStruct
```

> **Functional interpretation:** The MEMS die has two parts — the **movable microstructure** (senses the stimulus) and the **ASIC** (processes the signal). The package must protect both while allowing the stimulus to reach the microstructure. The "port" is the deliberately designed opening (e.g., a pressure inlet) through the lid.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Multi-Physics Coupling in MEMS Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MECH[Mechanical Domain] -->|Stress strain| COUPLE
    ELEC[Electrical Domain] -->|Voltage current| COUPLE
    THERM[Thermal Domain] -->|Heat flux| COUPLE
    FLUID[Fluidic Domain] -->|Pressure flow| COUPLE
    OPT[Optical Domain] -->|Light intensity| COUPLE
    CHEM[Chemical Domain] -->|Concentration| COUPLE
    COUPLE[Coupled Multi Physics Simulation] --> RESULT[Device Response]
    RESULT --> VALIDATE[Compare to Spec]
    VALIDATE -->|Mismatch| MECH
    VALIDATE -->|Mismatch| ELEC
    VALIDATE -->|Mismatch| THERM
    VALIDATE -->|Mismatch| FLUID
    VALIDATE -->|Mismatch| OPT
    VALIDATE -->|Mismatch| CHEM
```

> **Pedagogical point:** This is the central reason MEMS designers need tools like **COMSOL Multiphysics, ANSYS, or CoventorWare** — the domains are bidirectionally coupled, and analytic closed-form solutions exist only for idealized cases.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Hermetic Packaging Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[External Environment] --> LID[Lid Kovar or Ceramic]
    LID --> SEAL[Seal Material Solder Glass Epoxy]
    SEAL --> SUB[Substrate AlN or LTCC]
    SUB --> DA[Die Attach Eutectic or Conductive Epoxy]
    DA --> DIE[Silicon MEMS Die]
    DIE --> ASIC[ASIC Die]
    ASIC --> WB[Wire Bonds Gold or Aluminum]
    WB --> SUB
    SUB --> LEADS[External Leads or BGA Balls]
```

> **Reading note:** The order from top to bottom represents the **physical stack** of a typical hermetic MEMS module. The "Seal Material" row is the most critical design choice — it determines the package's hermeticity class (hermetic, semi-hermetic, or non-hermetic).

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Design Failure Mode Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MEMS Device Failure] --> B[Design Failure]
    A --> C[Fabrication Failure]
    A --> D[Packaging Failure]
    A --> E[Application Failure]
    B --> B1[Stiction]
    B --> B2[Pull In]
    B --> B3[Fatigue Fracture]
    C --> C1[Etch Residue]
    C --> C2[Mask Misalignment]
    C --> C3[Thin Film Stress]
    D --> D1[Hermeticity Loss]
    D --> D2[Die Attach Stress]
    D --> D3[Wire Bond Lift]
    D --> D4[Contamination]
    E --> E1[Over Range Shock]
    E --> E2[Thermal Runaway]
    E --> E3[EMI Susceptibility]
```

> **Exam tip:** When a KTU question says "Discuss failure modes in MEMS packaging," this tree gives a complete, examiner-friendly answer structure.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]
**"List any three key differences between IC packaging and MEMS packaging."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | IC Packaging | MEMS Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Purely protective enclosure | Must be functionally transparent to the measurand (pressure, acceleration, light, etc.) |
| 2 | No moving parts inside | Must accommodate moving microstructures without restricting motion |
| 3 | Hermeticity is desirable but not always mandatory | Hermeticity is often essential to prevent stiction, corrosion, and damping changes |
| 4 | Thermal management is the primary reliability concern | Multi-physics stress (thermal + mechanical + fluidic) must be co-managed |
| 5 | Standard JEDEC outlines | Mostly custom, application-specific geometries |

*(Any 3 well-explained points = 3 marks; examiner awards 1 mark per valid difference with explanation.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]
**"What is pull-in voltage in a capacitive MEMS actuator? Why is it a critical design parameter?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **pull-in voltage ($V_{PI}$)** is the threshold DC voltage at which the electrostatic attraction overcomes the mechanical spring restoring force, causing the movable plate to catastrophically snap onto the fixed plate.

For a parallel-plate actuator with spring constant $k$, initial gap $d_0$, and electrode area $A$:

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,k\,d_0^3}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

It is critical because (1 mark each):
1. It **defines the upper safe operating voltage** — exceeding it destroys the device.
2. It **constrains the dynamic range** of the actuator (usable displacement is limited to $d_0/3$).
