3. It **scales with $d_0^{3/2}$** — making the gap the dominant design lever for low-voltage operation.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice (A or B). Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Dec 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the various **mechanical design considerations** for a MEMS cantilever beam used as a mass sensor. Derive the spring constant and natural frequency expressions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **material selection criteria** for MEMS structural, sacrificial, and packaging layers. Support your answer with specific material examples and their properties. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Diagram:**

A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$ is anchored at one end, with a proof mass $m$ (or analyte adsorption) at the free end. The beam is typically fabricated from single-crystal silicon using surface or bulk micromachining.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Mechanical Design Considerations:**
- **Stiffness:** Must be low enough to give measurable deflection under small loads, but high enough to resist shock.
- **Stress concentration:** Sharp corners concentrate stress; use fillets.
- **Residual stress:** Thin-film deposition introduces built-in stress that can buckle the beam. Designs must use stress-relief features (e.g., dimples, serpentine springs).
- **Fatigue life:** Cyclic loading (vibration) can initiate fracture; silicon is brittle, so crack initiation is the limiting factor.
- **Stiction prevention:** Anti-stiction coatings (self-assembled monolayers), dimples, and proper release drying (critical point drying, freeze-drying) must be designed in.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Spring Constant Derivation:**

For a cantilever with end load $F$:

$$
\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{F L^3}{3 E I}, \quad I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
k = \dfrac{F}{\delta_{tip}} = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3} = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Natural Frequency:**

$$
f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}
$$

where $m_{eff}$ for a cantilever with end mass is $m_{eff} = m + 0.24\,\rho_{Si}\,L\,w\,t$ (the second term is the effective mass of the distributed beam itself).

$$
\boxed{\,f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3\,(m + 0.24\,\rho_{Si}\,L\,w\,t)}}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Structural Layer Criteria:**
- High Young's modulus for stiffness.
- Low density for low inertia.
- Low residual stress.
- Compatible with micromachining (selective etch vs. sacrificial layer).
- *Example:* **Polysilicon** for surface micromachining, **SOI device layer** for bulk micromachining.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Sacrificial Layer Criteria:**
- Must be selectively etchable relative to the structural layer (high etch selectivity).
- Must withstand the structural layer deposition temperature.
- *Example:* **SiO₂** (sacrificial) etched by **HF**; the structural layer is polysilicon. Or **photoresist / polyimide** sacrificed in oxygen plasma or acetone.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Packaging Layer Criteria:**
- CTE matching to silicon.
- Hermeticity (low moisture permeability).
- Mechanical strength.
- *Example:* **Pyrex 7740 glass** for anodic bonding, **Kovar** for metal packages, **AlN** for high-reliability ceramic packages.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Summary Table:**

| Layer | Material Example | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| Structural | Polysilicon, SOI Si | High $E$, low stress |
| Sacrificial | SiO₂, photoresist | High etch selectivity |
| Bonding partner | Pyrex, AuSn solder | CTE match, hermeticity |
| Electrical interconnect | Al, Au, Cu | Low resistivity, bondability |
| Anti-stiction | SAM (FDTS, OTS) | Low surface energy |

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** What are the major **packaging considerations** for MEMS devices? Explain the role of **hermetic sealing, die attach, and wire bonding** with relevant examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **parallel-plate capacitive accelerometer** has the following specifications:
- Proof mass: $m = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{kg}$
- Spring constant: $k = 2\,\text{N/m}$
- Initial gap: $d_0 = 4\,\mu\text{m}$
- Electrode area: $A = 1\,\text{mm}^2$
- Dielectric: air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$)

Compute: **(i)** the natural frequency, **(ii)** the pull-in voltage, and **(iii)** the maximum stable displacement. Comment on the design implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Major Packaging Considerations:**
1. **Hermetic sealing** — prevents moisture/particle ingress; required for inertial and optical MEMS.
2. **Die attach** — bonds the die to the package substrate; controls thermal & mechanical stress.
3. **Wire bonding / flip-chip** — electrical interconnects; gold ball bonding is most common.
4. **Interface port** — selective opening to the environment (pressure port, optical window, fluidic channel).
5. **Thermal management** — heat sinking, low-CTE substrate.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Hermetic Sealing:**
- **Metal can + glass-to-metal seal** — classic, used in automotive pressure sensors. Lid is Kovar, sealed to a Kovar header by soldering or resistance welding.
- **Ceramic package** — AlN or Al₂O₃ substrate, gold-tin (AuSn) solder seal ring. Used in high-reliability aerospace.
- **Wafer-level packaging** — caps are fabricated at wafer scale using another silicon wafer with cavities, bonded via AuSi eutectic or glass frit. Lowest cost at high volume.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Die Attach:**
- **Eutectic AuSi** (bonding at $\sim 363^\circ\text{C}$) — excellent thermal conductivity, hermetic, stress-prone.
- **Silver-glass** — lower stress, lower thermal conductivity.
- **Conductive epoxy** — cheap, non-hermetic, used in consumer-grade devices.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Wire Bonding:**
- **Gold ball bonding** — most common; wedge-wedge or ball-wedge.
- **Aluminum wedge bonding** — used when Au cannot contaminate the die.
- **Copper bonding** — emerging, lower cost, but oxidation-prone.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $m = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{kg}$, $k = 2\,\text{N/m}$, $d_0 = 4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $A = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$.

**Part (i) — Natural Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{5 \times 10^{-7}}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{5 \times 10^{-7}}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{6}} = 2000\,\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n = \dfrac{2000}{2\pi} \approx 318.3\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Part (ii) — Pull-in Voltage (3 marks):**

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,k\,d_0^3}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 2 \times (4 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

Computing the numerator:

$$
8 \times 2 \times 64 \times 10^{-18} = 1024 \times 10^{-18} = 1.024 \times 10^{-15}
$$

Computing the denominator:

$$
27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-6} = 27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-18} = 239.06 \times 10^{-18} = 2.39 \times 10^{-16}
$$

Ratio:

$$
\dfrac{1.024 \times 10^{-15}}{2.39 \times 10^{-16}} \approx 4.284
$$

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{4.284} \approx 2.07\,\text{V}
$$

**Part (iii) — Maximum Stable Displacement (1.5 marks):**

$$
x_{max} = \dfrac{d_0}{3} = \dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.33\,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Part (iv) — Design Implications (1 mark):**
- The pull-in voltage is small ($\sim 2\,\text{V}$), making the device **CMOS-compatible** without external high-voltage drivers.
- The natural frequency of $\sim 318\,\text{Hz}$ is appropriate for **low-frequency vibration / tilt sensing** but **not** for high-frequency shock sensing.
- The $1.33\,\mu\text{m}$ stable range limits **dynamic range**; a feedback (closed-loop) architecture can extend it by servo-controlling the voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget the **$1/2$ factor** in the electrostatic force formula $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial C/\partial x) V^2$ — losing 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Pull-in is assumed to occur at $x = d_0/2$ instead of $x = 2d_0/3$ — losing 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up **$V_{PI}$** (collapse voltage) with **$V_{PI}$ at the bond pads** — for CMOS ASICs the on-chip voltage may be charge-pumped to $10-30\,\text{V}$ to reach $V_{PI}$ — context matters.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to **state units** in the final answer. Always write "$V_{PI} = 2.07\,\text{V}$", not just "$2.07$".
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In pull-in derivations, students often equate $k$ directly to $\partial F_e/\partial x$ **without** also enforcing the force balance — you need **both** equations to get $x_{PI} = 2d_0/3$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- ⭐ MEMS packaging is **functional**, not just protective — it is part of the device.
- ⭐ The **5 design pillars**: Functional, Fabrication, Electrical, Reliability, System/Integration.
- ⭐ The **6 coupled physics domains**: Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Fluidic, Optical, Chemical.
- ⭐ Hermeticity is defined by **leak rate** ($\leq 10^{-8}\,\text{atm·cc/sec He}$), not by the material.
- ⭐ **Silicon is the dominant MEMS material** because of crystal perfection, native oxide, CMOS toolchain, and bondability.

### Critical Formulas (Must be at fingertips)
- ⭐ Spring constant (cantilever): $k = 3EI/L^3 = E w t^3 / (4 L^3)$
- ⭐ Spring constant (folded flexure): $k = n E w t / L$
- ⭐ Natural frequency: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$
- ⭐ Pull-in voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$
- ⭐ Pull-in displacement: $x_{PI} = 2 d_0 / 3$
- ⭐ Thermal stress at die-attach: $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}\Delta\alpha\,\Delta T$

### Material & Process "Cheat Codes"
- ⭐ Silicon CTE = $2.6 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{K}$; pair with **Pyrex 7740** for anodic bonding.
- ⭐ **AuSi eutectic** at $363^\circ\text{C}$ is the gold-standard hermetic die attach.
- ⭐ **HF releases SiO₂** sacrificial layers; use **critical point drying** to prevent stiction.
- ⭐ **Self-assembled monolayers (FDTS, OTS)** are the standard anti-stiction coatings.

### Common Examiner Traps
- ⭐ Pull-in is at $2d_0/3$, not $d_0/2$.
- ⭐ Sensitivity of piezoresistive sensor scales as $a^2/h^2$ (square law, not linear).
- ⭐ MEMS die-attach stress is in **plane-strain** (factor $1/(1-\nu)$), not plane-stress.
- ⭐ Capacitance change per unit displacement goes as $1/d^2$ — gap dominates.

### Reliability & Failure Modes to List
- ⭐ Stiction (in-use and release)
- ⭐ Pull-in collapse
- ⭐ Fatigue fracture (cyclic loading)
- ⭐ Hermeticity loss (pinholes, seal failure)
- ⭐ Die-attach stress (CTE mismatch)
- ⭐ Wire-bond lift (Kirkendall voids, thermal fatigue)
- ⭐ Contamination-induced drift

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark question, **always** include (1) a labeled diagram, (2) the governing equation, (3) at least one numerical example, and (4) a concluding design insight. Examiners reward structured, complete answers over verbose prose.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Stack Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wafer Stack Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In MEMS (Micro Electro Mechanical Systems) fabrication, **Wafer Stack Thickness** is defined as the **cumulative vertical dimension** of all the laminated/bonded wafer layers, interface materials, and intermediate films that collectively constitute a fully integrated MEMS device or packaged microsystem. It is measured along the wafer's normal axis (typically the Z-axis) and is a critical geometric parameter governing mechanical rigidity, thermal budget, electrical interconnect length, and overall form factor of the finished device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 - Wafer Bonding & Packaging):**
> Wafer stack thickness is the resultant physical dimension obtained after bonding processes such as **anodic bonding, fusion bonding, eutectic bonding, and adhesive bonding**. It is a function of the *individual wafer thicknesses*, *intermediate bonding layer thickness*, and *any thinning operations* (back-grinding, CMP, etch-back) performed on the constituent wafers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine building a **layered sandwich cake**:
- The **bottom bread slice** is the *handle wafer* (provides mechanical support).
- The **filling** is the *bonding layer* (anodic glass, gold-tin, SU-8, BCB).
- The **top bread slice** is the *device wafer* (contains the active MEMS structures).
- A **thin icing layer** on top represents *passivation or metallization*.

The "stack thickness" of this cake is the distance from the bottom of the handle wafer to the top of the device/passivation layer. If you squeeze the cake too thin, the cream spills out (bonding layer failure). If it's too thick, it doesn't fit in the lunchbox (package cavity depth mismatch). The MEMS engineer must therefore **balance mechanical robustness with package integration constraints** — exactly like a chef balancing cake height with container size.

## 1.3 Constituent Layers of a Typical MEMS Wafer Stack

A standard multi-wafer MEMS stack consists of the following sequential components (from bottom to top):

1. **Handle Wafer (Substrate)** — Bulk silicon, glass, or SiC providing structural backbone.
2. **Buried Oxide / Insulator Layer** — Thermally grown SiO₂ for electrical isolation (optional).
3. **Lower Bonding Interface** — Glass frit, Au-Sn, Au-Si, or polymer adhesive.
4. **Device Wafer (Active Layer)** — Contains microstructures (cantilevers, membranes, proof mass).
5. **Sacrificial Layer (if present)** — Removed during release etch.
6. **Structural Functional Layer** — Polysilicon, single-crystal Si, SiN, or metal.
7. **Metallization / Interconnect Layer** — Al, Cu, Au for electrical routing.
8. **Passivation Layer** — SiN, SiO₂, or polymer for environmental protection.
9. **Cap Wafer (optional)** — Seals the device in a hermetic or quasi-hermetic cavity.
10. **Upper Bonding Interface** — Seals the cap wafer to the device wafer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Wafer Thickness Reference Metrics (Bold Constants):**
> - 100 mm wafer: **525 µm ± 25 µm**
> - 150 mm wafer: **675 µm ± 25 µm**
> - 200 mm wafer: **725 µm ± 25 µm**
> - 300 mm wafer: **775 µm ± 25 µm**
> 
> These are the **SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International) standard thicknesses** for prime-grade wafers commonly encountered in KTU numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why Wafer Stack Thickness Matters

The total stack thickness is not merely a geometric parameter — it directly influences:

- **Mechanical stiffness** of released structures (bending rigidity scales as $t^3$).
- **Resonant frequency** of vibrating MEMS elements (scales as $1/t$ for flexural modes).
- **Thermal mass** and heat dissipation capacity.
- **Through-Silicon Via (TSV) aspect ratio** requirements.
- **Dicing blade clearance** and packaging yield.
- **Residual stress and wafer bow** (governed by the Stoney equation).
- **Hermeticity** and cavity pressure stability in capped devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Wafer Stack Thickness

The total stack thickness $T_{stack}$ of a generic multi-wafer MEMS assembly is expressed as a **linear superposition** of the thicknesses of all constituent layers:

$$
T_{stack} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \cdots + t_N
$$

where $t_i$ is the thickness of the $i^{th}$ layer and $N$ is the total number of distinct layers in the stack.

For a **typical 3-wafer MEMS stack** (handle + device + cap), the expression expands to:

$$
T_{stack} = t_{handle} + t_{bond,1} + t_{device} + t_{cap} + t_{bond,2} + t_{metal} + t_{passivation}
$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Substrate Selection:** Choose handle wafer thickness based on the mechanical load it must support. Thicker substrates reduce wafer bow but increase mass.
- **Step 2 — Device Wafer Thinning:** The device wafer is often thinned (via grinding, wet etching, or CMP) to define the structural layer thickness. This $t_{device}$ is the single most critical dimensional parameter for the MEMS transducer.
- **Step 3 — Bonding Layer Deposition:** The bonding interface (anodic, eutectic, or adhesive) adds a measurable thickness — typically **1 µm to 50 µm** depending on technique.
- **Step 4 — Cap Wafer Integration:** For hermetic packages, a cap wafer is bonded, contributing its bulk thickness (often 300–500 µm) plus a recessed cavity.
- **Step 5 — Surface Processing:** Metallization and passivation add sub-micron to few-micron layers.
- **Step 6 — Post-Bond Annealing:** Thermal cycling may cause interdiffusion that effectively alters the bonding layer thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Stack thickness problems frequently combine *layer addition* with *subtractive processing* (e.g., after bonding, the handle wafer is back-thinned from 675 µm to 200 µm). Always parse the fabrication sequence to identify which layers are added vs. removed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_{stack} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$ | Linear superposition of layer thicknesses | µm |
| 2 | $T_{eff} = t_{device} - t_{sacrificial}$ | Net movable layer thickness after release | µm |
| 3 | $\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \cdot \sigma \cdot t_f}{M_s \cdot t_s^2}$ | Stoney's curvature (wafer bow) due to film stress | m$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $f_n = \frac{\alpha_n^2}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot I}{\rho \cdot A \cdot L^4}}$ | Resonant frequency of cantilever/membrane (function of $t$) | Hz |
| 5 | $I = \frac{w \cdot t^3}{12}$ | Area moment of inertia (cubic dependence on thickness) | m$^4$ |
| 6 | $k = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Spring constant of cantilever (cubic in $t$) | N/m |
| 7 | $T_{thinned} = T_{initial} - R_{grind} \cdot t_{process}$ | Remaining thickness after back-grinding | µm |
| 8 | $C_{TSV} = \pi \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot \frac{t_{stack}}{ \ln(R_{out}/R_{in}) }$ | TSV parasitic capacitance (proportional to stack thickness) | F |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{stack} = \alpha_{CTE} \cdot \Delta T \cdot T_{stack}$ | Thermal expansion of stack | µm |
| 10 | $R_a = \frac{t_{stack}}{2 \sqrt{3 \cdot \sigma_{yield}/E}}$ | Critical aspect ratio limit for thin-film buckling | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The cubic dependence of bending stiffness $I$ and spring constant $k$ on thickness $t$ is the most heavily tested concept in KTU problems. A 2× increase in structural layer thickness produces an **8× increase in stiffness** — a non-intuitive but exam-critical relationship.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Implications

- **Inertial Sensors (Accelerometers, Gyroscopes):** The proof mass thickness directly sets the sensor's sensitivity and noise floor.
- **Pressure Sensors:** Membrane thickness defines the pressure-sensitive deflection range.
- **RF MEMS Switches:** Stacked electrode thickness determines pull-in voltage.
- **Microfluidic Devices:** Stack thickness sets the channel depth via SU-8 or deep-RIE control.
- **3D IC Integration:** TSV depth is a function of stack thickness and determines interconnect density.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Stack Thickness for a Multi-Wafer MEMS Assembly

**Problem Statement:** A MEMS device consists of a 675 µm handle wafer (Si), a 5 µm anodic bonding glass layer, a 525 µm device wafer (Si) that is subsequently thinned to 50 µm, a 300 µm cap wafer (glass), and a 2 µm Au-Sn eutectic seal. A 1 µm Al metallization and 0.5 µm SiN passivation are deposited on the cap exterior. Compute the final stack thickness.

### Step-by-Step Expansion

We identify the fabrication order: handle → lower bond → device wafer → thinning → cap bond → metal → passivation.

$$
T_{stack} = t_{handle} + t_{bond,1} + t_{device,thinned} + t_{cap} + t_{bond,2} + t_{metal} + t_{passivation}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
T_{stack} = 675 + 5 + 50 + 300 + 2 + 1 + 0.5
$$

Each term is added sequentially:

$$
T_{stack} = 680 + 50 + 300 + 2 + 1 + 0.5
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 730 + 300 + 2 + 1 + 0.5
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 1030 + 2 + 1 + 0.5
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 1032 + 1 + 0.5
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 1033 + 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{stack} = 1033.5 \; \mu m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Handle (675) + bond1 (5) = 680. Add thinned device (50) → 730. Add cap (300) → 1030. Add eutectic (2) → 1032. Add Al (1) → 1033. Add SiN (0.5) → 1033.5 µm. **The unthinned device wafer is NOT added** because thinning is a subtractive post-bond process.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoney Equation for Wafer Bow from Stack Mismatch

When a thin film of thickness $t_f$ and biaxial stress $\sigma$ is deposited on a substrate of thickness $t_s$ and biaxial modulus $M_s$, the curvature $\kappa$ of the composite is given by the **Stoney equation** (1909):

$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \cdot \sigma \cdot t_f}{M_s \cdot t_s^2}
$$

**Derivation from First Principles (Plate Bending Theory):**

For a thin film on a thick substrate, force and moment equilibrium must be satisfied. The film exerts a force per unit length:

$$
F = \sigma \cdot t_f
$$

This force, acting at the neutral plane offset by $t_s / 2$, produces a bending moment:

$$
M = F \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \sigma \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}
$$

The plate bending equation relates moment to curvature:

$$
M = \frac{M_s \cdot t_s^2}{6} \cdot \kappa
$$

Equating the two expressions for moment:

$$
\sigma \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{M_s \cdot t_s^2}{6} \cdot \kappa
$$

Solving for $\kappa$:

$$
\kappa = \frac{6 \cdot \sigma \cdot t_f \cdot t_s}{2 \cdot M_s \cdot t_s^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\kappa = \frac{6 \cdot \sigma \cdot t_f}{M_s \cdot t_s^2}}
$$

where $M_s = \frac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s}$ is the biaxial modulus of the substrate.

**Physical Interpretation:**
- Curvature scales **linearly** with film stress $\sigma$ and film thickness $t_f$.
- Curvature scales **inversely as the square** of substrate thickness $t_s$. This is why thicker handle wafers are used to suppress wafer bow in bonded stacks.

**Numerical Example:**

Given: $\sigma = 100$ MPa, $t_f = 1$ µm, $t_s = 525$ µm, $M_s$ for Si (100) $\approx 180$ GPa.

$$
\kappa = \frac{6 \cdot (100 \times 10^6) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})}{(180 \times 10^9) \cdot (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

$$
\kappa = \frac{6 \cdot 100 \times 10^0}{180 \times 10^9 \cdot 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\kappa = \frac{600}{49,610}
$$

$$
\boxed{\kappa \approx 0.0121 \; m^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R \approx 82.6 \; m}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cubic Dependence of Cantilever Stiffness on Stack Thickness

For a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$, the area moment of inertia is:

$$
I = \int_{-t/2}^{+t/2} y^2 \cdot w \cdot dy = w \cdot \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-t/2}^{+t/2}
$$

$$
I = w \cdot \left( \frac{(t/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-t/2)^3}{3} \right) = w \cdot \frac{2 \cdot t^3/8}{3}
$$

$$
I = w \cdot \frac{t^3}{12}
$$

The spring constant at the tip is:

$$
k = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^3} = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot L^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{k = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^3}{4 L^3}}
$$

**Critical Insight for KTU:** Doubling the structural layer thickness $t$ (e.g., from 25 µm to 50 µm in a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) device layer) increases stiffness by a factor of **8**. This non-linear relationship dominates the design of MEMS resonators, switches, and accelerometers.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Stack Thickness on MEMS Performance

| Stack Element | Thickness Change | Effect on Resonant Frequency | Effect on Sensitivity | Effect on Stress |
|---------------|------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Device Layer $t_{dev}$ | Increase | Increases (stiffer) | Decreases (less compliant) | Increases internal stress |
| Handle Wafer $t_{handle}$ | Increase | Negligible (clamped) | Negligible | Reduces bow |
| Bonding Layer $t_{bond}$ | Increase | Slight decrease (damping) | Variable | May add CTE mismatch |
| Cap Cavity Depth $t_{cavity}$ | Increase | Slight decrease (squeeze-film damping) | Increases (cavity volume) | Negligible |
| Passivation $t_{pass}$ | Increase | Marginal | Marginal | May dominate if highly stressed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Wafer Stack Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **sequential layer architecture** of a generic 3-wafer MEMS stack, including the bonding interfaces, the active device layer, and the cap with hermetic cavity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Substrate Mount And Carrier] --> B1[Handle Wafer Thickness 675um]
    B1 --> C1[Lower Bonding Interface Anodic Glass 5um]
    C1 --> D1[Buried Oxide BOX Layer Optional 1um]
    D1 --> E1[Device Wafer Silicon 525um Pre Thinning]
    E1 --> F1[Back Grinding And CMP Thinning]
    F1 --> G1[Thinned Device Layer 25um to 100um]
    G1 --> H1[Sacrificial Oxide Layer Release Etch]
    H1 --> I1[Released MEMS Structure Cantilever Or Membrane]
    I1 --> J1[Metallization Al Or Au 1um]
    J1 --> K1[Passivation SiN Or SiO2 0.5um]
    K1 --> L1[Upper Bonding Interface Au Sn Eutectic 2um]
    L1 --> M1[Cap Wafer Glass 300um With Cavity]
    M1 --> N1[Final Stack Thickness Sum Of All Layers]
    N1 --> O1[Dicing Singulation]
    O1 --> P1[Final Packaged MEMS Device]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow for Stack Thickness Evolution

The following diagram maps how the cumulative thickness **evolves chronologically** through the fabrication sequence — a common KTU question structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Handle Wafer 675um] --> S2[Step 2 Lower Anodic Bond Add 5um]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Device Wafer Bonded 525um Total 1205um]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Back Thinning Remove 475um]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Thinned Stack 730um]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Cap Bond Add 302um]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Final Stack 1032um Plus Passivation]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Inspection And Metrology]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Conceptual Block Representation)

A vertical section showing the geometric relationships between layers:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1A[Passivation SiN 0.5um] --> L1B[Metallization Al 1um]
    L1B --> L1C[Cap Wafer 300um With Cavity]
    L1C --> L1D[Eutectic Bond 2um]
    L1D --> L1E[Air Gap 10um Released Cavity]
    L1E --> L1F[Device Layer 50um Thinned SOI]
    L1F --> L1G[Buried Oxide 1um]
    L1G --> L1H[Anodic Glass Bond 5um]
    L1H --> L1I[Handle Wafer 675um]
    L1I --> L1J[Backside Coating Optional 0um]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The total stack extends from the bottom of the handle wafer (L1I) to the top of the passivation (L1A). The internal air gap (L1E) does NOT contribute to the solid stack thickness but defines the functional cavity depth — a distinction often tested in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Wafer Stack Thickness" as applied in MEMS fabrication. List any four constituent layers that contribute to the total stack thickness of a packaged MEMS device.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Wafer stack thickness is the total cumulative vertical dimension of all bonded/laminated wafer layers, bonding interfaces, metallization, and passivation films that make up an integrated MEMS device, measured along the wafer normal (Z-axis).

**Four Constituent Layers (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Handle wafer** (provides mechanical support).
2. **Bonding interface** (anodic glass, eutectic, or adhesive).
3. **Device wafer** (contains the active MEMS microstructure).
4. **Cap wafer** (provides hermetic sealing).
5. *(Optional fifth for completeness: metallization or passivation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the Stoney equation relating wafer curvature to film stress. What is the inverse-square dependence of curvature on substrate thickness called in the context of MEMS bonding?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Stoney Equation (2 Marks):**

$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{6 \sigma t_f}{M_s t_s^2}
$$

where $\kappa$ is curvature, $R$ is radius of curvature, $\sigma$ is biaxial film stress, $t_f$ is film thickness, $t_s$ is substrate thickness, and $M_s$ is the biaxial modulus of the substrate.

**Concept Identification (1 Mark):** The inverse-square dependence ($\kappa \propto 1/t_s^2$) is known as the **"Stoney bow suppression"** principle — it is the reason thicker handle wafers are chosen to minimize wafer bow in bonded MEMS stacks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Derive the Stoney equation for wafer curvature resulting from a stressed thin film on a thick substrate. Explain its significance in the context of wafer stack thickness design. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A MEMS accelerometer is fabricated using a 3-wafer stack: a 525 µm Si handle wafer, a 4 µm anodic bonding glass layer, a 525 µm Si device wafer thinned to 30 µm, a 400 µm Pyrex cap wafer, a 3 µm Au-Sn eutectic seal, and a 0.8 µm Al metallization layer on the cap. Calculate the total wafer stack thickness. If the device wafer is further thinned to 20 µm, what is the percentage change in the spring constant of the suspended proof-mass cantilever, assuming constant width and length? (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Identify the System:**
A thin film of thickness $t_f$ with biaxial stress $\sigma$ is deposited on an isotropic substrate of thickness $t_s$, Young's modulus $E_s$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu_s$. The substrate is assumed much thicker than the film ($t_s \gg t_f$).

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Force and Moment Equilibrium:**
The film exerts a force per unit width $F = \sigma t_f$. This acts at a lever arm of $t_s/2$ from the substrate's neutral plane, producing a bending moment $M = F \cdot t_s / 2$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Plate Bending Relation:**
By the classical plate bending equation, $M = (M_s t_s^2 / 6) \cdot \kappa$, where $M_s = E_s/(1 - \nu_s)$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Final Form:**
Equating and solving for $\kappa$ yields:

$$
\kappa = \frac{6 \sigma t_f}{M_s t_s^2}
$$

**Significance in Wafer Stack Design:**
- Predicts wafer bow after bonding when CTE mismatch exists.
- Justifies the use of thick handle wafers ($t_s \uparrow$) to suppress curvature.
- Guides the choice of low-stress bonding materials to minimize post-bond bow.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (3 Marks) — Sum All Layers:**

$$
T_{stack} = 525 + 4 + 30 + 400 + 3 + 0.8
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 529 + 30 + 400 + 3 + 0.8
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 559 + 400 + 3 + 0.8
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 959 + 3 + 0.8
$$

$$
T_{stack} = 962 + 0.8 = 962.8 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Spring Constant Ratio:**
Cantilever spring constant: $k \propto t^3$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{k_{new}}{k_{old}} = \left( \frac{t_{new}}{t_{old}} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{20}{30} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^3 = \frac{8}{27} \approx 0.296
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Percentage Change:**

$$
\Delta k = k_{new} - k_{old} = 0.296 k_{old} - k_{old} = -0.704 k_{old}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Percentage change} = -70.4\% \quad (\text{a }70.4\% \text{ decrease in stiffness})}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the concept of wafer stack thickness in a 3-wafer MEMS bonded structure. Identify the role of the device wafer, handle wafer, and cap wafer. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A silicon wafer of initial thickness 675 µm is back-ground at a rate of 5 µm/s. After grinding, a 2 µm polysilicon layer and 0.3 µm SiN layer are deposited. If the final stack thickness is 670 µm, what was the grinding duration? Also, calculate the resonant frequency of a cantilever of length 200 µm, width 20 µm, and thickness equal to the final thinned silicon layer. Assume E = 170 GPa, $\rho$ = 2330 kg/m³. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Explanation (4 Marks):**
A 3-wafer MEMS stack integrates:
- **Handle wafer:** Bottom support layer providing mechanical rigidity. Typically Si or glass, 300–725 µm thick.
- **Device wafer:** Middle layer containing the active MEMS structures. Often thinned to 5–100 µm to define structural thickness.
- **Cap wafer:** Top layer forming a sealed cavity. May include recessed cavity for proof-mass displacement.

The wafers are joined by bonding layers (anodic, eutectic, fusion, or adhesive). The total stack thickness is the sum of all layers along the Z-direction.

**Sketch (3 Marks):** Draw a vertical cross-section showing, from bottom to top: Handle (thick) → Bond 1 → Device (thin) → Bond 2 → Cap (with cavity). Label each layer with thickness.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Grinding Time Calculation:**

Let the thinned silicon thickness be $t_{Si,final}$. Then:

$$
t_{Si,final} + 2 + 0.3 = 670 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{Si,final} = 667.7 \; \mu m
$$

Material removed: $675 - 667.7 = 7.3 \; \mu m$. Time required:

$$
t_{grind} = \frac{7.3}{5} = 1.46 \; s
$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks) — Cantilever Frequency Formula:**

The fundamental flexural resonant frequency of a cantilever is:

$$
f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot I}{\rho \cdot A \cdot L^4}}
$$

where $I = w t^3 / 12$ and $A = w t$.

$$
f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot t^2}{12 \rho \cdot L^4}}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Numerical Evaluation:**

Using $t = 667.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $L = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $E = 170 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³:

$$
\frac{E t^2}{12 \rho L^4} = \frac{(170 \times 10^9)(667.7 \times 10^{-6})^2}{12 \cdot 2330 \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6})^4}
$$

$$
= \frac{(170 \times 10^9)(4.458 \times 10^{-7})}{12 \cdot 2330 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-21}}
$$

$$
= \frac{7.578 \times 10^4}{4.4736 \times 10^{-17}} = 1.694 \times 10^{21}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.694 \times 10^{21}} = 1.302 \times 10^{10.5} \approx 4.117 \times 10^5 \; rad^2/s^2 \; (\text{inside sqrt, units reduce to rad/s})
$$

$$
f_1 = \frac{3.516}{2\pi} \cdot 4.117 \times 10^5 \approx 0.5596 \cdot 4.117 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\boxed{f_1 \approx 2.30 \times 10^5 \; Hz \; = \; 230 \; kHz}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Wafer Stack Thickness Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Adding Unthinned AND Thinned Device Wafer Thickness (–2 Marks):** If a 525 µm device wafer is thinned to 50 µm, only 50 µm enters the stack — not 525 + 50. The unthinned portion is *removed*, not added.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Unit Conversion (–1 Mark):** Layer thicknesses in datasheets are often in µm, but stress is in MPa, and modulus in GPa. Mixed units are the #1 source of arithmetic errors.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring Cavity Gaps (–1 Mark):** Internal air gaps in capped devices do NOT contribute to the solid stack thickness, but they DO affect device performance. State this distinction explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Cubic vs. Linear Confusion in Spring Constant Problems (–2 Marks):** When thickness changes by ratio $r$, the spring constant changes by $r^3$ and the resonant frequency by $r$. Always write the proportionalities before plugging in numbers.
> 
> 5. **Omitting Bonding Layers:** Students often forget the 2–5 µm bonding interface in their stack summation. Always draw a labelled cross-section first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Wafer stack thickness = sum of all bonded layer thicknesses along the Z-axis.
- **Standard Wafer Thicknesses:** 100 mm → 525 µm, 150 mm → 675 µm, 200 mm → 725 µm, 300 mm → 775 µm (SEMI standards).
- **Stack Components:** Handle wafer + lower bond + device wafer (+ thinning) + cap wafer + upper bond + metallization + passivation.
- **Key Formula:** $T_{stack} = \sum t_i$ (linear superposition).
- **Stoney Equation:** $\kappa = 6\sigma t_f / (M_s t_s^2)$ — curvature scales as $1/t_s^2$.
- **Stiffness–Thickness Coupling:** $k \propto t^3$ and $f_n \propto t$ (for cantilevers) — cubic dependence is exam-critical.
- **TSV Capacitance:** $C \propto T_{stack}$ — thicker stacks mean more parasitic capacitance in 3D ICs.
- **Thinning Operations:** Back-grinding, CMP, wet/dry etching are *subtractive* — only the final thickness counts.
- **Cavity Gaps:** Do NOT add to solid stack thickness but matter for squeeze-film damping and hermeticity.
- **CTE Mismatch:** Different materials in the stack expand differently under thermal load — drives wafer bow and stress.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a labelled cross-section, then sum numerically — this avoids missing layers.
- **Unit Discipline:** µm for thickness, GPa for modulus, MPa for stress, Hz for frequency, kg/m³ for density.
- **Real Devices:** Accelerometers (Bosch, STMicro), gyroscopes (InvenSense), pressure sensors (Honeywell) all use multi-wafer stacks with precisely controlled thicknesses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Dicing Concerns, Thermal Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# WAFER DICING CONCERNS & THERMAL MANAGEMENT IN MEMS

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wafer Dicing Concerns, Thermal Management

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Wafer Dicing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wafer Dicing** is the final back-end-of-line (BEOL) semiconductor manufacturing process in which a fully processed wafer is mechanically, thermally, or chemically separated into individual functional **dies** (chips). In the context of MEMS, dicing is exceptionally critical because the wafer contains **released, fragile, free-standing microstructures** (beams, membranes, cantilevers, comb-drives) that were created by a sacrificial etch step *just before* dicing.

**Standard dicing methods used in MEMS packaging:**

| Method | Mechanism | Best Use Case in MEMS |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Sawing** | Diamond-impregnated blade rotating at high RPM | Standard Si dice with sufficient dicing street |
| **Stealth Dicing (SD)** | Laser-induced subsurface defects, then wafer expansion separates dies | Ultra-clean die edge, thin wafers, MEMS sensors |
| **Laser Ablation** | Direct laser vaporisation of the kerf | Hard materials (SiC, sapphire), brittle MEMS |
| **Plasma Dicing** | Deep RIE through the wafer thickness | Ultra-thin dies, singulation of MEMS wafers |
| **Dicing-Before-Grinding (DBG)** | Half-cut dicing, then back-grind to thin | TSV, 3D-stacked MEMS (IMU, microphones) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Cutting a Cake with Embedded Jewels"

Imagine a fully decorated cake where tiny sugar-crystal ornaments (MEMS cantilevers, membranes) are glued onto the top surface right before you cut the cake into slices (dies).

If you hack through the cake with a heavy, blunt knife (mechanical saw), the vibrations crack the brittle sugar crystals. If you use a sharp, narrow laser (stealth dicing), the jewels remain intact. **Wafer dicing concerns in MEMS are exactly this: how to slice the substrate without destroying the delicate micro-machined structures that have just been released.**

### 1.3 Thermal Management — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Management in MEMS** refers to the engineered control of heat generation, transport, and dissipation within a micro-system so that all functional elements (mechanical, electrical, and biological) operate within their specified temperature envelope. Unlike pure ICs, MEMS devices must control temperature for **three coupled reasons**: (1) electronic reliability, (2) mechanical dimensional stability (CTE mismatch), and (3) physical-domain functionality (e.g., thermal-bimorph actuators, bolometers, micro-fluidic reactions).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "A Tiny House with an Electric Heater"

Think of a MEMS chip as a tiny, sealed room with a 5 W electric heater inside (the actuator/IC). The room has thin walls (silicon die), and the furniture inside is made of **two different metals joined together** (bimetallic cantilever). If the heater runs too hot, the furniture warps (CTE mismatch), the room wall flexes, and the heat may even leak out and damage a sensitive thermometer (temperature sensor). **Thermal management is the design of the heater, walls, windows, and vents so the room stays at a comfortable, uniform temperature.**

### 1.5 Key Metrics & Constants Used in These Topics

- **Diamond grit size:** 2 – 20 µm (smaller grit → finer finish, slower feed)
- **Standard Si thermal conductivity:** **k_Si = 150 W/(m·K)** at 300 K
- **Standard SiO₂ thermal conductivity:** **k_SiO₂ = 1.4 W/(m·K)** at 300 K
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE):** Si ≈ **2.6 ppm/K**, Al ≈ **23 ppm/K**, Cu ≈ **17 ppm/K**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 Anatomy of Wafer Dicing Concerns

### A. Mechanical / Saw-Induced Defects

1. **Top-side chipping (TSC):** Small fragments break off the top edge of the kerf due to blade impact.
2. **Bottom-side chipping (BSC):** Same, but at the wafer exit side of the blade.
3. **Median / radial cracks:** Propagate vertically into the active die area; can be deep enough to reach MEMS membranes and rupture them.
4. **Lateral cracks:** Travel horizontally; delaminate dielectric/metal layers.
5. **Backside chipping (back-grind damage):** Particularly harmful in DBG flow.

**Why this matters for MEMS:** A crack of even 5 µm into the active area can sever a released 2 µm-thick membrane or a 1 µm-wide comb-finger gap.

### B. Dicing-Induced Contamination

- **Coolant water** carries Si debris, dissolved Cu from BEOL lines, and abrasive particles.
- Released MEMS structures can **stick** permanently if contaminated (known as *stiction*).
- Mitigation: **CO₂ snow-jet cleaning**, **megasonic cleaning**, **spin-rinse-dry (SRD)**.

### C. Dicing Street / Kerf Geometry

- **Dicing Street** = the sacrificial area between two adjacent dies where the saw cuts.
- **Kerf Width** = the actual material removed by the blade (typical: 25 – 75 µm).
- **Kerf Check / Quality Index:** TSC ≤ 5 µm, BSC ≤ 10 µm in production-grade dicing.

### D. Blade Selection Logic

| Blade Property | Options | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Grit size** | 2 – 20 µm diamond | Small grit → slow feed, fine edge; large grit → fast feed, chipping |
| **Bond type** | Resin, metal, electroplated | Resin: low chipping; Metal: long life; Electroplated: high precision |
| **Exposure** | Blade exposure height | Higher exposure → faster cut, more BSC |

### E. Process Parameters

- **Spindle speed (RPM):** 30 000 – 60 000 rpm typical.
- **Feed rate (mm/s):** 1 – 100 mm/s; **inversely related to chipping**.
- **Z1 / Z2 / Z3 cuts:** Stepped cut profiles to minimise TSC and BSC simultaneously.

### F. Dicing Tape and Wafer Mounting

- **UV-curable dicing tape:** Mounted on a stainless-steel ring (wafer frame).
- Adhesive strength must resist coolant's lift force yet allow clean die pickup after singulation.
- **Tape thickness:** typically 80 – 150 µm; CTE mismatch between tape and Si can bow the wafer.

---

## 2.2 Stealth Dicing (SD) and Plasma Dicing for MEMS

### Stealth Dicing (SD)
- A pulsed laser of wavelength **~1342 nm (Nd:YVO₄)** is focused **inside** the wafer, not on the surface.
- Creates a layer of modified (amorphous) Si at a precise depth.
- Subsequent **tape expansion** pulls the wafer apart along the modified layer.
- **No debris, no coolant, no top/bottom chipping** — ideal for MEMS.

### Plasma Dicing
- Photoresist is patterned to define die boundaries.
- **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE / Bosch process)** etches through the entire wafer thickness.
- Enables **die-shrink** because the kerf can be reduced to **< 10 µm** (vs. 50 µm for saw).
- Issues: very long etch time (3 – 6 hours per wafer), high equipment cost.

---

## 2.3 Thermal Management — Heat Transfer Modes in MEMS

| Mode | Governing Equation | Application in MEMS |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** (solid) | $q = -k \, A \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat spreader, die attach, thermal vias |
| **Convection** (fluid) | $q = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Forced-air / liquid cooling, microchannels |
| **Radiation** (vacuum/IR) | $q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^4$ | Bolometers, IR sensors, vacuum packages |

### A. Thermal Resistance Network (Lumped Model)

The dominant equation in KTU board problems is the **thermal resistance analogy** to Ohm's law:

$$T_j - T_a = P \cdot \theta_{ja}$$

where:
- $T_j$ = **junction temperature** (max allowed: usually 125 °C or 150 °C)
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature
- $P$ = power dissipated
- $\theta_{ja}$ = **junction-to-ambient thermal resistance** (°C/W)

Resistances in series add:
$$\theta_{ja} = \theta_{jc} + \theta_{cs} + \theta_{sa}$$

(where $jc$ = junction-to-case, $cs$ = case-to-sink, $sa$ = sink-to-ambient)

### B. Conduction Resistance (Planar Wall)

$$R_{cond} = \frac{L}{k \, A}$$

### C. Convection Resistance

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \, A}$$

### D. Spreading Resistance (Critical in MEMS!)

When heat flows from a small die into a larger package, it spreads in 3-D. For a circular heat source of radius $r_1$ on a semi-infinite substrate of radius $r_2$:

$$R_{spread} = \frac{1}{2 \, \pi \, k \, r_1} \left[1 - \frac{r_1}{2 r_2}\right]$$

This is **very significant in MEMS** because MEMS dice are often only 1 – 5 mm² while the package is much larger.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always include units. Always write 273.15 (or 273) for K ↔ °C conversion unless the problem allows absolute K only.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_j - T_a = P \cdot \theta_{ja}$ | Junction temperature rise | °C |
| 2 | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Conduction resistance | K/W |
| 3 | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | Convection resistance | K/W |
| 4 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann radiation | W |
| 5 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | m |
| 6 | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress | Pa |
| 7 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat carried by coolant fluid | W |
| 8 | $Nu = \dfrac{hD_h}{k_f}$ | Nusselt number (microchannel) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $D_h = \dfrac{4A_c}{P_w}$ | Hydraulic diameter | m |

> [!NOTE]
> In dicing, the "thermal" consideration is **dicing heat**: spindle + blade friction can locally raise die temperature to > 200 °C. This can damage **solder bumps** in flip-chip MEMS, so **coolant flow rate** is engineered to keep peak temperature below the solder reflow point (~ 220 °C for SAC305).

---

## 2.5 Why These Topics Matter in Real Engineering

- **Consumer MEMS (accelerometer, gyroscope in phones):** Survives drop, but dicing chips cause field failures 6 months later.
- **Automotive MEMS (airbag, ESP):** Must survive −40 °C to +125 °C, so thermal cycling stresses from CTE mismatch must be modelled.
- **Optical MEMS (DLP, LIDAR):** Thermal expansion shifts the mirror gap by nm → directly degrades performance.
- **Medical MEMS (microfluidics, lab-on-chip):** PCR-chip temperature must ramp at 5 °C/s — requires *deliberate* thermal design, not just cooling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Junction Temperature of a MEMS Pressure Sensor

A MEMS pressure sensor dissipates **P = 0.5 W**. The thermal resistance from junction to ambient is:
- Junction-to-case: $\theta_{jc} = 5$ °C/W
- Case-to-sink: $\theta_{cs} = 1$ °C/W
- Sink-to-ambient: $\theta_{sa} = 9$ °C/W
- Ambient: $T_a = 45$ °C

**Find:** Junction temperature $T_j$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the thermal network.**

The three resistances are in **series**, so:

$$
\theta_{ja} = \theta_{jc} + \theta_{cs} + \theta_{sa}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\theta_{ja} = 5 + 1 + 9 = 15 \;\; \text{°C/W}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the KTU standard heat-flow equation.**

$$
T_j = T_a + P \cdot \theta_{ja}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
T_j = 45 + (0.5)(15) = 45 + 7.5 = 52.5 \;\; \text{°C}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**

Maximum allowed $T_j$ for typical MEMS electronics is **125 °C**. Since 52.5 °C ≪ 125 °C, the design is safe with a 72.5 °C safety margin.

> **Final Answer: $T_j = 52.5$ °C** (with margin of 72.5 °C).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Heat-Sink Area for a Bolometer Array

A micro-bolometer IR array dissipates **P = 2 W**. The available thermal resistance from junction to ambient (excluding convection) is **10 °C/W**. The ambient is 25 °C, the maximum allowed junction temperature is **85 °C**, and the convection coefficient is **h = 25 W/(m²·K)**.

**Find:** The required heat-sink surface area $A_s$ such that the convection resistance brings the total $\theta_{ja} \le 30$ °C/W.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Maximum allowable total thermal resistance.**

$$
\theta_{ja, \max} = \frac{T_j - T_a}{P} = \frac{85 - 25}{2} = 30 \;\; \text{°C/W}
$$

**Step 2 — The conduction path is already fixed at 10 °C/W; therefore the convection resistance must satisfy:**

$$
\theta_{conv} = \theta_{ja, \max} - \theta_{cond} = 30 - 10 = 20 \;\; \text{°C/W}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the convection-resistance formula:**

$$
\theta_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A_s$:**

$$
A_s = \frac{1}{h \cdot \theta_{conv}} = \frac{1}{25 \times 20} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to cm² for intuitive use:**

$$
A_s = 0.002 \;\text{m}^2 = 20 \;\; \text{cm}^2
$$

> **Final Answer: $A_s = 20 \;\text{cm}^2$** (a 4.47 cm × 4.47 cm square heat sink).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermal Stress in a Bimetallic MEMS Cantilever

A MEMS cantilever is composed of Si (length $L_0$ = 200 µm) and an Au layer (thickness 2 µm). The temperature rises by **ΔT = 80 K**.
- CTE of Si: $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- CTE of Au: $\alpha_{Au} = 14.2 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- Young's modulus of Au: $E_{Au} = 78$ GPa

**Find:** The induced thermal stress in the Au layer (assuming Si constrains the deformation).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Differential free expansion (relative to Si):**

$$
\Delta L = (\alpha_{Au} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\Delta L = (14.2 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 80
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify step-by-step:**

$$
14.2 - 2.6 = 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{/K}
$$

$$
11.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 23.2 \times 10^{-10} \;\text{m}
$$

$$
23.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 80 = 1856 \times 10^{-10} = 1.856 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Thermal strain in the constrained layer:**

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{1.856 \times 10^{-7}}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.28 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law for stress in Au:**

$$
\sigma_{th} = E_{Au} \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = 78 \times 10^{9} \times 9.28 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute:**

$$
\sigma_{th} = 78 \times 9.28 \times 10^{5} = 723.84 \times 10^{5} = 7.24 \times 10^{7} \;\text{Pa} = 72.4 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Check against Au yield strength (~ 200 MPa):** 72.4 MPa < 200 MPa → elastic, safe.

> **Final Answer: $\sigma_{th} \approx 72.4$ MPa** (elastic regime, well below yield).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dicing-Induced Kerf-Defect Limit

A dicing blade removes a kerf of width $w_k$ = 50 µm. The maximum allowable chipping is **C_max = 10 µm** on each side. The total die-edge exclusion zone (the "keep-out" area that must be free of sensitive MEMS structures) is $E$.

**Find:** The minimum exclusion zone $E$ for safe design.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — The kerf width is the central material removed; chipping extends outward on both sides.**

$$
E = w_k + 2 \cdot C_{max}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
E = 50 + 2 \times 10 = 70 \;\; \text{µm}
$$

> **Final Answer: $E = 70$ µm.** Sensitive MEMS structures must be placed ≥ 70 µm from the die edge.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Which Method When?" (Dicing Decision Tree)

| Condition on Wafer | Recommended Dicing Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Released MEMS, low-k dielectric, bumps | **Stealth Dicing** | No chipping, no debris, no coolant contamination |
| Ultra-thin wafer (< 100 µm) | **DBG + Plasma** | Sawing thin wafer causes catastrophic breakage |
| Hard material (SiC, GaN) | **Laser Ablation** | Diamond blade wear is extreme |
| High-volume, standard Si | **Mechanical Sawing** | Lowest cost-per-die |
| 3D-stacked TSV | **Plasma Dicing** | Enables 10 µm kerf; supports through-silicon integration |
| Wafer with delicate membrane edges | **Stealth Dicing** | Preserves membrane integrity |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — "Which Cooling Path When?" (Thermal Decision Tree)

| Condition | Recommended Cooling | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Power density < 0.1 W/cm² | Natural convection | Simple, no fan |
| Power density 0.1 – 1 W/cm² | Forced air + heat sink | Cost-effective |
| Power density 1 – 10 W/cm² | Heat sink + microchannel liquid | High heat-flux removal |
| Power density > 10 W/cm² | Two-phase cooling / jet impingement | Film boiling limits overcome |
| Vacuum / sealed package | Radiation + high-k solid spreader | No convection possible |
| Localised hotspot | Thermal via + copper pillar | Direct low-resistance path |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 Wafer-Dicing Process Flow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Finished Wafer from Fab] --> A2[Inspect Edge Exclusion Zone]
    A2 --> A3[Apply Dicing Tape to Backside]
    A3 --> A4{Mount on Wafer Frame}
    A4 --> A5[Align Dicing Streets to Camera]
    A5 --> A6{Select Dicing Method}
    A6 --> A61[Mechanical Saw]
    A6 --> A62[Stealth Laser]
    A6 --> A63[Plasma DRIE]
    A6 --> A64[DBG - Pre Grind]
    A61 --> A7[Coolant Flush + Debris Removal]
    A62 --> A8[No Coolant - Clean Process]
    A63 --> A9[Vacuum Chuck Etch]
    A64 --> A10[Backside Grind Thin]
    A7 --> A11[Tape Expansion - Singulated Dies]
    A8 --> A11
    A9 --> A11
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[UV Cure for Tape Release]
    A12 --> A13[Pick and Place to Package]
    A13 --> A14[Final Die on Substrate]
```

---

## 4.2 Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical Analogy)

```mermaid
graph LR
    TJ[Junction Tj] -->|theta_jc| TC[Case Tc]
    TC -->|theta_cs| TS[Heat Sink Ts]
    TS -->|theta_sa| TA[Ambient Ta]
    P1[Power P] --> TJ
    P1 -.heat flow.-> TC
    P1 -.heat flow.-> TS
    P1 -.heat flow.-> TA
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU exam style):** Heat flows from the source $P$ through series thermal resistances (analogous to electrical resistors). The temperature drop across each resistance is $P \times \theta_i$, just as voltage drop is $I \times R$.

---

## 4.3 MEMS Die with Thermal Stack (Cross-Sectional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Ambient 25 C] --> L2[Heat Sink - Cu or Al]
    L2 --> L3[Thermal Interface Material - TIM]
    L3 --> L4[Package Substrate - Ceramic or Organic]
    L4 --> L5[Die Attach - Solder or Epoxy]
    L5 --> L6[MEMS Silicon Die]
    L6 --> L7[Active MEMS Layer - Released Structures]
    L6 --> L8[Integrated Circuit - ASIC]
    L7 -.heat conduction.-> L8
    L8 -.power dissipation.-> L6
    L6 -.through die attach.-> L5
```

---

## 4.4 Stealth-Dicing Mechanism (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[Nd YVO4 Laser 1342 nm] --> B2[Focussing Lens]
    B2 --> B3[Wafer - Top Surface Untouched]
    B3 --> B4[Subsurface Modified Layer - Amorphous Si]
    B4 --> B5[Tape Expansion Stage]
    B5 --> B6[Cleaved Dies - Clean Edges]
```

---

## 4.5 Microchannel Liquid Cooling Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[Coolant Inlet] --> M2[Microchannel Network - Width 50 to 200 um]
    M2 --> M3[Heated MEMS Die - Below Channel]
    M3 --> M4[Coolant Out - Heated]
    M2 -. convection heat transfer .-> M3
    M3 -. rejected heat to fluid .-> M4
```

---

## 4.6 Spreading-Resistance Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Small Heat Source - radius r1] --> S2[Conduction Spread in 45 deg Cone]
    S2 --> S3[Larger Substrate - radius r2]
    S3 --> S4[Heat Sink to Ambient]
```

(Heat enters at radius $r_1$, spreads outward at roughly a 45° angle through the substrate of radius $r_2$, then exits to ambient. The smaller $r_1$ is relative to $r_2$, the higher the spreading resistance.)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any three concerns associated with wafer dicing in MEMS fabrication."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Mechanical chipping (top/bottom side):** Diamond saw can cause micro-cracks that propagate into released MEMS structures, causing failure.
2. **Coolant-induced stiction:** Dicing coolant may carry debris that causes released microstructures to permanently stick to the substrate after drying.
3. **Die-edge exclusion zone loss:** Chip-cracks force designers to keep MEMS devices away from the die edge, reducing usable die area.

(Any three of the above — or — backside chipping, lateral cracks, contamination, dicing-tape residue, or thermal damage to solder bumps.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Define thermal management. Mention two heat-transfer modes relevant to MEMS."**

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal management** is the engineered control of heat generation, transport, and dissipation inside a micro-system so that all functional blocks (electrical, mechanical, fluidic) operate within their specified temperature limits.

Two relevant heat-transfer modes:

1. **Conduction** — heat flow through solid media (silicon die, package, heat sink); governed by $q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$.
2. **Convection** — heat transfer to a moving fluid (forced air, microchannel coolant); governed by $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$.

(Optionally mention **radiation**, $q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$, which is dominant in vacuum-packaged MEMS such as bolometers and IR sensors.)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Dicing-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the different wafer-dicing techniques used in MEMS packaging. Compare mechanical sawing and stealth dicing with respect to chipping, contamination, and suitability for released structures.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A MEMS accelerometer wafer is to be singulated. The dicing street is 80 µm wide. Maximum allowable chipping is 12 µm on each side. Calculate the minimum **die-edge exclusion zone** that must be kept free of MEMS structures. If the die size is 3 mm × 3 mm, what is the **usable active area** after accounting for the exclusion zone?

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Dicing techniques in MEMS:**

1. **Mechanical Sawing:** Diamond-encrusted blade rotating at 30 000 – 60 000 rpm cuts along dicing streets while DI water flushes away debris. Lowest cost, highest throughput, but causes top/bottom chipping and debris contamination.

2. **Stealth Dicing (SD):** IR laser (1342 nm) is focused **inside** the wafer to create an amorphous Si layer. Tape expansion later cleaves the wafer along this layer. Produces near-zero chipping, no debris, no coolant needed.

3. **Plasma Dicing:** A photoresist pattern defines die boundaries, then DRIE (Bosch process) etches through the entire wafer. Achieves < 10 µm kerf — ideal for die-shrink.

4. **Laser Ablation:** Direct laser vaporisation; used for hard, brittle materials (SiC, GaN, sapphire).

5. **Dicing-Before-Grinding (DBG):** Half-cut saw from front side, then back-grind to thin; used for TSV and 3D-stacked MEMS.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Mechanical Sawing | Stealth Dicing |
|---|---|---|
| **Top chipping** | 5 – 20 µm | < 1 µm |
| **Bottom chipping** | 10 – 30 µm | < 1 µm |
| **Coolant required** | Yes (DI water) | No |
| **Contamination risk** | High (debris) | Negligible |
| **Speed** | High | Moderate |
| **Suitability for released MEMS** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Cost per wafer** | Low | Higher |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Exclusion zone formula:**

$$
E = w_k + 2 \cdot C_{max}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
E = 80 + 2 \times 12 = 80 + 24 = 104 \;\text{µm}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective (usable) die size:**

The exclusion zone is on all four sides of the die, so the usable active dimension is:

$$
L_{usable} = L_{die} - 2E = 3000 - 2(104) = 3000 - 208 = 2792 \;\text{µm}
$$

**Step 4 — Usable active area:**

$$
A_{usable} = L_{usable}^2 = (2792 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 7.795 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2 \approx 7.80 \;\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage of original die area still usable:**

$$
\frac{A_{usable}}{A_{die}} = \frac{2792^2}{3000^2} = \frac{7.795 \times 10^6}{9 \times 10^6} = 0.866 = 86.6\%
$$

> **Final Answer:** Exclusion zone = **104 µm**; Usable area ≈ **7.80 mm²** (86.6 % of total die).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Thermal-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the three modes of heat transfer with relevant equations. Discuss why **spreading resistance** is critical in MEMS thermal design.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A MEMS gas sensor dissipates **0.8 W**. The package has:
- $\theta_{jc}$ = 4 °C/W
- $\theta_{cs}$ = 0.5 °C/W
- $\theta_{sa}$ = 10.5 °C/W
- Ambient $T_a$ = 50 °C

Compute: (i) the total thermal resistance, (ii) the junction temperature, and (iii) the safety margin if the maximum allowed $T_j$ is 125 °C.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Three modes of heat transfer:**

1. **Conduction** (solid): Fourier's Law

$$
q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}
$$

In MEMS, this is the dominant mode inside the die, package, and TIM.

2. **Convection** (fluid): Newton's Law of Cooling

$$
q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)
$$

In MEMS, this is the dominant mode from package surface to ambient air or from die to coolant in microchannels.

3. **Radiation** (electromagnetic): Stefan–Boltzmann Law

$$
q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4
$$

Dominant in **vacuum-packaged** MEMS (bolometers, IR sensors) where convection is impossible.

**Why spreading resistance is critical in MEMS:**

- MEMS dice are small (often 1 – 5 mm²), while heat sinks/PCBs are large (cm²).
- Heat must spread outward from a small footprint into a much larger one.
- The geometry creates a **spreading resistance** that can dominate the total $\theta_{ja}$.
- Formula for circular geometry:

$$
R_{spread} = \frac{1}{2\pi k r_1}\left[1 - \frac{r_1}{2r_2}\right]
$$

- For $r_1 \ll r_2$ (typical MEMS), this simplifies to $R_{spread} \approx 1/(2\pi k r_1)$.
- **Low $k$ materials (e.g., plastic package)** make this resistance *much* larger than for high-$k$ materials (Si, AlN, SiC).
- **Design solution:** use **thermal vias** and **copper pillars** to enlarge the effective $r_1$ reaching the heat sink.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Total thermal resistance** (series network):

$$
\theta_{ja} = \theta_{jc} + \theta_{cs} + \theta_{sa} = 4 + 0.5 + 10.5 = 15 \;\text{°C/W}
$$

**(ii) Junction temperature:**

$$
T_j = T_a + P \cdot \theta_{ja} = 50 + (0.8)(15) = 50 + 12 = 62 \;\text{°C}
$$

**(iii) Safety margin:**

$$
\Delta T_{margin} = T_{j,\max} - T_j = 125 - 62 = 63 \;\text{°C}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\theta_{ja} = 15$ °C/W; $T_j = 62$ °C; **margin = 63 °C** (very safe).

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 273.15 conversion** when mixing °C and K in radiation problems. The Stefan–Boltzmann law requires **absolute Kelvin**, while $T_j$ and $T_a$ are usually given in °C. Convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
>
> 2. **Adding thermal resistances in parallel by mistake.** The body-to-ambient path is typically a *series* network; **parallel paths only arise** when there are multiple heat-flow routes (e.g., convection + radiation to ambient from the same surface).
>
> 3. **Mixing up "kerf width" and "exclusion zone".** Kerf = material removed by blade. Exclusion zone = kerf + chipping allowance on both sides. Exam answers must show both terms.
>
> 4. **Using °C in $T^4$ without conversion.** This is the single most common 2-mark loss in radiation problems.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the die-edge exclusion zone** when calculating usable MEMS active area — easy 1-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Stating that "more coolant flow = better"** in dicing. Excessive flow can cause **vibration**, die-edge chipping, and coolant ingress into released structures. The correct statement is "**optimised flow**".

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Dicing & Thermal Management)**

- **Wafer dicing** is the singulation of a processed wafer into individual dies. In MEMS, this step is hazardous because the wafer contains *released* microstructures.
- **Mechanical sawing** is cheapest but causes top/bottom chipping, cracks, and debris.
- **Stealth dicing** uses an internal laser — best for MEMS, no chipping, no coolant.
- **Plasma dicing** uses DRIE for ultra-thin dies and very narrow kerf.
- **DBG** is used for thin-wafer/3D-stacked MEMS.
- **Dicing-induced defects** include top chipping, bottom chipping, lateral/median cracks, contamination, stiction, and thermal damage to bumps.
- **Die-edge exclusion zone** $E = w_k + 2C_{max}$.
- **Thermal management** controls heat generation, transport, and dissipation.
- **Three heat-transfer modes:** conduction ($q = -kA\dfrac{dT}{dx}$), convection ($q = hA\Delta T$), radiation ($q = \varepsilon\sigma A T^4$).
- **Thermal resistance** is the **Ohm's-law analog of temperature** to power: $T_j - T_a = P \cdot \theta_{ja}$.
- **Series thermal resistances add:** $\theta_{ja} = \theta_{jc} + \theta_{cs} + \theta_{sa}$.
- **Spreading resistance** is critical in MEMS because of the small-die/large-package geometry.
- **CTE mismatch** generates thermal stress: $\sigma_{th} = E\alpha\Delta T$.
- **Coolant flow** in dicing must be *optimised*, not maximised, to avoid vibration and chipping.
- **Vacuum-packaged MEMS** rely on **radiation** + **conduction** (no convection).
- **Microchannel cooling** handles power densities of 1 – 10 W/cm².
- **Thermal vias** and **copper pillars** reduce spreading resistance.
- Always convert to **absolute Kelvin** for any $T^4$ term.
- Always include **units** in numerical answers — losing 0.5 mark for missing units is the most common KTU board-exam penalty.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Isolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stress Isolation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), **Stress Isolation** (also termed *Mechanical Decoupling* or *Stress-Buffering*) is defined as a deliberate design methodology wherein micro-scale compliant structures are engineered to mechanically decouple sensitive active MEMS elements (such as piezoresistive pressure sensors, capacitive accelerometers, and resonant gyroscopes) from external mechanical disturbances. These disturbances typically arise from the **packaging-induced stress**, **thermal mismatch**, **substrate warpage**, and **die-attach stresses** introduced during the hermetic sealing process.

The principal goal of stress isolation is to ensure that the **functional transduction mechanism** (e.g., a piezoresistor bridge or a suspended proof mass) responds *only* to the intended measurand (pressure, acceleration, etc.) and *not* to parasitic package stress, thereby drastically improving the **long-term drift**, **zero-point stability**, and **temperature coefficient** of the sensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Stress isolation is classified under **Mechanical Design Considerations for MEMS Packaging**. Examiners frequently target the connection between packaging stress and the failure of piezoresistive sensor accuracy. Students must know at least two stress isolation techniques.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a sensitive laboratory weighing scale (a precision instrument) placed directly on a vibrating washing machine. The readings will fluctuate wildly because the vibration (the *package stress*) is being directly transmitted to the sensing element. If we now mount the weighing scale on a **set of soft rubber springs** before placing it on the washing machine, the rubber springs absorb and dissipate the vibration, and the scale registers only the weight placed on its own pan. **The rubber springs act as the stress-isolation structure** — they mechanically decouple the sensitive scale from the noisy environment. In MEMS, these "rubber springs" are microfabricated as flexures, tethers, meanders, or folded-beam suspensions etched from the same silicon wafer as the sensor itself.

### 1.3 The Core Problem: Sources of Parasitic Stress

The following sources of mechanical stress must be neutralized by the stress isolation architecture:

- **Thermo-Mechanical Stress:** Caused by the mismatch in the **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** between the silicon die ($\alpha_{Si} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$), the bonding glass, the metal package, and the PCB. Typical stress magnitudes range from **10 MPa to 100 MPa**.
- **Die-Attach Stress:** Stresses introduced by the curing and contraction of epoxy, solder, or glass-frit used to mount the die in its cavity package.
- **Hermetic-Seal Stress:** Stresses induced by the lid-sealing process, particularly in **anodic bonding** and **glass-frit sealing**.
- **External Mechanical Shock and Vibration:** Bench-handling shock, PCB bending, and acoustic noise.

### 1.4 The Boundary Condition

For an ideal stress-isolated sensor, the *effective stress* transmitted to the active region must approach zero:

$$\sigma_{\text{transmitted}} \rightarrow 0$$

while the *intended signal stress* is preserved:

$$\sigma_{\text{signal}} = f(\text{measurand}) \neq 0$$

The isolation efficiency $\eta$ is therefore mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_{\text{isolation}} = \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_{\text{transmitted}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}}\right) \times 100\%$$

A well-engineered MEMS stress isolation architecture routinely achieves $\eta_{\text{isolation}} \geq 95\%$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanics of Stress Propagation

When a stress $\sigma_{\text{app}}$ is applied at the package boundary of a MEMS chip, it propagates through the silicon substrate and reaches the sensing element. Without isolation, the stress at the sensor is given by a transfer function:

$$\sigma_{\text{sensor}} = T(\sigma_{\text{app}})$$

where $T$ is the stress transfer coefficient ($0 < T \leq 1$ for a fully bonded die). The role of the isolation architecture is to introduce a **low-pass mechanical filter** that attenuates the high-frequency and static components of the parasitic stress.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Decoupling Mechanism

The following ordered steps describe how mechanical decoupling is achieved through a compliant micro-suspension:

- **Step 1 — Anchor the Outer Frame:** The outer die is rigidly bonded to the package. The parasitic stress $\sigma_{\text{app}}$ enters through this frame.
- **Step 2 — Introduce a Compliant Region:** A region of low bending stiffness (a meander, folded beam, or long tether) is placed between the frame and the inner island.
- **Step 3 — Mechanically Decouple the Inner Island:** The inner floating island, which carries the active sensing elements, sits on this compliant region.
- **Step 4 — Attenuate the Stress:** As stress attempts to travel from the frame to the island, the compliant structure undergoes **deformation** (it bends, twists, or stretches), absorbing the mechanical energy.
- **Step 5 — Preserve the Signal Path:** The intended measurand (pressure, acceleration, force) is channeled *directly* to the inner island via a dedicated diaphragm or seismic mass, which bypasses the compliant isolation structure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every key equation that may appear in a KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) question on Stress Isolation.**

| **Symbol / Equation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Standard SI Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma = F / A$ | Mechanical stress (force per unit area) | Pascal (Pa) $\equiv N/m^{2}$ |
| $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_{0}$ | Engineering strain (dimensionless) | unitless |
| $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial linear elasticity) | Pa |
| $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress due to CTE mismatch | Pa |
| $k = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | Bending stiffness of a cantilever flexure | N/m |
| $k_{\text{axial}} = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t}{L}$ | Axial stiffness of a beam | N/m |
| $\eta_{\text{isolation}} = \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_{\text{out}}}{\sigma_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Stress isolation efficiency | unitless (0 to 1) |
| $\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Tip deflection of a cantilever under tip load | m |
| $I = \frac{w \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ | Area moment of inertia of a rectangular beam | $m^{4}$ |
| $f_{\text{isolator}} \ll f_{\text{signal}}$ | Frequency separation rule for vibration isolation | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Stress isolation is the *enabling technology* behind commercial **automotive TPMS (Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems)**, **invasive blood-pressure catheters**, and **aerospace altimeters**. Without stress isolation, these sensors would drift by tens of percent over their operational lifetime due to thermal cycling of the package.

### 2.4 The Frequency-Domain View

For dynamic stresses (vibration, acoustic noise), stress isolation behaves as a **low-pass mechanical filter**. The isolation structure (spring-mass-damper) has a natural frequency $f_{n}$. For applied stress frequencies $f \gg f_{n}$, the transmitted stress is attenuated by approximately **40 dB/decade**. This is analogous to the **vibration-isolation theory** in macro-scale mechanical engineering:

$$\text{Transmissibility} \quad T_{r} = \left\vert \frac{1}{1 - (f / f_{n})^{2}} \right\vert$$

For $f > \sqrt{2} \cdot f_{n}$, the system enters the **isolation regime** where $T_{r} < 1$, i.e., stress is actively attenuated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Stress on a Bonded MEMS Die

We consider the canonical KTU problem: a silicon die of side length $L_{\text{die}}$ is bonded to a Kovar package (CTE $\alpha_{K} \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$) at temperature $T_{1}$, and operated at temperature $T_{2}$.

The fundamental CTE mismatch strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = (\alpha_{\text{Si}} - \alpha_{K}) \cdot \Delta T$$

Using Hooke's Law $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, the *un-relieved* thermal stress in the silicon is:

$$\sigma_{\text{th, unrelieved}} = E_{\text{Si}} \cdot (\alpha_{\text{Si}} - \alpha_{K}) \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 1: Insert numerical values.**

$$E_{\text{Si}} = 130 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$$
$$\alpha_{\text{Si}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$$
$$\alpha_{K} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$$
$$\Delta T = (T_{2} - T_{1}) = -100^{\circ}C \quad \text{(cooling from 200^{\circ}C seal to 100^{\circ}C)}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the CTE difference.**

$$\Delta \alpha = (2.6 - 5.0) \times 10^{-6} = -2.4 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$$

**Step 3: Multiply the components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{th, unrelieved}} &= 130 \times 10^{9} \cdot (-2.4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (-100) \\
&= 130 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 130 \times 2.4 \times 10^{5} \\
&= 312 \times 10^{5} \\
&= 3.12 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Final answer and physical interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{th, unrelieved}} = 31.2 \text{ MPa}}$$

This **31.2 MPa** is the unmitigated stress that would impinge on a piezoresistive sensor, producing a fractional resistance change of:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_{l} \cdot \sigma = 50 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 31.2 \times 10^{6} = 1.56\%$$

which would translate into a full-scale sensor offset error — completely unacceptable.

### 3.2 Derivation: Isolation via a Folded-Flexure Suspension

Now we insert a folded-beam suspension of axial stiffness $k_{\text{flex}}$ between the die frame (where $\sigma_{\text{app}}$ acts) and the floating sensor island. The system is modeled as two springs in series:

$$\frac{1}{k_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{k_{\text{frame}}} + \frac{1}{k_{\text{flex}}}$$

If the frame is infinitely rigid ($k_{\text{frame}} \to \infty$), then:

$$k_{\text{eff}} \approx k_{\text{flex}}$$

The force transmitted to the floating island is:

$$F_{\text{island}} = \sigma_{\text{app}} \cdot A_{\text{frame}} \cdot \frac{k_{\text{flex}}}{k_{\text{frame}} + k_{\text{flex}}}$$

For the canonical KTU design where $k_{\text{flex}} \ll k_{\text{frame}}$:

$$F_{\text{island}} \approx \sigma_{\text{app}} \cdot A_{\text{frame}} \cdot \frac{k_{\text{flex}}}{k_{\text{frame}}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the isolation efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{isolation}} &= 1 - \frac{F_{\text{island}}}{F_{\text{applied}}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{k_{\text{flex}}}{k_{\text{frame}}} \\
&\approx 1 \quad \text{(when } k_{\text{flex}} / k_{\text{frame}} \to 0 \text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation proves that **the lower the axial stiffness of the compliant flexure relative to the frame, the higher the isolation efficiency.** In practice, the designer chooses $k_{\text{flex}}$ such that it is at least two orders of magnitude smaller than $k_{\text{frame}}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Stress Isolation Techniques

| **Technique** | **Stiffness Mechanism** | **Best For** | **Typical $\eta_{\text{isolation}}$** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Folded-Beam Flexure | Bending of long thin beams | Static + low-frequency isolation | 85% – 95% |
| Meander / Serpentine Spring | Combined bending + torsion | Compact-area isolation | 90% – 97% |
