| Long Tether / Cantilever | Axial stretching | High-temperature packages | 80% – 90% |
| Floating Island + Diaphragm | Diaphragm decouples membrane stress | Pressure sensors | 95% – 99% |
| Rigid Island on Glass Pillars | Pillars absorb CTE mismatch | Ceramic packages | 92% – 96% |
| Compliant Polymer Underfill | Polymer shear deformation | Plastic-molded packages | 70% – 85% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Question (a) of ESE Part B on Stress Isolation frequently expects the student to **compare two of the above techniques**. Memorize the trade-off: *higher compliance gives better isolation but a larger die footprint*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Isolation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Package Boundary] --> B[Applied Parasitic Stress sigma_app]
    B --> C[Rigid Die Frame Anchor]
    C --> D[Compliant Flexure Region]
    D --> E{Mechanical Decoupling Zone}
    E --> F[Floating Sensor Island]
    G[Intended Measurand e.g. Pressure] --> H[Diaphragm or Seismic Mass]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Active Piezoresistive Bridge]
    I --> J[Conditioned Electrical Output]
    K[Isolated Parasitic Stress sigma_iso approx 0] --> F
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Frequency-Domain Isolation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Parasitic Stress Signal f_stress] --> Q{Compare to f_n}
    Q --> R[f_stress less than f_n]
    Q --> S[f_stress greater than sqrt2 times f_n]
    R --> T[Stress Transmitted Poor Isolation]
    S --> U[Stress Attenuated 40 dB per decade Good Isolation]
    U --> V[Floating Sensor Island Receives Clean Signal]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Propagation with and without Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph W1[Without Stress Isolation]
        A1[Package Boundary sigma_app = 31 MPa] --> B1[Rigid Silicon Substrate]
        B1 --> C1[Direct Coupling to Piezoresistor]
        C1 --> D1[Sensor Offset Error of 1.5 percent]
    end
    subgraph W2[With Folded-Flexure Isolation]
        A2[Package Boundary sigma_app = 31 MPa] --> B2[Rigid Die Frame]
        B2 --> C2[Compliant Folded Flexure k_flex small]
        C2 --> D2[Floating Sensor Island sigma_iso approx 1 MPa]
        D2 --> E2[Sensor Offset Error of 0.05 percent]
    end
```

### 4.4 Top-Down Schematic Description of a Folded-Beam Flexure

A classic folded-beam stress isolator is constructed from **four beams arranged in a U-shape pattern**, connecting the outer die frame to an inner floating island. The beams have length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$, with $L \gg w \gg t$ to maximize compliance. When the die frame is strained by the package, the U-shape flexes sideways, transmitting only a small fraction of the strain to the inner island. The active sensing elements (e.g., four piezoresistors in a Wheatstone bridge) are placed on the inner island or on a dedicated diaphragm above it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for Exam:** When asked to draw a stress isolation structure in the ESE, sketch a square inner island connected to the surrounding frame by **at least two serpentine or folded springs** on opposite sides. Label the frame, the flexure, the island, and the active sensing element. This earns full marks for the diagram portion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define *stress isolation* in the context of MEMS packaging. Why is it necessary for piezoresistive pressure sensors?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Stress isolation is a mechanical design technique used in MEMS to decouple the active sensing element from parasitic mechanical stresses originating from the package, die-attach, and thermal expansion mismatch. It is necessary for piezoresistive pressure sensors because piezoresistors are highly sensitive to any in-plane stress. Un-isolated package stress (typically 10–100 MPa) superimposes onto the diaphragm stress and produces a large, temperature-dependent zero-point offset, destroying measurement accuracy.

---

**Q2.** State two structural techniques commonly employed for stress isolation in MEMS devices.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Folded-Beam Flexures** — long, thin beams bent into a U-shape that provide low axial stiffness in the direction of the package stress.
2. **Meander / Serpentine Springs** — long, zig-zag beam paths that absorb strain through combined bending and torsion.

*(Full-marks alternative: floating-island architecture with a stress-relief diaphragm, or glass-pillar compliant mounting.)*

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination Pattern – Dec 2024 Model]**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the operating principle of a **folded-beam stress isolation structure** used in a MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor. Derive the relationship between the axial stiffness of the flexure and the resulting stress isolation efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A silicon die (Young's modulus $E_{\text{Si}} = 130 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha_{\text{Si}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$) is bonded to a Kovar package ($\alpha_{K} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$). The sealing is performed at $T_{1} = 350^{\circ}C$ and the device operates at $T_{2} = 25^{\circ}C$. Calculate (i) the unrelieved thermal stress in the silicon, and (ii) the residual stress on the floating island if a folded-beam suspension of stiffness $k_{\text{flex}} = 50 \text{ N/m}$ is used and the frame stiffness is $k_{\text{frame}} = 50000 \text{ N/m}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a)** *Sketch & Operating Principle:* The folded-beam stress isolator consists of an outer rigid die frame connected to an inner floating sensor island through **two or more U-shaped folded beams**. The active piezoresistive Wheatstone bridge is placed on the inner island (or on a pressure-sensing diaphragm atop the island). When external package stress acts on the die frame, the U-shaped beams deform in bending and axial stretching, absorbing the strain energy. The pressure signal acts directly on the diaphragm and is transmitted to the island *without* passing through the flexure. Hence the sensor registers only the pressure-induced stress.

*Derivation:* As shown in Section 3.2, the force transmitted to the floating island is:

$$F_{\text{island}} = \sigma_{\text{app}} \cdot A_{\text{frame}} \cdot \frac{k_{\text{flex}}}{k_{\text{frame}} + k_{\text{flex}}}$$

The isolation efficiency is therefore:

$$\eta_{\text{isolation}} = 1 - \frac{k_{\text{flex}}}{k_{\text{frame}} + k_{\text{flex}}}$$

**(b)** *Step 1 — Temperature differential.*

$$\Delta T = T_{2} - T_{1} = 25 - 350 = -325^{\circ}C$$

*Step 2 — CTE mismatch.*

$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{\text{Si}} - \alpha_{K} = (2.6 - 5.0) \times 10^{-6} = -2.4 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$$

*Step 3 — Unrelieved thermal stress.*

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{th, unrelieved}} &= E_{\text{Si}} \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 130 \times 10^{9} \cdot (-2.4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (-325) \\
&= 130 \times 10^{9} \cdot 7.8 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.014 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 101.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 4 — Isolation ratio.*

$$\frac{k_{\text{flex}}}{k_{\text{frame}} + k_{\text{flex}}} = \frac{50}{50000 + 50} = \frac{50}{50050} \approx 9.99 \times 10^{-4}$$

*Step 5 — Residual stress on island.*

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{island}} &= \sigma_{\text{th, unrelieved}} \times 9.99 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 101.4 \times 10^{6} \times 9.99 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.013 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 0.101 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 6 — Isolation efficiency.*

$$\eta_{\text{isolation}} = 1 - 9.99 \times 10^{-4} \approx 99.9\%$$

The folded-beam suspension reduces the residual stress from **101.4 MPa to 0.101 MPa**, achieving near-perfect decoupling.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **folded-beam flexures** and **meander / serpentine springs** as stress isolation structures. Mention at least three points of comparison and discuss the trade-off between isolation efficiency and die footprint. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS accelerometer uses a stress-isolation meander to decouple its proof mass from the package. The meander has axial stiffness $k_{\text{meander}} = 100 \text{ N/m}$. During a thermal cycle, an in-plane package stress of $\sigma_{\text{pkg}} = 50 \text{ MPa}$ acts on a frame area $A_{\text{frame}} = 1 \text{ mm}^{2}$. Calculate the parasitic force transmitted to the proof mass and the resulting zero-g offset error if the proof-mass piezoresistor has a longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{l} = 50 \times 10^{-11} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7-Mark Format):**

| **Parameter** | **Folded-Beam Flexure** | **Meander Spring** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Footprint | Moderate (2 beams) | Large (long zig-zag path) |
| Stiffness | Medium | Very low (more compliant) |
| Isolation Efficiency | 85% – 95% | 90% – 97% |
| Fabrication | Single-mask bulk etch | Single-mask DRIE |
| Best Use | Pressure sensors | Accelerometers, gyroscopes |
| Trade-off | Less compliant, smaller die | Better isolation, larger die |

**Verdict:** The folded-beam flexure is chosen when die area is at a premium, whereas the meander spring is chosen when isolation efficiency is paramount.

**(b)** *Step 1 — Force applied at the frame.*

$$F_{\text{frame}} = \sigma_{\text{pkg}} \cdot A_{\text{frame}} = 50 \times 10^{6} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} = 50 \text{ N}$$

*Step 2 — Parasitic force transmitted (assume rigid frame, so $k_{\text{frame}} \gg k_{\text{meander}}$).*

For simplicity, assume $k_{\text{frame}} = 10^{6} \text{ N/m}$ (rigid):

$$F_{\text{transmitted}} = F_{\text{frame}} \cdot \frac{k_{\text{meander}}}{k_{\text{frame}} + k_{\text{meander}}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{transmitted}} &= 50 \cdot \frac{100}{10^{6} + 100} \\
&\approx 50 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} = 5 \text{ mN}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 3 — Effective parasitic stress on proof mass.*

Assuming the proof mass has an effective cross-sectional area $A_{\text{pm}} = 0.01 \text{ mm}^{2} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2}$:

$$\sigma_{\text{pm}} = \frac{F_{\text{transmitted}}}{A_{\text{pm}}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-8}} = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 4 — Fractional resistance change in the piezoresistor.*

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_{l} \cdot \sigma_{\text{pm}} = 50 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5 \times 10^{5} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.025\%$$

This is the **zero-g offset error** introduced by the parasitic package stress — substantially smaller than the un-isolated case.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> - **Unit mismatch:** Always state stress in **MPa or Pa** with explicit conversion. Writing $50 \times 10^{6}$ without units loses one mark.
> - **Sign convention:** For $\Delta T$ during cooling, $\Delta T$ is **negative**. The negative sign cancels with the negative $\Delta \alpha$, giving a **positive (compressive)** stress. Forgetting this loses one mark.
> - **Stiffness ratio:** Examiners expect students to recognize that *isolation efficiency depends on the ratio $k_{\text{flex}} / k_{\text{frame}}$*, not on the absolute value of $k_{\text{flex}}$.
> - **Sketch quality:** A neat, labeled diagram of the flexure structure is worth at least **2 of the 7 marks** in Part B. Do not skip it.
> - **Trade-off question:** When asked to "compare," students often write a paragraph instead of a table. A clean comparison table always scores higher.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stress isolation** is a mechanical decoupling technique used to protect sensitive MEMS sensing elements from package-induced parasitic stresses.
- The **three principal sources** of parasitic stress are: **CTE mismatch**, **die-attach cure shrinkage**, and **hermetic-seal induced strain**.
- The **fundamental formula** for unrelieved thermal stress is $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
- The **stress isolation efficiency** is $\eta = 1 - (k_{\text{flex}} / k_{\text{frame}})$ for a frame–flexure two-spring model.
- **Five canonical isolation structures** to memorize for KTU: (1) folded-beam flexure, (2) meander spring, (3) long tether, (4) floating-island on glass pillars, (5) compliant polymer underfill.
- The **frequency-domain rule**: stress isolation is effective when the applied stress frequency is greater than $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency of the compliant suspension.
- The **die-area trade-off**: higher isolation efficiency requires longer flexures, which consume more silicon area.
- **Piezoresistive sensors are the most sensitive to package stress**, so stress isolation is *mandatory* in their design. Capacitive sensors are moderately sensitive; resonant sensors are highly sensitive.
- Always **draw a labeled diagram** for any 7-mark Part B question on this topic — examiners allocate 2 marks for the figure alone.
- The **two-spring model** ($k_{\text{frame}}$ in series with $k_{\text{flex}}$) is the standard analytical tool expected in the KTU ESE.
- **Commercial example to cite:** Bosch BMP280 piezoresistive barometric pressure sensor uses an on-chip folded-beam stress isolator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protective Coating and Media Isolation. Wafer Level Packaging and Die Level Packaging. Hermetic Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protective Coating and Media Isolation. Wafer Level Packaging and Die Level Packaging. Hermetic Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Protective Coating in MEMS

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Protective Coating in MEMS refers to the deposition of thin organic or inorganic films over sensitive microstructures (beams, membranes, cantilevers, sensors) to shield them from mechanical damage, chemical corrosion, moisture, particulate contamination, and biofouling during fabrication, dicing, packaging, and end-use operation.

The protective coating is **not** a packaging layer itself; rather, it is a conformal thin film (typically **0.5 µm to 25 µm** thick) applied directly onto the microstructure surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasizes that protective coatings must be **conformal** (uniform over high-aspect-ratio features), **pin-hole free**, **low-stress** (to prevent curling of cantilevers), and **biocompatible** for biomedical MEMS (BioMEMS).

### Common Protective Coating Materials

| Material | Type | Key Property | Typical Application |
|----------|------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Parylene C** | Organic polymer | USFDA approved, conformal, low dielectric | Neural probes, implantable sensors |
| **BCB (Benzocyclobutene)** | Organic polymer | Low stress, photosensitive | RF MEMS, microfluidics |
| **Polyimide (PI-2611)** | Organic polymer | High thermal stability (> 300°C) | Pressure sensor diaphragm |
| **SU-8** | Epoxy | Photopatternable, thick film | Microfluidic channels |
| **SiC / Si₃N₄** | Inorganic ceramic | Hard, hermetic | Harsh environment sensors |
| **PTFE / Teflon** | Fluoropolymer | Low surface energy, anti-stiction | Anti-stiction layer |

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Invisible Raincoat":** Imagine a tiny cantilever beam inside a smart-phone's accelerometer. Without protection, humidity, dust, and handling shocks would corrode or snap the hair-thin silicon beam (typically 2–5 µm thick). A **protective coating acts exactly like an invisible raincoat** sprayed over the beam — it is ultra-thin, follows every contour (conformal), and blocks water/ions while still letting the beam flex and sense acceleration. For BioMEMS, the same coating is like a **biocompatible glove** preventing the body from attacking the implant.

---

## 1.2 Media Isolation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Media Isolation is the engineering strategy of separating the delicate MEMS sensing element from the harsh external medium (corrosive fluids, biological tissue, high-pressure gases, particulate-laden air) by interposing a chemically inert, mechanically robust barrier, while still allowing the target physical signal (pressure, force, chemical concentration) to transmit through the barrier.

Media isolation is the **bridge** between the MEMS sensor and the real world. It is *not* a coating but a **macroscopic structural barrier**.

### Two Principal Media Isolation Techniques

1. **Passive Isolation (Mechanical Barrier)**
   - A thick stainless steel, titanium, or Hastelloy diaphragm welded or bonded at the package inlet.
   - A small volume of inert **silicone oil (polydimethylsiloxane, PDMS)** fills the cavity between diaphragm and sensor.
   - Pressure from the external medium displaces oil → oil displaces sensor diaphragm.

2. **Active Isolation (Gel/Coating Isolation)**
   - The sensor is dipped/encapsulated in a soft, inert gel (e.g., silicone gel, fluorinert).
   - The gel permits ion/diffusive transport of analytes (for chemical sensors) while blocking large molecules and biofouling agents.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Diver's Wetsuit & Diving Bell":** A pressure sensor lowered into the deep ocean experiences crushing pressure and salty corrosion. The **silicone-oil-filled isolation diaphragm** works exactly like a **diver's diving bell** — the bell is open at the bottom to sea-water, but the diver inside sits in air. The bell walls keep corrosive salt out, yet the *pressure* transmits perfectly through the water column. The MEMS die inside is the "diver," and the silicone oil is the "air pocket."

---

## 1.3 Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Wafer-Level Packaging is a packaging technology in which the encapsulation, capping, or sealing of MEMS structures is performed *before* dicing — i.e., at the full-wafer level (typically 150 mm, 200 mm, or 300 mm diameter) — using microfabrication techniques such as wafer bonding, anodic bonding, or thin-film encapsulation. The result is a packaged chip that is essentially the same size as the bare die.

The most common WLP techniques are:

- **Wafer-to-Wafer Bonding (Anodic, Eutectic, Fusion, Adhesive)**
- **Thin-Film Encapsulation (TFE)** — alternating inorganic/organic stacks
- **Cap Wafer Processing** — a separate cap wafer with cavities etched in it is bonded over the device wafer.

---

## 1.4 Die-Level Packaging (DLP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Die-Level Packaging refers to packaging operations performed *after* the wafer has been diced into individual chips. Each die is individually mounted, wire-bonded, and sealed inside a ceramic or metal package, or molded inside a plastic overmold. DLP is the conventional IC packaging approach adapted for MEMS.

Common DLP implementations:
- **Ceramic Package (LCC, LGA, QFN)** — for high-reliability, hermetic medical/aerospace MEMS.
- **Plastic Overmold (PEM)** — for low-cost consumer MEMS (accelerometers in phones).
- **Pre-molded Lead-Frame** — for gyroscopes, microphones.

---

## 1.5 Hermetic Packaging

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Hermetic Packaging is a packaging scheme in which the MEMS device is enclosed inside a sealed cavity such that no gas, moisture, or contaminants can exchange with the external environment for the device's operational lifetime. The hermeticity is quantified by a **Helium Leak Rate** (typically < 1×10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s He).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — MIL-STD-883 Test Method 1014:** The KTU 2024 syllabus references this military standard. It defines Hermeticity by:
> - **Fine Leak Test (Helium Mass Spectrometry):** Reject limit = **5×10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s He** for cavity volume ≤ 0.05 cc.
> - **Gross Leak Test (Fluorocarbon Bubble Test):** Reject limit = bubble stream ≥ 1 bubble / 3 s at 125°C.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Sealed Glass Jar":** A hermetic package is exactly like a **sealed glass jar of pickles**. The lid is welded, the rubber gasket is compressed, and inside the pickles (the MEMS die) are safe from air, moisture, and bacteria *indefinitely*. The "pickling liquid" inside is replaced by **dry nitrogen or a vacuum**, and a tiny **getter pellet** (sodium-barium alloy or activated charcoal) sits inside, just like a **silica-gel sachet** in a vitamin bottle, absorbing any residual moisture released over decades.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Protective Coating

The protection mechanism is governed by three independent physics layers:

### Step 1 — Conformal Deposition
The coating must replicate sub-micron step heights. A **Step Coverage Ratio (SCR)** is defined as:

$$\text{SCR} = \frac{t_{\min}}{t_{\text{top}}}$$

where $t_{\min}$ is the thinnest coating at the bottom of a trench and $t_{\text{top}}$ is the coating thickness on the top horizontal surface. **SCR → 1** indicates a perfectly conformal coating (achieved by Parylene via chemical vapor deposition; **SCR ≈ 0.95**).

### Step 2 — Barrier Property
The coating must have a **low permeability coefficient (P)** to water vapor and oxygen. Permeability is given by:

$$P = D \cdot S$$

where $D$ is the diffusivity (m²/s) and $S$ is the solubility (cm³ STP / cm³·atm). Units of $P$: $\left[\frac{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{cmHg}}\right]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Parylene C has a water-vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of **~0.01 g·m⁻²/day** at 25°C/90% RH, which is 10× better than epoxy and 100× better than polyimide.

### Step 3 — Mechanical Compatibility
The **residual stress (σ)** in the coating is:

$$\sigma = \sigma_{\text{intrinsic}} + \sigma_{\text{thermal}}$$

where $\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = E_f \cdot (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$, with $E_f$ being the film Young's modulus, $\alpha$ the coefficient of thermal expansion, and $\Delta T$ the deposition-to-operation temperature difference.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that **tensile stress curls cantilevers *upward* and compressive stress curls them *downward***. For BioMEMS neural probes, **compressive stress** is preferred so the probe remains straight after implantation.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Media Isolation

A pressure sensor with media isolation can be modelled as a **two-diaphragm system** (oil-filled capsule).

The **external pressure** $P_{ext}$ acts on the isolation diaphragm. The **internal MEMS diaphragm** experiences pressure $P_{int}$ transmitted through the silicone oil column. In static equilibrium:

$$P_{int} = P_{ext} - \Delta P_{\text{oil}}$$

where $\Delta P_{\text{oil}}$ is the small pressure drop due to oil viscosity at low frequency (negligible in static / quasi-static conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** A thicker isolation diaphragm gives better chemical resistance but reduces sensitivity. Typical stainless-steel isolation diaphragms are **0.05 mm to 0.2 mm** thick, with a diameter of **5 mm to 25 mm**.

---

## 2.3 Operational Logic of Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP)

WLP proceeds through five tightly controlled steps:

1. **Device wafer fabrication** — MEMS structures released by sacrificial etching.
2. **Cap wafer preparation** — A second silicon or glass wafer is etched (KOH, DRIE) to form cavities (~ 50–500 µm deep) above each MEMS die.
3. **Wafer bonding** — The cap wafer is bonded to the device wafer using one of the techniques listed below.
4. **Wafer thinning** (optional) — The bonded stack is back-ground to < 200 µm for ultra-thin packages.
5. **Singulation** — The wafer is diced; each "chip" is already packaged.

### Comparison of Wafer Bonding Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

| Bonding Type | Temperature | Hermeticity | Alignment | Cost | Used For |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|------|----------|
| **Anodic** (Si–Glass) | 300–450°C | Excellent | ± 5 µm | Medium | Pressure sensors, accelerometers |
| **Eutectic** (Au-Sn, Au-Si) | 280–400°C | Excellent | ± 2 µm | High | RF MEMS, optical MEMS |
| **Fusion** (Si–Si) | 800–1100°C | Excellent (best) | ± 1 µm | Very High | High-precision gyroscopes |
| **Adhesive** (BCB, SU-8) | 150–250°C | **Not hermetic** | ± 10 µm | Low | Microfluidic chips, MOEMS |
| **Glass-Frit** | 380–450°C | Good | ± 25 µm | Low | Automotive pressure sensors |

---

## 2.4 Operational Logic of Die-Level Packaging (DLP)

After dicing, each die undergoes:

1. **Die attach** — Epoxy or eutectic solder (Au-Sn, Au-Si, Au-Ge) attaches die to lead-frame / substrate.
2. **Wire bonding** — Gold or aluminum wires (25 µm diameter) connect die pads to package leads. For MEMS, **wedge bonding** is preferred to minimize ultrasonic energy.
3. **Sealing** — Lid (metal or ceramic) is welded, soldered, or seam-sealed.
4. **Optional overmold** — Plastic molding compound (epoxy novalac) encapsulates the assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Isolation:** DLP must incorporate a **stress-isolation mount** (silicone gel or soft die-attach) to prevent package-induced stress (from CTE mismatch) from reaching the MEMS sensor — this is the #1 cause of MEMS offset drift in the field.

---

## 2.5 Operational Logic of Hermetic Packaging

A hermetic package consists of three components:

1. **Hermetic cavity** — vacuum or N₂/Ar backfilled.
2. **Hermetic seal** — metal-to-metal, glass-to-metal, or ceramic-to-metal.
3. **Getter** — chemically absorbs residual and permeating gases over the device lifetime.

### Sealing Techniques

- **Parallel Seam Sealing (PSSeal):** Two rolling electrodes pass a large current (kA) across a Kovar lid, locally melting metal to seal ceramic packages. Speed: 4–10 packages / sec.
- **Laser Sealing:** Nd:YAG or diode laser locally heats solder preform (Au-Sn). Highly localized, ideal for optical windows.
- **Resistance Sealing:** Solder preform melted by passing current through metallization ring.
- **Reactive Sealing:** Cu-Sn or Ni-Sn intermetallic formation at < 300°C.

### The Getter

A **getter** is a reactive material placed inside the cavity. After activation (heating to 300–500°C in vacuum), it chemically absorbs:

- H₂O (water vapor)
- O₂, H₂ (residual gases)
- CO, CO₂ (outgassing from polymer residues)

Common getters: **St 707 (Zr-Fe), St 787 (Zr-Co-REE), Nano-Porous Carbon, CaO desiccant.**

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Step Coverage Ratio | $\text{SCR} = t_{\min} / t_{\text{top}}$ | Dimensionless | Evaluate conformal coating |
| 2 | Permeability | $P = D \cdot S$ | cm³·cm/(cm²·s·cmHg) | Predict moisture ingress |
| 3 | Thermal stress in film | $\sigma_{th} = E_f (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \Delta T$ | Pa | Predict cantilever curl |
| 4 | Helmholtz-DM equation (residual gas damping) | $\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_n^2 x = \frac{F(t)}{m}$ | SI | Resonator design (Q-factor) |
| 5 | Q-factor of sealed resonator | $Q = \frac{\sqrt{k m}}{c}$ | Dimensionless | Predict damping in WLP cavity |
| 6 | Hermeticity (He leak rate) | $L = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V}{t}$ | atm·cc/s | MIL-STD-883 test |
| 7 | Helium leak — fine limit | $L_{\text{reject}} = 5\times10^{-9}$ | atm·cc/s | Pass/fail criterion |
| 8 | Fick's 1st law (moisture ingress) | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | kg/m²s | Lifetime prediction |
| 9 | Time to reach critical moisture | $t_{crit} = \frac{L^2}{6D}$ | seconds | Hermetic lifetime estimate |
| 10 | Outgassing rate of polymer | $q = K_s (P_{sat} - P_{cav})$ | Pa·m³/s | Predict cavity contamination |
| 11 | Anodic bond voltage | $V_{bond} \approx 800 - 1500$ | Volts DC | Process parameter |
| 12 | Eutectic Au-Sn temperature | $T_{eut} = 280$ | °C | Bond temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Warning:** In the table above, the vertical pipe inside a math expression would break a Markdown table; we have used $\vert$ and $\cdot$ notation where necessary. When writing inline math such as $\vert x \vert$, use the LaTeX command `\vert` to avoid table breakage.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive TPMS (Tyre Pressure Monitoring):** Hermetic + media-isolated pressure sensor survives 10 years in 130°C brake-dust environment.
- **Smartphone accelerometer:** WLP + parylene coating = sub-1 mm thickness.
- **Implantable cochlear microphone:** Parylene-C-coated, gel-isolated, hermetically sealed in titanium.
- **Aerospace gyroscope:** Fusion-bonded WLP with vacuum cavity for Q > 50,000.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Moisture Ingress Lifetime in a Hermetic Package

A classic KTU 2017 and 2022 problem. The goal is to estimate the **time required for moisture to reach a critical concentration** inside a hermetic package of wall thickness $L$.

**Given:**
- Wall thickness: $L = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5\times10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- Moisture diffusivity through Kovar lid: $D = 1\times10^{-13} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Initial cavity concentration: $C_0 = 0$
- External humidity: $C_s$ (assume saturated)
- Critical concentration: $C_{crit} = 50\% \text{ of } C_s$

**Governing equation — Fick's 2nd Law in 1-D:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Boundary conditions:**
$$C(0, t) = C_s, \quad C(L, t) = C_0, \quad C(x, 0) = C_0$$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalize.**

Let $\theta = \dfrac{C - C_0}{C_s - C_0}$, $\; \xi = \dfrac{x}{L}$, $\; \tau = \dfrac{D t}{L^2}$.

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \tau} = \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial \xi^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply separation of variables.** Assume $\theta(\xi, \tau) = X(\xi) \cdot T(\tau)$.

$$\frac{1}{T}\frac{dT}{d\tau} = \frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2 X}{d\xi^2} = -\lambda^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve the spatial ODE.**

$$\frac{d^2 X}{d\xi^2} + \lambda^2 X = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; X(\xi) = A\cos(\lambda \xi) + B\sin(\lambda \xi)$$

Applying $X(0)=0$ and $X(1)=0$ (since $\theta = 0$ at $\xi = 0$ far from source, and at the far wall, ignoring source replenishment):

$$\lambda_n = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 4 — Solve the temporal ODE.**

$$T_n(\tau) = e^{-\lambda_n^2 \tau} = e^{-(n\pi)^2 \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Superpose solutions.**

$$\theta(\xi, \tau) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\pi \xi)\, e^{-(n\pi)^2 \tau}$$

**Step 6 — Apply initial condition** $\theta(\xi, 0) = 1$:

$$1 = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\pi \xi)$$

This is a Fourier sine series, so:

$$B_n = 2 \int_0^1 \sin(n\pi \xi)\, d\xi = \frac{2}{n\pi}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{2}{n\pi}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]$$

For odd $n$, $B_n = \dfrac{4}{n\pi}$; for even $n$, $B_n = 0$.

**Step 7 — Concentration at the far wall** ($\xi = 1$, i.e., $x = L$):

$$\theta(1, \tau) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{4}{n\pi} \sin(n\pi)\, e^{-(n\pi)^2 \tau}$$

But $\sin(n\pi) = 0$ for all integer $n$! This is because we placed a *sink* at $x = L$. For a cavity of *finite* volume with constant uptake, the more physically useful form uses the **average concentration** in the cavity:

$$\bar{\theta}(\tau) = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\,e^{-((2n+1)\pi)^2 \tau}$$

**Step 8 — Approximate by the leading term** $n=0$:

$$\bar{\theta}(\tau) \approx 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\,e^{-\pi^2 \tau}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for critical time.** Set $\bar{\theta} = 0.5$ (50% saturation):

$$0.5 = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\,e^{-\pi^2 \tau}$$

$$e^{-\pi^2 \tau} = \frac{\pi^2}{16}$$

$$\tau_{crit} = \frac{1}{\pi^2}\ln\!\left(\frac{16}{\pi^2}\right) = \frac{1}{\pi^2}\ln(1.6211) = \frac{0.4834}{9.8696} = 0.04898$$

**Step 10 — Convert back to physical time.**

$$t_{crit} = \tau_{crit} \cdot \frac{L^2}{D} = 0.04898 \cdot \frac{(5\times10^{-4})^2}{1\times10^{-13}}$$

$$t_{crit} = 0.04898 \cdot \frac{2.5\times10^{-7}}{1\times10^{-13}} = 0.04898 \cdot 2.5\times10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{crit} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{5}\ \text{seconds} \approx 34\ \text{hours}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even a "hermetic" Kovar lid with a 0.5 mm wall allows moisture to reach 50% saturation in **~1.4 days** if the diffusivity is $10^{-13}$ m²/s. This is why **glass-to-metal seals** with $D \approx 10^{-18}$ m²/s are preferred, pushing the lifetime to > 30 years.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Resonant Frequency Shift in a Sealed Cavity (Q-Factor & Damping)

A MEMS gyroscope's vibrating proof mass is inside a sealed WLP cavity. The squeeze-film damping force in the cavity affects the Q-factor.

**Given:**
- Proof mass area: $A = 200 \times 200\ \mu\text{m}^2 = 4\times10^{-8}\ \text{m}^2$
- Gap to substrate: $d_0 = 2\ \mu\text{m} = 2\times10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- Cavity pressure: $P = 10\ \text{Pa}$ (vacuum)
- Frequency: $f = 10\ \text{kHz}$

**Step 1 — Effective damping coefficient** (Blech model for rarefied gas):

$$c = \frac{\mu A}{d_0} \cdot \beta(\text{Kn})$$

where $\mu$ is gas viscosity and $\beta(\text{Kn})$ accounts for slip-flow at low pressure (Knudsen number $\text{Kn} = \lambda / d_0$).

**Step 2 — At $P = 10$ Pa**, the mean free path $\lambda \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-3}/P[\text{Pa}]$ m:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.6\times10^{-3}}{10} = 6.6\times10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

$$\text{Kn} = \frac{6.6\times10^{-4}}{2\times10^{-6}} = 330 \gg 1$$

**Step 3 — In free-molecular regime** (Kn > 10), the damping is:

$$c = \frac{P \cdot A}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}} = \frac{10 \times 4\times10^{-8}}{\sqrt{2\pi \times 4.65\times10^{-26} \times 1.38\times10^{-23} \times 293}}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator computation.**

$$m k_B T = 4.65\times10^{-26} \times 1.38\times10^{-23} \times 293 = 1.881\times10^{-46}$$

$$2\pi m k_B T = 1.182\times10^{-45}$$

$$\sqrt{1.182\times10^{-45}} = 3.44\times10^{-23}$$

**Step 5 — Numerator.**

$$P \cdot A = 10 \times 4\times10^{-8} = 4\times10^{-7}$$

**Step 6 — Result.**

$$c = \frac{4\times10^{-7}}{3.44\times10^{-23}} = 1.16\times10^{16}\ \text{N·s/m}$$

**Step 7 — Q-factor.**

For a proof mass $m_p = 5\times10^{-9}$ kg and stiffness $k = 100$ N/m:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_p}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{5\times10^{-9}}} = 1.41\times10^{5}\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$Q = \frac{m_p \omega_n}{c} = \frac{5\times10^{-9} \times 1.41\times10^{5}}{1.16\times10^{16}} \to \text{negligible damping}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q \to \infty \text{ in vacuum; in practice limited to } 10^4 - 10^5 \text{ by anchor loss and thermoelastic damping}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for WLP:** The vacuum-cavity WLP is what makes MEMS gyroscopes work. Without hermetic vacuum encapsulation, **squeeze-film air damping would crush Q to < 1**, and the gyroscope could not resolve rotation rates < 1°/s.

---
