topics.title: Die Attach Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Die Attach Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Attach Process** is the critical **packaging operation** in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) and semiconductor manufacturing in which a **bare semiconductor die** (the silicon chip containing the active MEMS/sensor/IC circuitry) is mechanically, thermally, and often electrically bonded onto a **substrate, lead-frame, or package carrier** using a specialized bonding material.

This process forms the foundational **first-level interconnect** in the packaging hierarchy. It must satisfy three simultaneous engineering functions:
1. **Mechanical Support** – Holding the die firmly against vibration, shock, and thermal expansion mismatches.
2. **Thermal Dissipation** – Conducting heat generated within the die to the external package/heat sink.
3. **Electrical Interconnection** – (In some configurations such as **conductive die attach**) Providing a low-resistance path between the die backside and the package ground.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Die Attach is positioned within *Module 4 – MEMS Packaging*. It is studied alongside wire bonding, flip-chip assembly, and encapsulation. The examiner frequently tests (a) classification of die attach methods, (b) process flow, and (c) the role of material properties (CTE, glass transition temperature, Young's modulus).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wall Mounting" Picture

Imagine you are mounting a **delicate, expensive, heat-generating electronic component** (the die) onto a **wall** (the substrate). 

| Real-World Analogy | MEMS Die Attach Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The component itself | Silicon Die (chip) |
| The wall | Substrate / Lead-frame / Package base |
| The adhesive / bolts | Die Attach Material (epoxy / solder / eutectic) |
| The way you spread the glue | Dispensing / Stamping / Screen Printing |
| The curing time / clamp pressure | Curing Profile (temperature, time, force) |

Just as you would never use paper glue to mount a heavy TV, you cannot use any random material to attach a MEMS die. The choice of die attach material directly determines the **device's reliability, thermal performance, and lifetime**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering metrics are evaluated when selecting a die attach process. KTU examiners expect students to be fluent in these terms:

- **Young's Modulus (E):** Expressed in **GPa (Gigapascals)**. Determines mechanical stiffness of the bond.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE):** Expressed in **ppm/°C (parts per million per degree Celsius)**. Must closely match silicon's CTE (~**2.6 ppm/°C**) to avoid thermal stress.
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** Expressed in **°C**. Above Tg, polymeric adhesives soften dramatically.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Expressed in **W/m·K (Watts per meter-Kelvin)**. Higher values mean better heat dissipation.
- **Bond Line Thickness (BLT):** Expressed in **micrometers (µm)**. Typical range: **25 µm – 75 µm**.
- **Die Shear Strength:** Expressed in **MPa (Megapascals)**. A standard MIL-STD-883 reliability metric.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** Remember the **"3-C's" of Die Attach** — *Conduct* (heat/electricity), *Connect* (mechanically), *Compensate* (for thermal stress).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Die Attach Methods

Die attach processes are broadly classified by the **state/phase** of the bonding material used:

### A. Based on Bonding Material Type

| S.No. | Method | Bonding Material | Typical Process Temp. | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Epoxy Adhesive (Polymer)** | Silver-filled or non-conductive epoxy | 150 °C – 300 °C cure | Low cost, low stress, widely used |
| 2 | **Eutectic Bonding** | Au-Si (88% Au / 12% Si), Au-Sn, Au-Ge | 363 °C (Au-Si eutectic) | Hermetic, high reliability, high thermal conductivity |
| 3 | **Solder Bonding** | Pb-Sn, SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu) lead-free | 183 °C – 232 °C | Electrically & thermally conductive |
| 4 | **Glass Frit Bonding** | Low-melting glass paste | 400 °C – 450 °C | Used for sealing, hermetic packages |
| 5 | **Thermocompression** | Pure gold or aluminum with heat + force | 300 °C – 400 °C | Solid-state bonding, no melting |

### B. Based on Application Mechanism

- **Electrically Conductive Die Attach:** Uses Ag-filled epoxy, solder, or eutectic. Required when the die backside is an electrical contact (e.g., power devices, RF MEMS).
- **Thermally Conductive but Electrically Insulating Die Attach:** Uses epoxy loaded with ceramic fillers (e.g., Al₂O₃, BN, diamond). Required for sensors where the backside must NOT be shorted.
- **Hermetic Die Attach:** Eutectic or glass frit, used in aerospace and medical implants.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Flow (Generic)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks for a **flowchart/diagram (5 marks)** describing the die attach process. Memorize the steps below.

1. **Substrate Preparation** — The package substrate/lead-frame is cleaned using plasma, solvent, or UV-ozone to remove contaminants.
2. **Material Dispensing** — Die attach paste/epoxy is applied via **dispensing needle, screen/stencil printing, or stamping**.
3. **Die Pick-and-Place** — A high-precision die bonder (e.g., ASM, Kulicke & Soffa) picks the die from a **wafer/tray** and places it onto the bonding site with positional accuracy of **±5 µm to ±25 µm**.
4. **Bonding (Curing / Reflow)** — Heat and pressure are applied:
   - **Epoxy:** Cured at 150–300 °C for 30 minutes to 2 hours.
   - **Solder:** Reflow at peak temperature ~30 °C above melting point.
   - **Eutectic:** Scrubbing action (ultrasonic/mechanical) at eutectic temperature.
5. **Inspection** — Visual inspection, X-ray, or acoustic microscopy to detect voids.
6. **Curing (Final)** — Some epoxies require a separate oven cure post-bonding.

---

## 2.3 Key Material Properties — The Engineer's Decision Matrix

| Property | Epoxy (Ag-filled) | Eutectic Au-Si | Solder (SAC) | Glass Frit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K)** | 1 – 25 | ~250 | 50 – 60 | ~1.5 |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | High (with Ag filler) | Excellent | Excellent | Insulator |
| **CTE (ppm/°C)** | 30 – 50 (adjustable) | 12 – 16 | 22 – 25 | 3 – 9 |
| **Young's Modulus (GPa)** | 1 – 10 | 80 (Au-dominated) | 30 – 50 | 60 – 90 |
| **Process Temperature (°C)** | 150 – 300 | 363 | 217 – 260 | 400 – 450 |
| **Cost** | Low | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| **Best for** | General MEMS/IC | High-power, hermetic | Power, RF | Hermetic sealing |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Die attach technology is the **unsung hero** of the electronics industry. Without it:

- **Smartphones** would overheat within seconds (die attach dissipates heat from the SoC).
- **Automotive MEMS accelerometers (airbags)** would fail in cold weather.
- **Pacemakers and medical implants** would suffer moisture ingress without hermetic die attach.
- **5G RF MEMS filters** would lose signal integrity due to poor grounding.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Example:** A modern Intel CPU uses a **solder TIM (Thermal Interface Material)** die attach — a high-lead solder alloy — between the silicon die and the integrated heat spreader. The same engineering principle scales from KTU lab MEMS projects to multi-billion transistor processors.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Case)** | $\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}$ | °C/W | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $A$ = die area |
| 2 | **Thermal Mismatch Stress** | $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Pa | $\Delta\alpha$ = CTE mismatch, $\Delta T$ = temperature change |
| 3 | **Die Shear Strength** | $\tau = \dfrac{F_{shear}}{A_{die}}$ | MPa | MIL-STD-883 standard test |
| 4 | **Bond Line Thickness Range** | $25 \le BLT \le 75$ | µm | Process-controlled |
| 5 | **Volume Resistivity (Epoxy)** | $\rho_v = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{cond}}$ | Ω·cm | Ag-filled: $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-3}$ Ω·cm |
| 6 | **Voids Percentage (Quality)** | $\%Voids = \dfrac{A_{voids}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Must be < 5% for automotive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The thermal resistance formula $\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}$ is the **single most-likely numerical problem** from this topic. A 7-mark problem typically asks: *"If BLT doubles, what happens to thermal resistance?"* — Answer: It doubles (linear relationship).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation #1: Thermal Resistance of Die Attach Layer

### Problem Statement
Derive an expression for the **steady-state thermal resistance** of a die attach layer that transfers heat from the silicon die to the substrate.

### Given
- Bond Line Thickness = $BLT$ (m)
- Thermal conductivity of die attach material = $k$ (W/m·K)
- Die area in contact with attach material = $A$ (m²)
- Heat flow direction = 1-D, perpendicular to die surface

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**

Fourier's law in 1-D form states that the heat flux $q$ through a homogeneous material is proportional to the temperature gradient:

$$
q = -k \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}
$$

The negative sign indicates heat flows from high temperature to low temperature. In magnitude, we consider:

$$
q = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}
$$

**Step 2: Convert Heat Flux to Heat Rate**

Heat flux $q$ has units of W/m². To obtain total heat rate $Q$ (in Watts) flowing through the die attach layer:

$$
Q = q \cdot A = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{BLT}
$$

Here, $A$ is the cross-sectional area through which heat flows, and $BLT$ replaces $\Delta x$ (the thickness of the die attach layer).

**Step 3: Define Thermal Resistance**

By analogy with Ohm's law ($V = I \cdot R$), thermal resistance $\theta$ is defined as:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\theta$**

Rearranging the heat rate equation from Step 2:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{Q \cdot BLT}{k \cdot A}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- A **thicker** bond line → higher thermal resistance → worse heat dissipation.
- A **higher** thermal conductivity material → lower thermal resistance → better heat flow.
- A **larger** die area → lower thermal resistance → better heat spreading.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why high-power LEDs use **thin solder layers** with **high-k silver sintering** materials rather than thick epoxy.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Derivation #2: Thermal Mismatch Stress on Die

### Problem Statement
Derive the stress induced in a die attach bond line due to **CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion) mismatch** between the silicon die and the substrate during a temperature change.

### Given
- Young's Modulus of die attach layer = $E$ (Pa)
- CTE of die (silicon) = $\alpha_{die}$ (ppm/°C)
- CTE of substrate = $\alpha_{sub}$ (ppm/°C)
- Temperature change from stress-free state = $\Delta T$ (°C or K)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Define the Strain Due to Thermal Expansion**

When two bonded materials cool together from the bonding temperature, each wants to contract by an amount proportional to its own CTE. The **mismatch in contraction** is the source of stress.

Total differential expansion:

$$
\delta_{total} = (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L
$$

where $L$ is the characteristic length over which the mismatch occurs.

**Step 2: Define the Differential Strain (Mismatch Strain)**

The differential strain $\varepsilon$ is the ratio of the differential expansion to the original length:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta_{total}}{L} = (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T
$$

For clarity, define $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}$.

**Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law**

Within the elastic limit, stress is proportional to strain. For a 1-D approximation:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 4: Combine the Equations**

Substituting the expression for $\varepsilon$:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- If $\alpha_{sub} > \alpha_{die}$ (typical case: ceramic/PCB substrate expands more than silicon), the substrate "stretches" the die upon cooling → **tensile stress** on die.
- Excessive stress leads to **die cracking, delamination, or bond-line fracture**.
- To minimize stress: choose substrate with $\alpha_{sub} \approx \alpha_{die}$ (e.g., AlN, BeO, or silicon-matched ceramics).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Memory Hack:** Think "**EEE Delta T**" → **E**·**E**·$\Delta\alpha$·$\Delta T$ = Young's modulus × strain × temperature change.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2019-Style)

**Problem:** A silicon die (CTE = 2.6 ppm/°C) is attached using a silver-filled epoxy (CTE = 45 ppm/°C, E = 5 GPa) to an alumina substrate. If the assembly cools from 200 °C to 25 °C, calculate the thermal stress.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta T = 200 - 25 = 175 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta\alpha = 45 - 2.6 = 42.4 \text{ ppm/°C} = 42.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}
$$

$$
E = 5 \text{ GPa} = 5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\sigma = (5 \times 10^9) \times (42.4 \times 10^{-6}) \times (175)
$$

$$
\sigma = 5 \times 42.4 \times 175 \times 10^3
$$

$$
\sigma = 37{,}100 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} = 37.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\sigma \approx 37.1 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excessive stress warning:** Typical die shear strength is ~30–50 MPa for Ag-epoxy. The calculated 37.1 MPa is dangerously close to the bond-line failure limit. Engineers would **lower the curing temperature** or use a **low-CTE filler** to reduce $\Delta\alpha$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram #1 — Generic Die Attach Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Wafer Dicing] --> B[Step 2: Die Pick from Tape]
    B --> C[Step 3: Substrate Cleaning]
    C --> D[Step 4: Attach Material Dispensing]
    D --> E[Step 5: Die Placement via Bonder]
    E --> F[Step 6: Bonding Heat and Pressure]
    F --> G[Step 7: Curing or Reflow]
    G --> H[Step 8: Quality Inspection X-ray and Shear Test]
    H --> I{Defects Found?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Reject and Rework]
    I -- No --> K[Pass to Next Process Wire Bonding]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** describing the sequential operations of a die attach workstation. Use this for any 5-mark "process flowchart" question in KTU exams.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram #2 — Classification Tree of Die Attach Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Die Attach Methods]
    root --> polymer[Polymer Adhesives]
    root --> metallic[Metallic Bonds]
    root --> glass[Glass and Ceramic]

    polymer --> polyEpoxy[Silver Filled Epoxy]
    polymer --> polyAcrylic[Acrylic Adhesives]
    polymer --> polySilicone[Silicone Adhesives]

    metallic --> eutectic[Eutectic Bonding Au-Si]
    metallic --> solder[Solder Bonding Pb-Sn or SAC]
    metallic --> tc[Thermocompression Bonding]

    glass --> frit[Glass Frit Bonding]

    eutectic --> eutroApp[Use: High Power and Hermetic]
    solder --> solApp[Use: RF and Power Devices]
    polyEpoxy --> polyApp[Use: General MEMS and Cost Sensitive]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section (ASCII Block Diagram)

For a clearer physical picture of the die attach stack:

```
            ┌─────────────────────────┐
            │  Wire Bonds (Gold/Al)   │  ← Top electrical contacts
            └────────┬────────────────┘
                     │
            ┌────────┴────────────────┐
            │      SILICON DIE        │  ← Active MEMS/IC region
            │   (Backside metallized) │
            └────────┬────────────────┘
                     │
   ══════════════════╪══════════════════   ← Bond Line Interface
   ┃ DIE ATTACH LAYER (25–75 µm)        ┃
   ┃ • Ag-Epoxy / Solder / Eutectic     ┃
   ══════════════════╪══════════════════
                     │
            ┌────────┴────────────────┐
            │     SUBSTRATE /         │  ← Heat sink / Lead-frame
            │     PACKAGE BASE        │
            └─────────────────────────┘
                     │
            ┌────────┴────────────────┐
            │   EXTERNAL PINS /       │  ← PCB connection
            │   HEAT SPREADER         │
            └─────────────────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The die attach layer is literally the **"glue"** between the **silicon die** and the **package world**. It is the bridge between the microscopic chip and the macroscopic PCB.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram #3 — Failure Mode Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Die Attach Failures] --> F1[Void Formation]
    F --> F2[Delamination]
    F --> F3[Die Cracking]
    F --> F4[Insufficient Wetting]

    F1 --> C1[Cause: Trapped air or outgassing]
    F2 --> C2[Cause: CTE mismatch stress]
    F3 --> C3[Cause: Excessive bonding force]
    F4 --> C4[Cause: Contaminated surface]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "What are the common defects in die attach and their causes?" is a **frequently repeated 3-mark question**. Master these four failure modes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Define the die attach process. List any **three** primary functions it must perform.

**Model Answer:**

Die attach is the packaging step in which a bare semiconductor die is bonded onto a substrate or lead-frame using an adhesive, solder, or eutectic material.

The three primary functions are:
1. **Mechanical support** — holding the die against shock and vibration.
2. **Thermal dissipation** — conducting heat from the die to the package.
3. **Electrical interconnection** — providing backside ground contact (in conductive die attach).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Differentiate between **eutectic die attach** and **epoxy die attach** based on process temperature, thermal conductivity, and typical applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Eutectic Die Attach (Au-Si) | Epoxy Die Attach (Ag-filled) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Temperature** | ~363 °C | 150–300 °C |
| **Thermal Conductivity** | ~250 W/m·K (very high) | 1–25 W/m·K |
| **Application** | High-power, hermetic, aerospace | General MEMS, cost-sensitive consumer |

Eutectic is **metallic**, providing excellent heat flow but at a higher cost. Epoxy is **polymer-based**, cheaper and lower-temperature, but with limited heat dissipation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(a)* Describe the **step-by-step process flow** of a typical die attach operation in MEMS packaging. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a **neat sketch**, explain the **classification of die attach methods** based on bonding materials. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Process Flow (7 Marks)

| Step # | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Substrate Preparation** | Cleaning via plasma / solvent to remove oxides and organics. |
| 2 | **Material Dispensing** | Epoxy/solder paste dispensed via needle, screen, or stencil printer. |
| 3 | **Die Pick-and-Place** | A die bonder picks the die from a wafer ring and aligns it to the substrate with **±5–25 µm** accuracy. |
| 4 | **Bonding** | Heat (~150–363 °C) and force are applied to cure epoxy, reflow solder, or form eutectic. |
| 5 | **Curing / Cooling** | Epoxies require post-cure in an oven; solders cool under controlled ramp. |
| 6 | **Inspection** | X-ray, SAM (Scanning Acoustic Microscopy), and die-shear tests verify bond quality. |

### Part (b) — Classification (7 Marks)

Refer to **Mermaid Diagram #2 (Section 4.2)**. The four major classes are:

1. **Polymer Adhesives** — Ag-filled epoxy, silicones, acrylics.
2. **Eutectic Bonding** — Au-Si, Au-Sn, Au-Ge.
3. **Solder Bonding** — Pb-Sn, SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu).
4. **Glass Frit Bonding** — Low-melting glass paste.

**Sketch (Description for Answer Script):**

```
                 DIE ATTACH METHODS
       ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
   Polymer        Metallic         Glass/Ceramic
   Adhesives      Bonds            Bonds
       │              │                │
  ┌────┴────┐    ┌────┴────┐      ┌────┴────┐
  Ag-Epoxy  Silicone  Eutectic  Solder   Glass Frit
                                       (Low-T Glass)
```

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(a)* Explain the **three critical material properties** that govern the selection of a die attach material. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the **thermal resistance** of a die attach layer. A die attach bond line is **50 µm thick**, has **thermal conductivity 5 W/m·K**, and covers a die area of **25 mm²**. Calculate the thermal resistance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Three Critical Material Properties (7 Marks)

The three most critical properties are:

1. **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Determines heat dissipation capability. Higher k means lower junction temperature.
2. **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE):** Must closely match silicon (~2.6 ppm/°C). Mismatch causes stress-induced failure.
3. **Elastic Modulus / Young's Modulus (E):** Low-modulus materials absorb stress and prevent die cracking, but high-modulus materials offer better mechanical rigidity.

*(Auxiliary mention: Glass Transition Temperature Tg for polymers, electrical conductivity for ground contact.)*

### Part (b) — Derivation & Calculation (7 Marks)

**Derivation:**

From Fourier's Law:

$$
q = k \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{BLT}
$$

Heat rate:

$$
Q = q \cdot A = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{BLT}
$$

By analogy with Ohm's law, thermal resistance:

$$
\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $BLT = 50 \text{ µm} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $k = 5 \text{ W/m·K}$
- $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

Substituting:

$$
\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{125 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{50}{125} = 0.4 \text{ °C/W}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\theta_{JC} = 0.4 \text{ °C/W}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert µm and mm² to SI units (m and m²) leads to wrong answers. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Thermal Resistance with Electrical Resistance:** They share the same algebraic form but have entirely different units (°C/W vs Ω).
> 3. **Omitting the Sketch in 7-mark "Explain" Questions:** A labelled diagram fetches at least 2–3 additional marks even if the text is incomplete.
> 4. **Writing "epoxy" when you mean "Ag-filled epoxy":** Always specify the *type* of adhesive for full credit.
> 5. **Forgetting Bond Line Thickness (BLT):** The examiner's 14-mark question often gives BLT in µm — students ignore it and lose easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Die attach = bonding the bare die to a substrate/package.
- [ ] **3 Primary Functions:** Mechanical, Thermal, Electrical.
- [ ] **3-C's of Die Attach:** Conduct, Connect, Compensate.
- [ ] **Major Methods:** Epoxy, Eutectic (Au-Si, 363 °C), Solder, Glass Frit, Thermocompression.
- [ ] **Key Formula #1:** $\theta_{JC} = \dfrac{BLT}{k \cdot A}$
- [ ] **Key Formula #2:** $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$
- [ ] **Silicon CTE = 2.6 ppm/°C** — must match substrate.
- [ ] **BLT range = 25–75 µm**
- [ ] **Au-Si Eutectic Point = 363 °C** (memorize this number).
- [ ] **Common Failures:** Voids, Delamination, Die Cracking, Poor Wetting.
- [ ] **Inspection Methods:** X-ray, SAM (Scanning Acoustic Microscopy), Die Shear Test.
- [ ] **Process Flow Order:** Clean → Dispense → Place → Bond → Cure → Inspect.
- [ ] **Ag-filled epoxy k = 1–25 W/m·K** vs **Eutectic k = ~250 W/m·K**.
- [ ] **MIL-STD-883** = industry reliability test standard.
- [ ] **Hermetic packages** use eutectic or glass frit; **non-hermetic** use epoxy.

> **Final Note:** Die attach is often invisible in the final product but is **the most critical reliability-determining step** in MEMS packaging. A KTU graduate who masters this topic is equipped for careers in **semiconductor packaging, automotive electronics, medical MEMS, and 5G RF engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solder Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solder Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, a **solder alloy** is a fusible metal alloy, typically with a melting point **below 450 °C**, used to create permanent metallurgical bonds between two or more surfaces through the processes of **wetting, intermetallic formation, and solidification**. Solder alloys serve as the primary **electrical, mechanical, and thermal interconnect material** in MEMS packaging, wafer-level packaging (WLP), flip-chip assembly, and hermetic sealing operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEICT612 – Module 4):**
> Solder alloys are studied as the *enabling bonding media* for MEMS device integration. The student is expected to understand alloy composition, phase behavior, wetting thermodynamics, intermetallic compound (IMC) growth, and reliability under thermo-mechanical stress.

### 1.1.1 Key Functional Roles in MEMS

1. **Electrical Interconnection** – Provides low-resistivity ohmic paths between ICs, MEMS dies, and substrates.
2. **Mechanical Attachment** – Acts as a structural adhesive absorbing die-substrate CTE mismatch.
3. **Thermal Conduction** – Dissipates heat from active MEMS devices to the package.
4. **Hermetic Sealing** – Forms gas-tight enclosures for sensors (pressure, gyroscopes, accelerometers).
5. **Self-Alignment** – Surface tension of molten solder enables sub-micron passive alignment in flip-chip bonding.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "Molecular Lego Glue with a Trigger":**
> Think of a solder alloy as a *smart metallic glue* that is **solid at room temperature** but becomes a free-flowing liquid when heated just past its melting point. Once it touches a clean metal surface, it spreads out (like a droplet of water on a freshly waxed car bonnet, but reversed — it *likes* the metal), chemically locks on, and then freezes back into a solid joint that is mechanically strong and electrically conductive. The "trigger" is heat, and the "lock" is the formation of **intermetallic compounds (IMCs)** at the interface.

### 1.3 Common Solder Alloy Families in MEMS

| Family | Example Composition | Melting Point (°C) | Typical MEMS Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tin–Lead (Sn–Pb) | 63Sn/37Pb (eutectic) | **183** | Legacy flip-chip, low-cost packaging |
| Tin–Silver–Copper (SAC) | 96.5Sn/3Ag/0.5Cu | **217** | RoHS-compliant WLP, BGA |
| Tin–Silver (Sn–Ag) | 96.5Sn/3.5Ag | **221** | High-reliability sensors |
| Gold–Tin (Au–Sn) | 80Au/20Sn (eutectic) | **280** | Hermetic lids, optoelectronic packages |
| Gold–Silicon (Au–Si) | 88Au/12Si (eutectic) | **363** | Si-die bonding, eutectic bonding |
| Gold–Germanium (Au–Ge) | 88Au/12Ge (eutectic) | **356** | High-temp MEMS die attach |
| Indium-based | 100In, 50In/50Pb | **156 – 200** | Cryogenic & low-stress bonding |

> [!NOTE]
> The **eutectic composition** is the *exact* ratio at which the alloy transitions directly from solid to liquid at a single temperature (no pasty/slushy range). This is highly preferred in MEMS because it enables **fast, well-controlled reflow** with minimal stress on delicate microstructures.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics for Solder Selection

The following four metrics dominate alloy selection in MEMS design:

- **Melting Temperature (T_m)** – Determines reflow profile compatibility.
- **Young's Modulus (E)** – Governs mechanical compliance under thermal cycling.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, α)** – Must be matched to Si (~2.6 ppm/°C) and the substrate.
- **Electrical Resistivity (ρ)** – Determines current-carrying and signal integrity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Soldering — A Step-by-Step Conceptual Walk-Through

The joining process with a solder alloy proceeds through **five sequential physical/chemical stages**:

1. **Surface Preparation & Fluxing**
   - Native oxides, organic contaminants, and adsorbed moisture are removed using a **flux** (rosin, no-clean, or water-soluble).
   - Flux reduces surface tension and chemically scavenges oxygen.

2. **Heating to Above Liquidus**
   - The assembly is ramped past the alloy's **liquidus temperature** (T_L).
   - For a eutectic alloy, the entire mass transitions to liquid at a *single* temperature.

3. **Wetting and Spreading**
   - The molten solder droplet spreads on the metallized pad (typically Cu, Ni/Au, or ENIG).
   - Spreading is governed by the balance of interfacial energies (see Young's equation below).

4. **Intermetallic Compound (IMC) Formation**
   - At the solder–pad interface, atoms diffuse across the boundary forming IMCs such as **Cu₆Sn₅**, **Cu₃Sn**, **Ni₃Sn₄**, or **AuSn₄**.
   - IMCs provide the *metallurgical lock* that gives the joint its strength.

5. **Solidification on Cooling**
   - The joint cools, nucleates a solid phase, and freezes.
   - A fine-grained, near-eutectic microstructure is desired for maximum mechanical robustness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "Why" matters for KTU:**
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Why is eutectic composition preferred in MEMS flip-chip bonding?"* — The answer lies in steps 2 and 5: a single, sharp melting point eliminates the **slushy/pasty phase** that would otherwise allow the die to drift during reflow, destroying sub-micron alignment.

## 2.2 Wetting Thermodynamics — The Core Theory

The ability of molten solder to spread on a substrate is mathematically captured by **Young's Equation (1805)**:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = Solid–Vapor interfacial energy (mJ/m²)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = Solid–Liquid interfacial energy (mJ/m²)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = Liquid–Vapor interfacial energy (mJ/m²)
- $\theta$ = **Equilibrium contact angle** (degrees)

A **smaller θ** indicates **better wetting**:
- $\theta < 30°$ → Excellent wetting (desirable)
- $30° \le \theta \le 90°$ → Acceptable wetting
- $\theta > 90°$ → Non-wetting (joint will not form)

The **work of adhesion** $W_a$ quantifies the bond strength at the interface:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV} (\cos\theta + 1)$$

The **spreading coefficient** $S$ defines the thermodynamic driving force for spreading:

$$S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$$

If $S > 0$, complete spreading occurs (θ → 0°).

## 2.3 Intermetallic Compound (IMC) Growth Kinetics

IMC growth is a **diffusion-controlled** process that follows the empirical **parabolic growth law**:

$$x(t) = \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $x(t)$ = IMC layer thickness (m)
- $D_{\text{eff}}$ = Effective diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $t$ = Time at temperature (s)

The diffusion coefficient is **temperature-dependent** through the **Arrhenius relation**:

$$D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $D_0$ = Pre-exponential factor (m²/s)
- $Q$ = Activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

## 2.4 Thermo-Mechanical Reliability

During thermal cycling, the solder joint experiences **shear strain** due to CTE mismatch between die and substrate:

$$\Delta \gamma = (\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{die}}) \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $\Delta \gamma$ = Inelastic shear strain range
- $\alpha_{\text{sub}}, \alpha_{\text{die}}$ = CTE of substrate and die (ppm/°C)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature swing (°C)

Fatigue life is predicted by the **Coffin–Manson relation**:

$$N_f = \left(\frac{\Delta \gamma}{2 \varepsilon_f'}\right)^{1/c}$$

Where:
- $N_f$ = Cycles to failure
- $\varepsilon_f'$ = Fatigue ductility coefficient
- $c$ = Fatigue ductility exponent (typically −0.5 to −0.7 for solders)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ | Young's wetting equation | mJ/m² |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta + 1)$ | Work of adhesion | mJ/m² |
| 3 | $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$ | Spreading coefficient | mJ/m² |
| 4 | $x(t) = \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} \cdot t}$ | IMC growth (parabolic) | m |
| 5 | $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \cdot \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius diffusion | m²/s |
| 6 | $\Delta \gamma = (\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{die}}) \Delta T$ | CTE-induced shear strain | dimensionless (ppm·°C) |
| 7 | $N_f = (\Delta \gamma / 2 \varepsilon_f')^{1/c}$ | Coffin–Manson fatigue life | cycles |
| 8 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Shear/axial stress in joint | Pa |
| 9 | $R_{\text{joint}} = \rho \cdot L / A$ | Electrical resistance of joint | Ω |
| 10 | $P_{\text{joule}} = I^2 R_{\text{joint}}$ | Joule heating (EM driver) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This compact set of ten equations is the *minimum* a KTU 2024 student must memorize to solve any derivation, numerical, or design problem on solder alloys in MEMS packaging.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP):** Solder balls (SAC405, SAC305) replace wire bonds for fine-pitch, high-I/O MEMS-CMOS integration.
- **Hermetic Lid Sealing:** Au–Sn (80/20) eutectic is the industry standard for sealing MEMS pressure-sensor lids because of its high strength and absence of flux residue.
- **Eutectic Si Bonding:** Au–Si (363 °C) directly bonds silicon dies without intermediate solder, exploiting silicon's natural eutectic with gold.
- **Under-Bump Metallization (UBM):** Solder alloys are *only* as good as their UBM — adhesion, wetting, and IMC control are inseparable design concerns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young's Equation from Surface Energy Balance

We will derive **Young's Equation** by considering a small virtual displacement $\delta x$ of the liquid–vapor contact line along the solid surface, at constant volume of the liquid droplet.

**Step 1 — Identify the three interfaces and their energies.**

At the three-phase contact line (solid–liquid–vapor), three interfaces meet:
- Solid–Vapor interface of energy $\gamma_{SV}$
- Solid–Liquid interface of energy $\gamma_{SL}$
- Liquid–Vapor interface of energy $\gamma_{LV}$

**Step 2 — Impose virtual displacement.**

Let the contact line advance by an infinitesimal amount $\delta x$ on the solid surface.

The horizontal projection of the liquid–vapor interface *shortens* by $\delta x \cos\theta$, while the solid–vapor interface is *replaced* by solid–liquid of the same horizontal extent $\delta x$.

**Step 3 — Net change in surface energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta G &= (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV}) \cdot \delta x + \gamma_{LV} \cdot (-\cos\theta) \cdot \delta x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium ($\delta G = 0$) and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV}) - \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta &= 0 \\
\gamma_{SV} &= \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta \qquad \text{(Young's Equation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Students often lose marks by forgetting the *sign convention* on the L–V displacement. Always state explicitly that the contact line movement *consumes* solid–vapor area and *creates* solid–liquid area.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parabolic IMC Growth (Fick's Second Law, 1-D Semi-Infinite Case)

Consider diffusion of species (e.g., Cu) across the solder–pad interface, with the solder side acting as a semi-infinite medium.

**Step 1 — Fick's Second Law in 1-D.**

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{\text{eff}} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Boundary & Initial Conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= C_0 \quad \text{(uniform initial concentration)} \\
C(0, t) &= C_s \quad \text{(constant surface concentration, } t \ge 0 \text{)} \\
C(\infty, t) &= C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Error-Function Solution.**

The classical solution to this boundary-value problem is:

$$
C(x, t) = C_s - (C_s - C_0) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} t}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the IMC layer position.**

The IMC front is defined by the locus where $C = C^*$, a critical stoichiometric concentration. The argument of the error function is therefore a constant:

$$
\frac{x}{2 \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} t}} = K \quad (\text{constant})
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x(t)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 2K \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} t} \\
x(t) &= \sqrt{k \cdot t}, \quad \text{where } k = 4K^2 D_{\text{eff}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **parabolic growth law** $x(t) = \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} t}$ used in Section 2.3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coffin–Manson Relation from Empirical Plastic-Strain Data

The Coffin–Manson law is fundamentally empirical, but can be motivated from low-cycle fatigue theory.

**Step 1 — Define cyclic inelastic strain range.**

For a solder joint under fully-reversed thermal cycling:

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{in} = (\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{die}}) \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Experimental observation.**

Empirically, the number of cycles to failure $N_f$ is related to plastic strain amplitude $\Delta \varepsilon_p / 2$ by a power law:

$$
\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' \cdot (N_f)^{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify plastic with total inelastic strain (in low-cycle regime).**

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_p \approx \Delta \gamma
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_f$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta \gamma}{2} &= \varepsilon_f' (N_f)^{c} \\
N_f &= \left(\frac{\Delta \gamma}{2 \varepsilon_f'}\right)^{1/c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Coffin–Manson relation** referenced in Section 2.4.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — IMC Thickness After Reflow

**Given:**
- Solder joint held at 250 °C (T = 523 K) for $t = 600$ s.
- $D_0 = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Q = 105$ kJ/mol.
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

**Find:** Expected Cu₆Sn₅ IMC thickness.

**Step 1 — Compute $D_{\text{eff}}$.**

$$
D_{\text{eff}} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{105{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 523}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{105{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 523} = \frac{105{,}000}{4347.4} = 24.15
$$

$$
D_{\text{eff}} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot e^{-24.15} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 3.27 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
D_{\text{eff}} = 5.56 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IMC thickness.**

$$
x = \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} \cdot t} = \sqrt{5.56 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 600}
$$

$$
x = \sqrt{3.34 \times 10^{-13}} = 5.78 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 0.58 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU Industry Context):** IMC layers in production MEMS joints are typically **0.5 – 2.0 µm** thick. Thicker than ~5 µm, the joint becomes **brittle** and prone to fracture along the IMC plane. This is why a single long reflow cycle is *worse* than multiple short ones of equivalent thermal budget.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Solder Alloys in MEMS

| Property | 63Sn/37Pb | SAC305 (96.5Sn/3Ag/0.5Cu) | 80Au/20Sn | 88Au/12Si |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Melting Point (°C) | 183 | 217 | 280 | 363 |
| Eutectic? | Yes | No (near-eutectic) | Yes | Yes |
| Electrical Resistivity (µΩ·cm) | 14.5 | 13.0 | 16.0 | 30.0 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 35 | 50 | 68 | 80 |
| CTE (ppm/°C) | 24.5 | 22.0 | 16.0 | 12.0 |
| Flux Required? | Yes | Yes | No | No (forms own bond) |
| Typical MEMS Use | Legacy packages | RoHS WLP/BGA | Hermetic lids | Si-die bonding |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Very High | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
