# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 marks]**
> **Q:** Define a **solder alloy** and list the two functional roles it plays in MEMS packaging.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A solder alloy is a fusible metal alloy (typically T_m < 450 °C) that, when melted, wets metallic surfaces to form a permanent metallurgical bond upon solidification. In MEMS packaging it serves two primary roles: **(i) Electrical and mechanical interconnect** between the MEMS die, substrate, and external leads, and **(ii) Hermetic sealing** of the sensor cavity using alloys such as 80Au/20Sn or 88Au/12Si. Secondary roles include thermal management and passive self-alignment via molten-surface tension.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 marks]**
> **Q:** Why is a **eutectic composition** preferred over a non-eutectic alloy in MEMS flip-chip reflow?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A eutectic alloy melts and solidifies at a single, sharp temperature (no pasty/slushy range). This ensures that during reflow the die is never suspended in a partially molten state, thereby **preventing drift of the aligned die** under vibration or surface-tension forces. The narrow melting range also enables **faster ramp rates**, **finer microstructure**, and **lower residual thermal stress** — all critical for sub-100 µm MEMS features.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice Set, 14 marks]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Wetting, IMC, and Joint Reliability

**(a)** Derive **Young's Equation** starting from a virtual displacement of the three-phase contact line. Explain the physical meaning of each interfacial energy term and discuss how the contact angle $\theta$ governs the **wettability** of a solder alloy on a typical MEMS metallization (Cu, Ni/Au). (7 marks)

**(b)** A MEMS engineer is using SAC305 solder on a Cu pad. The joint is held at **T = 250 °C** for **600 s**. The IMC growth follows the parabolic law $x(t) = \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} \cdot t}$ with $D_0 = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and $Q = 105$ kJ/mol. Compute the Cu₆Sn₅ IMC thickness and comment on whether the joint is within the safe design window (0.5 – 2.0 µm). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1:** Define the three interfacial energies acting on the contact line: $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$.

**Step 2:** Imagine an infinitesimal advance $\delta x$ of the contact line along the solid surface. The change in free energy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta G &= \gamma_{SL}\,\delta x - \gamma_{SV}\,\delta x - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\,\delta x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** At thermodynamic equilibrium, $\delta G = 0$. Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} &= \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \\
\gamma_{SV} &= \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**
- $\gamma_{SV}$: energy of the bare, clean pad.
- $\gamma_{SL}$: energy once the molten solder wets it.
- $\gamma_{LV}$: free surface energy of the molten droplet.
- A **small θ (< 30°)** means the system gains more energy by replacing S–V with S–L — i.e., **strong wetting** and a high-quality bond.
- On Cu and Ni/Au (ENIG) pads with proper fluxing, SAC305 typically achieves θ ≈ 10° – 20°, indicating excellent wettability.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $D_{\text{eff}}$ at 250 °C = 523 K.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{eff}} &= 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{105{,}000}{8.314 \cdot 523}\right) \\
&= 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot e^{-24.15} \\
&= 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 3.27 \times 10^{-11} \\
&= 5.56 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IMC thickness.**

$$
x = \sqrt{5.56 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 600} = \sqrt{3.34 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 5.78 \times 10^{-7}\text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{x \approx 0.58 \, \mu\text{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Design Verdict.**
The IMC thickness of 0.58 µm lies **within the safe design window (0.5 – 2.0 µm)**. The joint is at the **low end**, indicating good mechanical compliance but only a *thin* metallurgical lock. To improve bond robustness for high-reliability aerospace/MEMS applications, the engineer may extend the dwell time to ~1500 s (yielding ≈ 0.91 µm) — but should avoid exceeding 5 µm, beyond which the joint becomes brittle.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alloy Selection, CTE, and Fatigue

**(a)** Compare **63Sn/37Pb**, **SAC305**, **80Au/20Sn**, and **88Au/12Si** solder alloys for use in (i) hermetic lid sealing of a MEMS pressure sensor, and (ii) RoHS-compliant wafer-level packaging. Justify the choice in each case using **melting point, flux requirement, CTE, and cost**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A MEMS accelerometer is soldered to an FR-4 substrate ($\alpha_{\text{sub}} = 14$ ppm/°C) using SAC305 ($\alpha_{\text{die\ side}} = 22$ ppm/°C effective at the die). The device is thermally cycled between **−40 °C and +125 °C**. Using the Coffin–Manson relation with $\varepsilon_f' = 0.325$ and $c = -0.57$, estimate the **cycles to failure** $N_f$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Use Case | Recommended Alloy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Hermetic lid seal of MEMS pressure sensor | **80Au/20Sn** | T_m = 280 °C is *above* subsequent plastic-package molding temperatures; **flux-free** reflow eliminates residue that could outgas and contaminate the sensor cavity; high strength (68 GPa) tolerates pressure differentials; CTE ≈ 16 ppm/°C is reasonably close to Si. |
| RoHS-compliant WLP | **SAC305** | Lead-free (RoHS/ELV compliant); T_m = 217 °C is compatible with standard SMT profiles; excellent wetting on ENIG/OSP Cu pads; widely available as solder spheres for BGA/WLCSP. |
| NOT recommended for either | 63Sn/37Pb | Banned under RoHS; contains toxic Pb. |
| NOT recommended for either | 88Au/12Si | T_m = 363 °C is too high for standard MEMS packaging — risks damaging integrated circuits and previously-deposited metallizations. |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature swing.**

$$
\Delta T = 125 - (-40) = 165 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute shear strain range.**

$$
\Delta \gamma = (\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{die}}) \cdot \Delta T = (22 - 14) \times 10^{-6} \cdot 165
$$

$$
\Delta \gamma = 8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 165 = 1.32 \times 10^{-3} = 0.00132
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Coffin–Manson relation.**

$$
N_f = \left(\frac{\Delta \gamma}{2 \varepsilon_f'}\right)^{1/c} = \left(\frac{0.00132}{2 \cdot 0.325}\right)^{1/(-0.57)}
$$

$$
N_f = \left(\frac{0.00132}{0.65}\right)^{-1.754} = (0.00203)^{-1.754}
$$

$$
N_f = (493)^{-1} \text{ in log domain} \Rightarrow N_f \approx 1{,}930 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.**
A fatigue life of **~1900 thermal cycles** is *marginal* for automotive under-hood or aerospace applications (which demand 3000 – 5000 cycles). Recommended mitigations:
- Switch to a lower-CTE substrate (e.g., **AlN** with α ≈ 4.5 ppm/°C, or **LTCC**).
- Add **underfill** to distribute strain.
- Use a **compliant solder alloy** such as Sn–Ag–Bi or add a low-modality **solder cap**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Coffin–Manson:** Students frequently compute $(\alpha_{\text{die}} - \alpha_{\text{sub}})$ instead of the correct (sub − die). This *inverts* the sign but the magnitude still works — except for the **direction of strain**, which is a 1-mark conceptual deduction in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Always use ppm (10⁻⁶) for CTE and °C for ΔT. A common error yields Δγ in *ppm·°C* (dimensionless-looking but wrong magnitude).
> 3. **Forgetting exponent sign:** In $N_f = (\ldots)^{1/c}$, with $c < 0$, the *inversion* is critical. Sketch the algebra on the answer sheet — partial credit depends on showing the inversion step.
> 4. **IMC vs total thickness:** Students often quote *total joint thickness* as IMC thickness. IMC is a *thin interfacial layer*, typically 0.5 – 5 µm — orders of magnitude smaller than the joint itself.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Solder Alloys (MEMS Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Solder alloy = fusible metal alloy (T_m < 450 °C) used for permanent metallurgical bonding in MEMS.
- **Eutectic vs Non-Eutectic:** Eutectic = single T_m, no pasty range; always preferred for MEMS flip-chip.
- **Key Alloys:** 63Sn/37Pb (183 °C), SAC305 (217 °C), 80Au/20Sn (280 °C), 88Au/12Si (363 °C), 88Au/12Ge (356 °C), In-based (156–200 °C).
- **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ — governs wettability.
- **Wettability criterion:** θ < 30° = good; θ > 90° = non-wetting.
- **Work of Adhesion:** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta + 1)$.
- **Spreading Coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$; $S > 0$ = complete spreading.
- **IMC Growth:** $x(t) = \sqrt{D_{\text{eff}} t}$ — parabolic, diffusion-controlled.
- **Arrhenius Diffusion:** $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- **Design Rule:** IMC thickness should remain in 0.5 – 2.0 µm; > 5 µm = brittle failure.
- **Shear Strain from CTE:** $\Delta \gamma = (\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{die}}) \Delta T$.
- **Fatigue Life:** $N_f = (\Delta \gamma / 2 \varepsilon_f')^{1/c}$ (Coffin–Manson).
- **MEMS-Specific Uses:** Flip-chip BGA (SAC), hermetic lids (Au–Sn), Si-die bonding (Au–Si), cryogenic (In).
- **Reliability Threats:** Thermal fatigue, electromigration, IMC overgrowth, voiding, Sn-whisker growth.
- **Process Sequence:** Surface prep + flux → heat above liquidus → wetting → IMC formation → solidification.
- **Constants to Memorize:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); Si CTE ≈ 2.6 ppm/°C; 63Sn/37Pb T_m = 183 °C; SAC305 T_m = 217 °C; Au–Sn T_m = 280 °C; Au–Si T_m = 363 °C.
- **RoHS Context:** Pb-based solders (63Sn/37Pb) are *banned* in commercial products; SAC305 is the *industry default* lead-free replacement.
- **UBM Role:** Under-bump metallization (Cu/Ni/Au or ENIG) prevents Cu diffusion and anchors IMC growth — *always* specify UBM in design answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bonding Process Using Solder Alloy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bonding Process Using Solder Alloy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solder Bonding (Solder Alloy Wafer Bonding)** is a *thermocompression-based wafer-level packaging and interconnection technique* in which a low-melting-point metal alloy (the **solder**) is reflowed between two metallized surfaces to form a permanent mechanical, electrical, and hermetic (or near-hermetic) joint. In MEMS, it is classified under **fusion bonding** family and is widely used for die-attach, capping (package-level sealing), 3-D stacking, and electrical interconnect formation.

The process leverages the principle of **liquid-phase wetting** — when a solder alloy is heated above its *melting point (T_m)*, surface tension forces drive the molten metal to wet pre-deposited metallic pads (called **Under-Bump Metallization, UBM**), and on cooling, the alloy solidifies into a robust metallurgical bond.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Chocolate Wafer" Model of Solder Bonding**
> Imagine two dry biscuits (the two wafers/chips). You dip a knife in melted chocolate (the molten solder) and spread a thin layer on the surface of one biscuit. The chocolate *wets* and *spreads* over the rough surface, filling micro-pores. Now you press the second biscuit on top. As the chocolate cools, it hardens and locks both biscuits together, sealing the jam (the MEMS cavity) inside. That is exactly how **Au-Sn (80/20) solder bonding** works in MEMS packaging — except the chocolate is replaced by a precisely engineered metal alloy, and the biscuits are silicon/glass wafers with patterned metal pads.

## Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Eutectic Au-Sn (80 wt% Au / 20 wt% Sn):** Melting point **T_m = 280 °C** — a benchmark value.
- **Eutectic Sn-Pb (63/37):** T_m = **183 °C** (legacy, restricted under RoHS).
- **Eutectic Au-Si (97.6/2.4):** T_m = **363 °C** — silicon-rich, used in Si-to-Si bonding.
- **SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu, e.g., SAC305):** T_m ≈ **217 °C** — modern lead-free.
- **Surface tension of molten Au-Sn at 300 °C:** ≈ **0.5 N/m**.
- **Bonding force range in MEMS solder bonding:** typically **1 – 10 MPa**.
- **Bonding ambient:** forming gas (**N₂ + 4% H₂**) or **high vacuum (< 10⁻³ mbar)** for hermeticity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The solder bonding process is governed by **three sequential metallurgical phenomena**:

1. **Wetting & Spreading**
   - Molten solder contacts the UBM (e.g., Ti/Ni/Au, Cu, or Ni).
   - **Driving force:** reduction in interfacial free energy → described by the **Young-Dupré equation** (see formula sheet).
   - Wettability is quantified by the **contact angle θ**; θ < 90° implies good wetting.

2. **Interfacial Reaction & Intermetallic Compound (IMC) Formation**
   - The liquid solder reacts with the UBM to form **brittle but conductive IMCs** (e.g., Au-Sn → AuSn, AuSn₂, AuSn₄).
   - Controlled IMC growth is essential — *excessive IMC weakens the joint*.

3. **Solidification on Cooling**
   - On controlled cooldown (typical ramp 1–5 °C/s), the alloy crystallizes at the eutectic temperature.
   - For **eutectic Au-Sn**, two phases co-solidify: **AuSn (δ-phase, hexagonal)** and **AuSn₂ (ζ-phase)**.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why pre-metallize the bond frame?** Bare Si/glass cannot be wetted by molten solder. The **UBM** (commonly **Ti 100 nm / Ni 200 nm / Au 50 nm**) provides an adhesion layer (Ti), a diffusion barrier (Ni), and an oxide-free wetting surface (Au).
- **Why a forming gas (N₂/H₂) ambient?** Prevents **solder oxidation** (especially critical for Sn-rich alloys) and reduces oxide-induced voiding.
- **Why use eutectic compositions?** Eutectic alloys melt at a **single, sharp temperature** (no pasty range) → uniform reflow, no die-shift, and excellent planarity — critical for MEMS cavities with mechanical moving parts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Symbols & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young-Dupré Wetting Equation | $\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid-vapor surface energy (N/m), $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid-liquid, $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid-vapor, $\theta$ = contact angle (deg) | Wetting occurs when $\theta \lt 90°$ |
| Work of Adhesion | $W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl}$ | Units: N/m | Energy required to separate interface |
| Laplace Capillary Pressure | $\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{r}$ | $r$ = effective seal gap (m), $\Delta P$ (Pa) | Determines hermeticity of solder seal |
| Solder Joint Height (approx.) | $h_{solder} \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_{solder}}{A_{frame}}$ | $V$ = volume (m³), $A$ = bond-frame area (m²) | Used in solder-bump volume design |
| Shear Strength (rule-of-thumb) | $\tau \;\approx\; 30\;\text{to}\;50\;\text{MPa}$ for eutectic Au-Sn | $\tau$ (MPa) | On Si with proper UBM |
| Eutectic Au-Sn Melting Point | $T_m \;=\; 280\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | — | Alloys: Au(80 wt%)–Sn(20 wt%) |
| Diffusional IMC Growth (parabolic) | $x \;=\; \sqrt{D\cdot t}$ | $x$ = IMC thickness (m), $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $t$ = time (s) | Valid for isothermal aging |
| Bonding Pressure (typical) | $P_{bond} \;=\; 1\;\text{to}\;10\;\text{MPa}$ | Pressure (MPa) | Applied via thermocompression bonder |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MEMS pressure sensors & accelerometers** — hermetic Au-Sn cap sealing of silicon cavities (Bosch, STMicroelectronics, Infineon production lines).
- **RF-MEMS & SAW filters** — solder bonding for low-loss, low-parasitic packages.
- **Optoelectronics & VCSELs** — Au-Sn sealing of laser diode packages.
- **Power electronics** — die-attach of GaN/SiC dies using **SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu)** or **Au-Ge** solders.
- **3-D IC stacking** — micro-bump solder (Cu-pillar + Sn-cap) for through-silicon-via (TSV) interconnects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always mention the **UBM stack** and the **forming-gas / vacuum ambient** in your answer — these are the two most frequently missed "engineering-completeness" points.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work of Adhesion from Young-Dupré Equation

**Goal:** Derive the *work of adhesion* $W_a$ which quantifies the thermodynamic strength of the solder–UBM interface.

**Starting point (Young's Equation):**
$$\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate the solid–liquid term.**
$$\gamma_{sl} \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the definition of work of adhesion $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$.**
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \left(\gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\right)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the bracket.**
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — The $\gamma_{sv}$ terms cancel.**
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv}\,(1 \;+\; \cos\theta)$$

**Step 5 — Final Dupré–Young form.**
$$\boxed{\,W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv}\,\bigl(1 + \cos\theta\bigr)\,}$$

**Engineering meaning:** Lower contact angle $\theta$ → higher work of adhesion → better wetting → stronger solder bond. For eutectic Au-Sn on Au-UBM, $\theta \approx 10°$–$20°$, giving near-maximum $W_a \approx 2\gamma_{lv}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hermetic-Seal Laplace Pressure

**Goal:** Derive the maximum pressure a solder seal ring can withstand before the molten (or solid) meniscus ruptures.

**Geometry:** A solder seal of width $w$ and stand-off height $h$ forms a curved meniscus approximated as a cylindrical segment of radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Young-Laplace for a cylindrical interface (single principal curvature).**
$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{lv}}{R_{curv}}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric relation between meniscus radius $R_{curv}$ and the stand-off height $h$ with contact angle $\theta$:**
$$R_{curv} \;=\; \frac{h}{2\cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the Laplace relation.**
$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{lv}\cdot 2\cos\theta}{h} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Final seal-pressure expression.**
$$\boxed{\,\Delta P_{max} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{h_{solder}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration for Au-Sn:** $\gamma_{lv} \approx 0.5\;\text{N/m}$, $\theta = 15°$, $h = 20\,\mu\text{m} = 2\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}$.

$$\Delta P_{max} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.5 \times \cos 15°}{2\times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; \frac{1.0 \times 0.966}{2\times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 4.83\times 10^{4}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 0.48\;\text{atm}$$

This corresponds to a **seal-pressure tolerance ≈ 0.5 atm**, which is more than sufficient for most MEMS cavity pressures (typically 0.1–1 atm absolute).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solder Alloy Selection Chart

| Property | Au-Sn (80/20) | Sn-Pb (63/37) | Au-Si (97.6/2.4) | SAC305 (Sn-Ag-Cu) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Eutectic T_m | **280 °C** | **183 °C** | **363 °C** | **217 °C** |
| Electrical conductivity | High | Medium | High | Medium |
| Thermal fatigue resistance | Excellent | Poor | Good | Good |
| Hermeticity | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Moderate |
| RoHS compliance | Yes | No | Yes | Yes |
| Typical UBM | Ti/Ni/Au or Ni/Au | Cu or Ni | Bare Si | Cu or Ni(P)/Au |
| MEMS application | Cap sealing | Legacy die-attach | Si-to-Si bonding | Power die-attach |
| Wetting on Au | Excellent | Good | N/A (forms Au-Si eutectic) | Moderate |

## 3.4 IMC Growth Estimate (Parabolic Diffusion)

**Given:** Au-Sn aged at 200 °C for 100 hours. Diffusivity $D \approx 1.5\times 10^{-16}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Convert time to seconds.**
$$t = 100 \times 3600 \;=\; 3.6\times 10^{5}\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply $x = \sqrt{D\,t}$.**
$$x \;=\; \sqrt{(1.5\times 10^{-16})\times(3.6\times 10^{5})}$$
$$x \;=\; \sqrt{5.4\times 10^{-11}} \;\approx\; 7.35\times 10^{-6}\;\text{m} \;\approx\; 7.4\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.** An IMC layer of ≈ 7 µm is within the safe operating window; values above 25 µm are considered mechanically risky (brittle Au-Sn IMCs).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solder Bonding — Block-Level Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wafer 1: Si / Glass with UBM Pads] --> B[Step 1: Deposit Solder Pre-form by Electroplating or Screen Printing]
    B --> C[Step 2: Align Wafer 1 to Cap Wafer 2 in Bonding Tool]
    C --> D[Step 3: Apply Controlled Bonding Force 1 to 10 MPa]
    D --> E[Step 4: Heat Above Eutectic T_m in N2 plus 4 percent H2 Ambient]
    E --> F[Step 5: Solder Reflow and Wetting on UBM]
    F --> G[Step 6: Cool Down to Form Eutectic Seal]
    G --> H[Step 7: Hermetic MEMS Cavity Sealed]
    H --> I[Step 8: Dicing into Individual Packaged Dies]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Architecture of a Solder-Sealed MEMS Device

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Device Wafer Layer]
        A1[Si Substrate with MEMS Sensor Element]
        A2[UBM Metal Stack Ti Ni Au]
    end
    subgraph L2[Solder Seal Layer]
        B1[Eutectic Au-Sn 80 20 Solder Ring]
        B2[Intermetallic Compound AuSn and AuSn2]
    end
    subgraph L3[Cap Wafer Layer]
        C1[Glass or Si Cap Wafer]
        C2[Cavity Recess for MEMS Movement]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Bonding-Tool Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Heated Chuck Top] --> P2[Cap Wafer]
    P2 --> P3[Solder Seal Frame]
    P3 --> P4[Device Wafer]
    P4 --> P5[Heated Chuck Bottom]
    P5 --> P6[Servo Pressure Actuator 1 to 10 MPa]
    P1 --> P7[Forming Gas Inlet N2 plus H2]
    P7 --> P8[Bonding Chamber]
    P8 --> P9[Vacuum Pump less than 10 power minus3 mbar]
```

## 4.4 IMC Phase Evolution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Initial Liquid Au-Sn 280 C] --> S2[Cooling to 217 C forms AuSn delta]
    S2 --> S3[Aging at 200 C forms AuSn2 zeta]
    S3 --> S4[Extended Aging forms AuSn4 brittle]
    S4 --> S5[Failure Mode Brittle Fracture]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above are *block-level functional representations* of the physical process — they map cause-and-effect and layer-to-layer interactions rather than photolithographic cross-sections.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]**
**Define solder bonding. List any three solder alloys used in MEMS packaging with their eutectic temperatures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Solder bonding is a thermocompression wafer-level bonding technique in which a low-melting-point metal alloy is reflowed between two metallized surfaces to form a mechanical, electrical, and hermetic seal. Three commonly used solder alloys are:

1. **Eutectic Au-Sn (80 wt% Au / 20 wt% Sn)** — T_m = **280 °C**.
2. **Eutectic Sn-Pb (63/37)** — T_m = **183 °C** (legacy, RoHS-restricted).
3. **SAC305 (Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu)** — T_m ≈ **217 °C** (modern lead-free).

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for alloys with temperatures = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
**What is Under-Bump Metallization (UBM)? Why is it essential in solder bonding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

UBM is the **multi-layer metal stack** (e.g., Ti / Ni / Au, or Ti / Cu / Ni / Au) deposited on the bond-frame region of the wafer before solder reflow.

**Why essential:**
- Provides **adhesion** to the Si/glass substrate (Ti layer).
- Acts as a **diffusion barrier** preventing solder–Si inter-diffusion (Ni layer).
- Supplies an **oxide-free, wettable surface** for the molten solder (Au top layer).

Without UBM, the molten solder would **de-wet, ball up, and form voids**, leading to a failed hermetic seal. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks for roles = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **step-by-step process of eutectic Au-Sn solder bonding** for MEMS cap sealing. Explain the role of the bonding ambient and the UBM stack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Young-Dupré equation** for solder wetting and explain how the **work of adhesion** controls joint quality. A molten Au-Sn solder has $\gamma_{lv} = 0.50\;\text{N/m}$ and forms a contact angle of 20° on a Ni/Au UBM. Calculate the work of adhesion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Wafer Preparation.**
Two wafers are used: a **device wafer** (with the MEMS sensor + metallized bond frame) and a **cap wafer** (glass or Si with a cavity recess + matching bond frame). The UBM stack **Ti (100 nm) / Ni (200 nm) / Au (50 nm)** is sputter-deposited on both bond frames. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Solder Pre-form Deposition.**
A eutectic Au-Sn pre-form is deposited on the cap-wafer bond frame by **electro-plating, screen printing of paste, or pre-formed foil lamination**. Typical thickness 5–25 µm. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Wafer Alignment.**
Both wafers are aligned in a wafer bonder (e.g., SUSS Microtec) with sub-micron precision and brought into contact. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Bonding Cycle.**
- The bonding chamber is purged with **forming gas (N₂ + 4% H₂)** to prevent Sn-oxide formation; alternatively, vacuum < 10⁻³ mbar is used for hermetic-grade seals.
- A **bonding force of 1–10 MPa** is applied via the heated chucks.
- The chuck temperature is ramped above **T_m = 280 °C** (typically 310–330 °C) to initiate **reflow and wetting**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Reflow & Wetting.**
The molten Au-Sn wets the Au-top of the UBM, the **interfacial reaction forms intermetallic AuSn (δ) and AuSn₂ (ζ)** phases, and the meniscus self-aligns the cap to the device wafer. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Cool-down and Dicing.**
The stack is cooled at a controlled rate (1–5 °C/s) below 280 °C, solidifying the seal into a hermetic joint. Finally, the bonded wafer pair is diced into individual packaged dies. *(1 mark)*

**Role of UBM:** Ti = adhesion to Si, Ni = diffusion barrier, Au = oxide-free wetting surface. *(Bonus 1 mark for UBM role)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Statement of Young's Equation at the three-phase contact line.**
$$\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$
where $sv$, $sl$, $lv$ denote solid-vapor, solid-liquid, and liquid-vapor interfaces. *(2 marks for setup)*

**Step 2 — Definition of Work of Adhesion.**
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl}$$
This is the energy released per unit area when the liquid–solid interface forms from the separated surfaces. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Derivation by substitution.**
Eliminate $\gamma_{sl}$ using Young's equation:
$$\gamma_{sl} \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \bigl(\gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\bigr)$$
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$
*(2 marks for derivation)*

**Step 4 — Numerical Calculation.**
Given $\gamma_{lv} = 0.50\;\text{N/m}$ and $\theta = 20°$:
$$W_a \;=\; 0.50\,\bigl(1 + \cos 20°\bigr)$$
$$W_a \;=\; 0.50\,\bigl(1 + 0.9397\bigr)$$
$$W_a \;=\; 0.50 \times 1.9397 \;\approx\; 0.970\;\text{N/m}$$
*(2 marks for calculation)*

**Conclusion:** A work of adhesion of ≈ 0.97 N/m indicates **near-complete wetting** — characteristic of high-quality Au-Sn solder joints on Au UBM.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** Compare **eutectic Au-Sn, Au-Si, and SAC305** solder alloys for MEMS packaging in terms of melting point, hermeticity, RoHS compliance, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular Au-Sn solder seal ring of mean radius $R = 5\;\text{mm}$ and cross-section $w \times h = 200\;\mu\text{m} \times 20\;\mu\text{m}$ is to be deposited on a MEMS cap wafer. The cap cavity will operate at an internal pressure of 0.5 atm. Using the Laplace-pressure equation, determine whether the seal can withstand the pressure differential. Assume $\gamma_{lv} = 0.5\;\text{N/m}$ and $\theta = 15°$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

| Parameter | Au-Sn (80/20) | Au-Si (97.6/2.4) | SAC305 (Sn-Ag-Cu) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eutectic T_m | **280 °C** | **363 °C** | **217 °C** |
| Hermeticity | Excellent (low IMC brittleness) | Excellent | Moderate (Sn whisker / oxidation) |
| RoHS | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Application | MEMS cap sealing, RF packages, VCSELs | Si-to-Si wafer bonding, high-temp sensors | Power electronics die-attach |

*(7 marks distributed across: 3 marks for tabulated comparison + 2 marks for application discussion + 2 marks for engineering-justification based on T_m vs. process temperature.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the design requirement.**
The pressure differential across the seal is:
$$\Delta P_{req} \;=\; \vert P_{external} - P_{internal} \vert \;=\; \vert 1.0 - 0.5 \vert \;\text{atm} \;=\; 0.5\;\text{atm}$$
Convert to SI: $0.5 \times 101325 \;\approx\; 5.07\times 10^{4}\;\text{Pa}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Apply the Laplace-pressure equation for the seal meniscus.**
$$\Delta P_{max} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{h}$$
Substitute $\gamma_{lv} = 0.5\;\text{N/m}$, $\theta = 15°$, $h = 20\;\mu\text{m} = 2\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}$:
$$\Delta P_{max} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.5 \times \cos 15°}{2\times 10^{-5}}$$
$$\Delta P_{max} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 0.9659}{2\times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 4.83\times 10^{4}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 0.477\;\text{atm}$$
*(3 marks)*

**Step 3 — Compare to the requirement.**
$$\Delta P_{max} \;=\; 0.477\;\text{atm} \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \Delta P_{req} \;=\; 0.5\;\text{atm}$$

The seal capacity is **marginally insufficient** (safety margin = −4.6%). *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Engineering recommendation.**
To meet the requirement safely, the designer should:
- **Reduce the stand-off height $h$** to ≈ 18 µm (gives $\Delta P_{max} \approx 0.53\;\text{atm}$ — a 6% safety margin), **OR**
- **Improve wetting** to lower $\theta$ (e.g., through a fresh Au-UBM, no oxide), **OR**
- **Widen the seal frame** to reduce local capillary stress concentration. *(1 mark for engineering recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the UBM stack** — examiners award 1–2 marks for explicitly naming Ti/Ni/Au and explaining each layer's role.
> 2. **Writing Sn-Pb as a current production alloy** — Sn-Pb is RoHS-restricted; students who list it without noting this typically lose a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the bonding ambient** — always mention **N₂/H₂ forming gas OR vacuum** — many students omit it.
> 4. **Numerical errors in Laplace calculation** — common mistake: using $h$ in mm instead of m; double-check SI unit consistency.
> 5. **Confusing hermetic with "sealed"** — hermeticity implies a quantitative leak-rate (e.g., < 10⁻⁸ mbar·L/s, MIL-STD-883); a mere "closed" cavity is not hermetic.
> 6. **Not comparing to design requirement** in numerical problems — always close with a **YES/NO decision + recommendation**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Solder bonding** = reflow of a low-melting metal alloy between metallized surfaces → mechanical + electrical + hermetic joint.
- ⚡ **Most-used alloy in MEMS:** **Eutectic Au-Sn (80/20), T_m = 280 °C** — gold standard for cap sealing.
- ⚡ **UBM stack:** **Ti (adhesion) / Ni (barrier) / Au (wettable top)** — non-negotiable for reliable joints.
- ⚡ **Ambient:** Forming gas (**N₂ + 4% H₂**) for standard use, **vacuum (< 10⁻³ mbar)** for hermetic packages.
- ⚡ **Key formula:** **Young-Dupré** $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$; **Laplace seal pressure** $\Delta P = 2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta / h$.
- ⚡ **IMC phases** to know: **AuSn (δ), AuSn₂ (ζ), AuSn₄ (brittle, avoid)**.
- ⚡ **Bonding force:** 1–10 MPa; **cool-down rate:** 1–5 °C/s; **reflow temperature:** 30–50 °C above T_m.
- ⚡ **Applications:** MEMS pressure sensors, accelerometers, RF-MEMS, VCSELs, 3-D IC micro-bumps, power die-attach.
- ⚡ **Advantages over other bonding methods:** low temperature, self-alignment, hermetic + electrical in one step, fluxless.
- ⚡ **Disadvantages:** IMC brittleness, cost of Au, strict UBM process control, limited to planar bond frames.
- ⚡ **Comparison baselines:** Au-Sn (280 °C) > Au-Si (363 °C) > SAC (217 °C) > Sn-Pb (183 °C, RoHS-restricted).
- ⚡ **Examiner's favourites:** UBM, ambient, IMC control, Laplace-pressure calculation, alloy selection justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organic Adhesive Wiring and Interconnections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organic Adhesive Wiring & Interconnections** refer to **polymer-matrix based conductive bonding technologies** that use thermosetting or thermoplastic organic resins loaded with metallic or non-metallic conductive fillers to provide simultaneous **electrical conduction** and **mechanical fixation** between a MEMS die / IC chip and its substrate, package, or another die.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with Module 4 — *Packaging & Interconnections in MEMS*), organic adhesive interconnections are classified under **non-solder, low-temperature, lead-free, fine-pitch compatible bonding techniques** that replace traditional Sn-Pb reflow soldering, especially where:
- The MEMS structure cannot tolerate temperatures above **~150 °C – 200 °C**.
- The metallisation is non-wettable (e.g., **Au, Al, Pt, indium-tin-oxide**).
- The interconnect pitch is below **~100 µm** (where solder bridging becomes a yield hazard).

The three principal families are:

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Conduction Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **ICA** | Isotropic Conductive Adhesive | Conducts in **all 3 directions** |
| **ACA / ACF** | Anisotropic Conductive Adhesive / Film | Conducts **only in Z-direction** |
| **NCA** | Non-Conductive Adhesive | Mechanically bonds; conduction via direct pad-to-pad contact only |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Metal-Loaded Glue"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a tube of ordinary super-glue. Now imagine you mix into it a thick paste of tiny steel ball-bearings until the glue itself is "stuffed" with metal particles. When this metallic glue is squeezed between two metal plates, the trapped ball-bearings touch each other and the two plates, **creating an electrical highway through the glue**, while the glue itself simultaneously **sticks the plates together**. That, in essence, is an *Isotropic Conductive Adhesive* (ICA). The **Anisotropic** version is the same idea, but with so few ball-bearings that conduction only happens *vertically* (across the bond-line), not sideways — preventing short-circuits between neighbouring pads.

## 1.3 Why Organic Adhesives Are Central to MEMS Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 4:** MEMS devices contain released micro-structures (cantilevers, membranes, proof masses) that are extremely **fragile, temperature-sensitive, and often surface-micromachined on the same die as the signal-conditioning ASIC**. Organic adhesive interconnections satisfy the *Hermetic-or-Not, Robust, Low-Stress, Fine-Pitch* packaging requirement better than flip-chip solder for many MEMS products.

Key engineering drivers:

- **Low Process Temperature:** Cure typically **80 °C – 200 °C**, well below SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu) solder reflow (~250 °C).
- **Fine Pitch Capability:** ACA/NCA routinely achieve **40 µm pitch**; commercial ACFs reach **10 µm**.
- **Lead-Free & RoHS/REACH Compliant:** No Pb, no flux residues that corrode MEMS structures.
- **Mechanical Compliance:** Polymer matrix absorbs **CTE mismatch stress** between silicon (CTE ≈ **2.6 ppm/K**) and PCB/ceramic (CTE ≈ **6–17 ppm/K**).
- **Compatible with Non-Wettable Surfaces:** Bonds Au, Al, even ITO without metallisation change.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Expected Values)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **typical** (board-answer) numerical values examiners expect you to quote:

- **Bulk resistivity (ICA):** $10^{-4}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ to $10^{-3}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$
- **Contact resistance (single ACA joint):** $\le 30\ \text{m}\Omega$
- **Bond-line thickness:** **20 µm – 50 µm** (ACA), **10 µm – 25 µm** (NCA)
- **Cure temperature:** **150 °C – 200 °C** (thermoset), **200 °C – 300 °C** (thermoplastic)
- **Shelf life:** **6 – 12 months** at $-10\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$):** **120 °C – 160 °C** for typical epoxy
- **Volume fraction of filler:** **25 – 35 vol%** (ICA), **5 – 10 vol%** (ACA)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Constituents of an Organic Adhesive

Every organic adhesive is a **two-phase composite**:

**Phase 1 — Polymer Matrix (Organic Binder):**
- *Epoxy resins* (DGEBA-type): most common, excellent adhesion, low shrinkage.
- *Silicone resins*: ultra-low stress, high T flexibility, used in optical MEMS.
- *Polyurethane*: flexible, moisture-resistant.
- *Acrylics / cyanate esters*: fast cure, low ionic contamination.
- *Phenolics / polyimides*: high $T_g$ for aerospace / automotive grades.

**Phase 2 — Conductive Filler Particles:**
- **Ag flakes** (most common in ICA — high conductivity, $6.3 \times 10^{5}\ \text{S/m}$).
- **Ag-coated polymer spheres** (used in ACF — lower cost, controlled diameter).
- **Ni, Cu, Au** (special applications).
- **Carbon nanotubes / graphene** (emerging nano-filled ICAs for MEMS).
- **Ni/Au-coated polystyrene** (ACF spheres, 3 µm – 10 µm diameter).

> [!TIP]
> For ICA, the **filler shape matters**: flakes give **lower percolation threshold** than spheres because flat faces touch more reliably at lower volume fractions.

## 2.2 The Three Conduction Regimes — Logical Breakdown

### Step-1 — Insulating State
If filler volume fraction $V_f < V_c$ (percolation threshold), no continuous metallic path exists → resistivity $\rho \approx 10^{14}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ (pure polymer).

### Step-2 — Percolation Threshold $V_c$
A sharp insulator-to-conductor transition occurs at the **critical volume fraction** $V_c$. Below this, conductivity is negligible; above it, conductivity jumps by **~10 orders of magnitude**.

### Step-3 — Conduction Mechanisms
Above $V_c$, current flows by:
1. **Direct metallic contact** (dominant in cured ICA).
2. **Tunnelling conduction** (thin polymer film between adjacent particles).
3. **Hopping / thermionic emission** (at high temperature / low filler loadings).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete cheat-sheet** you should memorise for the ESE. All formulae are mathematically derived in Section 3.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Percolation threshold (3-D spheres) | $V_c = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1.27}{r}\right)$ — generalised: $V_c \approx 0.16 \text{ to } 0.35$ | $r$ = particle radius scaling term | ICA design |
| 2 | Effective conductivity (Bruggeman) | $\sigma_m = \sigma_f \!\left(\dfrac{V_f - V_c}{1 - V_c}\right)^{t}$ | $\sigma_f$ filler conductivity, $t \approx 1.65$–$2.0$ | Above percolation |
| 3 | Power-law conductivity (scaling) | $\sigma \propto (V_f - V_c)^{t}$ | $t$ critical exponent | Statistical percolation |
| 4 | Joint contact resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho_c}{A_c}$ | $\rho_c$ = constriction resistivity, $A_c$ = true contact area | Single asperity |
| 5 | Multi-asperity Holm resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho}{2na}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \sigma}{H}}$ | $\rho$ bulk resistivity, $n$ no. of spots, $\sigma$ asperity deviation, $H$ hardness | Microscopic contact |
| 6 | Bond-line thermal resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{t_b}{k_b A_b}$ | $t_b$ bond-line thickness (m), $k_b$ thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $A_b$ bond area (m²) | Steady-state heat flow |
| 7 | Effective thermal conductivity | $k_{eff} = k_p(1-V_f) + k_f V_f$ | $k_p$ polymer, $k_f$ filler conductivity | Rule of mixtures (parallel) |
| 8 | Series (Maxwell) thermal model | $k_{eff} = k_p \dfrac{2k_p + k_f - 2V_f(k_p - k_f)}{2k_p + k_f + V_f(k_p - k_f)}$ | Same | Filler-in-matrix |
| 9 | CTE of composite | $\alpha_c = \alpha_p V_p + \alpha_f V_f$ (linear); or **Turner model** for finite $K$ | $V_p, V_f$ matrix & filler volume fractions | Stress analysis |
| 10 | Curing kinetics (Arrhenius) | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)^{n}$ | $\alpha$ conversion, $E_a$ activation energy, $n$ reaction order | Cure schedule design |
| 11 | Number of conductive particles between pads (ACA) | $N = \dfrac{\pi r_p^2}{r_s^2}$ | $r_p$ pad radius, $r_s$ sphere radius | ACA yield analysis |
| 12 | Capture rate of an ACA pad | $C = \dfrac{N_{avail}}{A_{pad}}$ | — | Catch efficiency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Display Industry (LCD/OLED driver ICs):** ACF is the **dominant interconnection** for COG (Chip-On-Glass) and FOG (Film-On-Glass) — used in **every smartphone, monitor, and TV**.
- **MEMS Microphone & Accelerometer Packaging:** Die-stacked MEMS + ASIC using **NCA underfill + flip-chip stud bumping**.
- **RFID Inlays:** Low-temperature ICA replaces solder for **PET-based printed antennas**.
- **Automotive Sensor ECUs:** ACF bonds survive **$-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $+150\ ^\circ\text{C}$** thermal cycling per AEC-Q100.
- **Aerospace / Defence:** Polyimide-based ICAs qualified for **space-grade hermetic MEMS packages**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Percolation Threshold for Spherical Fillers

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently asked derivation for the 14-mark question. We derive the *minimum volume fraction* of conductive filler needed to form a continuous conductive path through the polymer matrix.

**Starting premise:** Consider a 3-D simple-cubic arrangement of identical spheres of radius $r_s$ dispersed in a polymer. Conduction occurs when at least two adjacent spheres are within the *tunnelling distance* $\delta$ of each other.

**Step 1 — Volume of a single sphere:**
$$V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_s^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of the unit cube enclosing the sphere:**
$$V_{cube} = (2r_s)^{3} = 8 r_s^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric packing fraction of a single sphere:**
$$P = \dfrac{V_{sphere}}{V_{cube}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_s^{3}}{8 r_s^{3}} = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236$$

**Step 4 — Generalised expression including neighbour-shell coordination:**
For *N* coordination number (the average number of touching neighbours per sphere in a random close-packed lattice, $N \approx 6$ for 3-D),
$$P_{random} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7405 \quad \text{(HCP/FCP limit)}$$

**Step 5 — Geometric percolation threshold for a 3-D continuum random packing:**
Empirically, *Sher & Zallen* (1970) found the universal invariant:
$$V_c \cdot Z \approx 1.5$$
where $Z = $ co-ordination of the 3-D lattice. For a face-centred cubic lattice ($Z = 12$):
$$V_c = \dfrac{1.5}{12} = 0.125$$

**Step 6 — Realistic engineering correction:**
For polydisperse (mixed-size) irregular Ag flakes (not perfect spheres) used in commercial ICAs, the **practical percolation threshold is between 25 % and 35 %** by volume. Board answers generally quote:
$$V_c^{ICA} \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.30$$
$$V_c^{ACA} \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ACA needs far less filler than ICA:** In ACA, only the **Z-direction conduction** matters. A single particle sandwiched between the two bond pads is enough. Lateral continuity is *undesirable*. So the *isotropic* percolation model does not apply; instead, the *capture rate* (Equation 11 in the cheat-sheet) governs the design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Conductivity Above Percolation (Scaling Law)

**Step 1 — Define the order parameter:** Let $p$ be the fraction of occupied bonds in the 3-D network. Percolation begins at $p_c$.

**Step 2 — Define the correlation length:**
$$\xi \propto \vert p - p_c \vert^{-\nu}$$
where $\nu \approx 0.88$ in 3-D.

**Step 3 — Conductivity proportional to the connected-cluster density:** Above $p_c$, the conductivity of the network follows the **scaling law**:
$$\sigma(p) = \sigma_0 \left(p - p_c\right)^{t}$$
where $t \approx 1.65$ for 3-D networks and $t \approx 2.0$ for 2-D.

**Step 4 — Convert to volume fraction:** Replacing $p$ by the volume fraction $V_f$ (assuming each site has equal volume),
$$\sigma_m = \sigma_f \!\left(\dfrac{V_f - V_c}{1 - V_c}\right)^{t}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical ICA at $V_f = 0.30$, $V_c = 0.20$, $\sigma_f = 6.3 \times 10^5\ \text{S/m}$):**
$$\sigma_m = 6.3 \times 10^{5} \times \left(\dfrac{0.30 - 0.20}{1 - 0.20}\right)^{1.65}$$
$$= 6.3 \times 10^{5} \times \left(\dfrac{0.10}{0.80}\right)^{1.65}$$
$$= 6.3 \times 10^{5} \times (0.125)^{1.65}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the bracket term:**
$$\ln(0.125) = -2.0794 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (0.125)^{1.65} = e^{-2.0794 \times 1.65} = e^{-3.4311} \approx 0.0324$$

**Step 7 — Final value:**
$$\sigma_m \approx 6.3 \times 10^{5} \times 0.0324 \approx 2.04 \times 10^{4}\ \text{S/m}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to resistivity for comparison with solder:**
$$\rho_m = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_m} \approx 4.9 \times 10^{-5}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$$

This is **~10× higher than Sn-Pb solder** ($\rho \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$) — which is why ICA joint geometries are kept short and wide.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Contact Resistance of an Anisotropic Conductive Film Joint

**Step 1 — Geometry:** A circular bond pad of radius $r_p$ sandwiches an array of conductive spheres, each of radius $r_s$. Spheres are trapped at the bond-line.

**Step 2 — Number of trapped spheres per pad (2-D square packing):**
$$N = \dfrac{\pi r_p^{2}}{(2r_s)^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi r_p^{2}}{4 r_s^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Constriction resistance of a single sphere between two flat pads (Holm):**
$$R_{single} = \dfrac{\rho_f}{2\pi r_s} + \dfrac{\rho_p}{4 r_s}$$
where $\rho_f$ = filler (Ag-coated Ni) resistivity, $\rho_p$ = pad (Au) resistivity.

**Step 4 — Parallel combination of N spheres:**
$$R_{joint} = \dfrac{R_{single}}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** Pad radius $r_p = 50\ \mu\text{m}$, sphere radius $r_s = 3\ \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_f = 7 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ (Ni), $\rho_p = 2.2 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ (Au).

$$N = \dfrac{\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{4(3 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{36 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 218$$

$$R_{single} = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi \times 3 \times 10^{-6}} + \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 3 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$= \dfrac{7 \times 10^{-6}}{1.885 \times 10^{-5}} + \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-8}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$= 0.371\ \Omega + 0.00183\ \Omega \approx 0.373\ \Omega$$

$$R_{joint} = \dfrac{0.373}{218} \approx 1.71\ \text{m}\Omega$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board-rule of thumb:** A well-formed ACA/ACF joint shows $R_{joint} \le 30\ \text{m}\Omega$. Our derivation yields **1.71 mΩ** — comfortably within spec, validating the model.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Cycling Stress at the Bond-Line

**Step 1 — Total shear strain from CTE mismatch:**
$$\gamma = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
where $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_{substrate} - \alpha_{Si}$ and $\Delta T = T_{cure} - T_{operating}$.

**Step 2 — Maximum shear stress in the bond-line (Bogy model, simplified):**
$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{G \cdot \gamma}{f(L/h)}$$
where $G$ = shear modulus of cured adhesive, $L$ = chip half-length, $h$ = bond-line thickness, $f$ is a geometric function (≈ 1 for squat bonds).

**Step 3 — Energy release rate for delamination (KTU standard):**
$$G_c = \dfrac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)^{2} \Delta T^{2} h E^{*}}{2}$$
where $E^{*}$ = effective modulus, $h$ = bond thickness.

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:** $E^{*} = 3\ \text{GPa}$, $h = 30\ \mu\text{m}$, $\Delta\alpha = 12\ \text{ppm/K}$, $\Delta T = 200\ \text{K}$.
$$G_c = \dfrac{(12 \times 10^{-6})^{2} (200)^{2} (30 \times 10^{-6}) (3 \times 10^{9})}{2}$$
$$= \dfrac{(1.44 \times 10^{-10})(4 \times 10^{4})(9 \times 10^{-5})(3 \times 10^{9})}{2}$$
$$= \dfrac{(1.44 \times 10^{-10})(1.08 \times 10^{10})}{2} = \dfrac{1.555}{2} \approx 0.78\ \text{J/m}^{2}$$

This is **below the typical adhesion energy of a well-cured epoxy on Al/Si** ($\sim 5 \text{ J/m}^{2}$), confirming that a properly cured organic adhesive joint **will not delaminate** under the stated thermal excursion.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Trade-off

| Property | ICA | ACA / ACF | NCA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Percolation needed | Yes (high $V_f$) | No (Z-only) | No |
| Pitch limit | ~200 µm | ~10 µm | ~5 µm |
| Short-circuit risk | High | None (intrinsic) | None |
| Process window | Wide | Narrow (pressure-sensitive) | Very narrow |
| Cost | Low | High (film) | Lowest |
| Typical application | Die attach, RFID | COG, FOG, MEMS flip-chip | Low-cost MEMS underfill |
| Joint resistance | 5–20 mΩ | 1–10 mΩ | 5–30 mΩ |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[MEMS Die Released Structures on Top] --> S1[NCA or ACF Bump Deposition]
    S1 --> A1[ASIC Die Signal Conditioning]
```

## 4.4 Bond-Line Cross-Section Architecture (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TopPad[Top Metallisation Au on Si MEMS] --> TopAffected
    subgraph BondLine[BOND LINE 20 to 50 um]
        S1[Ag Coated Sphere 5 um] -.->|Z conduction| S2[Ag Coated Sphere 5 um]
        S2 -.->|Z conduction| S3[Ag Coated Sphere 5 um]
        S3 -.->|Z conduction| S4[Ag Coated Sphere 5 um]
        EpoxyMatrix[Insulating Epoxy Matrix between spheres blocks X Y conduction]
    end
    S1 --> BotAffected
    S2 --> BotAffected
    S3 --> BotAffected
    S4 --> BotAffected
    BotAffected --> BotPad[Bottom Metallisation Au on Substrate Glass or PCB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above models the **anisotropic** behaviour: each sphere has a *direct vertical* path through the bond-line (Z), but the lateral paths are blocked by insulating epoxy. This is the **defining feature of ACA/ACF** that prevents adjacent-pad short circuits.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between **ICA** and **ACA** with respect to conduction mechanism and pitch capability.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ICA — Isotropic Conductive Adhesive | ACA — Anisotropic Conductive Adhesive |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction | Conducts in **all 3 directions** above percolation threshold $V_c \approx 25 \text{–} 30\%$ | Conducts **only in Z-direction** through trapped particles |
| Filler loading | High (25–35 vol%) | Low (5–10 vol%) |
| Pitch limit | $\ge 200\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\le 40\ \mu\text{m}$ (down to 10 µm) |
| Short-circuit risk | **Yes** — random particle chains may bridge pads | **Negligible** — intrinsic anisotropy |
| Typical use | Die attach, RFID, consumer electronics | COG/FOG displays, MEMS flip-chip |

**(1 mark for defining each, 1 mark for comparison table, 1 mark for application.)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why are **organic adhesives preferred over solder** for MEMS packaging? List any **three reasons**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Low processing temperature** ($\le 200\ ^\circ\text{C}$) prevents thermal damage to released MEMS micro-structures and pre-calibrated sensors. **(1 mark)**
2. **Fine-pitch compatibility** — ACF achieves $\le 10\ \mu\text{m}$ pitch where solder bridging is uncontrollable. **(1 mark)**
3. **Mechanical compliance** — polymer matrix absorbs CTE mismatch stress between silicon and substrate, improving thermomechanical reliability. **(1 mark)**

*(Bonus credit line: lead-free, RoHS-compliant, no flux residue that can corrode MEMS.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *With Internal Choice*

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and conduction mechanism** of an **Anisotropic Conductive Film (ACF)** used for flip-chip bonding of MEMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **percolation threshold expression** for a 3-D random dispersion of spherical conductive fillers in a polymer matrix. Comment on how the value differs for **ICA vs ACA**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Construction (3 marks):**

ACF is a **three-layer composite film**:
- **Top release film** (PET, ~50 µm) — protective carrier.
- **Middle adhesive layer** — thermoset epoxy resin (15–35 µm thick) loaded with **5–10 vol%** of **Ni/Au-coated polymer spheres** (diameter **3 µm – 10 µm**).
- **Bottom release film** (PET) — protective liner peeled before bonding.

**Step 2 — Bonding Process Flow (2 marks):**

1. **Pre-clean** substrate pads (plasma O₂).
2. **Laminate** ACF onto substrate at **80–100 °C** (tacky state).
3. **Peel off bottom release film**.
4. **Place** IC/MEMS die (with Au-stud or Ni/Au bumps) on tacky ACF.
5. **Thermocompress**: apply **30–100 MPa** at **180–220 °C** for **10–30 s**.
6. The epoxy cures, **trapping conductive spheres** between the bumps and pads.

**Step 3 — Conduction Mechanism (2 marks):**
- **Z-direction:** Each trapped sphere forms a **metallic bridge** between the chip bump and the substrate pad → ohmic conduction.
- **X-Y direction:** Because filler loading is **below the lateral percolation threshold** ($< 10\ \text{vol}\%$), there is **no continuous metallic path sideways** → no shorts.
- The anisotropy is **intrinsic**, not geometric, hence reliable.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Geometric Setting (1 mark):**
Consider identical spheres of radius $r_s$ dispersed randomly in a polymer. Conduction requires two adjacent spheres to be within the tunnelling distance $\delta$ of each other.

**Step 2 — Volume of a single sphere (1 mark):**
$$V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_s^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of enclosing cube (1 mark):**
$$V_{cube} = (2r_s)^{3} = 8 r_s^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Site-occupation probability at percolation (2 marks):**
Using the *Sher–Zallen invariant* $p_c \cdot Z \approx 1.5$, where $Z = 12$ for FCC:
$$p_c = \dfrac{1.5}{12} = 0.125$$
For random close packing the geometric packing fraction is $\pi/(3\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.74$, hence the **effective volume-fraction percolation threshold** is:
$$V_c \approx p_c \times P_{rcp} = 0.125 \times 0.74 \approx 0.0925 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c \approx 10\%$$

**Step 5 — Comment on ICA vs ACA (2 marks):**

- For **ICA** (need 3-D conduction): $V_c \approx 25\text{–}30\ \text{vol}\%$. Below this, the bulk is insulating. Above it, conduction follows $\sigma \propto (V_f - V_c)^{t}$.
- For **ACA** (need only 1-D vertical conduction): $V_c \approx 5\text{–}10\ \text{vol}\%$ suffices. The threshold is essentially the **single-particle capture probability** rather than a network percolation. Hence ACA uses **far less filler**, lowering cost and avoiding lateral shorts.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the **material constituents** of an organic conductive adhesive. Discuss the role of **polymer matrix**, **conductive filler**, and **curing agent**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flip-chip joint uses an ACF with the following parameters: pad radius $r_p = 60\ \mu\text{m}$, sphere radius $r_s = 4\ \mu\text{m}$, filler resistivity $\rho_f = 8 \times 10^{-6}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$, pad metallisation resistivity $\rho_p = 2.4 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$. Compute the **number of conductive spheres trapped per pad** and the **total joint resistance**. Comment on whether the joint satisfies the KTU spec of $R_{joint} \le 30\ \text{m}\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Polymer Matrix (2.5 marks):**
The polymer matrix is the **mechanical backbone** of the adhesive. Common choices:
- **Epoxy (DGEBA):** excellent adhesion, low shrinkage, moderate $T_g$.
- **Silicone:** flexible, low stress, optical transparency.
- **Polyimide / cyanate ester:** high $T_g$ ($> 250\ ^\circ\text{C}$), aerospace grade.

Role: (i) provides **mechanical adhesion** to both die and substrate, (ii) **insulates laterally** in ACA, (iii) absorbs **thermomechanical stress** through elastic deformation.

**Step 2 — Conductive Filler (2.5 marks):**
- **Ag flakes** (ICA, 1–10 µm aspect ratio 5–50): highest conductivity, low percolation threshold.
- **Ag-coated Ni spheres** (ACA, 3–10 µm diameter): uniform size, prevents lateral conduction.
- **Au-coated polymer spheres** (ACA, low cost, light weight).
- **CNT/Graphene** (emerging nano-filler): high conductivity at sub-1 vol% loading.

Role: provides the **electrical conduction path** through the otherwise-insulating matrix.

**Step 3 — Curing Agent (2 marks):**
- **Amines (DICY, DDS)** for epoxy — latent cure at elevated T.
- **Anhydrides** — long pot-life, low exotherm.
- **Phenolic hardeners** — fast cure for high-throughput production.

Role: triggers **cross-linking reaction** that converts the liquid resin into a rigid thermoset network. The cure kinetics follow Arrhenius:
$$\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)^{n}$$
governing the cure schedule (temperature ramp, dwell time).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Number of spheres per pad (2 marks):**
$$N = \dfrac{\pi r_p^{2}}{(2 r_s)^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi (60 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{(8 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$
$$= \dfrac{\pi \cdot 3600 \times 10^{-12}}{64 \times 10^{-12}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 3600}{64}$$
$$= \dfrac{11309.7}{64} \approx 176.7 \;\Rightarrow\; N \approx 177 \text{ spheres}$$

**Step 2 — Single-sphere Holm constriction resistance (2.5 marks):**
$$R_{single} = \dfrac{\rho_f}{2\pi r_s} + \dfrac{\rho_p}{4 r_s}$$
$$= \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} + \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$= \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{2.513 \times 10^{-5}} + \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$= 0.3183\ \Omega + 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega \approx 0.320\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Total joint resistance (1.5 marks):**
$$R_{joint} = \dfrac{R_{single}}{N} = \dfrac{0.320}{177} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega = 1.81\ \text{m}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Spec compliance (1 mark):**
$$R_{joint} = 1.81\ \text{m}\Omega \;\ll\; 30\ \text{m}\Omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{SPEC SATISFIED.}$$

The joint has **~16× margin** over the spec, indicating a robust design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic (KTU ESE 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing ICA and ACA conduction thresholds.** ICA needs ~25–30 vol%; ACA needs only ~5–10 vol%. Many students write the *same* threshold for both — **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to justify why ACF is preferred over solder for MEMS.** Just stating "low temperature" without linking it to *released MEMS structures* loses the application-context mark.
> 3. **Mixing up "isotropic percolation" with "anisotropic capture rate."** Percolation theory applies to ICA. For ACA the conduction is a *deterministic single-particle bridge*, not a percolation phenomenon. Examiners dock **−3 marks** if you treat ACA as a 3-D percolation problem.
> 4. **Unit errors in joint-resistance derivation.** The contact-resistance derivation involves $2\pi r_s$ and $4 r_s$ factors with **metres**, not micrometres. Forget the $10^{-6}$ conversion and you get $\sim 10^{12}$ error. **−2 marks.**
> 5. **Skipping the bond-line thickness.** When asked "what is the bond-line thickness in ACF?", answer **20 µm – 50 µm**. Writing "very thin" or "a few microns" loses the numerical mark.
> 6. **No diagram for ACF construction.** Always include the **three-layer release-film / adhesive / release-film** structure. **−2 marks if missing.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — pin this on your wall the night before ESE.**

- **Organic Adhesive** = *Polymer Matrix* + *Conductive Filler* + *Curing Agent* (+ additives like coupling agents, surfactants, hardeners).
- **Three families:** **ICA** (isotropic, high filler), **ACA / ACF** (anisotropic, low filler, Z-only), **NCA** (no filler, pad-to-pad direct contact).
- **ICA percolation threshold $V_c$** ≈ **25 – 30 vol%**. Below this = insulator; above = conductor following $\sigma \propto (V_f - V_c)^{t}$, $t \approx 1.65$ (3-D).
- **ACA $V_c$** is not a percolation problem — it's a **single-particle capture** model. Loading is just **5 – 10 vol%**.
- **Standard cure schedule:** **150 – 200 °C for 30 min** (epoxy) or **180 – 220 °C, 10 – 30 s** (ACF thermocompression).
- **Typical bond-line thickness:** **20 – 50 µm** (ACA/ACF), **10 – 25 µm** (NCA).
- **Joint resistance target:** $\le 30\ \text{m}\Omega$ per I/O pad (single-particle bridge model).
- **Pitch limit:** ACF achieves **~10 µm** pitch — beyond solder capability.
- **Process steps:** pre-clean → laminate ACF → peel release film → place die → thermocompress → cure → test.
- **Advantages over solder:** low T, fine pitch, lead-free, flux-free, CTE-mismatch tolerant.
- **Disadvantages:** lower electrical/thermal conductivity than solder, possible silver-migration under humidity, narrow process window.
- **Key equation to memorise (board-favourite):**
$$V_c \cdot Z \approx 1.5, \quad \sigma_m = \sigma_f\!\left(\dfrac{V_f - V_c}{1 - V_c}\right)^{t}, \quad R_{joint} = \dfrac{\rho_f/(2\pi r_s) + \rho_p/(4 r_s)}{N}$$
- **Applications to recite:** COG/FOG displays (ACF), RFID inlays (ICA), MEMS microphone/accelerometer stack (NCA + underfill), automotive AEC-Q100 sensors (ACF).
- **Always end with the spec check:** compare $R_{joint}$ computed to the KTU spec of $\le 30\ \text{m}\Omega$ and explicitly state *"SPEC SATISFIED"* or *"SPEC VIOLATED"* — examiners reward the explicit verdict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wire Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT612 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wire Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Wire Bonding: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wire Bonding** is a *solid-state, thermo-mechanical interconnection* technique used in microelectronic and MEMS packaging to establish electrical and mechanical continuity between the die (chip) pad and the package lead frame, substrate, or PCB. A fine metal wire (typically **25–75 µm** in diameter) is deformed and bonded to metallized surfaces using a combination of *force, heat, and/or ultrasonic energy*, forming a low-resistance, gas-tight, and mechanically robust joint.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEICT612 — MEMS)**, wire bonding is studied as the *first-level interconnect* (also called the *chip-to-package interconnect*) and forms a critical part of the **MEMS Packaging & Assembly** module. It is the *most commercially dominant* interconnect technology, accounting for over **90%** of all interconnections in the integrated circuit (IC) and MEMS packaging industry.

### 1.2 Types of Wire Bonding (KTU Taxonomy)

| # | Type | Bonding Mechanism | Typical Wire |
|---|------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Ball (Nail-Head) Bonding** | Capillary tool forms a free-air ball; first bond = ball, second bond = crescent/stitch | Au, Cu |
| 2 | **Wedge (Stud / Stitch) Bonding** | Wedge tool deforms wire; both bonds are wedge-shaped, directional | Al, Au |
| 3 | **Ribbon Bonding** | Flat ribbon replaces round wire; used in high-power / microwave MEMS | Al, Au |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tiny Nail-and-Hammer on a Microchip"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are a jeweler joining two tiny gold chains to a pendant and a ring. Instead of soldering, you press the chain ends against the metal surfaces and *hammer* them flat until they fuse. Now shrink the chains to **half the width of a human hair (≈ 50 µm)** and the pendant to a *silicon chip the size of a fingernail*. That is essentially what a wire bonder does — it uses a precision capillary (the "hammer") to deform a metal wire against a metal pad under controlled force, heat, and ultrasonic vibration, producing a permanent metallic bond.

> [!TIP]
> **Why wire bonding matters in MEMS:** Unlike standard ICs, MEMS devices often contain *moving microstructures* (beams, membranes, cantilevers). Wire bonds must therefore be *low-stress, low-loop, and high-flexibility* to avoid imparting mechanical load onto the fragile MEMS element — making **process optimization** a critical learning outcome of this module.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Board High-Yield)

- **Wire diameter:** **25 µm, 32 µm, 50 µm, 75 µm** (1 mil ≈ 25.4 µm)
- **Bond pad pitch:** As low as **35 µm** (fine-pitch bonding) in advanced packaging
- **Pull strength:** **> 3 grams-force** for 25 µm Au wire (industry standard)
- **Shear strength:** **> 30 g** for ball bond on Al pad
- **Bonding temperature:** **100 °C – 400 °C** depending on technique
- **Ultrasonic frequency:** **60 kHz – 120 kHz**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do not confuse **wire bonding (chip-to-package)** with **wafer-level packaging (WLP)** interconnects such as flip-chip bumps or TSVs. Wire bonding is a *post-dicing* operation; flip-chip and WLP are *wafer-level* operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Bonding Mechanisms (Core of Module 4)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **three fundamental bonding mechanisms**. A 14-mark question is almost guaranteed to test the comparative analysis of these three. Master them thoroughly.

### 2.1.1 Thermocompression Bonding (TC)

- **Principle:** Bonding is achieved by simultaneous application of *heat* and *force* (no ultrasonic energy).
- **Temperature range:** **300 °C – 400 °C**
- **Force:** **0.1 N – 0.5 N** per bond
- **Bonding time:** A few hundred milliseconds
- **Material pair:** Au wire on Au metallized pad (Au-Au system gives excellent interdiffusion).
- **Mechanism:** At elevated temperature, the contact surfaces undergo *atomic interdiffusion* and *creep deformation*, forming a metallurgical bond.

$$\text{Bond Formation} \propto (T \cdot \sigma \cdot t)$$

where $T$ is temperature, $\sigma$ is applied stress, and $t$ is bonding time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** High temperatures may damage temperature-sensitive MEMS elements (e.g., polymer-based bio-MEMS, optical coatings).

### 2.1.2 Ultrasonic Bonding (US / US-Wedge)

- **Principle:** Bonding is achieved at *room temperature* using *ultrasonic vibration* (typically **60–120 kHz**) combined with *clamp force*. No external heating.
- **Temperature range:** **25 °C – 150 °C** (stage may be slightly warmed)
- **Force:** **0.05 N – 0.3 N**
- **Material pair:** **Al wire on Al pad** (most common for power devices and MEMS).
- **Mechanism:** Ultrasonic energy softens the metal locally via *acoustic softening* and removes surface oxides by *micro-slip*; the two clean surfaces are then pressed into atomic contact.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Al-Al is so popular in MEMS:** Aluminium metallization is the standard *top-layer* material in CMOS and MEMS processes. Al-Al wire bonding avoids the need for a gold-compatible barrier layer, reducing cost.

### 2.1.3 Thermosonic Bonding (TS) — The Industrial Standard

- **Principle:** Combines *heat + ultrasonic vibration + force*. This is the **dominant industry method** for gold ball bonding.
- **Temperature range:** **100 °C – 200 °C** (significantly lower than TC)
- **Force:** **0.05 N – 0.2 N**
- **Ultrasonic frequency:** **60 kHz – 120 kHz**
- **Material pair:** Au wire on Al pad (most common); also Cu wire on Al pad.
- **Mechanism:** Mild heat + ultrasonic energy produces *softening + oxide scrubbing + interdiffusion* at much lower temperatures than thermocompression.

### 2.2 Detailed Process Flow — Gold Ball Bonding (KTU Favourite)

1. **Clamp & Feed:** Wire is fed through the ceramic capillary tool.
2. **EFO (Electronic Flame-Off):** A high-voltage spark melts the wire tip, forming a **free-air ball (FAB)** of diameter $\approx 1.5 \times$ wire diameter.
3. **First Bond (Ball Bond):** The ball is pressed against the die pad with force + ultrasonic. The ball deforms into a *fish-tail / nail-head* shape.
4. **Loop Formation:** Capillary rises and moves laterally, creating a *loop height* (typically **75 µm – 200 µm**).
5. **Second Bond (Stitch / Crescent):** Wire is bonded to the lead frame / substrate.
6. **Wire Clamp & Tear:** Clamp closes, capillary rises, breaking the wire at the heel and leaving a *fish-tail tail*.
7. **Repeat:** Next bond cycle starts with a new EFO ball.

### 2.3 Critical Bonding Parameters — The "Big Five"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 7-mark sub-question will typically ask: *"List and explain the process parameters affecting bond quality."* Memorize these **five**:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Bond |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Bonding Force** | $F$ | 0.05–0.5 N | Insufficient → weak bond; Excess → cratering of Si |
| 2 | **Ultrasonic Power** | $P_{US}$ | 0.5–5 W | Too low → non-stick; Too high → pad peel |
| 3 | **Bonding Temperature** | $T$ | 25–400 °C | Higher T → softer metal → lower required force |
| 4 | **Bonding Time** | $t$ | 10–100 ms | Longer → more interdiffusion; risk of over-bonding |
| 5 | **Ultrasonic Frequency** | $f$ | 60–120 kHz | Sets vibration amplitude; constant per machine |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A_{ball} = \pi \, d_{wire}^{2}$ | Cross-sectional area of wire (used for current-carrying) | $\mu m^{2}$ |
| 2 | $D_{FAB} \approx 1.5 \cdot d_{wire}$ | Free-air ball diameter relation | $\mu m$ |
| 3 | $F_{pull} \geq 3 \text{ g}$ (for 25 µm Au) | Minimum acceptable pull strength | g-force |
| 4 | $R_{contact} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \, a}$ | Spreading resistance of a circular contact, where $a$ is bond radius | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $\sigma_{bond} = \dfrac{F}{A_{bond}}$ | Bond shear / interfacial stress | Pa |
| 6 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Ultrasonic horn resonance relation | Hz |
| 7 | $E_{bond} = P_{US} \cdot t$ | Total ultrasonic energy delivered to the bond | J |
| 8 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{R_{wire} + R_{contact}}{R_{pure\,Al}}$ | Contact-resistance ratio (quality metric) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) were used inside the table cells to avoid Markdown table breaking — LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` substitutes are not required for these equations.**

### 2.5 Failure Modes (KTU Board Loves This)

- **Non-stick (lift-off):** Insufficient US energy or force; contaminated pad.
- **Cratering:** Excessive force punches through the bond pad, damaging the underlying Si — *the most feared failure in MEMS due to fragile structures*.
- **Peeling:** Pad metallization separates from die.
- **Heel Break:** Wire fractures at the heel of the bond due to stress concentration.
- **Splash / Tail Short:** Excessive FAB size shorts to adjacent wires.
- **Kirkendall Voiding:** Long-term reliability issue at Au-Al interface (forms brittle Au-Al intermetallics like **AuAl₂, Au₅Al₂, Au₂Al** — purple plague).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wire bonding is ubiquitous in:
- **RF-MEMS** filters and switches
- **MEMS microphones and accelerometers**
- **Pressure sensors** (automotive, medical)
- **Bio-MEMS / Lab-on-Chip** devices
- **Power MEMS** and high-current applications (use Al or Cu ribbon bonding)
- **Optoelectronic MEMS (MOEMS)** packaging

> [!TIP]
> **Industry fact:** *Apple's iPhone* MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers (Bosch / STMicroelectronics) use *Au ball bonding on Al pads* — the textbook thermosonic case study.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Free-Air Ball (FAB) Diameter from Surface Tension

The **free-air ball** is formed when the wire tip is melted by EFO. In equilibrium, the molten droplet is held by surface tension, so its diameter is set by a balance between *gravitational* and *surface-tension* forces.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation:** The mass of the molten ball equals the mass of the wire that was melted.

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{ball} &= m_{wire\,melted} \\[4pt]
\rho_{Au} \cdot V_{ball} &= \rho_{Au} \cdot A_{wire} \cdot L_{melt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute volumes:** $V_{ball} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{D_{FAB}}{2}\right)^{3}$ and $A_{wire} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{D_{FAB}}{2}\right)^{3} &= \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot L_{melt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Surface-tension equilibrium (capillary length):** For a hanging molten droplet, the *capillary length* governs stability:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{cap} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{\rho g}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\gamma$ is the *surface tension* (for Au, $\gamma \approx 1.1 \,\text{N/m}$) and $g$ is gravity.

**Step 4 — Empirical result:** When $L_{melt} \leq L_{cap}$, the droplet pinches off into a stable ball. The empirical relation widely used in industry (and accepted in KTU model answers) is:

$$D_{FAB} \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot d_{wire}$$

For 25 µm Au wire, this gives $D_{FAB} \approx 37.5$–62.5 µm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the conversion:** We started from mass conservation (intuitive), then introduced the capillary length to justify why the molten metal forms a *sphere* (minimum surface energy) rather than dripping. The empirical 1.5× factor is a KTU-accepted industry approximation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spreading Resistance of a Ball Bond

For high-frequency MEMS (RF-MEMS), the bond contact resistance is critical. The *spreading resistance* of a circular contact is derived from electrostatics analogy.

**Step 1 — Current flow analogy:** The current lines spread radially from a circular contact of radius $a$ into a semi-infinite conductor.

**Step 2 — Resistance of a hemispherical shell at radius $r$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
dR &= \rho \cdot \dfrac{dr}{2 \pi r^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from contact radius to infinity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{contact} &= \int_{a}^{\infty} \rho \cdot \dfrac{dr}{2 \pi r^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi} \left[-\dfrac{1}{r}\right]_{a}^{\infty} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi a}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final form (often cited without the $2\pi$):**

$$R_{contact} = \dfrac{\rho}{2 a} \;\; \text{(approx., in ohm-m units)}$$

For a **25 µm Au ball bond** on Al pad: $a \approx 12.5 \,\mu m$, $\rho_{Au} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-8} \,\Omega m$.

$$R_{contact} \approx \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 12.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 9.6 \times 10^{-4} \,\Omega \approx 1 \,\text{m}\Omega$$

**Interpretation:** Bond contact resistance is in the *milliohm* range — negligible compared with wire resistance for most MEMS signal-level applications, but significant for **high-current / power-MEMS** cases.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bond Strength Model (Force Balance on a Ball Bond)

Consider a ball bond of diameter $D_{FAB}$ deformed into a flattened disc of thickness $h$ and radius $r_d$ under force $F$.

**Step 1 — Volume conservation during deformation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{ball} &= V_{disc} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{D_{FAB}}{2}\right)^{3} &= \pi r_{d}^{2} \cdot h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress at the bond interface:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{F}{\pi r_{d}^{2}} = \dfrac{F}{A_{bond}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Pull-strength relation (industry heuristic):** Empirical correlation between *pull force* $F_{pull}$ and *ball bond diameter*:

$$F_{pull} = k \cdot D_{FAB}^{1.5}$$

where $k \approx 0.15$ (g/µm^{1.5}) for Au ball bonds on Al pads.

**Numerical example (25 µm Au wire):**

$$D_{FAB} \approx 1.5 \times 25 = 37.5 \,\mu m$$

$$F_{pull} = 0.15 \times (37.5)^{1.5} \approx 0.15 \times 229.7 \approx 34.5 \,\text{g-force}$$

This is well above the **3 g** minimum industry spec — explaining why 25 µm Au wire is the industry default for fine-pitch, high-reliability MEMS packaging.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Decision Variable | Thermocompression | Ultrasonic | Thermosonic |
|-------------------|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| Temperature-sensitive MEMS? | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Au wire? | ⚠️ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Al wire? | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Cu wire? | ❌ | ⚠️ | ✅ |
| Fine pitch (< 60 µm)? | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ |
| High-current power MEMS? | ❌ | ✅ (Al) | ⚠️ |
| Cost-sensitive consumer MEMS? | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |

(✅ recommended | ⚠️ possible with care | ❌ not preferred)

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 32 µm gold wire is used for thermosonic ball bonding. The free-air ball diameter is 1.6 times the wire diameter. Calculate:
(a) The free-air ball diameter.
(b) The bond area, assuming a flattened disc with thickness equal to 1.5 times the wire diameter.
(c) The minimum acceptable pull force using the empirical relation $F_{pull} = 0.15 D_{FAB}^{1.5}$ (with $D$ in µm, $F$ in g).

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{FAB} &= 1.6 \times d_{wire} = 1.6 \times 32 = 51.2 \,\mu m
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):** Using volume conservation with $h = 1.5 d_{wire} = 48 \,\mu m$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{51.2}{2}\right)^{3} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (25.6)^{3} \approx 70{,}225 \,\mu m^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{bond} &= \dfrac{V}{h} = \dfrac{70{,}225}{48} \approx 1{,}463 \,\mu m^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{pull} &= 0.15 \times (51.2)^{1.5} = 0.15 \times 366.0 \approx 54.9 \,\text{g-force}
\end{aligned}$$

✅ This exceeds the **3 g** minimum industry spec — bond is acceptable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wire Bonding Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ctrl["CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"]
        ctrlA["HMI Touch Panel"]
        ctrlB["Microcontroller PLC"]
        ctrlC["Bond Recipe Memory"]
    end

        pwrA["Pneumatic Pressure Regulator"]
        pwrB["Ultrasonic Generator 60 to 120 kHz"]

    end

    subgraph motion["MOTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        motA["X Y Z Theta Axes"]
        motB["Bond Head Z Actuator"]
        motC["Pattern Recognition Camera"]
        motD["Wire Spool Feeder"]
    end

    subgraph bondTool["BONDING TOOL"]
        toolA["Ceramic Capillary"]
        toolB["Flange and Horn Transducer"]
```
