# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects. Why is this duality essential for MEMS devices?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Piezoelectric Effect | Converse Piezoelectric Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Phenomenon | Applied stress generates charge | Applied voltage generates strain |
| MEMS Use | Sensing (stress/force → voltage) | Actuation (voltage → displacement) |
| Coefficient | $d$ (C/N) | Same $d$ (m/V) by reciprocity |

**Why essential:** The same piezoelectric coefficient $d$ governs both effects (Maxwell reciprocity), allowing the **same material** to be used as both sensor and actuator in a single MEMS device — this is the basis of **resonant MEMS** like piezo-electrically transduced gyroscopes and filters.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the force in a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator proportional to $V^2$ and not $V$? What is the consequence for AC vs. DC actuation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The electrostatic force is the **gradient of stored energy**:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

Since energy stored is $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$, taking the spatial derivative yields a $V^2$ dependence. The $V^2$ relationship means:

1. **The force is always attractive** (regardless of voltage polarity) — because $V^2$ is always positive.
2. **For DC actuation**, the force is constant (no mechanical motion from force change).
3. **For AC actuation** at frequency $\omega$, the force has components at $0$ and $2\omega$:
$$V^2 = V_0^2 \sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{V_0^2}{2} (1 - \cos(2\omega t))$$
The actuation frequency is therefore **double** the electrical drive frequency — a frequently tested subtlety.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the pull-in voltage of a parallel-plate electrostatic MEMS actuator. Explain why this phenomenon limits the tunable range of capacitive sensors.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A MEMS capacitive accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 1\ \mu\text{g}$, suspension spring constant $k = 1\ \text{N/m}$, plate area $A = 100 \times 100\ \mu\text{m}^2$, and initial gap $d_0 = 2\ \mu\text{m}$. Calculate (i) the pull-in voltage, (ii) the linear sensing range (in terms of displacement), and (iii) the sensitivity $\Delta C/\Delta a$ (capacitance change per unit acceleration in fF/g). Assume $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air gap).

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Geometry and forces:** The movable plate is suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$. When a voltage $V$ is applied between the plates, an attractive electrostatic force pulls the movable plate toward the fixed plate.

**Step 2 — Force equations:** The electrostatic force and spring restoring force are, respectively:

$$F_e(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2}, \quad F_s(x) = kx$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition:** Setting $F_e = F_s$ and requiring the slope condition for stability $\frac{\partial F_s}{\partial x} > \frac{\partial F_e}{\partial x}$, the simultaneous solution yields:

$$x_{PI} = \frac{d_0}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:** Substituting back gives the pull-in voltage:

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}}$$

**Step 5 — Limitation on sensor range (3 marks of 7):**
Below $V_{PI}$, the sensor is stable and the displacement is single-valued. As the gap shrinks to $d_0/3$, the spring force cannot balance the rapidly increasing electrostatic force — the plate snaps to contact. Therefore, the **useful linear sensing range** is restricted to $0 \leq x < d_0/3$, i.e., only **33% of the gap** is usable. This is the **fundamental tradeoff** in parallel-plate capacitive MEMS.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given:**
$m = 10^{-9}$ kg, $k = 1$ N/m, $A = (100 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 10^{-8}$ m², $d_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

**Part (i) Pull-in voltage:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-8}}}$$

Numerator:

$$8 \times 1 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 6.4 \times 10^{-17}$$

Denominator:

$$27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-8} = 2.39 \times 10^{-18}$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-17}}{2.39 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{26.78} \approx 5.18\ \text{V}$$

**Part (ii) Linear sensing range:**

$$\Delta x_{\max} = \frac{d_0}{3} = \frac{2\ \mu\text{m}}{3} \approx 0.667\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Part (iii) Sensitivity $\Delta C / \Delta a$:**

Under acceleration $a$, the proof-mass displacement is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{m a}{k}$$

The corresponding capacitance (linearized) is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F} = 44.27\ \text{fF}$$

The change in capacitance per unit acceleration:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{\Delta a} = \frac{C_0}{d_0} \cdot \frac{m}{k} = \frac{44.27 \times 10^{-15}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} \times \frac{10^{-9}}{1}$$

$$= 22.135 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{-9} = 2.21 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{F/g} = 22.1\ \text{fF/g}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a charge amplifier used as the front-end readout for piezoelectric MEMS sensors. Derive the expression for output voltage and discuss the role of feedback resistor $R_f$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoelectric MEMS pressure sensor uses PZT with $d_{33} = 400$ pC/N. The active area is $1 \text{ mm}^2$ and the applied force varies from 0 to 10 mN. The sensor is connected to a charge amplifier with feedback capacitance $C_f = 10$ pF. Calculate the range of output voltage.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Block Diagram (refer to Figure 4.2 above).** The piezoelectric element acts as a **charge source** $Q = d_{33} F A$ in parallel with the inherent capacitance $C_p$ of the piezo element. The non-inverting input of the op-amp is grounded, and the inverting input is a **virtual ground**.

**Derivation:**

At the virtual-ground node, the charge from the piezo flows entirely through the feedback capacitor $C_f$. By the op-amp's virtual-ground property, no current flows into its inputs. Therefore:

$$Q = -C_f V_{out} \implies V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} F A}{C_f}$$

**Role of $R_f$:** The feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected in parallel with $C_f$ to provide a **DC discharge path**, preventing the op-amp from drifting to saturation due to the input bias current. The lower cutoff frequency of the system is:

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$

A higher $R_f$ lowers the cutoff frequency, allowing low-frequency signals to be measured accurately.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given:** $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, $A = 1\ \text{mm}^2 = 10^{-6}$ m², $F = 0$ to $10\ \text{mN} = 10^{-2}$ N, $C_f = 10\ \text{pF} = 10^{-11}$ F.

**Maximum charge:**

$$Q_{\max} = d_{33} \cdot F_{\max} \cdot A = (400 \times 10^{-12}) \times (10^{-2}) \times (10^{-6})$$

$$Q_{\max} = 400 \times 10^{-20} = 4 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{C} = 4\ \text{fC}$$

**Maximum output voltage:**

$$V_{out,\max} = -\frac{Q_{\max}}{C_f} = -\frac{4 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-11}} = -4 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{V} = -0.4\ \mu\text{V}$$

**Minimum output voltage:** $V_{out,\min} = 0$ V (at $F = 0$).

**Output range:** $0$ to $-0.4\ \mu\text{V}$ (the negative sign indicates the inverting amplifier configuration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Comment:** A 0.4 µV signal is extremely small and would be buried in op-amp offset and noise. In practice, the designer would either (i) increase the piezo area, (ii) cascade a second-stage amplifier, or (iii) use a charge amplifier with a *smaller* $C_f$ (e.g., 1 pF gives 4 µV output). This is a typical KTU "design tradeoff" follow-up question.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE Module 4 — Electrical:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_r$ in the pull-in formula.** The full expression is $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)}$. Dropping $\varepsilon_r$ (especially when the medium is not air) costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $d_{33}$ and $g_{33}$.** $d_{33}$ is in C/N (charge output); $g_{33}$ is in V·m/N (voltage output). The relation is $g_{33} = d_{33}/\varepsilon^E$. KTU will test this.
> 3. **Wrong direction of pull-in force.** The electrostatic force is *always attractive*, even when you reverse the voltage polarity. Students write $F \propto V$ (linear) instead of $F \propto V^2$ (quadratic) — this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Charge amplifier sign error.** The inverting charge amplifier has $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$. Dropping the negative sign loses a mark and often masks physical insight.
> 5. **Units in piezo voltage formula.** $V_{oc} = g_{33} F t$ — students forget the thickness $t$ in the denominator of $g_{33}$ (which is V·m/N) is what produces the correct volt output.
> 6. **Failure to specify bandwidth in noise calculations.** Johnson noise is $\propto \sqrt{\Delta f}$. A calculation without explicit $\Delta f$ gets zero.
> 7. **Mixing up gauge factor $G$ and piezoresistive coefficient $\pi$.** $G = 1 + 2\nu + \pi Y$, where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio and $Y$ is Young's modulus.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

### Core Equations (Must Memorize Symbol-by-Symbol)
- Parallel-plate capacitance: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$
- Linearized capacitive sensitivity: $\Delta C / C_0 = \Delta d / d_0$
- Electrostatic force: $F = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2 d^2)$
- Pull-in voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$
- Pull-in displacement: $\Delta x_{PI} = d_0 / 3$
- Piezo charge: $Q = d_{33} F A$
- Piezo voltage: $V_{oc} = g_{33} F t$
- Charge amplifier output: $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$
- Johnson noise: $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$
- Piezoresistive gauge: $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$
- Resonant frequency: $f_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$

### Conceptual Takeaways
- Capacitive MEMS = high sensitivity + low power + CMOS compatible → dominant commercial technology.
- Electrostatic actuation is **quadratic in V** and always attractive — restrict motion to $d_0/3$ in gap-closing designs.
- Piezoelectric materials enable **bilateral** sensing/actuation through the *same* coefficient $d$.
- Piezoresistive readout in silicon is unrivaled for **DC and low-frequency** strain measurement.
- Charge amplifier is the **universal piezo interface** — without it, the high-impedance charge dissipates instantly.
- **Johnson noise sets the fundamental floor** of any resistive MEMS readout — there is no "noise-free" measurement.
- Closed-loop (force-balance) readout is preferred for high-precision, wide-dynamic-range applications.

### Key Numbers for Numericals
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $V_T = 25.85$ mV at 300 K
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Pull-in ratio = 1/3 (gap-closing parallel plate)

### Design Tradeoffs to Always Mention in Descriptive Answers
- **Sensitivity vs. Linear Range:** Larger area or smaller gap → higher sensitivity but smaller pull-in margin.
- **Bandwidth vs. Noise:** Wider bandwidth integrates more Johnson noise.
- **Open-loop vs. Closed-loop:** Open-loop is fast but nonlinear; closed-loop is linear but bandwidth-limited.
- **Capacitive vs. Piezoelectric readout:** Capacitive needs carrier excitation; piezo is self-generating but high-impedance.
- **Spring stiffness vs. Sensitivity:** A softer spring (lower $k$) gives higher mechanical sensitivity but lower resonant frequency and worse shock tolerance.
